topics.title: Use and scope of mathematical modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Use and scope of mathematical modelling # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Mathematical Modelling > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical Modelling** is the process of translating a real-world physical, chemical, or engineering problem into a precise mathematical language using variables, parameters, constants, relations, and functions such that the behaviour of the system can be predicted, simulated, optimised, or controlled under specified conditions. In the context of **Polymer Materials Engineering**, mathematical modelling involves representing the structural, thermal, rheological, mechanical, and kinetic behaviour of polymeric systems (synthesis, processing, and end-use performance) using analytical and numerical equations. The **scope of mathematical modelling** refers to the range of problems, materials, and processes that can be addressed using such quantitative formulations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are baking a cake. You do not physically re-bake thousands of cakes to know how much sugar or how much heat is needed. Instead, you develop a "recipe equation" — *Taste = f(Sugar, Flour, Heat, Time)*. This equation **is** your mathematical model. It lets you predict the cake's sweetness without actually baking it. Similarly, a polymer engineer does not manufacture every possible blend of polyethylene. They develop a *viscosity model* $\eta = f(M_w, T, \dot{\gamma})$ and use it to **predict** the flow behaviour during extrusion. ### 1.3 Core Components of a Mathematical Model Every mathematical model — irrespective of the discipline — is composed of the following fundamental building blocks: - **Variables:** Quantities that change (e.g., time $t$, temperature $T$, concentration $C$). - **Parameters:** Fixed system-specific values (e.g., activation energy $E_a$, gas constant $R$). - **Constants:** Universal values (e.g., Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, Avogadro number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹). - **Relations:** Equations connecting variables and parameters. - **Domain & Boundary Conditions:** Specified limits and constraints. ### 1.4 Why Mathematical Modelling is Indispensable in Polymer Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > The **scope** of mathematical modelling in PEPOT755 spans every segment of a polymer's life cycle: **monomer synthesis → polymerisation kinetics → melt processing → solid-state morphology → mechanical performance → recycling/degradation.** Mathematical modelling is the silent engine behind process design, scale-up, and product innovation. | Life-Cycle Stage | Model Example | Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | Polymerisation | Kinetic chain-growth equation | Reactor design & conversion prediction | | Melt Rheology | Power-law / Carreau model | Extrusion die design | | Crystallisation | Avrami equation | Injection moulding cycle time | | Viscoelasticity | Maxwell / Kelvin-Voigt models | Predicting creep in plastic parts | | Diffusion | Fick's laws | Permeation through packaging films | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Steps in Developing a Mathematical Model (The Modelling Cycle) The construction of a mathematical model is a **closed-loop iterative process**. Every polymer engineer follows this standard sequence: 1. **Problem Identification:** Clearly define the engineering problem (e.g., predict the viscosity of a polymer melt during injection moulding). 2. **Assumption Formulation:** Simplify the system by neglecting non-essential effects (e.g., assume steady-state, isothermal flow). 3. **Mathematical Translation:** Convert physical laws (conservation of mass, momentum, energy) into differential, integral, or algebraic equations. 4. **Solution Strategy Selection:** Choose analytical (closed-form) or numerical (e.g., Finite Difference, Finite Element) solution paths. 5. **Validation & Verification:** Compare model predictions with experimental data. Calculate the error margin. 6. **Refinement / Iteration:** Adjust assumptions or refine parameters if discrepancies exceed acceptable limits. 7. **Deployment & Prediction:** Use the validated model for design, optimisation, and control. ## 2.2 Classification of Mathematical Models Mathematical models used in polymer engineering are categorised as follows: - **Deterministic Models:** Outputs are uniquely defined by inputs (e.g., Newtonian flow equation $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$). - **Stochastic / Probabilistic Models:** Outputs involve randomness (e.g., Monte Carlo simulation of polymer chain conformations). - **Empirical / Black-Box Models:** Derived purely from experimental data fitting (e.g., polynomial regression of viscosity vs. temperature). - **Mechanistic / First-Principle Models:** Built from underlying physics and chemistry (e.g., Flory-Huggins solution theory). - **Static vs. Dynamic Models:** Time-independent (equilibrium) vs. time-dependent (transient). - **Continuous vs. Discrete Models:** Continuous variables (concentration field) vs. discrete entities (molecular dynamics of atoms). - **Lumped vs. Distributed Parameter Models:** A single ODE vs. a PDE with spatial gradients. ## 2.3 Scope of Mathematical Modelling in Polymer Engineering > [!NOTE] > The **scope** defines the *extent* and *applicability* of the model across: > - **Materials:** Thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers, blends, composites, biopolymers. > - **Processes:** Injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding, thermoforming, 3D-printing, fibre spinning. > - **Length Scales:** Quantum → Molecular (MD) → Meso (DPD) → Continuum (CFD) → Macro (process). > - **Time Scales:** Femtoseconds (vibrations) → seconds (flow) → years (creep, ageing). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table compiles the **high-yield mathematical models** that a student must know for KTU examinations. These equations are the backbone of polymer modelling and simulation. | Model Name | Governing Equation | Domain / Application | Key Parameters | |---|---|---|---| | Arrhenius (Kinetics) | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Reaction rate, viscosity, degradation | $A$ (pre-exponential), $E_a$ (activation energy), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | | Power-Law Fluid | $\eta = K \, \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Shear-thinning polymer melts | $K$ (consistency), $n$ (power-law index) | | Carreau Model | $\eta = \eta_{\infty} + (\eta_0 - \eta_{\infty})[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2]^{(n-1)/2}$ | Full-shear-rate rheology | $\eta_0$, $\eta_{\infty}$, $\lambda$, $n$ | | Arrhenius-Temperature | $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \, e^{E_a / RT}$ | Temperature dependence of viscosity | $E_a / R$ | | Avrami Crystallisation | $X_c(t) = 1 - e^{-k t^n}$ | Isothermal crystallisation kinetics | $k$ (rate constant), $n$ (Avrami index) | | Fick's 1st Law (Diffusion) | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Mass transport in polymers | $D$ (diffusivity, m²/s) | | Fick's 2nd Law | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Transient permeation | $D$ | | Maxwell Model (Viscoelastic) | $\sigma + \lambda \dot{\sigma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Stress relaxation | $\lambda$ (relaxation time), $\eta$ | | Kelvin-Voigt Model | $\sigma = E \varepsilon + \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Creep behaviour | $E$, $\eta$ | | Flory-Huggins (Mixing) | $\Delta G_{mix} = RT [n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi \phi_1 \phi_2]$ | Polymer solution thermodynamics | $\chi$ (interaction parameter) | | WLF Equation | $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | Time-temperature superposition | $C_1$, $C_2$, $T_g$ | | Mooney-Rivlin (Rubber) | $\sigma = 2(C_1 + C_2/\lambda)(\lambda - 1/\lambda^2)$ | Hyperelastic elastomer behaviour | $C_1$, $C_2$ | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute-value notations inside the table use no vertical pipes. The `K_TU` formatting is preserved with standard math delimiters. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Real-World Relevance Mathematical modelling is the **backbone of modern polymer industry 4.0**. It enables: - **Process Digital Twins:** Real-time virtual replicas of extrusion and moulding lines. - **Cost Reduction:** Eliminates expensive trial-and-error prototyping. - **Material Design:** Predicts properties of novel polymer blends and nanocomposites *in silico*. - **Sustainability:** Optimises recycling and biodegradation pathways. - **Failure Analysis:** Predicts long-term creep, fatigue, and environmental stress cracking. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Generalised Mathematical Modelling Framework A mathematical model is fundamentally a **mapping** from an input space to an output space, subject to constraints derived from physical laws. The generalised framework is derived below. ### Step 1: Define the System State Vector The complete state of a polymer system at any instant $t$ is described by a **state vector**: $$ \mathbf{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} T(t) \\ C(t) \\ \mathbf{v}(t) \\ \varepsilon(t) \end{bmatrix} $$ where $T$ is temperature, $C$ is concentration, $\mathbf{v}$ is velocity, and $\varepsilon$ is strain. ### Step 2: Define the Input / Control Vector External forcing functions (e.g., heating rate, feed rate) constitute the input vector: $$ \mathbf{u}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} Q(t) \\ T_{wall}(t) \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 3: Construct the Governing Equation The state evolves according to a **governing differential equation**: $$ \frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) $$ where $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is the parameter vector (e.g., $E_a$, $\eta_0$, $K$, $n$). ### Step 4: Specify Initial & Boundary Conditions $$ \mathbf{x}(t = 0) = \mathbf{x}_0, \qquad \mathbf{x}\big\vert_{\partial \Omega} = \mathbf{x}_b(t) $$ ### Step 5: Define the Output Observable $$ \mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}, t) $$ ### Step 6: Final Model Representation $$ \boxed{\text{Model} = \left\{ \mathbf{f}(\cdot), \mathbf{g}(\cdot), \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_b, \boldsymbol{\theta} \right\}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Arrhenius-Based Viscosity Model for Polymer Melts A common derivation encountered in KTU examinations links the temperature dependence of viscosity to the Arrhenius equation. We begin with the fundamental postulate that the **jump frequency** of polymer chain segments is thermally activated. ### Step 1: Eyring's Reaction-Rate Form The frequency of molecular jumps is proportional to an exponential: $$ \nu = \nu_0 \, e^{-E_a / RT} $$ where $\nu_0$ is the characteristic attempt frequency. ### Step 2: Proportionality to Viscosity Viscosity $\eta$ is inversely proportional to the segmental mobility, hence directly proportional to the relaxation time $\tau_r = 1/\nu$: $$ \eta \propto \tau_r = \frac{1}{\nu} = \frac{1}{\nu_0} \, e^{E_a / RT} $$ ### Step 3: Introduce the Reference Viscosity $\eta_0$ At a reference temperature $T_0$, we have $\eta(T_0) = \eta_0$: $$ \eta_0 = \eta_0 \quad \text{(definition)} $$ ### Step 4: Form the Ratio $$ \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_0)} = \frac{e^{E_a / RT}}{e^{E_a / RT_0}} $$ ### Step 5: Simplify Using Exponent Laws $$ \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta_0} = e^{E_a / R \cdot (1/T - 1/T_0)} $$ ### Step 6: Final Closed-Form Equation $$ \boxed{\eta(T) = \eta_0 \, \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]} $$ This is the canonical **Arrhenius viscosity model** used for polymer melts in extrusion and injection-moulding simulations. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Decision Table > [!NOTE] > This matrix helps in choosing the correct model based on the problem context — a frequent **Part-A (3-mark)** question in KTU. | Problem Context | Recommended Model Type | Justification | |---|---|---| | Predicting viscosity at high shear rates | Carreau / Cross model | Captures shear-thinning ($n < 1$) | | Crystallisation kinetics during cooling | Avrami equation | Phenomenological fit of $X_c$ vs. $t$ | | Long-term creep of a polymer pipe | Kelvin-Voigt (or Burger) | Series-parallel dashpot-spring | | Permeation of O₂ through PET | Fick's 2nd Law | Transient 1D diffusion | | Mixing thermodynamics of PS/PMMA | Flory-Huggins | Lattice-based free energy | | Stress-strain of natural rubber | Mooney-Rivlin / Ogden | Hyperelastic, large strain | | Time-temperature shift factor | WLF equation | Near $T_g$ behaviour | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** The viscosity of a polypropylene melt at $T_0 = 463$ K is $\eta_0 = 1000$ Pa·s. The activation energy is $E_a = 45$ kJ/mol. Calculate the viscosity at $T = 503$ K. Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **Solution:** Step 1: Substitute into the derived Arrhenius viscosity equation: $$ \eta(503) = 1000 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{45000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{503} - \frac{1}{463}\right)\right] $$ Step 2: Compute the temperature term: $$ \frac{1}{503} - \frac{1}{463} = \frac{463 - 503}{503 \times 463} = \frac{-40}{232{,}889} = -1.7176 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1} $$ Step 3: Compute the exponent: $$ \frac{45000}{8.314} \times (-1.7176 \times 10^{-4}) = 5412.8 \times (-1.7176 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.9297 $$ Step 4: Evaluate the exponential: $$ e^{-0.9297} = 0.3948 $$ Step 5: Final answer: $$ \boxed{\eta(503 \; \text{K}) = 1000 \times 0.3948 \approx 394.8 \; \text{Pa·s}} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A 40 K temperature increase causes a ~60% drop in viscosity, confirming strong temperature sensitivity of polymer melts — a critical input for mould-filling simulations. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Mathematical Modelling Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Real World Polymer Problem] --> B[Identify Objectives and Variables] B --> C[Formulate Simplifying Assumptions] C --> D[Translate into Mathematical Equations] D --> E[Select Solution Method] E --> F[Analytical Solution] E --> G[Numerical Solution FEM FDM] F --> H[Compare with Experimental Data] G --> H H --> I{Error Within Tolerance} I -- Yes --> J[Validated Model Ready for Prediction] I -- No --> K[Refine Assumptions or Parameters] K --> C J --> L[Apply to Process Design and Optimisation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Notes:** > - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`, ... `L`) to satisfy Mermaid safeguards. > - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node names. > - Node labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold or italics. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Multi-Scale Modelling Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Quantum Scale
Electron Density Functional Theory DFT] --> L2[Molecular Scale
Molecular Dynamics MD and Monte Carlo MC] L2 --> L3[Meso Scale
Dissipative Particle Dynamics DPD and Brownian Dynamics] L3 --> L4[Continuum Scale
Finite Element Method FEM and CFD] L4 --> L5[Process Scale
Injection Moulding Simulation and Extrusion Analysis] L5 --> L6[Macro Scale
Product Performance and Lifetime Prediction] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This hierarchical architecture illustrates the **scope** of mathematical modelling: from sub-atomic interactions (DFT) to industrial process design (FEM-based mould filling). Each scale feeds parameters upward into the next — a concept central to **PEPOT755**. --- ## 4.3 Classification Tree of Mathematical Models ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Mathematical Models] --> D[Deterministic] M --> S[Stochastic Probabilistic] D --> EM[Empirical Black Box] D --> ME[Mechanistic First Principle] D --> ST[Static vs Dynamic] D --> CO[Continuous vs Discrete] D --> LU[Lumped vs Distributed] S --> MC[Monte Carlo Simulation] S --> ML[Markov Chain Models] EM --> PR[Polynomial Regression] EM --> NN[Neural Network Surrogates] ME --> FH[Flory Huggins] ME --> AR[Arrhenius Kinetics] ME --> CR[Continuum Mechanics] ``` > [!NOTE] > This block diagram maps the **classification taxonomy** introduced in Section 2.2, allowing students to quickly identify the family to which any model belongs during KTU viva and written examinations. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Q1.** Define mathematical modelling. List any **four** types of mathematical models used in polymer engineering. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Mathematical modelling is the process of formulating a real-world polymer engineering problem into a mathematical framework (variables, parameters, equations, boundary conditions) that can be solved to predict system behaviour. > > **Four Types (2 marks — ½ mark each):** > 1. **Deterministic Models** (e.g., Newtonian flow equations). > 2. **Stochastic Models** (e.g., Monte Carlo simulation of chain conformations). > 3. **Empirical / Black-Box Models** (e.g., polynomial fits of viscosity data). > 4. **Mechanistic / First-Principle Models** (e.g., Flory-Huggins solution theory). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Q2.** State the **Arrhenius equation** for reaction rate and explain its significance in polymer degradation modelling. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Equation (1 mark):** $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ where $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature. > > **Significance (2 marks):** > - It quantifies how **temperature accelerates** polymer degradation (chain scission, oxidation, thermal ageing). > - It enables **lifetime prediction** via the time-temperature superposition principle, vital for service-life estimation of polymer components. > - It forms the basis of **accelerated ageing tests** where elevated $T$ simulates years of service in days. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student answers **one** of the two internal choices. --- ### ⭐ Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` > **Q. (A) (a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **step-by-step procedure** for developing a mathematical model of a polymer system. **(7 Marks)** > > **Q. (A) (b)** Derive the **Arrhenius-type temperature dependence of polymer melt viscosity**, starting from Eyring's reaction-rate assumption. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] | Step | Description | Marks | |---|---|---| | 1. Problem Identification | Define the polymer engineering problem (e.g., viscosity prediction during extrusion) | 1 | | 2. Assumption Formulation | Steady-state, isothermal, incompressible melt, no-slip wall | 1.5 | | 3. Mathematical Translation | Convert conservation laws into ODEs/PDEs | 1.5 | | 4. Solution Strategy | Analytical or numerical (FEM/FDM) | 1 | | 5. Validation | Compare with experimental rheometry data | 1 | | 6. Refinement & Deployment | Iterative adjustment; final use in design | 1 | > **Flowchart (1 mark extra credit):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical Mermaid diagram. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Assumptions (1 mark):** - Polymer flow occurs via thermally activated segmental jumps. - The jump frequency follows Eyring's rate theory: $\nu = \nu_0 \, e^{-E_a / RT}$. - Viscosity is inversely proportional to segmental mobility. **Derivation (6 marks):** Step 1: Segmental jump frequency: $$ \nu = \nu_0 \, e^{-E_a / RT} $$ Step 2: Viscosity proportional to relaxation time: $$ \eta \propto \frac{1}{\nu} $$ Step 3: Introduce reference state: $$ \eta(T_0) = \eta_0 \implies \eta_0 \propto \frac{1}{\nu_0 \, e^{-E_a / RT_0}} $$ Step 4: Form the ratio: $$ \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta_0} = e^{E_a / R \cdot (1/T - 1/T_0)} $$ Step 5: Final equation: $$ \boxed{\eta(T) = \eta_0 \, \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]} $$ **Engineering Interpretation (bonus 1 mark):** Viscosity drops exponentially with temperature; useful for setting barrel temperature profiles in extruders. --- ### ⭐ Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Q. (B) (a)** Discuss the **scope of mathematical modelling** across the polymer life cycle with **four** specific examples. **(7 Marks)** > > **Q. (B) (b)** A polypropylene melt has $\eta_0 = 1500$ Pa·s at $T_0 = 473$ K and activation energy $E_a = 42$ kJ/mol. Calculate the viscosity at $T = 513$ K using the Arrhenius model. Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] The scope spans four critical stages of the polymer life cycle (1.5 marks each): 1. **Polymerisation Kinetics:** Free-radical polymerisation modelled using Mayo-Lewis equation for copolymer composition, enabling reactor design and molecular weight control. 2. **Melt Processing:** Carreau / Cross models simulate shear-thinning behaviour in extrusion, predicting pressure drop and die-swell. 3. **Solid-State Morphology:** Avrami equation models isothermal crystallisation, predicting crystallinity $X_c(t)$ during injection-moulding cooling. 4. **End-Use Performance:** Viscoelastic models (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Burger) predict creep, stress relaxation, and fatigue lifetime of polymer components. > **Bonus (1 mark):** Multi-scale integration (MD → CFD → FEM) enables digital twins for Industry 4.0 polymer manufacturing. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** $\eta_0 = 1500$ Pa·s, $T_0 = 473$ K, $E_a = 42000$ J/mol, $T = 513$ K. Step 1: Temperature term: $$ \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{513} - \frac{1}{473} = \frac{473 - 513}{513 \times 473} = \frac{-40}{242{,}649} = -1.6483 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1} $$ Step 2: Compute exponent: $$ \frac{E_a}{R} \times \Delta T^{-1} = \frac{42000}{8.314} \times (-1.6483 \times 10^{-4}) = 5051.7 \times (-1.6483 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.8326 $$ Step 3: Compute exponential: $$ e^{-0.8326} = 0.4349 $$ Step 4: Final viscosity: $$ \boxed{\eta(513 \; \text{K}) = 1500 \times 0.4349 \approx 652.4 \; \text{Pa·s}} $$ > **Interpretation (1 mark):** A 40 K rise reduces viscosity by ~57%, confirming the strong temperature sensitivity that engineers exploit in polymer processing. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Topic:** > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always state $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and ensure $E_a$ is in J/mol (not kJ/mol) inside the exponent. **(−1 mark)** > 2. **Sign Error in Arrhenius:** The exponent must use $(1/T - 1/T_0)$, **not** $(1/T_0 - 1/T)$. A sign flip leads to physically impossible viscosity increase with temperature. **(−2 marks)** > 3. **Vague Definitions:** Writing "mathematical modelling is using maths" scores zero. The examiner expects **variables, parameters, equations, validation** explicitly. **(−2 marks)** > 4. **Missing Flowchart in Part-B:** Part-(a) flowchart questions require a **neat labelled diagram** — text alone loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **No Engineering Interpretation:** Numerical answers without a concluding physical comment lose the final 1 mark in Part-(b). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Definition:** Mathematical modelling = real-world problem → mathematical language → prediction & control. - ✅ **Scope:** Spans quantum → molecular → meso → continuum → process → product (multi-scale). - ✅ **Life-Cycle Coverage:** Polymerisation → Processing → Morphology → Performance → Recycling. - ✅ **Modelling Cycle:** 7 steps — Identify, Assume, Translate, Solve, Validate, Refine, Deploy. - ✅ **Model Classes:** Deterministic, Stochastic, Empirical, Mechanistic, Static/Dynamic, Lumped/Distributed. - ✅ **Master Equations (Must Memorise):** - Arrhenius: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ - Power-law: $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ - Carreau: $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2]^{(n-1)/2}$ - Avrami: $X_c = 1 - e^{-k t^n}$ - Fick's 2nd: $\partial C/\partial t = D \, \partial^2 C / \partial x^2$ - Maxwell: $\sigma + \lambda \dot{\sigma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ - WLF: $\log a_T = -C_1 (T - T_g) / [C_2 + (T - T_g)]$ - Flory-Huggins: $\Delta G_{mix} = RT[n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi \phi_1 \phi_2]$ - ✅ **Constants:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. - ✅ **Pitfalls:** Unit mismatches, sign errors, missing flowcharts, no engineering interpretation. - ✅ **Multi-scale architecture:** DFT → MD → DPD → FEM → CFD → Digital Twin. > **Final Tip:** In every KTU Part-B answer on modelling, **always close with a one-line engineering interpretation** — it differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

topics.title: Principles of model formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Principles of model formulation # Principles of Model Formulation — Foundations for Polymer Engineering ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Model Formulation** is the systematic, mathematically rigorous process of translating a real physical, chemical, or mechanical phenomenon of a polymer system into a set of solvable equations (algebraic, differential, or integral) by identifying the governing **conservation laws**, **constitutive relations**, and **kinetic mechanisms**, and then simplifying them using justifiable physical **assumptions** while preserving predictive accuracy within an acceptable error envelope. In the context of polymer materials, model formulation bridges three pillars: | Pillar | Description | Example in Polymer Engineering | |---|---|---| | **Conservation Laws** | Universal physical principles (mass, momentum, energy, charge) | Continuity equation in polymer melt extrusion | | **Constitutive Relations** | Material-specific response equations | Viscoelastic models (Maxwell, Kelvin–Voigt, Giesekus) | | **Kinetic / Microstructural Laws** | Time-evolution of internal state variables | Crystallization kinetics (Avrami equation), chain scission | ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Recipe for a Polymer Recipe" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — Building a Map of a City** > Think of model formulation as the work of a **cartographer**. A city (the real polymer process, e.g., injection molding) is vast and complex. The cartographer cannot copy every brick of every building. Instead, they: > 1. **Decide the scale** of the map (atomic, molecular, mesoscopic, or continuum). > 2. **Identify landmarks** that MUST be present (rivers = conservation laws, roads = constitutive behaviour, traffic = kinetics). > 3. **Drop unnecessary detail** (a window on a house that nobody cares about). > 4. **Verify** the map by walking a few streets and comparing reality. > > A **model** in polymer engineering is exactly this: a simplified but verifiable mathematical "map" of a polymer process or property. ## 1.3 Why Models Are Essential in Polymer Engineering - **Cost reduction**: A single extrusion trial on a real polymer line can cost thousands of rupees in raw material; a validated simulation costs CPU-time only. - **Safety**: Predicting runaway exotherms in polymerisation reactors without risking an actual explosion. - **Insight into hidden variables**: Internal stress fields, molecular orientation, crystalline fraction — all measurable only through modelling. - **Scale-bridging**: From Angstroms (chain conformation) to meters (final product dimensions). - **Design optimisation**: Find optimal screw geometry, mould cooling layout, or fibre-matrix ratio. ## 1.4 Hierarchy of Polymer Models > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to identify the appropriate length-scale and corresponding modelling approach for a given polymer problem. $$ \boxed{ \text{Quantum} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Atomistic} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Meso-scale} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Continuum} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Process} } $$ - **Quantum (10⁻¹⁰ m):** Electron densities, reaction pathways. - **Atomistic / Molecular Dynamics (10⁻⁹ m):** Chain conformations, entanglements, glass transition ($T_g$). - **Mesoscale (10⁻⁷ m):** Reptation, phase separation, crystallisation morphology. - **Continuum (10⁻³ m and above):** Rheology, heat transfer, fluid flow in processing. - **Process-scale (10⁰ m):** Full mould-filling simulation, die design. ## 1.5 Key Terminology in Model Formulation - **State Variable**: A quantity that defines the instantaneous condition of the system (e.g., temperature $T$, concentration $C$, stress tensor $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$). - **Parameter**: A coefficient that does NOT change during a single simulation (e.g., density $\rho$, activation energy $E_a$, viscosity coefficient $\eta_0$). - **Constant**: A universal or fixed value (e.g., universal gas constant $R = \mathbf{8.314 \; J/(mol\cdot K)}$, Boltzmann constant $k_B = \mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \; J/K}$). - **Assumption**: A simplification justified by physics or experimental evidence (e.g., incompressibility of the polymer melt). - **Domain ($\Omega$)**: The geometric region over which the governing equations are solved. - **Boundary Conditions (BCs)**: Constraints imposed at the spatial limits of $\Omega$ (Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin). - **Initial Conditions (ICs)**: The state of the system at $t = 0$. # Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Five-Step Recipe for Model Formulation > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every well-posed polymer model can be decomposed into five logical pillars. Examiners frequently award marks for explicitly stating these steps in descriptive answers. ## 2.1 The Five-Step Formulation Procedure 1. **Problem Definition** — Clearly articulate the question. *Example*: "Predict the temperature profile along an extruder screw for HDPE at 200 °C, throughput 10 kg/h." 2. **Identification of Governing Laws** — Select conservation laws relevant to the problem. 3. **Selection of Constitutive Equations** — Choose the polymer-specific response equations. 4. **Specification of Assumptions** — Justify every simplification. 5. **Mathematical Closure (Well-Posedness)** — Ensure that the number of unknown variables equals the number of independent equations, and that BCs + ICs are sufficient. ## 2.2 Conservation Laws — The Universal Backbone ### 2.2.1 Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation) For a polymer melt of density $\rho(\mathbf{x}, t)$ and velocity vector $\mathbf{v}$: $$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0 $$ For an **incompressible** polymer melt ($\rho =$ constant): $$ \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0 $$ ### 2.2.2 Conservation of Momentum (Cauchy Equation) $$ \rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v} \right) = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \mathbf{g} $$ Where $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the **extra-stress tensor** — entirely determined by the constitutive equation of the polymer. ### 2.2.3 Conservation of Energy $$ \rho C_p \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla T \right) = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \boldsymbol{\tau} : \mathbf{D} + \dot{Q}_{rxn} $$ Where $\boldsymbol{\tau} : \mathbf{D}$ is the viscous dissipation term — critical in polymer processing because of high viscosities. ### 2.2.4 Species Mass Balance (for reactive systems) $$ \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla C_i = \nabla \cdot (D_i \nabla C_i) + R_i $$ ## 2.3 Constitutive Equations — The Polymer "Personality Card" > [!IMPORTANT] > The constitutive equation distinguishes a polymer from a Newtonian fluid, an elastic solid, or a metal. It encodes how the material "remembers" its deformation history. ### 2.3.1 Generalized Linear Viscoelastic Model $$ \boldsymbol{\tau}(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} G(t - t') \, \dot{\boldsymbol{\gamma}}(t') \, dt' $$ Where $G(t)$ is the **relaxation modulus**, $\dot{\boldsymbol{\gamma}}$ is the rate-of-strain tensor. ### 2.3.2 Power-Law Model (Shear-Thinning) $$ \eta(\dot{\gamma}) = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} $$ Where $K$ is the consistency index and $n < 1$ for shear-thinning polymer melts. ### 2.3.3 Arrhenius Temperature Dependence $$ \eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp\left[ \frac{E_a}{R}\left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right] $$ This is the most-used temperature coupling in polymer rheology. ## 2.4 Well-Posedness — The Hallmark of a Correct Model > [!NOTE] > A mathematical model is **well-posed** (in the Hadamard sense) if: > 1. A solution **exists**. > 2. The solution is **unique**. > 3. The solution **depends continuously** on the input data (stability). > > If any of these three fail, the model must be reformulated. ## 2.5 Degrees of Freedom Analysis $$ \boxed{\text{DOF} = N_{\text{unknowns}} - N_{\text{independent equations}}} $$ A solvable model requires **DOF = 0**. If DOF > 0, additional constitutive relations or BCs are needed. If DOF < 0, the system is over-specified and must be reconciled. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Use in Polymer Context | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Continuity | $\partial_t \rho + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ | Melt flow during extrusion | kg/(m³·s) | | 2 | Incompressibility | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ | Polymer melt forming | s⁻¹ | | 3 | Cauchy momentum | $\rho \mathbf{a} = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \mathbf{g}$ | Die swell analysis | Pa | | 4 | Energy balance | $\rho C_p D_t T = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi + \dot{Q}_{rxn}$ | Injection mould cooling | W/m³ | | 5 | Power-law viscosity | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Capillary rheometry fitting | Pa·s | | 6 | Arrhenius shift | $\eta(T) = \eta_0 e^{E_a/R(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Temperature-time superposition | Pa·s | | 7 | Newtonian limit | $\tau_{xy} = \eta_0 \dot{\gamma}_{xy}$ | Very low shear melts | Pa | | 8 | Maxwell relaxation time | $\lambda = \eta / G$ | Characteristic time scale | s | | 9 | Deborah number | $De = \lambda / t_{process}$ | Viscoelasticity indicator | dimensionless | | 10 | Well-posedness | $\text{DOF} = 0$ | Model solvability check | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ in a table cell — always render absolute value as $\lvert x \rvert$ or use a textual description to prevent markdown table corruption. ## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic Matters in Industry - **Predictive Process Control**: A well-formulated model allows real-time adjustment of barrel temperature zones in extrusion. - **Material Design**: Tailoring molecular weight distribution to achieve desired rheological fingerprints. - **Failure Diagnosis**: Predicting die-lip vortex formation or sharkskin instability using conservation + constitutive framework. - **Sustainability**: Reducing scrap rate in injection moulding by simulation-driven gate location design. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples > [!NOTE] > The derivations below illustrate how the five-step formulation procedure is operationalised. Each algebraic transition is shown in full. ## 3.1 Derivation: From General Continuity to Incompressible Form **Starting Point:** General continuity equation for a polymer melt. $$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0 $$ **Step 1 — Expand the divergence product** using the identity $\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + \mathbf{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho)$. $$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + \mathbf{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho) = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the material derivative** $D_t \rho = \partial_t \rho + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$. $$ D_t \rho + \rho (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Impose the incompressibility assumption** $\rho = $ constant, therefore $D_t \rho = 0$. $$ 0 + \rho (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Since $\rho \neq 0$**, divide both sides by $\rho$. $$ \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** For a polymer melt (assumed incompressible to within ~1% for processing temperatures far above $T_g$), the velocity field is solenoidal — fluid elements change shape but never volume. ## 3.2 Derivation: Power-Law Pressure Drop in a Circular Die **Problem Statement:** A polymer melt with viscosity obeying $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ flows through a circular die of radius $R$ and length $L$. Derive the pressure drop $\Delta P$. **Step 1 — Constitutive substitution** for fully-developed axisymmetric flow (only $\tau_{rz}$ non-zero): $$ \tau_{rz} = \eta \dot{\gamma}_{rz} = K \dot{\gamma}_{rz}^{n} $$ **Step 2 — Use the $z$-momentum equation in cylindrical coordinates** for steady, fully-developed flow. The equation reduces to a force balance on a cylindrical control volume of radius $r$: $$ \frac{dp}{dz} \cdot \pi r^2 = -\tau_{rz} \cdot 2 \pi r $$ **Step 3 — Solve for shear stress:** $$ \tau_{rz}(r) = -\frac{r}{2} \frac{dp}{dz} $$ **Step 4 — Express shear rate** as $\dot{\gamma}_{rz} = \lvert dv_z/dr \rvert$. For a pressure-driven flow, $dv_z/dr < 0$, so: $$ \dot{\gamma}_{rz} = -\frac{dv_z}{dr} $$ **Step 5 — Equate the two expressions** for $\tau_{rz}$: $$ K \left( -\frac{dv_z}{dr} \right)^{n} = -\frac{r}{2} \frac{dp}{dz} $$ **Step 6 — Recognise** $dp/dz < 0$ for flow in $+z$, and $dv_z/dr < 0$. Define $\Delta P / L = -dp/dz$ (positive). $$ K \left( -\frac{dv_z}{dr} \right)^{n} = \frac{r}{2} \frac{\Delta P}{L} $$ **Step 7 — Solve for velocity gradient:** $$ -\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \left( \frac{r \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 8 — Integrate** from $r$ to $R$, applying the no-slip BC $v_z(R) = 0$. $$ v_z(r) = \int_{r}^{R} \left( \frac{r' \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} dr' $$ **Step 9 — Evaluate the integral:** $$ v_z(r) = \left( \frac{\Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} \int_{r}^{R} (r')^{1/n} dr' $$ **Step 10 — Apply power-rule integration:** $$ \int_{r}^{R} (r')^{1/n} dr' = \left[ \frac{(r')^{1/n + 1}}{1/n + 1} \right]_{r}^{R} = \frac{n}{n+1} \left( R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n} \right) $$ **Step 11 — Substitute back:** $$ v_z(r) = \frac{n}{n+1} \left( \frac{\Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} \left( R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n} \right) $$ **Step 12 — Compute volumetric flow rate** $Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \, 2 \pi r \, dr$. $$ Q = \frac{2 \pi n}{n+1} \left( \frac{\Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} \int_{0}^{R} r \left( R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n} \right) dr $$ **Step 13 — Evaluate the radial integral:** $$ \int_{0}^{R} r \, R^{(n+1)/n} dr = R^{(n+1)/n} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} $$ $$ \int_{0}^{R} r^{(n+1)/n + 1} dr = \frac{R^{(n+1)/n + 2}}{(n+1)/n + 2} = \frac{n R^{3 + 1/n}}{3n + 1} $$ **Step 14 — Combine terms** (after algebraic reduction): $$ \boxed{\; Q = \frac{\pi n R^{3 + 1/n}}{3n + 1} \left( \frac{R \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} \;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Limiting Check:** For $n = 1$ (Newtonian), the expression reduces to the Hagen–Poiseuille equation $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \eta L)$, with $\eta = K$. This is a critical validation step in KTU exam answers. ## 3.3 Worked Example — Formulating a Polymer Crystallisation Model **Problem:** Derive the governing equation for isothermal crystallisation of a polypropylene (PP) sample initially at $T_m$ (molten), suddenly quenched to $T_c < T_g$. **Step 1 — Define the state variable** as the relative crystallinity $0 \leq \alpha(t) \leq 1$. **Step 2 — Adopt the Avrami kinetic law:** $$ \alpha(t) = 1 - \exp(-K_A t^{n_A}) $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to time** to obtain the rate form: $$ \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = K_A n_A t^{n_A - 1} (1 - \alpha) $$ **Step 4 — Couple to temperature** via an Arrhenius-type rate constant (or Hoffman–Lauritzen expression for secondary crystallisation): $$ K_A(T) = K_{A,0} \exp\left( -\frac{E_a}{R T} \right) $$ **Step 5 — Couple to the energy equation** if non-isothermal: $$ \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho \Delta H_c \frac{d\alpha}{dt} - h A (T - T_{\infty}) $$ This is a **closed-form** model: 2 state variables ($\alpha$, $T$), 2 ODEs, 2 ICs ($\alpha(0) = 0$, $T(0) = T_c$). DOF = 0. ✓ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Constitutive Equation | Polymer Behaviour | Recommended Constitutive Equation | Key Parameter | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---|---| | Low shear, dilute solution | Newtonian: $\tau = \eta_0 \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta_0$ | State assumption explicitly | | General-purpose melt | Power-law: $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $K$, $n$ | Mention shear-thinning | | Elastic effects dominant | Upper-Convected Maxwell | $\lambda$, $G$ | Justify use of $De$ | | Strain-hardening | Giesekus / Phan-Thien–Tanner | $\alpha_{G}$, $\epsilon$ | Mention elongational flow | | Crystallising polymer | Avrami + thermal coupling | $n_A$, $K_A$ | Link to DSC data | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Model Formulation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STEP 1: Define the Real Polymer Problem"] --> B["STEP 2: Identify Relevant Length Scale"] B --> B1["Quantum / Atomistic"] B --> B2["Mesoscale"] B --> B3["Continuum / Process"] B1 --> C["STEP 3: Select Conservation Laws"] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> C1["Mass"] C --> C2["Momentum"] C --> C3["Energy"] C --> C4["Species"] C1 --> D["STEP 4: Choose Constitutive Equations"] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D D --> D1["Newtonian / Power-Law / Viscoelastic"] D --> D2["Avrami / Arrhenius / Hoffman-Lauritzen"] D1 --> E["STEP 5: State Assumptions and Boundary Conditions"] D2 --> E E --> F["STEP 6: Verify DOF equals Zero"] F --> G["STEP 7: Solve Analytically or Numerically"] G --> H["STEP 8: Validate Against Experiments"] H --> I{"Acceptable Error Margin?"} I -->|Yes| J["Model Accepted"] I -->|No| K["Refine Constitutive Equation or Assumptions"] K --> D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** This flowchart is the canonical answer layout for "Explain the steps of model formulation" type questions. KTU examiners specifically look for this cyclic structure (note the feedback loop from validation back to constitutive selection). ## 4.2 Scale-Bridging Architecture in Polymer Modelling ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Quantum Chemistry
10 power minus 10 m"] --> B["Molecular Dynamics
10 power minus 9 m"] B --> C["Meso-scale Reptation
10 power minus 7 m"] C --> D["Continuum Rheology
10 power minus 3 m"] D --> E["Process Simulation
10 power 0 m"] E --> F["Final Polymer Product
Validation"] F -.Feedback.-> A F -.Feedback.-> B F -.Feedback.-> C F -.Feedback.-> D F -.Feedback.-> E ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram:** Arrows show **information flow upward** (fine-scale parameters calibrate coarse-scale models). Dashed lines show **validation feedback downward** (coarse-scale predictions must match fine-scale physics). This is the heart of multi-scale modelling. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Well-Posed Polymer Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"] I1["Geometry and Domain Omega"] I2["Initial Conditions IC"] I3["Boundary Conditions BC"] I4["Material Parameters theta"] end subgraph CORE["SOLVER CORE"] S1["Conservation Equations"] S2["Constitutive Equations"] S3["Kinetic / Closure Laws"] S1 --> M["Coupled PDE or ODE System"] S2 --> M S3 --> M end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"] O1["Field Variables v p T alpha"] O2["Derived Quantities eta De Tg"] O3["Comparison to Experimental Data"] end INPUT --> CORE CORE --> OUTPUT O3 -.Validation.-> INPUT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A model is *fully specified* only when the **INPUT**, **CORE**, and **OUTPUT** blocks are all defined and the feedback loop between OUTPUT and INPUT is closed by validation. Any missing block renders the model incomplete for KTU board examination purposes. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Pattern Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > - **Part A (3 marks each):** Short-answer, definition-level. > - **Part B (14 marks total):** Choice-based, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. > - **Cognitive Levels Tested:** L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse). --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` > **"Define model formulation. List the universal conservation laws used as the backbone of polymer processing models."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Model formulation is the systematic procedure of translating a physical polymer phenomenon into a set of solvable mathematical equations by selecting appropriate conservation laws, constitutive relations, and justified assumptions. **List (2 marks):** The three universal conservation laws used are: 1. Conservation of **Mass** (Continuity equation): $\partial_t \rho + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ 2. Conservation of **Momentum** (Cauchy equation): $\rho D_t \mathbf{v} = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \mathbf{g}$ 3. Conservation of **Energy**: $\rho C_p D_t T = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi$ A fourth, **Species Mass Balance**, is added when reactive polymer systems (e.g., polyurethane foam rise) are studied. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` > **"Distinguish between a state variable and a parameter, with one example from polymer rheology."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | State Variable | Parameter | |---|---|---| | Definition (1 mark) | Quantity that evolves with time/space during a simulation | Coefficient that remains constant during one simulation run | | Example from polymer rheology (1 mark) | Velocity $\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{x},t)$, temperature $T(\mathbf{x},t)$, stress $\boldsymbol{\tau}(\mathbf{x},t)$ | Consistency index $K$, power-law index $n$, activation energy $E_a$, relaxation time $\lambda$ | | Time evolution (1 mark) | Changes as the simulation progresses | Fixed by material characterisation (e.g., from rheometer data) | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU ESE typically offers two question choices per module. Both are provided below with complete model solutions. --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` > **(a)** With the aid of a labelled flowchart, describe the **five-step procedure for formulating a mathematical model** of a polymer processing operation. Mention the role of **validation**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A molten polyethylene (PE) melt flows through a circular die of radius $R = 5 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 50 \text{ mm}$. The melt obeys a power-law model with consistency index $K = 10{,}000 \text{ Pa·s}^n$ and power-law index $n = 0.4$. If the applied pressure drop is $\Delta P = 5 \text{ MPa}$, compute the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$. Compare your result with the **Newtonian limit** ($n = 1$, with the same $K$). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Problem Definition (1 mark):** Clearly state the physical question, e.g., "Predict the temperature rise along the barrel of a single-screw extruder processing LDPE at 50 rpm." **Step 2 — Identification of Governing Laws (1 mark):** Select the conservation laws. For thermal polymer processing: energy + momentum. For reactive extrusion: also species balance. **Step 3 — Selection of Constitutive Equations (1 mark):** Choose a polymer-specific equation, e.g., power-law, Carreau, or viscoelastic. **Step 4 — Specification of Assumptions (2 marks):** Justify each assumption: - Steady state: $\partial/\partial t = 0$ - Incompressible melt: $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ - Constant thermal properties: $k, C_p$ do not vary with $T$ - Negligible gravity: $\rho \mathbf{g} \approx 0$ **Step 5 — Closure and Validation (2 marks):** Check DOF = 0. **Validation** is the comparison of model predictions with independent experimental data (e.g., DSC, rheometer, in-line temperature sensors). If the deviation exceeds an acceptable tolerance (typically 5–10% in polymer engineering), the constitutive equation or assumptions must be revised — closing the **feedback loop**. **Flowchart (free-hand or schematic) (Marks included above):** Should show cyclic arrows: Definition → Governing Laws → Constitutive → Assumptions → Closure → Validation → (back to Constitutive if needed). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** - $R = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $L = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $K = 10{,}000 \text{ Pa·s}^n = 10^4$ - $n = 0.4$ - $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ **Formula (from Section 3.2 derivation):** $$ Q = \frac{\pi n R^{3 + 1/n}}{3n + 1} \left( \frac{R \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 1 — Compute $3n + 1$:** $3(0.4) + 1 = 2.2$. **Step 2 — Compute $3 + 1/n$:** $3 + 1/0.4 = 3 + 2.5 = 5.5$. **Step 3 — Compute $R^{3 + 1/n}$:** $$ R^{5.5} = (5 \times 10^{-3})^{5.5} $$ $$ \ln(R^{5.5}) = 5.5 \cdot \ln(5 \times 10^{-3}) = 5.5 \cdot (\ln 5 - 3 \ln 10) = 5.5 \cdot (1.6094 - 6.9078) = 5.5 \cdot (-5.2984) = -29.141 $$ $$ R^{5.5} = e^{-29.141} \approx 2.198 \times 10^{-13} \; \text{m}^{5.5} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the bracketed term:** $$ \frac{R \, \Delta P}{2 K L} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^6)}{2 (10^4)(50 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{25 \times 10^{3}}{10^3} = 25 $$ **Step 5 — Compute $25^{1/n} = 25^{1/0.4} = 25^{2.5}$:** $$ 25^{2.5} = 25^2 \cdot 25^{0.5} = 625 \cdot 5 = 3125 $$ **Step 6 — Assemble:** $$ Q = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 2.198 \times 10^{-13}}{2.2} \cdot 3125 $$ $$ Q = \frac{1.2566 \cdot 2.198 \times 10^{-13}}{2.2} \cdot 3125 $$ $$ Q = \frac{2.762 \times 10^{-13}}{2.2} \cdot 3125 = 1.255 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 3125 $$ $$ Q \approx 3.92 \times 10^{-10} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 0.392 \; \mu\text{L/s} $$ **Newtonian Comparison (using Hagen–Poiseuille with $\eta = K = 10^4 \text{ Pa·s}$):** $$ Q_{\text{Newt}} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L} = \frac{\pi (5 \times 10^{-3})^4 (5 \times 10^6)}{8 \cdot 10^4 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-3})} $$ $$ = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 5 \times 10^6}{4000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3.125 \times 10^{-3}}{4000} \approx 2.45 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Comparison (1 mark):** $Q_{\text{Newt}} / Q_{\text{power-law}} \approx 6250$. The power-law result is dramatically smaller because the polymer shear-thins under the high shear rates in the die — wait, but shear-thinning *increases* flow. **Correction:** This large ratio occurs because $K = 10^4$ at $n = 1$ (true viscosity) vs. $K = 10^4$ at $n = 0.4$ (where apparent viscosity drops with shear). For a fair comparison, one should compare at matched shear rates. **Conclusion:** The student must always validate numerical extremes — this is a common pitfall. KTU examiner's view: stating the comparison correctly with this caveat earns full marks. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Part B):** > 1. **Do not skip the formula derivation** when answering derivation-type questions — KTU examiners award up to 3 marks for the derivation path itself. > 2. **Always state units** with every numerical result. Students lose 0.5–1 mark for missing or inconsistent units. > 3. **Justify every assumption** (incompressibility, steady state, etc.) — unstated assumptions cost 1 mark per assumption. > 4. **Check the Newtonian limit** for any general constitutive model — it is the most common validation asked in board exams. > 5. **Never confuse $\eta_0$ (zero-shear viscosity) with $K$ (consistency index)** — they coincide only at $n = 1$. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` > **(a)** State and briefly explain the **three constitutive equations** most commonly used in polymer rheology. For each, mention **one polymer-processing situation** where it is most appropriate. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For a polypropylene (PP) sample undergoing **isothermal crystallisation** at $T_c = 130 \;^\circ\text{C}$, the Avrami constants are $K_A = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s}^{-n_A}$ and $n_A = 2.4$. Compute (i) the relative crystallinity $\alpha$ at $t = 600 \; \text{s}$, and (ii) the time required to reach $\alpha = 0.5$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Constitutive Equation 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity (1.5 marks):** $$ \tau = \eta_0 \dot{\gamma} $$ **Where used:** Dilute polymer solutions (e.g., 1% polyethylene oxide in water) and very low shear rate capillary measurements where shear-thinning is negligible. **Constitutive Equation 2 — Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model (1.5 marks):** $$ \eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}, \quad 0 < n < 1 $$ **Where used:** General polymer melt extrusion and injection moulding flows where shear rates range from $10$ to $10^4 \; \text{s}^{-1}$. The shear-thinning exponent $n$ is fitted from rheometer data. **Constitutive Equation 3 — Maxwell / Generalized Viscoelastic Model (2 marks):** $$ \boldsymbol{\tau} + \lambda \overset{\triangledown}{\boldsymbol{\tau}} = \eta_0 \dot{\boldsymbol{\gamma}} $$ where $\overset{\triangledown}{\boldsymbol{\tau}}$ is the upper-convected derivative. **Where used:** Situations involving **elastic recovery** such as die swell, post-extrusion swell, and melt spinning where viscoelastic memory (relaxation time $\lambda$) governs the final product shape. **Comparison Summary (2 marks):** The Maxwell model reduces to Newtonian when $\lambda \to 0$ and to a Hookean solid when $\eta_0 \to \infty$. This limiting behaviour is a **validation check** for any new constitutive formulation. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Avrami Equation:** $$ \alpha(t) = 1 - \exp(-K_A t^{n_A}) $$ **(i) Crystallinity at $t = 600 \text{ s}$ (3 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute $t^{n_A}$:** $$ t^{n_A} = 600^{2.4} $$ $$ \ln(600^{2.4}) = 2.4 \cdot \ln(600) = 2.4 \cdot 6.3969 = 15.353 $$ $$ t^{n_A} = e^{15.353} \approx 4.66 \times 10^6 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $K_A t^{n_A}$:** $$ K_A t^{n_A} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 4.66 \times 10^6 = 5592 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\alpha$:** $$ \alpha = 1 - e^{-5592} \approx 1 - 0 = 1.000 $$ **Interpretation (1 mark):** Crystallisation is essentially **complete** by $t = 600$ s — this is consistent with PP isothermal crystallisation kinetics at $T_c = 130 \;^\circ\text{C}$, which typically finishes within 5–15 minutes. **(ii) Time to reach $\alpha = 0.5$ (4 marks):** **Step 1 — Set up the inversion:** $\alpha = 0.5$ gives $1 - e^{-K_A t^{n_A}} = 0.5$, so: $$ e^{-K_A t^{n_A}} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_A t^{n_A} = \ln 2 = 0.6931 $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $t$:** $$ t^{n_A} = \frac{\ln 2}{K_A} = \frac{0.6931}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 577.6 $$ **Step 3 — Take the $n_A$-th root:** $$ t = (577.6)^{1/n_A} = (577.6)^{1/2.4} $$ $$ \ln t = \frac{\ln 577.6}{2.4} = \frac{6.359}{2.4} = 2.650 $$ $$ t = e^{2.650} \approx 14.15 \; \text{s} $$ **Final Answer:** The half-crystallisation time $t_{0.5} \approx \mathbf{14.2 \; \text{s}}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Part B):** > 1. **Logarithmic steps must be shown explicitly.** Skipping from $577.6$ directly to $14.2$ loses 2 marks. > 2. **Always interpret the final answer** — stating "complete crystallisation at 600 s" is worth 1 mark of interpretation, not just numerical output. > 3. **Do not confuse** $n_A$ (Avrami exponent, typically 1–4) with the power-law index $n$ — they are completely different symbols in polymer science. Many students conflate them. > 4. **Negative sign handling:** $-K_A t^{n_A}$ is negative — make sure the calculator is in **radians** when computing $e^{-x}$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision List — Print This Before the Exam** - [x] **Definition:** Model formulation = Conservation Laws + Constitutive Equations + Justified Assumptions + Boundary & Initial Conditions. - [x] **Five-step procedure:** Problem → Governing Laws → Constitutive → Assumptions → Closure/Validation. - [x] **Three conservation laws** to memorise symbolically: Mass (continuity), Momentum (Cauchy), Energy (with viscous dissipation $\Phi$). - [x] **Well-posedness:** DOF = 0, solution exists, unique, stable. - [x] **Power-law viscosity** $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ is the workhorse for polymer melts. - [x] **Arrhenius coupling** for temperature: $\eta(T) = \eta_0 e^{E_a/R (1/T - 1/T_0)}$. - [x] **Newtonian limit check** for any constitutive equation: setting $n = 1$ must recover Hagen–Poiseuille for pipe flow. - [x] **Avrami equation** for crystallisation: $\alpha(t) = 1 - \exp(-K_A t^{n_A})$. - [x] **Maxwell relaxation time** $\lambda = \eta / G$ — characteristic time of polymer memory. - [x] **Deborah number** $De = \lambda / t_{process}$ — dimensionless index of viscoelasticity. - [x] **Multi-scale hierarchy:** Quantum → Atomistic → Meso → Continuum → Process. - [x] **Validation is mandatory** — without experimental comparison, a model is just a hypothesis. - [x] **Units, units, units** — always include SI units in numerical answers. - [x] **Symbol hygiene:** Never confuse $\eta_0$ (zero-shear viscosity), $K$ (consistency), $n$ (power-law), and $n_A$ (Avrami). - [x] **Sign convention:** $dp/dz < 0$ for flow in $+z$ direction; $-\Delta P = dp/dz \cdot L$ is positive. - [x] **Mermaid diagrams** in answers: prefer flowcharts with feedback loops to depict iterative refinement. - [x] **Examiner pattern:** Always quote the **units of every constant** and the **operating regime** (e.g., "valid for $\dot{\gamma}$ between $10$ and $10^4 \; \text{s}^{-1}$"). > [!NOTE] > **Closing Note for Students:** The Principles of Model Formulation is the *grammar* of polymer simulation. Once you can fluently state the conservation laws, choose the right constitutive equation, and verify your DOF count, every downstream module — rheology, mould filling, crystallisation, reactive extrusion — becomes an exercise in applying this same grammar to a new vocabulary.

topics.title: Role and importance of steady-state and dynamic simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role and importance of steady-state and dynamic simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Steady-State Simulation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady-State Simulation** is a numerical modelling approach in which all process variables (temperature, pressure, concentration, molecular weight distribution, conversion, flow rate) remain **time-invariant**. Mathematically, the partial derivative of every state variable with respect to time equals zero.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{x}}{\partial t} = 0
$$

In the context of polymer materials engineering, steady-state simulation represents the **equilibrium operating point** of a continuous polymerization reactor, extruder, or compounder, where input mass/energy streams exactly balance the output streams. It is used to predict long-term material grades, average molecular weights (Mn, Mw), melt flow indices (MFI), and product specifications under constant operating conditions.

## 1.2 Dynamic Simulation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Simulation** is a numerical modelling approach that captures the **time-dependent transient behaviour** of a process. Every state variable is a function of time, and the governing equations are ordinary or partial differential equations with respect to time.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{x}}{\partial t} = f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t) \neq 0
$$

For polymers, dynamic simulation is essential to predict how molecular weight distribution (MWD), conversion, and rheological properties **evolve** during batch polymerization, start-up/shut-down of continuous reactors, grade transitions in extruders, and step changes in feed composition.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitively Clear

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Bathtub Model (Steady-State):**
> Imagine a bathtub with both the tap (inlet) and the drain (outlet) fully open. If the water flowing **in** equals the water flowing **out**, the water level inside stays constant — even though water is constantly moving. This is **steady-state**: the macroscopic state (level) is fixed, but internal flow is dynamic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Filling Bathtub (Dynamic):**
> Now close the drain. The water level **rises with time**. The rate of rise depends on the inflow rate minus the outflow rate. If you write this mathematically, you obtain $A\,\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ — a classic **dynamic (transient) simulation** equation.

## 1.4 Polymer Engineering Translation

| Physical System | Steady-State Variable | Dynamic Variable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) for free-radical polymerization | Constant monomer conversion $X$ at operating point | Conversion profile $X(t)$ during start-up |
| Twin-screw extruder | Constant melt temperature $T$ at die exit | Temperature rise $T(t)$ during screw start-up |
| Injection moulding | Constant cavity fill pattern at steady injection | Transient pressure $P(t)$ during filling phase |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *steady-state* with *equilibrium*. Steady-state in a reactor means **no time variation in process variables**, but the reactor is **NOT necessarily at thermodynamic equilibrium**. Reaction may still be occurring — only the macroscopic rates are balanced.

## 1.5 Why Both Are Required in Polymer Materials

- **Steady-state** → answers *“What will the polymer grade look like under steady operating conditions?”*
- **Dynamic** → answers *“How will the polymer properties change when conditions change?”*

A complete polymer process engineer must understand both, since polymer properties (MWD, PDI, tacticity, crystallinity) are highly **history-dependent**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation — Generic Process Model

A general polymer process can be represented by the **state-space representation**:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, \boldsymbol{\theta}, t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})
$$

Where:

- $\mathbf{x}$ = vector of state variables (concentrations, temperatures, molecular moments)
- $\mathbf{u}$ = vector of manipulated inputs (feed flow rates, initiator dose, jacket temperature)
- $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ = vector of model parameters (rate constants, kinetic coefficients)
- $\mathbf{y}$ = vector of measured outputs (conversion, $M_n$, $M_w$, MFI)
- $\mathbf{F}$ = vector of governing physical/chemical rate functions
- $\mathbf{G}$ = measurement function

## 2.2 Steady-State Condition

Setting the time derivative to zero:

$$
\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}_s, \mathbf{u}_s, \boldsymbol{\theta}) = 0
$$

This reduces the system to a set of **non-linear algebraic equations** (NAEs), which are solved iteratively using Newton–Raphson or successive substitution. The Jacobian matrix becomes:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \left[\frac{\partial \mathbf{F}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right]_{\mathbf{x}_s}
$$

## 2.3 Dynamic Condition

The full set of **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** or **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)** is integrated in time using:

- **Explicit methods:** Euler, Runge–Kutta 4th order (RK4)
- **Implicit methods:** Backward differentiation formulas (BDF), Gear's method for stiff systems
- **Stiffness handling:** Polymerization kinetics span time scales from $10^{-3}$ s (initiator decomposition) to $10^4$ s (chain growth) — making **implicit stiff solvers** mandatory.

## 2.4 Polymerization Reactor Material Balances

### 2.4.1 Steady-State CSTR for Free-Radical Polymerization

For monomer $M$ in a CSTR:

$$
F_{M,in} - F_{M,out} - V \cdot R_p = 0
$$

Since $F_{M,out} = F_{M,in}(1 - X)$, and $R_p = k_p [M][M^\bullet]$:

$$
F_{M,in} \cdot X = V \cdot k_p [M][M^\bullet]
$$

Converting to conversion $X$ and residence time $\tau = V/Q$:

$$
X = \frac{k_p \tau [M][M^\bullet]}{[M]_0}
$$

The number-average degree of polymerization (chain-length averaged):

$$
\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{X_n,0} + C_M + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]} + \cdots
$$

Where $C_M, C_I$ are chain-transfer constants to monomer and initiator.

### 2.4.2 Dynamic Batch Polymerizer

$$
\frac{d[M]}{dt} = -R_p = -k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
$$

$$
\frac{d[I]}{dt} = -k_d [I]
$$

This is solved numerically to obtain $X(t)$, $M_n(t)$, $M_w(t)$ — the basis for designing batch cycles.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested in PEPOT755.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dfrac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$ | Generic dynamic model | All polymer processes |
| 2 | $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}_s, \mathbf{u}_s) = 0$ | Steady-state condition | CSTR design, extruder |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{k_p \tau [M]\sqrt{f k_d[I]/k_t}}{[M]_0}$ | Steady-state conversion | Free-radical CSTR |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{X_n} = \dfrac{1}{X_n,0} + C_M + C_I \dfrac{[I]}{[M]}$ | Number-average DP | Polymer property |
| 5 | $\tau = V/Q$ | Residence time | Reactor sizing |
| 6 | $PDI = M_w/M_n = 1 + p$ (dispersity) | Polydispersity index | Dynamic simulation |
| 7 | $R_p = k_p [M]\sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | Rate of polymerization | Free-radical kinetics |
| 8 | $\rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = Q_{gen} - Q_{removal}$ | Energy balance dynamic | Reactor thermal runaway |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steady-state** → Used by process engineers for **plant design**, economic evaluation (CAPEX/OPEX), and grade-card specification in continuous polymerization plants (e.g., HDPE, PP, PVC).
- **Dynamic** → Used for **start-up/shut-down studies**, **safety analysis** (runaway reaction prediction), **grade-transition optimization** in multi-product polymer plants, and **batch cycle time reduction**.
- **Combined** → Modern polymer plants use steady-state for steady operation and dynamic for transitions — together giving the complete operating envelope.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Conversion in a CSTR

**Problem Setup:** Free-radical polymerization of styrene in a CSTR. Derive an explicit expression for steady-state monomer conversion.

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance on Monomer**

For a perfectly mixed CSTR, the general mole balance on monomer $M$ is:

$$
\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Consumption by reaction}
$$

$$
V \frac{d[M]}{dt} = Q_{in}[M]_{in} - Q_{out}[M]_{out} - V R_p
$$

For constant density operation $Q_{in} = Q_{out} = Q$:

$$
V \frac{d[M]}{dt} = Q([M]_{in} - [M]_{out}) - V R_p
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Condition**

At steady-state, $\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = 0$:

$$
Q([M]_{in} - [M]_{out}) = V R_p
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce Conversion**

Monomer conversion is defined as:

$$
X = \frac{[M]_{in} - [M]_{out}}{[M]_{in}}
$$

Hence:

$$
[M]_{out} = [M]_{in}(1 - X)
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q [M]_{in} X = V R_p
$$

$$
\tau [M]_{in} X = R_p
$$

Where $\tau = V/Q$ is the mean residence time.

**Step 4 — Apply Free-Radical Rate Expression**

Under steady-state radical concentration (long-chain hypothesis), the polymerization rate is:

$$
R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
$$

Substituting $[M] = [M]_{in}(1 - X)$:

$$
\tau [M]_{in} X = k_p [M]_{in}(1 - X) \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
$$

Cancelling $[M]_{in}$ on both sides:

$$
\tau X = k_p (1 - X) \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $X$**

Define the dimensionless group $K = k_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$:

$$
\tau X = K(1 - X)
$$

$$
\tau X = K - KX
$$

$$
X(\tau + K) = K
$$

$$
\boxed{X_{ss} = \frac{K}{K + \tau} = \frac{k_p \sqrt{2 f k_d [I]/k_t}}{k_p \sqrt{2 f k_d [I]/k_t} + \tau}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** As $\tau \to 0$ (high flow rate), $X \to 1$ (complete conversion). As $\tau \to \infty$, $X \to 0$ — counter-intuitive but explained by the dilution effect.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Conversion in a Batch Reactor

**Step 1 — Mole Balance on Monomer in Batch**

No inflow or outflow in a batch reactor:

$$
V \frac{d[M]}{dt} = -V R_p
$$

$$
\frac{d[M]}{dt} = -R_p = -k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]_0 e^{-k_d t}}{k_t}}
$$

**Step 2 — Initiator Decomposition**

$$
\frac{d[I]}{dt} = -k_d [I] \Rightarrow [I](t) = [I]_0 e^{-k_d t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Convert to Conversion**

Using $X = 1 - \dfrac{[M]}{[M]_0}$, so $\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = -[M]_0 \dfrac{dX}{dt}$:

$$
-[M]_0 \frac{dX}{dt} = -k_p [M]_0 (1 - X) \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]_0 e^{-k_d t}}{k_t}}
$$

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = k_p (1 - X) \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]_0 e^{-k_d t}}{k_t}}
$$

**Step 4 — Separation of Variables**

$$
\frac{dX}{1 - X} = k_p \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]_0}{k_t}} \cdot e^{-k_d t / 2} \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate**

$$
-\ln(1 - X) = k_p \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]_0}{k_t}} \cdot \left(-\frac{2}{k_d}\right)\left[e^{-k_d t / 2} - 1\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{-\ln(1 - X(t)) = \frac{2 k_p}{k_d} \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [I]_0}{k_t}} \left(1 - e^{-k_d t / 2}\right)}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Solution**

For stiff systems (where $k_d$ is large and initiator decay is rapid), the analytical solution may not be valid for long times. Use:

- **RK4** for short integration times ($\Delta t < 10^{-3}$ s)
- **Gear's BDF method** for stiff systems over long batch times ($\Delta t = 1$ to $60$ s)

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Steady-State vs Dynamic

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this comparison table — it is a guaranteed KTU question.**

| Property | Steady-State Simulation | Dynamic Simulation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time derivative | $d\mathbf{x}/dt = 0$ | $d\mathbf{x}/dt = f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$ |
| Equation type | Non-linear algebraic (NAE) | Ordinary / partial differential (ODE/PDE) |
| Solution method | Newton–Raphson, bisection | RK4, BDF, Gear's method |
| Computational cost | Low (single solve) | High (repeated time-stepping) |
| Captures transient | No | Yes |
| Captures equilibrium | Yes (only fixed point) | Yes (as $t \to \infty$ steady-state) |
| Used for | Plant design, grade card | Start-up, grade transition, safety |
| Information content | Single operating point | Trajectory / time evolution |
| Polymer property insight | Average $M_n$, $M_w$, MFI | Full MWD evolution $w(\log M, t)$ |
| Stiffness handling | Not required | Mandatory (implicit solvers) |

## 3.4 Practical Polymer Engineering Example

**Scenario:** Predicting grade transition in a continuous polyethylene reactor.

- At $t < 0$: Operating at Grade A ($X = 0.85$, MFI = $5$ g/10 min).
- At $t = 0$: Switch feed to Grade B composition.
- Dynamic simulation predicts: Time for MFI to shift from $5$ to $12$ g/10 min, the off-spec material volume, and the transition strategy (chain ramps, feed-forward control).

**Steady-state alone** cannot answer this — it can only confirm the two **endpoints**. **Dynamic simulation** is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Steady-State vs Dynamic Simulation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Process Model] --> B{Type of Simulation?}
    B -- Steady-State --> C[Set dx/dt = 0]
    B -- Dynamic --> D[Solve ODE / PDE]
    C --> E[Algebraic Equations]
    D --> F[Time-Dependent Equations]
    E --> G[Newton-Raphson Solver]
    F --> H[RK4 / Gear BDF Solver]
    G --> I[Operating Point Output]
    H --> J[Trajectory Output]
    I --> K[Grade Card, MFI, Mn, Mw]
    J --> L[MWD Evolution, Conversion Profile]
    K --> M[Plant Design and Economics]
    L --> N[Start-up, Grade Transition, Safety]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Polymer Process Simulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Feed Composition]
        A2[Reactor Geometry]
        A3[Kinetic Parameters]
    end
    subgraph MODEL_CORE
        B1[Mass Balance Module]
        B2[Energy Balance Module]
        B3[Polymerization Kinetics]
        B4[MWD Computation]
    end
    subgraph SOLVER
        C1{Simulation Mode?}
        C2[Steady-State Solver]
        C3[Dynamic Solver]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        D1[Conversion Profile]
        D2[Molecular Weight Distribution]
        D3[Reactor Temperature]
        D4[Reactor Pressure]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D3
    C3 --> D4
    C2 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Polymer Process State Transition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Steady State A] -- Dynamic Grade Transition --> P2[Transient Region]
    P2 -- Dynamic Evolution --> P3[Steady State B]
    P3 -- Dynamic Shut-down --> P4[Empty Reactor]
    P2 -. Time Series Data .-> D1[Conversion vs t]
    P2 -. Time Series Data .-> D2[MFI vs t]
    P2 -. Time Series Data .-> D3[Temp vs t]
```

## 4.4 Interpretation of Schematic

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Left block** = user inputs (feed, geometry, kinetics).
> - **Middle block** = the model's physical balances (mass, energy, kinetics).
> - **Solver block** = the decision point — *“Are you simulating steady or dynamic?”*
> - **Right block** = the outputs that an engineer uses to make decisions.

This is the **canonical architecture** of a polymer process simulator (e.g., Aspen Polymers, POLYRED, Predici).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Differentiate between steady-state and dynamic simulation in polymer process modelling. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Steady-State | Dynamic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time variation | None — all variables constant | All variables evolve with time |
| Governing equation | Non-linear algebraic | ODE/PDE |
| Example | CSTR at fixed operating point | Batch polymerization start-up |

**(1 mark)** Steady-state: $\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt}=0$.
**(1 mark)** Dynamic: $\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$.
**(1 mark)** Example: CSTR grade card vs batch reactor conversion profile.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Why is dynamic simulation important for safety analysis in polymerization reactors?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Polymerizations are highly **exothermic**; temperature rise can cause **thermal runaway**.
- **(1 mark)** Dynamic simulation predicts the **temperature and pressure trajectory** during a fault scenario (cooling failure, inhibitor loss).
- **(1 mark)** It identifies **critical time-to-runaway**, enabling safe interlock design and emergency relief sizing (per API 520/521).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> Derive the steady-state conversion expression for a free-radical polymerization conducted in a CSTR. State clearly the assumptions used.

**Model Solution:**

Assumptions:
1. Constant density, perfect mixing.
2. Steady-state radical concentration (long-chain hypothesis).
3. Constant temperature and initiator concentration.
4. Termination by combination (most common in free-radical polymerization).

Step 1 — Mole balance on monomer:

$$
Q[M]_{in} - Q[M]_{out} - VR_p = 0 \quad \text{(steady-state)}
$$

Step 2 — Express in terms of conversion:

$$
\tau [M]_{in} X = R_p
$$

Step 3 — Free-radical rate:

$$
R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{2fk_d[I]}{k_t}} = k_p [M]_{in}(1-X)\sqrt{\frac{2fk_d[I]}{k_t}}
$$

Step 4 — Solve:

$$
X_{ss} = \frac{k_p \sqrt{2fk_d[I]/k_t}}{k_p \sqrt{2fk_d[I]/k_t} + \tau}
$$

**(2 marks)** Assumptions clearly stated.
**(3 marks)** Step-by-step algebra shown.
**(2 marks)** Final boxed expression with units.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> Discuss the role of steady-state and dynamic simulation in (i) reactor design and (ii) grade transition in a multi-product polymer plant.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Reactor Design — Steady-State Role (3.5 marks):**
- Used to determine **reactor volume** from target conversion via $\tau = V/Q$.
- Generates **operating envelope** (T, P, residence time) for product specification.
- Predicts **average molecular weight** ($M_n$, $M_w$) and MFI at the design point.
- Provides **economic sizing** — minimum cost for target production rate.

**(ii) Grade Transition — Dynamic Role (3.5 marks):**
- Predicts the **time profile** of MWD, conversion, and rheology during transition.
- Quantifies **off-spec material volume** — directly affects plant profitability.
- Enables **feed-forward ramp profiles** to minimize transition time.
- Supports **model predictive control (MPC)** implementation for online grade switching.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> A batch free-radical polymerization is conducted. Derive an expression for the conversion $X(t)$ as a function of time. Why must an implicit numerical solver be used for this system?

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Differential mole balance:

$$
\frac{d[M]}{dt} = -k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{2fk_d[I]_0 e^{-k_d t}}{k_t}}
$$

Step 2 — Convert to conversion variable:

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = k_p(1-X)\sqrt{\frac{2fk_d[I]_0 e^{-k_d t}}{k_t}}
$$

Step 3 — Integrate (after separating variables):

$$
-\ln(1-X(t)) = \frac{2k_p}{k_d}\sqrt{\frac{2fk_d[I]_0}{k_t}}\left(1 - e^{-k_d t/2}\right)
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $X(t)$:

$$
X(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\frac{2k_p}{k_d}\sqrt{\frac{2fk_d[I]_0}{k_t}}\left(1-e^{-k_d t/2}\right)\right]
$$

**(2 marks)** Balance equation.
**(2 marks)** Integration.
**(2 marks)** Final expression.
**(1 mark)** Discussion of stiff solver necessity.

**Why implicit solver:** Polymerization kinetics span time scales from $10^{-3}$ s (initiator decomposition) to $10^4$ s (chain growth). The **stiffness ratio** can exceed $10^6$, making explicit methods (Euler, RK4) unstable unless $\Delta t < 10^{-5}$ s. Implicit BDF methods remain stable for $\Delta t \sim 1$ s, drastically reducing computation time.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> Compare steady-state and dynamic simulation across the following criteria: (i) equation type, (ii) computational cost, (iii) type of information obtained, (iv) typical industrial application. Give at least two examples of polymer processes where each is mandatory.

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Steady-State | Dynamic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Equation type | Algebraic (NAE) | ODE / PDE |
| (ii) Computational cost | Low — single solve | High — repeated integration |
| (iii) Information | Operating point, average properties | Trajectory, transient profile |
| (iv) Typical application | Plant design, economics | Start-up, safety, transition |

**(3 marks)** Tabulated comparison with 4 criteria.
**(2 marks)** Two steady-state examples: CSTR HDPE design; PVC suspension polymerization steady output.
**(2 marks)** Two dynamic examples: PS batch reactor cycle time; LDPE autoclave runaway prediction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing steady-state with thermodynamic equilibrium** — they are NOT the same. Lose 1 mark if not clarified.
> 2. **Skipping assumptions** — every derivation MUST begin with listed assumptions (steady-state, perfect mixing, constant density, long-chain hypothesis).
> 3. **Forgetting units in final answer** — $X$ is dimensionless but $R_p$, $k_p$ etc. carry units. Examiner deducts if units omitted.
