Discuss the challenges of **bridging these scales** in a unified simulation framework. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Derive the **Rouse relaxation time** and **Reptation time** expressions, and explain how their dependence on degree of polymerization $N$ creates a fundamental computational barrier for simulating high-molecular-weight polymers. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks) The modelling of polymer systems requires a hierarchy of methods because no single computational technique can handle the entire spectrum of phenomena. **Length scales and governing methods:** 1. **Electronic scale** ($< 10^{-9}$ m): Governed by the Schrödinger equation. Methods include **Density Functional Theory (DFT)**, Hartree–Fock, and Ab Initio. Used to compute bond energies, electronic polarizability, and reactivity. 2. **Atomistic scale** ($10^{-9}$–$10^{-8}$ m): Governed by Newton's second law with empirical force fields (e.g., OPLS, TraPPE, PCFF). Methods include **Molecular Dynamics (MD)** and **Monte Carlo (MC)**. Captures chain conformations, bond rotations, and short-range interactions. 3. **Mesoscopic scale** ($10^{-8}$–$10^{-6}$ m): Governed by coarse-grained potentials and Langevin dynamics. Methods include **Dissipative Particle Dynamics (DPD)**, **Brownian Dynamics**, and the **Reptation model**. Captures entanglements, phase separation, and polymer dynamics. 4. **Microscale** ($10^{-6}$–$10^{-3}$ m): Governed by free-energy functionals. Methods include **Phase-Field modelling** and **Kinetic Monte Carlo**. Captures crystallization, lamellar growth, and morphology. 5. **Macroscale** ($> 10^{-3}$ m): Governed by conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy). Methods include **Finite Element Method (FEM)** and **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)**. Captures bulk deformation, flow, and structural response. **Challenges in bridging scales:** - **Equation incompatibility**: Each scale uses different governing equations that cannot be directly substituted into one another. - **Information loss**: Coarse-graining throws away degrees of freedom irreversibly. - **Error accumulation**: Small errors at the electronic scale propagate and amplify at the macroscale. - **Computational cost**: Sequential multi-scale simulation is essentially running $K$ simulations instead of one. > **Marks Awarded:** 1 mark per scale (5 marks total) + 2 marks for discussion of bridging challenges. --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks) **Rouse Time Derivation:** The Rouse model treats a polymer chain as $N$ beads connected by springs, with friction coefficient $\zeta$ per bead. The mean-square end-to-end distance is: $$\langle R^2 \rangle = b^2 N$$ The characteristic relaxation time of a single chain is the time for the chain to diffuse a distance $\sqrt{\langle R^2 \rangle}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{Rouse} &= \frac{\zeta \langle R^2 \rangle}{3\pi^2 k_B T} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\zeta b^2 N}{3\pi^2 k_B T} \cdot N \\[4pt] &= \frac{\zeta b^2 N^2}{3\pi^2 k_B T} \end{aligned} $$ Thus, $\tau_{Rouse} \propto N^2$. **Reptation Time Derivation:** For entangled chains ($N > N_e$), de Gennes' reptation model restricts motion to a tube of diameter $a \approx b\sqrt{N_e}$. The curvilinear diffusion coefficient along the tube is: $$D_{tube} = \frac{k_B T}{N \zeta}$$ The chain must diffuse a distance equal to its contour length $L = N l_0$ to fully escape the tube: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{rep} &= \frac{L^2}{\pi^2 D_{tube}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{(N l_0)^2 \cdot N \zeta}{\pi^2 k_B T} \\[4pt] &\propto N^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Computational barrier discussion:** - For $N = 1000$ (mildly entangled): $\tau_{Rouse} \sim 10^2$ ns. - For $N = 10{,}000$ (commercial polyethylene): $\tau_{rep} \sim$ **seconds to hours**. - Computational cost scales as $N^3$, so a **10× increase in molecular weight** requires a **1000× longer simulation**. - This makes full-chain reptation essentially **impossible** to simulate atomistically, forcing modellers to use coarse-grained approaches that sacrifice chemical detail. > **Marks Awarded:** 3 marks for Rouse derivation, 3 marks for Reptation derivation, 1 mark for computational-barrier discussion. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Q: (a)** Define the **reptation model of polymer dynamics** as proposed by de Gennes. Explain the concepts of the **tube**, **primitive path**, and **tube disengagement time** with the help of a diagram. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Discuss the **modelling difficulties caused by the lack of translational symmetry** in polymers. How does this differ from the modelling of crystalline metals? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks) **Definition:** The **reptation model**, proposed by **Pierre-Gilles de Gennes** (1971), describes the diffusion of a long entangled polymer chain through a "tube" formed by topological constraints imposed by surrounding chains. **Key concepts:** 1. **The Tube**: A topological constraint formed by entanglements with neighboring chains. The tube has diameter $a \approx b\sqrt{N_e}$ and length approximately equal to the chain's contour length $L = N l_0$. 2. **The Primitive Path**: The shortest path connecting the two ends of the chain through the entanglement network. It is the **backbone of the tube**. The chain is free to move along this path but cannot move perpendicular to it. 3. **Tube Disengagement Time ($\tau_d$)**: The time required for the chain to fully escape its original tube and assume a new random configuration. Given by $\tau_d \propto N^3$. **Physical picture (described verbally for board):** - Imagine a garden hose lying in a dense pile of identical hoses. - Each hose is too long and too tangled to move sideways — it can only **slither** forward or backward like a snake. - The "tube" is the imaginary cylinder around the hose, defined by where it touches its neighbors. - After time $\tau_d$, the hose has completely vacated its original tube and adopted a new one. > **Marks Awarded:** 2 marks for tube concept, 2 marks for primitive path, 2 marks for disengagement time, 1 mark for physical picture. --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks) **Lack of translational symmetry in polymers:** Unlike crystalline metals, where atoms sit on a regular Bravais lattice that repeats infinitely in three dimensions, polymer materials rarely exhibit long-range translational symmetry. This creates several modelling difficulties: 1. **No simple unit cell**: Polymers have a monomer as their basic chemical unit, but this unit does not repeat in a simple periodic fashion. Conformations, entanglements, and amorphous regions all differ from point to point. 2. **Amorphous regions dominate**: 40–60% of a typical semi-crystalline polymer is amorphous, with no long-range order. These regions cannot be represented by a periodic supercell. 3. **Crystallites themselves are complex**: Polymer crystals consist of folded chains, lamellae, and spherulites — not simple atomic lattices. 4. **Statistical nature**: The relevant property of a polymer chain is the **statistical average** $\langle \cdot \rangle$ over many conformations, not a single deterministic structure. **Comparison with crystalline metals:** | Property | Crystalline Metals | Polymers | |---|---|---| | Unit cell | Small, well-defined (1–10 atoms) | None, or extremely complex | | Translational symmetry | Yes, long-range | No, except in crystallites | | Governing model | Deterministic, periodic | Statistical, ensemble-based | | Electronic structure | Band theory applicable | Localized, disorder dominates | | Typical simulation | Few atoms, periodic boundaries | Millions of atoms, no periodicity | > **Marks Awarded:** 3 marks for explaining lack of symmetry, 2 marks for consequences, 2 marks for comparison with metals. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "polymers cannot be modelled" — this is wrong. They can, but with significant limitations. Always state the modelling *method* alongside the *difficulty*. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing Rouse time ($\propto N^2$) with Reptation time ($\propto N^3$). The transition occurs at $N = N_e$ (entanglement molecular weight). Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this confusion. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Drawing neat diagrams but not labelling axes or scales. Always annotate length scales (e.g., $10^{-9}$ m) on diagrams. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing generic statements like "polymers are complex" without quantifying the difficulty. Always provide a **number** (order of magnitude, scaling law, or formula). > - **Common Mistake 5:** Mixing up the "tube" (entanglement constraint) with a literal physical tube. The tube is an **emergent topological concept**, not a tangible object. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Modelling Difficulties** - ✅ Polymers are **multi-scale** (8 orders of magnitude in length, 15+ in time). - ✅ No single modelling method covers all scales — must use **multi-scale coupling**. - ✅ **Rouse time** scales as $\tau \propto N^2$; **Reptation time** scales as $\tau \propto N^3$. - ✅ The **time-scale gap** makes atomistic MD infeasible for macroscopic time. - ✅ The **length-scale gap** makes continuum FEM blind to atomic detail. - ✅ **Entanglements** create topological constraints captured by the tube model. - ✅ Polymers lack **long-range translational symmetry** — no simple unit cell. - ✅ **Coarse-graining** trades atomic detail for computational tractability. - ✅ **Error propagates** across scales — small electronic errors can become large macroscopic errors. - ✅ The **WLF equation** governs time–temperature equivalence: $\log a_T = -C_1(T-T_0)/[C_2+(T-T_0)]$. - ✅ Characteristic ratio $C_\infty$ measures chain stiffness; Kuhn length $b = C_\infty \cdot l_0$. - ✅ **De Gennes (1971)** introduced reptation — a landmark in polymer physics. - ✅ **Multi-scale bridging** uses scale-bridging operators $\mathcal{M}_{i \to i+1}$ that introduce cumulative error. - ✅ Polymer modelling difficulty is both **computational** (CPU time) and **conceptual** (no universal formalism). - ✅ Always quote a **quantitative** difficulty: scaling law, order of magnitude, or specific numerical example.

topics.title: Degree-of-freedom analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degree-of-freedom analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Modelling and Simulation of Polymer Materials**, the term **Degree-of-Freedom (DOF)** refers to the minimum number of independent coordinates, parameters, or variables required to uniquely specify the instantaneous configuration (position and orientation) of every element in a polymer system — whether the system is described at the **atomistic scale** (individual atoms and bonds), the **mesoscale** (repeat units, Kuhn segments, or coarse-grained beads), or the **macroscale** (continuum / finite element nodes).

Mathematically, if a system possesses $N$ particles in 3-dimensional Euclidean space, the system initially possesses $3N$ Cartesian coordinates. Because any free body in space can execute **3 rigid-body translations** and **3 rigid-body rotations** without altering the internal geometry, the number of *internal* degrees of freedom is given by:

$$F_{\text{internal}} = 3N - 6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Degree-of-freedom analysis in polymer modelling is the foundational counting principle used to (i) classify polymer chain architectures, (ii) select appropriate simulation ensembles (NVT, NPT, μVT), (iii) develop coarse-grained mappings, and (iv) validate force-field topologies in Molecular Dynamics (MD) and Monte Carlo (MC) simulations.

In the classical **mechanism theory** sense (Kutzbach–Grübler criterion), applied to polymer chain conformations, the mobility $M$ of a kinematic chain is:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where $n$ is the number of links (rigid sub-chains or bonds), $j_1$ is the number of single-DOF (lower) kinematic pairs (rotatable bonds, hinges), and $j_2$ is the number of two-DOF (higher) pairs. For a planar projection, the criterion simplifies accordingly.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a long **tangled headphone cable** lying in your bag. If I were blindfolded and someone told me the cable is somewhere in the room, I would need to specify:
- Where one end of the cable is positioned (3 numbers — $x$, $y$, $z$),
- Which direction the cable is pointing (2 more numbers — pitch and yaw, because the cable can roll along its own length without us noticing),
- How it **bends** at every internal joint.

Each **rotatable bond** in a polymer chain behaves exactly like the swivel joints in this cable. A polyethylene chain with 100 carbons has 99 internal C–C bonds that can each rotate almost freely. Counting DOF is essentially **counting the number of free swivels and stretches** in the chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Polymer Analogy:**
> - **Polyethylene (PE):** Highly flexible chain → many DOF → low glass transition ($T_g \approx -120\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$).
> - **Polystyrene (PS):** Bulky phenyl rings restrict rotation → fewer effective DOF → high $T_g \approx 100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$.
> 
> The number of accessible DOF directly governs a polymer's **stiffness, $T_g$, and processability**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude |
|:------:|:--------|:-----------------:|
| $N$ | Number of atoms / segments in chain | $10^{2}$ – $10^{6}$ |
| $n_{bb}$ | Number of rotatable backbone bonds | $n_{bb} = N_{bb} - 1$ |
| $\phi, \psi, \omega$ | Torsion (dihedral) angles around bonds | $[-\pi, +\pi]$ rad |
| $\theta$ | Bond angle | $\approx 109.5^\circ$ (sp³) |
| $l$ | Bond length | $\approx 1.54\,\text{Å}$ (C–C) |
| $C_\infty$ | Characteristic ratio (Flory) | $4$ – $10$ for vinyl polymers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of DOF in Polymer Systems

A polymer model can be represented at different resolutions, and the DOF count changes with each:

1. **All-atom (AA) representation**
   - Every C, H, O, N, etc., is a particle.
   - $3N$ Cartesian DOF; subtract 6 rigid-body modes → $3N - 6$ internal DOF.
   - Includes bond stretching ($l$), bond-angle bending ($\theta$), and dihedral torsion ($\phi$).

2. **United-atom (UA) representation**
   - CH, CH₂, CH₃ groups lumped into single beads.
   - Reduces DOF by a factor of $\approx 3$–$5\times$.

3. **Coarse-grained (CG) representation**
   - Multiple monomers represented by 1 super-atom.
   - Effective DOF reduced by $\approx 10$–$100\times$ per Kuhn segment.
   - Soft effective potentials replace detailed force fields.

4. **Continuum / Finite Element (FE) representation**
   - Each element node carries translational (and sometimes rotational) DOF.
   - Tetrahedral element: 4 nodes × 3 DOF = 12 DOF per element.

---

### 2.2 The Three Categories of Internal DOF in an Atomistic Polymer

For a chain of $N$ atoms:

| # | DOF Type | Symbol | Count | Energy Term |
|:-:|:---------|:------:|:-----:|:-----------:|
| 1 | Bond stretching | $l_i$ | $N - 1$ | $E_{\text{bond}} = \sum k_l (l - l_0)^2$ |
| 2 | Bond-angle bending | $\theta_i$ | $N - 2$ | $E_{\text{angle}} = \sum k_\theta (\theta - \theta_0)^2$ |
| 3 | Dihedral (torsion) | $\phi_i$ | $N - 3$ | $E_{\text{tors}} = \sum V_n [1 + \cos(n\phi - \gamma)]$ |
| 4 | Non-bonded (LJ, Coulomb) | pair $(i,j)$ | $\sim N^2$ | $E_{\text{nb}} = \sum \left[ 4\varepsilon\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right] + \frac{q_i q_j}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$ |

**Total internal DOF check** (excluding non-bonded):

$$F = \underbrace{(N-1)}_{\text{bond}} + \underbrace{(N-2)}_{\text{angle}} + \underbrace{(N-3)}_{\text{torsion}} = 3N - 6 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The identity $3N - 6$ is **exact** for any nonlinear molecule / polymer fragment in 3D. For a linear chain (no constraint on terminal rotation), the count becomes $3N - 5$ because only 2 rotational rigid-body modes are removed.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Where Used |
|:-:|:--------|:-----------:|:----------:|
| 1 | $F_{\text{int}} = 3N - 6$ | Internal DOF of non-linear N-atom body | All-atom DOF count |
| 2 | $F_{\text{int}} = 3N - 5$ | Internal DOF of linear chain | End-group analysis |
| 3 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Kutzbach mobility (spatial) | Mechanism-style chain DOF |
| 4 | $M_p = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Kutzbach mobility (planar) | 2-D projections |
| 5 | $F = 3N_{\text{atoms}} - 2N_{\text{bonds}} - N_{\text{rings}}$ | Generic DOF (topological) | Cross-linked networks |
| 6 | $C_\infty = 1 + 2\sum \langle \cos\phi\rangle + 2\sum \langle\cos\phi \cos\phi'\rangle + \cdots$ | Flory characteristic ratio | Chain flexibility |
| 7 | $n_K = C_\infty \cdot n_{\text{backbone}} / 2$ | Kuhn segments | Coarse-graining |
| 8 | $F_{\text{node}} = 3$ (3D) / $2$ (2D) | DOF per FE node | FEA |
| 9 | $\langle R^2 \rangle = C_\infty \cdot n \cdot l^2$ | End-to-end distance | Chain statistics |
| 10 | $T_g \approx T_g^{\text{ref}} + k \cdot (1 - C_\infty^{-1})$ | Empirical $T_g$–flexibility link | Polymer design |

> The symbols $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denote **ensemble averages**; $\vert \cdot \vert$ is intentionally rendered as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why DOF Analysis Matters |
|:-------|:-------------------------|
| **Tire & elastomer design** | Determines vulcanization network mobility and cross-link density effects on $T_g$. |
| **Biomedical polymers** | DOF reduction in PEG/PLGA controls drug-release kinetics. |
| **Membrane separation** | Free-volume DOF governs O₂/CO₂ selectivity. |
| **3D printing of polymers** | Constrained DOF under shear dictates extrudate swell. |
| **MD simulation setup** | Correct DOF count prevents "flying ice cube" artifact (energy equipartition violation). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — The $3N - 6$ Rule for Internal DOF

**Starting premise.** Consider $N$ point masses in 3-dimensional Euclidean space. Each mass has three Cartesian coordinates, so the **gross DOF** of the system is:

$$
D_{\text{gross}} = 3N
$$

**Step 1 — Remove translational redundancy.** The entire assembly can be translated by a single vector $(T_x, T_y, T_z)$ without changing any internal geometry. These three translations are not independent degrees of freedom of the *internal* state. Subtract 3:

$$
D_1 = 3N - 3
$$

**Step 2 — Remove rotational redundancy.** A non-linear body can be rotated about the origin, characterised by three Euler angles $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$. Such rotation does not alter bond lengths or bond angles:

$$
D_2 = 3N - 3 - 3 = 3N - 6
$$

**Step 3 — Verification by alternative counting.** Independent internal coordinates are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bond lengths } \vert l_i \vert & : N - 1 \\
\text{Bond angles } \theta_i & : N - 2 \\
\text{Dihedrals } \phi_i & : N - 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
(N-1) + (N-2) + (N-3) = 3N - 6 \quad \text{✓}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **strictly linear** chain (e.g., a single C–C–C–C bond), the molecule possesses a 3-fold or higher symmetry axis, so only **2 independent rotations** are required, giving $F = 3N - 5$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Kutzbach–Grübler Mobility for a Polymer Backbone

**Goal.** Express the number of *accessible conformations* of a vinyl polymer chain as a function of its bond count and joint types.

**Setup.** Treat the polymer backbone as a spatial kinematic chain:
- Number of links: $n$ (rigid C–C bond segments).
- Number of revolute joints (rotatable C–C single bonds): $j_1$.
- Number of universal joints (e.g., sp² linkages with restricted rotation): $j_2$.

**Kutzbach spatial criterion:**

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Step-by-step substitution for polyethylene (PE) $-\!\left[\text{CH}_2\!-\!\text{CH}_2\right]_n\!-$:**

Let the chain have $N_{\text{C}} = 10$ carbon atoms. Then:

- Number of links: $n = N_{\text{C}} - 1 = 9$.
- Number of rotatable bonds: $j_1 = 9$.
- Higher pairs: $j_2 = 0$ (all C–C bonds are simple rotations).

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{PE}} & = 3(9 - 1) - 2(9) - 0 \\
& = 3(8) - 18 \\
& = 24 - 18 \\
& = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical interpretation.** Out of the nominal $3N - 6 = 24$ internal DOF, 6 remain *unconstrained* (i.e., represent the chain's gross mobility in 3D — the 3 translations and 3 rotations of the chain as a whole). The remaining 18 internal DOF govern **bond stretching and angle bending**, while the 9 dihedral DOF define the **conformational freedom**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — DOF for a Cross-linked Epoxy Network

A diglycidyl ether of bisphenol A (DGEBA) cured with $m$ equivalents of diamine forms a network. Estimate the DOF per repeat unit assuming each cross-link consumes 2 rotatable bonds.

For a network with $N_{\text{atom}}$ atoms, $N_{\text{bond}}$ bonds, and $N_{\text{ring}}$ rings, the generic topological formula is:

$$
F = 3N_{\text{atom}} - 2N_{\text{bond}} - N_{\text{ring}}
$$

**Substitute** for a representative DGEBA-amine network (per repeat unit):
- Atoms per repeat: $N_{\text{atom}} \approx 25$ (12 C, 12 H, 1 O, plus cross-link).
- Bonds per repeat: $N_{\text{bond}} \approx 26$ (single + 1 ring closure per epoxide).
- Rings: $N_{\text{ring}} = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{unit}} & = 3(25) - 2(26) - 1 \\
& = 75 - 52 - 1 \\
& = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After cross-linking, **rotational DOF collapse** into a single network DOF of order $F_{\text{net}} \approx 3$ (the macroscopic body's rigid-body motion). The number 22 above represents the *intrinsic* vibrational / bond-stretch flexibility of a single network strand, not conformational freedom.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DOF for Common Polymers

| Polymer | Repeat unit atoms $N$ | Internal DOF (per repeat) | Effective rotatable bonds | $T_g$ (°C) |
|:-------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------------:|:-------------------------:|:----------:|
| Polyethylene (PE) | 6 | $3(6) - 6 = 12$ | 2 | $-120$ |
| Polypropylene (PP) | 9 | $3(9) - 6 = 21$ | 2 | $-10$ |
| Polystyrene (PS) | 16 | $3(16) - 6 = 42$ | 2 | $100$ |
| Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) | 15 | $3(15) - 6 = 39$ | 2 | $105$ |
| Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) | 6 | $12$ | 2 | $80$ |
| Nylon-6,6 | 22 | $60$ | 7 | $50$ |
| Natural rubber (cis-PIP) | 17 | $45$ | 4 | $-73$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **dramatic rise in $T_g$** as the number of bulky side groups increases is a direct consequence of DOF suppression — fewer accessible conformations → lower configurational entropy → higher temperature needed to unlock segmental motion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Hierarchical DOF Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer System] --> B{Resolution Level}
    B -->|Atomistic| C[All-Atom AA Model]
    B -->|Intermediate| D[United-Atom UA Model]
    B -->|Mesoscale| E[Coarse-Grained CG Model]
    B -->|Macroscale| F[Continuum FE Model]

    C --> G[3N Cartesian DOF]
    G --> G1[Subtract 3 translations]
    G1 --> G2[Subtract 3 rotations]
    G2 --> H[Internal DOF = 3N - 6]

    H --> I[Bond Stretch l_i]
    H --> J[Bond Angle theta_i]
    H --> K[Dihedral Torsion phi_i]
    H --> L[Non-bonded Pairs]

    D --> M[CH, CH2, CH3 beads]
    E --> N[Kuhn Segments]
    F --> O[Nodes and Elements]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DOF Mapping in MD Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Topology] --> P2[DOF Counter]
    P2 --> P3[Set up Bonds]
    P2 --> P4[Set up Angles]
    P2 --> P5[Set up Dihedrals]
    P2 --> P6[Set up Non-bonded Pairs]
    P3 --> P7[Force Field Assembly]
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Integrator Verlet]
    P8 --> P9[Trajectory Output]
    P9 --> P10[DOF Validation Check]
    P10 -->|Pass| P11[Equilibrium MD]
    P10 -->|Fail| P12[Flying Ice Cube Fix]
    P12 --> P8
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Rigid-Body vs Internal DOF Removal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[3N Cartesian Coordinates] --> S1[3 Translations Tx Ty Tz]
    S0 --> S2[3 Rotations Euler Angles]
    S1 --> S3[Subtract from Gross DOF]
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4[Internal DOF = 3N - 6]
    S4 --> S5[Bond Lengths N-1]
    S4 --> S6[Bond Angles N-2]
    S4 --> S7[Dihedrals N-3]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*  
**Define the term "degree of freedom" as applied to polymer chain modelling. How is it related to the glass transition temperature of a polymer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Degree of freedom (DOF) is the **minimum number of independent variables required to specify the configuration of a polymer chain** at a given instant. For an $N$-atom chain in 3D, the internal DOF equals $3N - 6$, comprising bond stretching, angle bending, and dihedral torsion coordinates.
>
> A polymer with **more accessible DOF** has higher conformational entropy in the melt; conversely, a polymer with restricted DOF (e.g., bulky side groups) requires higher thermal energy to unlock segmental motion. Hence, $T_g$ is empirically **inversely correlated with chain flexibility** (a proxy for DOF): flexible PE has $T_g \approx -120\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, while rigid PS has $T_g \approx 100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$. **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for $3N-6$ formula, 1 mark for $T_g$ correlation.)**

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*  
**With the help of the Kutzbach criterion, explain how you would determine the mobility of a polyethylene chain consisting of 6 carbon atoms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Kutzbach spatial mobility: $\;M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
> For PE with $N_{\text{C}} = 6$: links $n = 5$, rotatable bonds $j_1 = 5$, $j_2 = 0$.
> 
> $$M = 3(5-1) - 2(5) = 12 - 10 = 2$$
> 
> The chain possesses **2 unconstrained conformational degrees of freedom** beyond rigid-body motion (the 3+3 = 6 modes being already excluded). The remaining mobility reflects the dihedral flexibility of the backbone. **(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for physical interpretation.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the internal degrees of freedom of a polymer chain consisting of $N$ atoms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene (PP) chain has 50 carbon atoms in its backbone. Determine (i) the gross Cartesian DOF, (ii) the internal DOF, (iii) the number of bond-stretch, bond-angle, and dihedral coordinates. Comment on its expected $T_g$ relative to polyethylene. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Gross DOF.** Each of the $N$ atoms requires 3 coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in 3D space:

$$D_{\text{gross}} = 3N$$

**Step 2 — Translational removal.** A free rigid body can translate by $(T_x, T_y, T_z)$ without altering internal geometry. Subtract 3:

$$D_1 = 3N - 3$$

**Step 3 — Rotational removal.** A non-linear body has 3 rotational degrees of freedom (Euler angles). Subtract 3:

$$D_2 = 3N - 6$$

**Step 4 — Independent coordinate enumeration (verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bond lengths:} & \quad N - 1 \\
\text{Bond angles:} & \quad N - 2 \\
\text{Dihedrals:} & \quad N - 3 \\
\text{Sum:} & \quad (N-1) + (N-2) + (N-3) = 3N - 6 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — 1 mark gross, 1 mark translation, 1 mark rotation, 2 marks enumeration, 2 marks verification and final statement.)**

---

**(b) Solution for PP (7 marks):**

PP repeat unit is $-\!\left[\text{CH}_2\!-\!\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)\right]\!-$. Backbone carbon count $N_{\text{C}} = 50$.

**(i) Gross DOF:**

$$D_{\text{gross}} = 3 \times 50 = 150$$

**(ii) Internal DOF:**

$$D_{\text{int}} = 3(50) - 6 = 144$$

**(iii) Sub-categories:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bond lengths } (l_i) & : N - 1 = 49 \\
\text{Bond angles } (\theta_i) & : N - 2 = 48 \\
\text{Dihedral torsions } (\phi_i) & : N - 3 = 47
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
49 + 48 + 47 = 144 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Comment on $T_g$:** Although PP has the same number of *intrinsic* bond-stretch / angle / torsion DOF as PE per backbone atom, the **methyl side group** restricts dihedral rotation through steric hindrance. The **effective** conformational DOF is reduced compared to PE. Hence, $T_g^{\text{PP}} \approx -10\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, which is **higher** than $T_g^{\text{PE}} \approx -120\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$. **(7 marks — 1+1+2+1+2 distribution.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** State and explain the Kutzbach criterion for spatial mechanisms. Apply it to a polymer chain having 8 rotatable backbone bonds and 7 rigid links. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the DOF analysis of an all-atom model versus a coarse-grained (Kuhn-segment) model of a polyethylene chain of degree of polymerisation $DP = 1000$. Justify why CG models are computationally advantageous. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) (7 marks):**

**Kutzbach spatial criterion:**

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where:
- $n$ = number of links (rigid sub-chains),
- $j_1$ = number of single-DOF (lower) pairs,
- $j_2$ = number of two-DOF (higher) pairs.

**Explanation (4 marks):** The criterion counts the **mobility** of a kinematic chain — the number of inputs needed to fully control its configuration. Each link contributes 3 spatial DOF; constraints from $j_1$ pairs remove 2 DOF each, and $j_2$ pairs remove 1 DOF each; finally, one link is treated as the reference frame, removing 3 of its DOF.

**Application (3 marks):** Given $n = 7$, $j_1 = 8$, $j_2 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M & = 3(7 - 1) - 2(8) - 0 \\
& = 18 - 16 \\
& = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

The chain has **2 mobile degrees of freedom**, indicating a constrained (yet flexible) configuration space. **(7 marks total.)**

---

**(b) (7 marks):**

**All-atom (AA) model for PE with $DP = 1000$:**

- Atoms per repeat: 6 (2 C + 4 H).
- Total atoms: $N = 6 \times 1000 = 6000$.
- Internal DOF: $3(6000) - 6 = 17994$.

**Coarse-grained (CG) model:**

- 1 Kuhn segment $\approx$ $C_\infty \cdot n_{\text{backbone}} / 2 = 6.7 \times 2000 / 2 \approx 13$ backbone bonds.
- Bead count: $1000 / 13 \approx 77$ beads.
- 3 DOF per bead → $\approx 231$ DOF.

**Computational advantage (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{F_{\text{AA}}}{F_{\text{CG}}} = \frac{17994}{231} \approx 78\times
$$

CG models offer **~78× DOF reduction**, allowing:
- Larger time steps ($\Delta t_{\text{CG}} \approx 10$–$100\,\text{fs}$ vs. $\Delta t_{\text{AA}} = 1\,\text{fs}$).
- Longer trajectories in equivalent wall-clock time.
- Direct access to **mesoscale phenomena** (entanglement, reptation) inaccessible to AA.

**(7 marks — 2 marks AA, 2 marks CG, 3 marks justification.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract rigid-body modes** (–6). Many students write $3N$ as the answer and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing planar and spatial Kutzbach equations.** Use $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ for **3-D** chains only.
> 3. **Confusing DOF with degrees of polymerisation (DP).** DP is the count of repeat units; DOF counts independent coordinates.
> 4. **In CG modelling, students forget to convert monomer count to bead count** using the characteristic ratio $C_\infty$, losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Units and significant figures:** always report DOF as a pure dimensionless integer; never carry units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **DOF = independent coordinates** needed to fully specify a polymer configuration.
- [ ] **General formula (non-linear):** $F_{\text{int}} = 3N - 6$.
- [ ] **General formula (linear chain):** $F_{\text{int}} = 3N - 5$.
- [ ] **Three internal DOF types:** bond stretch $(N-1)$, bond angle $(N-2)$, dihedral $(N-3)$.
- [ ] **Kutzbach spatial mobility:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [ ] **Planar Kutzbach mobility:** $M_p = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ (same form, applied to 2-D projections).
- [ ] **Generic topological DOF:** $F = 3N_{\text{atom}} - 2N_{\text{bond}} - N_{\text{ring}}$.
- [ ] **Flory characteristic ratio** $C_\infty$ is a measure of chain stiffness that **scales with effective DOF**.
- [ ] **Glass transition temperature $T_g$** is *inversely* related to conformational DOF.
- [ ] **Coarse-graining reduces DOF** by factors of $10$–$100\times$, enabling long-time/large-scale MD.
- [ ] **All-atom DOF for PE ($DP=1000$):** $17994$.
- [ ] **Kuhn-bead DOF for PE ($DP=1000$, $C_\infty = 6.7$):** $\approx 231$.
- [ ] **FEA node DOF:** 3 per node in 3D, 2 per node in 2D.
- [ ] **Energy equipartition** in MD demands correct DOF count to avoid the "flying ice cube" artefact.
- [ ] **PE has $T_g \approx -120\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$** (flexible, high DOF); **PS has $T_g \approx 100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$** (rigid, restricted DOF).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of design variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Selection of design variables # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Modelling and Simulation of Polymer Materials**, the **Selection of Design Variables** refers to the systematic identification and mathematical representation of the independent parameters (also termed *decision variables* or *control variables*) that uniquely define a polymer system, its processing route, and its final product geometry. These variables are the **degrees of freedom** available to the designer or engineer to manipulate in order to achieve a desired set of performance objectives, while satisfying physical, chemical, thermal, and economic constraints. Mathematically, the design variable vector for a polymer design problem is represented as: $$\mathbf{X} = [X_1, X_2, X_3, \ldots, X_n]^T$$ where each $X_i$ is a scalar parameter belonging to a defined feasible domain $D_i$, bounded by lower and upper limits $X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Design variables form the **input layer** of any polymer process simulation model. Whether the underlying model is a molecular dynamics simulation, a finite element analysis of injection moulding, or a continuum-scale constitutive equation, the design variables act as the *boundary inputs* whose values drive every downstream response (mechanical, thermal, optical, rheological). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Polymer "Recipe & Knob" Analogy Imagine that you are a polymer chef preparing a high-performance **epoxy adhesive** for an aerospace application: - The **composition knobs** on your mixing console represent the *material design variables* — the weight fraction of the bisphenol-A diglycidyl ether (DGEBA) resin, the ratio of the diamine hardener, the percentage of the toughening rubber phase, and the molecular weight of the prepolymer. - The **oven knobs** represent the *process design variables* — the curing temperature (typically between **120 °C and 180 °C** for aerospace epoxies), the cure time, the applied pressure, and the heating/cooling ramp rates. - The **mould knobs** represent the *geometric design variables* — the bond-line thickness, the overlap length, and the fillet radius. Every knob you turn (i.e., every design variable you select) shifts the properties of the final cured part. The art of *selection* is knowing **which knobs matter the most** for your objective (e.g., maximising lap-shear strength) and **which knobs can be ignored** to save computational cost. This is precisely what the KTU Module-1 learning outcome CO1 demands — the structured selection of the most influential variables for polymer system modelling. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants The following standard metrics are widely used when quantifying design variables in polymer systems: - **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** — typically reported in **°C** or **K**. - **Molecular Weight ($M_w$, $M_n$)** — reported in **g·mol⁻¹** or **Da**. - **Polydispersity Index ($PDI = M_w / M_n$)** — a dimensionless number. - **Degree of Polymerisation ($X_n$)** — dimensionless. - **Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** — in **s⁻¹**. - **Viscosity ($\eta$)** — in **Pa·s**. - **Cure Conversion ($\alpha$)** — dimensionless, ranging from **0 to 1**. - **Crystallinity ($\chi_c$)** — dimensionless fraction from **0 to 1**. > [!NOTE] > **Remember:** Design variables are *not* outputs of the simulation — they are the **inputs** that the engineer actively chooses or specifies. Outputs (such as tensile strength, modulus, or final part shrinkage) are **responses** or **objectives** that depend on the chosen design variables. ## 1.4 Why Selection Matters in Polymer Modelling A polymer system is inherently **multi-scale** — spanning atomistic (~Å), mesoscopic (~nm), and macroscopic (~cm) length scales. At each scale, a different set of design variables dominates: | Scale | Dominant Design Variables | Typical Model | |-------|---------------------------|---------------| | Atomistic / Molecular | Bond length, bond angle, dihedral angle, partial charges | Molecular Dynamics (MD), DFT | | Mesoscopic | Chain stiffness, entanglement density, $X_n$, $M_w$ | Coarse-grained Monte Carlo | | Macroscopic | Composition, $T$, $P$, $\dot{\gamma}$, mould geometry | FEA (Moldflow, Abaqus) | Selecting the wrong scale's variables for a given engineering problem leads to **model mismatch** — a leading cause of simulation failure in polymer processing. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Design Variables Design variables for polymer systems can be classified along four orthogonal axes. A KTU-style answer should demonstrate awareness of all four axes. ### Axis 1: By Physical Origin 1. **Material (Composition) Variables** — intrinsic to the polymer chemistry. - Examples: wt% of styrene in SBR, epoxy-to-hardener stoichiometric ratio $r$, plasticiser content. 2. **Process Variables** — controllable during fabrication. - Examples: barrel temperature profile $T_{barrel}(z)$, injection velocity $v_{inj}$, screw rotational speed $N$, holding pressure $P_h$. 3. **Geometric / Design Variables** — define product shape. - Examples: wall thickness $t$, rib height $h_{rib}$, gate diameter $d_g$. 4. **Microstructural Variables** — emerge from chemistry and processing. - Examples: $M_w$, $X_n$, $T_g$, $\chi_c$, crosslink density $\nu_e$. ### Axis 2: By Mathematical Nature - **Continuous variables** — can assume any real value within bounds (e.g., $T$ in °C). - **Discrete variables** — chosen from a finite enumerated set (e.g., type of catalyst: A, B, or C). - **Integer variables** — restricted to whole numbers (e.g., number of reinforcing fibre layers). ### Axis 3: By Role in Optimisation - **Primary (active) variables** — directly optimised. - **Passive (fixed) variables** — held constant during a particular optimisation study. - **State variables** — determined by the governing PDE/ODE system given the design variables. ### Axis 4: By Sensitivity Ranking - **Sensitive / dominant variables** — small changes cause large output changes. - **Insensitive variables** — output barely changes. - **KTU Tip:** Sensitivity analysis (e.g., the Sobol' index or the one-at-a-time method) is the rigorous tool to perform this ranking. ## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Polymer Design Problem The standard KTU formulation of an optimisation-driven polymer design problem is as follows. Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ be the design variable vector: $$\mathbf{X} = [X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n]^T, \quad X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U$$ The general non-linear programming (NLP) problem is then: $$\text{Find } \mathbf{X} \text{ to minimise } f(\mathbf{X})$$ subject to the inequality constraints: $$g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$ the equality constraints: $$h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$ and the side (bound) constraints: $$X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ In polymer engineering, $f(\mathbf{X})$ is often the **mass** of a moulded part, the **cycle time**, the **residual stress**, or the **negative** of a mechanical property such as tensile strength. ## 2.3 Criteria for Selection of Design Variables The KTU-recommended five-step selection protocol is: 1. **Define the engineering objective** clearly (e.g., minimise warpage in a moulded polycarbonate part). 2. **List all candidate variables** that could, in principle, influence the objective. 3. **Apply dimensional and physical-validity filtering** (e.g., eliminate variables that violate conservation laws). 4. **Perform sensitivity / screening analysis** (e.g., Plackett–Burman design or Latin Hypercube Sampling) to rank influence. 5. **Confirm physical independence** — ensure no two selected variables are perfectly correlated, otherwise the design space collapses. ## 2.4 The Design Space and Feasible Region The set of all admissible values of $\mathbf{X}$ forms the **design space** $\mathcal{D}$: $$\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0, \, h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0, \, X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U\}$$ A **feasible design** is any $\mathbf{X} \in \mathcal{D}$, while an **infeasible design** violates at least one constraint. The **optimum design** $\mathbf{X}^*$ satisfies: $$f(\mathbf{X}^*) = \min_{\mathbf{X} \in \mathcal{D}} f(\mathbf{X})$$ ## 2.5 Polymer-Specific Examples of Design Variables | Engineering Application | Selected Design Variables | Typical Bounds | |--------------------------|---------------------------|----------------| | Injection-moulded polypropylene gear | $T_{melt}$, $T_{mould}$, $v_{inj}$, $P_h$, $t_{cool}$ | 200–260 °C, 20–80 °C, 10–100 mm/s, 40–80 MPa, 10–60 s | | Carbon-fibre/epoxy laminate | $V_f$, fibre orientation angle $\theta$, ply stacking sequence | 0.40–0.65, ±90°, [0/±45/90] | | Extruded PVC pipe | $T_{die}$, draw-down ratio DDR, calibrator vacuum, line speed | 180–210 °C, 1.0–1.5, 0.2–0.8 bar, 0.5–5 m/min | | Polymer electrolyte membrane | sulfonation degree $DS$, $M_w$, water uptake $\lambda$ | 0.3–0.9, 50k–300k Da, 5–25 | ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** The following table is a *cheat-sheet* of the most testable equations. Commit the definitions of each symbol to memory — KTU examiners frequently test variable definitions rather than the algebra itself. | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Symbol Glossary & Units | |---|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Design variable vector | $\mathbf{X} = [X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n]^T$ | $n$ = number of design variables | | 2 | Side (bound) constraints | $X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U$ | $L, U$ denote lower & upper | | 3 | Inequality constraints | $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0$, $j = 1,\ldots,m$ | $m$ = number of inequality constraints | | 4 | Equality constraints | $h_k(\mathbf{X}) = 0$, $k = 1,\ldots,p$ | $p$ = number of equality constraints | | 5 | Design space | $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{X} \mid g_j, h_k, \text{bounds}\}$ | subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ | | 6 | Polydispersity Index | $PDI = M_w / M_n$ | dimensionless; ideal $= 1$ | | 7 | Degree of polymerisation | $X_n = M_n / M_0$ | $M_0$ = monomer molar mass (g/mol) | | 8 | Crosslink density | $\nu_e = \rho / M_c$ | $\rho$ = density, $M_c$ = network chain molar mass | | 9 | Crystallinity from DSC | $\chi_c = \Delta H_f / \Delta H_f^0$ | $\Delta H_f$ = measured fusion enthalpy | | 10 | Sensitivity coefficient | $S_i = (\partial f / \partial X_i) \cdot (X_i / f)$ | dimensionless, used for ranking | | 11 | Normalised sensitivity | $\overline{S}_i = S_i / \sum \vert S_j \vert$ | weights to $[0,1]$ | | 12 | Cure kinetics (Kamal) | $d\alpha/dt = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$ | $k_i$ = Arrhenius rate constants | | 13 | Arrhenius for $k$ | $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | $E_a$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | | 14 | Carreau viscosity model | $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)[1+(\lambda\dot\gamma)^2]^{(n-1)/2}$ | $n, \lambda$ are fitted parameters | | 15 | Composite rule of mixtures | $E_c = V_f E_f + (1-V_f) E_m$ | $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction | | 16 | Carathéodory's theorem | feasible $\mathbf{X}$ requires $n \geq p + q$ | $p$ eq. + $q$ active ineq. constraints | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Carathéodory's theorem:** This is the *existence condition* for a feasible polymer design with $n$ design variables, $p$ equality constraints, and $q$ active inequality constraints. KTU 2024 expects students to state this explicitly when justifying their choice of $n$ in a design problem. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The discipline of selecting the *right* design variables governs every modern polymer engineering workflow: - **Automotive:** Selecting the right combination of glass-fibre content, mould temperature, and holding pressure is what enables the mass production of the 30 % glass-fibre-reinforced polyamide air-intake manifolds now standard in passenger cars. - **Biomedical:** Selecting the correct co-monomer ratio and crosslink density dictates the swelling behaviour and drug-release kinetics of contact lenses and hydrogel wound dressings. - **Electronics:** The dielectric constant and loss tangent of a polymer capacitor film are governed by design choices such as the polarity of the base resin (PP, PET, PEN) and the addition of high-$k$ ceramic nanofillers. - **Aerospace:** Carbon-fibre-reinforced PEEK (poly-ether-ether-ketone) composites require careful selection of crystallinity, fibre volume fraction, and consolidation pressure to meet aerospace certification standards. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Physical Insight to the Mathematical Design Vector We will now walk through the *complete* derivation of how one arrives at the design variable vector $\mathbf{X}$ for a representative polymer engineering problem. **Problem statement (typical KTU-style):** Consider an **extrusion-grade low-density polyethylene (LDPE)** to be processed into a film for packaging. The film must satisfy: - Tensile strength $\sigma_t \geq 12$ MPa - Haze $\leq 6$ % - Melt flow index (MFI) between 1.5 and 3.0 g/10 min The engineer is free to vary: (i) the weight-average molecular weight $M_w$, (ii) the long-chain branching index $LCB$, (iii) the die temperature $T_{die}$, and (iv) the take-up velocity $v_{tu}$. **Step 1 — Identify candidate variables from physics.** From polymer rheology, the tensile strength of LDPE correlates with $M_w$ and the entanglement density $\rho_e \propto M_w^{-1}$. Haze is dominated by crystallinity $\chi_c$, which in turn depends on the cooling rate (and hence on $T_{die}$ and $v_{tu}$). MFI is dictated by $M_w$ and LCB through the Carreau model. **Step 2 — Filter out dependent variables.** Since $\chi_c$ is a *function* of $T_{die}$ and $v_{tu}$ (not an independent choice), it cannot be a design variable — it is a response. **Step 3 — Define the design vector.** $$\mathbf{X} = [X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4]^T = [M_w, \, LCB, \, T_{die}, \, v_{tu}]^T$$ **Step 4 — Express bound constraints from physics and the problem statement.** $$80{,}000 \leq X_1 \leq 250{,}000 \quad [\text{g/mol}]$$ $$0.0 \leq X_2 \leq 4.0 \quad [\text{branches / 1000 C}]$$ $$160 \leq X_3 \leq 220 \quad [^{\circ}\text{C}]$$ $$5 \leq X_4 \leq 40 \quad [\text{m/min}]$$ **Step 5 — Express the performance constraints as inequality functions $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0$.** $$g_1(\mathbf{X}) = 12 - \sigma_t(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0$$ $$g_2(\mathbf{X}) = \text{Haze}(\mathbf{X}) - 6 \leq 0$$ $$g_3(\mathbf{X}) = 1.5 - \text{MFI}(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0$$ $$g_4(\mathbf{X}) = \text{MFI}(\mathbf{X}) - 3.0 \leq 0$$ **Step 6 — Define the objective.** A common objective in extrusion-grade LDPE design is to minimise the specific energy consumption $E_{sp}$ (kWh/kg), which is itself a function of $T_{die}$, $v_{tu}$ and $M_w$. $$f(\mathbf{X}) = E_{sp}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot v_{tu} \cdot A} \int_{0}^{L} \eta(T, \dot\gamma) \dot\gamma^2 \, dz$$ where $A$ is the die cross-section, $L$ the die length, and $\eta$ the shear-dependent viscosity. **Step 7 — Assemble the final NLP.** $$\min_{\mathbf{X}} f(\mathbf{X}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0, \; j = 1,\ldots,4, \; X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U$$ This is the canonical form KTU examiners expect to see in a 14-mark question. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity-Based Variable Selection When the engineer has *too many* candidate variables (say, $n_{cand} = 20$) and the simulation cost is prohibitive, a **sensitivity-based screening** is performed. Below is the complete derivation of the screening metric. **Step 1 — Define the local (one-at-a-time) sensitivity.** For a response $Y = f(\mathbf{X})$: $$S_i = \frac{\partial f}{\partial X_i} \bigg|_{\mathbf{X}_0}$$ **Step 2 — Non-dimensionalise using a finite-difference approximation.** For a small perturbation $\Delta X_i$ (typically 1–5 % of the bound): $$S_i \approx \frac{f(X_i + \Delta X_i) - f(X_i)}{\Delta X_i}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to a dimensionless, comparable form.** $$\overline{S}_i = S_i \cdot \frac{X_i^{ref}}{f^{ref}}$$ where $X_i^{ref}$ and $f^{ref}$ are reference values (often mid-range and the response at the centre point). **Step 4 — Rank and threshold.** Compute $\overline{S}_i$ for all $i = 1, \ldots, n_{cand}$, then retain only those with $\vert \overline{S}_i \vert \geq \overline{S}_{threshold}$ (e.g., $\overline{S}_{threshold} = 0.05$). This yields the **reduced variable set** of size $n \ll n_{cand}$. **Worked numeric example:** Suppose the candidate variables for an epoxy cure are $X_1$ = cure temperature, $X_2$ = ramp rate, $X_3$ = hold time, $X_4$ = initial viscosity, $X_5$ = filler loading. The response is the final glass transition temperature $T_g$. At the centre point $\mathbf{X}_0 = [180, 2, 120, 0.8, 15]^T$, a 1 % perturbation gives: | Variable | $\Delta Y$ per $\Delta X_i$ | $\overline{S}_i$ | Rank | Retain? | |----------|----------------------------|------------------|------|---------| | $X_1$ (°C) | $+0.45$ | $+0.81$ | 1 | ✓ | | $X_2$ (°C/min) | $-0.10$ | $-0.18$ | 2 | ✓ | | $X_3$ (min) | $+0.08$ | $+0.14$ | 3 | ✓ | | $X_4$ (Pa·s) | $+0.01$ | $+0.02$ | 4 | ✗ (below threshold 0.05) | | $X_5$ (wt%) | $+0.04$ | $+0.07$ | 5 | ✓ | Therefore, the selected design variables are $\{X_1, X_2, X_3, X_5\}$ and $X_4$ is dropped from the model. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dimensional Consistency Check for a Polymer Variable Set To pass KTU valuation, the units of every $X_i$ and every $g_j$ must be **dimensionally consistent**. The systematic procedure is: **Step 1 — Convert every $X_i$ to its SI base units.** **Step 2 — Substitute into the response function.** **Step 3 — Verify that the resulting $f(\mathbf{X})$ and $g_j(\mathbf{X})$ reduce to a single, consistent SI unit (e.g., Pa for stress, °C for $T$, dimensionless for haze).** **Example check for tensile strength $\sigma_t$ via the rule of mixtures:** $$\sigma_t = V_f \sigma_f + (1 - V_f) \sigma_m$$ The units are: $$[\sigma_t] = [\text{dimensionless}] \cdot [\text{MPa}] + [\text{dimensionless}] \cdot [\text{MPa}] = [\text{MPa}]$$ ✓ Dimensionally consistent. Both $V_f$ and $(1 - V_f)$ are dimensionless volume fractions. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Choosing Variables | Engineering Question | Recommended Variable Set | Justification | |----------------------|--------------------------|---------------| | Is the goal strength-driven? | $V_f$, fibre orientation, $M_w$, crystallinity | These directly govern load transfer | | Is the goal process-driven (cycle time)? | $T_{melt}$, $T_{mould}$, $v_{inj}$, $P_h$ | These dominate cooling & packing | | Is the goal optical (transparency)? | $\chi_c$, additive content, cooling rate | These control light scattering | | Is the goal barrier (permeability)? | Crystallinity, orientation, density | These dictate free-volume | | Is the goal bio-medical (drug release)? | Crosslink density, hydrophilicity, $M_w$ | These control diffusion & swelling | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow for Selection of Design Variables The following Mermaid diagram captures the **algorithmic decision flow** that an engineer (or a KTU student solving a 14-mark question) follows when selecting design variables for a polymer system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Define Engineering Objective
e.g., minimise warpage in moulded PP part"] --> B["Enumerate All Candidate Variables
n_cand up to 20+ physical and process parameters"] B --> C["Apply Physical Validity Filter
remove impossible / redundant variables"] C --> D["Run Screening Design
e.g., Plackett-Burman or OFAT"] D --> E["Compute Normalised Sensitivity Bars
S_i for each candidate variable"] E --> F{"Is Bars_i greater than threshold
typically 0.05"} F -- Yes --> G["Retain X_i in reduced set"] F -- No --> H["Drop X_i from design vector"] G --> I["Combine retained X_i into vector X"] H --> I I --> J["Define Bound Constraints
X_i^L and X_i^U from physics and specs"] J --> K["Define Inequality Constraints g_j of X less than or equal to 0
performance requirements"] K --> L["Define Equality Constraints h_k of X equal to 0
mass / energy balance if any"] L --> M["Assemble Final Design Problem
min f of X subject to constraints"] M --> N["Solve via NLP Solver
e.g., SQP, GA, or response surface"] N --> O["Validate Optimum X_star
Check physics and run confirmatory simulation"] O --> P["End: Document Selected Design Variables"] ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchical Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Polymer Design Variables ```mermaid flowchart LR DV["Design Variables X for Polymer System"] --> M["Material Variables"] DV --> P["Process Variables"] DV --> G["Geometric Variables"] DV --> S["Microstructural Variables"] M --> M1["Composition wt%"] M --> M2["Additive loading"] M --> M3["Catalyst type"] P --> P1["Temperature T"] P --> P2["Pressure P"] P --> P3["Shear rate gamma_dot"] P --> P4["Cure time t_cure"] G --> G1["Wall thickness t"] G --> G2["Gate diameter d_g"] G --> G3["Fibre orientation theta"] S --> S1["Molecular weight M_w"] S --> S2["Crosslink density nu_e"] S --> S3["Crystallinity chi_c"] S --> S4["Glass transition T_g"] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Interaction Between Design Variables, Constraints, and Objective ```mermaid flowchart LR X["Design Variable Vector X
n independent inputs"] --> SIM["Polymer Process / Property Model
CFD, FEA, MD, Constitutive"] P["Process Conditions T, P, gamma_dot"] --> SIM M["Material Database
rheology, thermodynamics"] --> SIM SIM --> Y["Response Vector Y
stress, MFI, haze, T_g"] Y --> OBJ["Objective Function f of X
minimise cost or maximise property"] Y --> CONS["Constraint Functions g_j and h_k
performance limits"] OBJ --> OPT["NLP Solver finds X_star"] CONS --> OPT OPT --> X ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Iterative Refinement of Variable Selection ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant E as Engineer participant M as Model / Simulation participant A as Sensitivity Analyzer E->>M: Define initial candidate set X_cand M-->>E: Simulated response Y for each candidate E->>A: Request sensitivity of Y to each X_i A-->>E: Normalised sensitivity list Bars_i E->>E: Threshold and rank variables E->>M: Submit reduced design vector X M-->>E: Feasible and optimum design X_star E->>E: Document and validate ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` **Define the term "design variable" as used in the modelling and optimisation of polymer systems. Give two examples of process design variables in injection moulding of thermoplastics.** **Model Answer:** A *design variable* is an independent parameter whose value is freely chosen by the designer to define a polymer system, its processing route, or its geometry. The set of design variables forms the input vector $\mathbf{X} = [X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n]^T$ of the optimisation problem. They must be **independent, controllable, and physically meaningful**. **Two examples of process design variables in injection moulding:** 1. **Melt temperature** $T_{melt}$ — typically in the range **200–280 °C** for polypropylene, governing viscosity and fill behaviour. 2. **Injection velocity** $v_{inj}$ — typically in the range **10–100 mm/s**, governing shear rate, molecular orientation, and surface finish. *(Alternate valid examples: holding pressure $P_h$, mould temperature $T_{mould}$, cooling time $t_{cool}$, packing pressure profile.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]` **Differentiate between a continuous and a discrete design variable, citing one polymer-engineering example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Continuous Design Variable | Discrete Design Variable | |--------|----------------------------|---------------------------| | Definition | Can assume any real value within bounds | Chosen from a finite, enumerated set | | Mathematical nature | Real-valued $X \in [X^L, X^U]$ | Integer, categorical, or binary | | Polymer example 1 | Curing temperature $T$ in °C | Choice of catalyst type (A, B, or C) | | Polymer example 2 | Glass-fibre volume fraction $V_f$ | Number of fabric plies in a laminate (e.g., 4, 8, 12) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` **(a)** *With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **systematic procedure** for the selection of design variables in a polymer engineering optimisation problem. Clearly indicate the role of sensitivity analysis in this procedure.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *For the design of a carbon-fibre-reinforced epoxy composite bracket used in an aerospace application, the engineer intends to vary four candidate variables: fibre volume fraction $V_f$, fibre orientation angle $\theta$, cure temperature $T_{cure}$, and post-cure time $t_{pc}$. The response of interest is the longitudinal tensile modulus $E_L$. Formulate the complete design-optimisation problem in standard NLP form, defining the design variable vector, the objective function, and at least three representative constraints.* **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Systematic Procedure for Selection of Design Variables (7 Marks)** The procedure comprises **five sequential steps**: 1. **Define the engineering objective** explicitly (e.g., minimise mass, maximise stiffness-to-weight ratio, minimise warpage). The objective becomes the response $Y$ on which sensitivity is later judged. 2. **Enumerate all candidate variables** that can, in principle, affect $Y$. For a polymer system, these typically fall into four classes — material, process, geometric, and microstructural. Brainstorm with the design team and consult literature; aim for a superset ($n_{cand} \geq 20$). 3. **Apply physical-validity filtering.** Eliminate variables that violate conservation laws, that cannot be physically realised, or that are perfectly correlated with others (correlation coefficient $\rho > 0.95$). 4. **Perform sensitivity-based screening.** Use a space-filling design (Latin Hypercube, Plackett–Burman) to evaluate the response $Y$ at perturbed values of each candidate. Compute the normalised sensitivity $\overline{S}_i$. Rank the variables and retain only those with $\vert \overline{S}_i \vert \geq \overline{S}_{threshold}$ (commonly **0.05**). 5. **Confirm physical independence and assemble the final vector** $\mathbf{X} = [X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n]^T$, then attach bounds $X_i^L, X_i^U$ and performance constraints $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0$. The **role of sensitivity analysis** is to objectively reduce the dimensionality of the design space from $n_{cand}$ to a manageable $n$. This is critical because: - Each additional design variable multiplies the computational cost of the simulation by a factor of 2–10. - Insensitive variables add noise without improving the optimum. - Sensitivity analysis converts an *artistic* design choice into a *scientific* one. **Schematic:** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes (the Mermaid flow from "Start: Define Objective" to "End: Document Selected Design Variables"). --- **(b) NLP Formulation for the Composite Bracket (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Define the design variable vector.** $$\mathbf{X} = [X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4]^T = [V_f, \, \theta, \, T_{cure}, \, t_{pc}]^T$$ **Step 2 — State the bounds (from physics and certification limits).** $$0.40 \leq X_1 \leq 0.65$$ $$-90^{\circ} \leq X_2 \leq +90^{\circ}$$ $$120 \leq X_3 \leq 180 \quad [^{\circ}\text{C}]$$ $$60 \leq X_4 \leq 240 \quad [\text{min}]$$ **Step 3 — Define the objective function.** The aerospace requirement is to **minimise the bracket mass** $m$ subject to a stiffness constraint. For a unidirectional composite (rule of mixtures): $$E_L(\mathbf{X}) = X_1 \cdot E_f + (1 - X_1) \cdot E_m$$ where $E_f \approx 230$ GPa (carbon fibre) and $E_m \approx 3.5$ GPa (epoxy). The bracket mass for a fixed geometry (volume $V_0$ of composite) is: $$m = \rho(X_1) \cdot V_0 = \left[ X_1 \rho_f + (1 - X_1) \rho_m \right] V_0$$ Hence the objective: $$f(\mathbf{X}) = m(\mathbf{X}) = \left[ X_1 \rho_f + (1 - X_1) \rho_m \right] V_0$$ with $\rho_f \approx 1.80$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m \approx 1.20$ g/cm³. **Step 4 — Define the constraints.** $$g_1(\mathbf{X}) = 150 - E_L(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0 \quad \text{(stiffness requirement, GPa)}$$ $$g_2(\mathbf{X}) = \text{CTE}_{\alpha}(\mathbf{X}) - 1 \times 10^{-6} \leq 0 \quad \text{(thermal expansion limit, K}^{-1}\text{)}$$ $$g_3(\mathbf{X}) = T_g(X_3, X_4) - 150 \leq 0 \quad \text{(must exceed max service temp)}$$ $$g_4(\mathbf{X}) = 0.65 - X_1 \leq 0 \quad \text{(already in bounds, repeated for emphasis)}$$ **Step 5 — Final NLP statement.** $$\min_{\mathbf{X}} f(\mathbf{X}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0, \; j = 1,\ldots,4, \; X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U$$ The number of design variables is $n = 4$, inequality constraints $m = 4$, and no equality constraints ($p = 0$). Carathéodory's existence condition requires $n \geq p + q$, where $q$ is the number of active inequality constraints at the optimum. With at most $q = 4$ active constraints, we need $n \geq 4$, which is satisfied. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** *Explain, with a suitable polymer-engineering example, the difference between **material**, **process**, and **geometric** design variables. Why is the choice of microstructural variables (such as $M_w$, $T_g$, crystallinity) often deferred to a later stage of selection?* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *In the rotational moulding of a polyethylene powder part, the engineer considers three process variables: oven temperature $T_{oven}$, heating time $t_{heat}$, and cooling rate $r_{cool}$. The objective is to maximise the degree of sintering $D_s$ (dimensionless, ranging from 0 to 1). Using the data below, perform a **one-at-a-time (OFAT) sensitivity analysis** at the centre point $\mathbf{X}_0 = [280, 18, 1.5]^T$ (units: °C, min, °C/min). A 5 % perturbation gives the following responses:* | Variable | Centre Response | Perturbed Response | |----------|-----------------|---------------------| | $T_{oven}$ | 0.78 | 0.84 | | $t_{heat}$ | 0.78 | 0.81 | | $r_{cool}$ | 0.78 | 0.74 | *Rank the variables by normalised sensitivity and identify which to retain for the design-optimisation study.* **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Material, Process, and Geometric Variables (7 Marks)** | Class | Definition | Rotational Moulding Example | |-------|------------|------------------------------| | Material | Variables intrinsic to the polymer's chemistry | PE grade, $M_w$, additive content, pigment loading | | Process | Variables controllable during fabrication | $T_{oven}$, $t_{heat}$, $r_{cool}$, rotational speed $N$ | | Geometric | Variables defining product shape | Wall thickness $t$, part diameter $D$, draft angle | **Why microstructural variables are deferred:** Microstructural descriptors such as $M_w$, $T_g$, crystallinity $\chi_c$, and crosslink density $\nu_e$ are typically **responses** of the material-and-process choices rather than *independent* inputs. For example, $\chi_c$ emerges from the interaction of cooling rate and molecular weight; $T_g$ is fixed by the chemistry and only slightly tuneable by additives. Selecting them as design variables would lead to: - **Identifiability problems** — two variables that are deterministically linked cannot both be decision variables. - **Infeasible design space** — demanding a specific $T_g$ while freely choosing $T_{cure}$ may lead to contradiction. - **Loss of physical realism** — the simulation may push the microstructural variable to a value that the chosen chemistry cannot deliver. Hence KTU 2024 recommends a **two-stage approach**: - Stage 1: Select chemistry and process variables. - Stage 2: Predict microstructural responses using the validated model and use them only as constraints or targets. --- **(b) OFAT Sensitivity Analysis (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Compute the raw sensitivity for each variable.** Using the 5 % perturbation, the perturbation magnitudes are: $$\Delta X_1 = 0.05 \times 280 = 14 \quad [^{\circ}\text{C}]$$ $$\Delta X_2 = 0.05 \times 18 = 0.9 \quad [\text{min}]$$ $$\Delta X_3 = 0.05 \times 1.5 = 0.075 \quad [^{\circ}\text{C/min}]$$ **Step 2 — Compute the raw sensitivity $S_i = \Delta Y / \Delta X_i$.** $$S_1 = \frac{0.84 - 0.78}{14} = \frac{0.06}{14} \approx 4.286 \times 10^{-3} \quad [^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}]$$ $$S_2 = \frac{0.81 - 0.78}{0.9} = \frac{0.03}{0.9} \approx 33.33 \times 10^{-3} \quad [\text{min}^{-1}]$$ $$S_3 = \frac{0.74 - 0.78}{0.075} = \frac{-0.04}{0.075} \approx -533.3 \times 10^{-3} \quad [\text{min/}^{\circ}\text{C}]$$ **Step 3 — Non-dimensionalise using the centre values $X_i^0$ and $Y^0 = 0.78$.** $$\overline{S}_i = S_i \cdot \frac{X_i^0}{Y^0}$$ $$\overline{S}_1 = 4.286 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{280}{0.78} \approx 1.539$$ $$\overline{S}_2 = 33.33 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{18}{0.78} \approx 0.769$$ $$\overline{S}_3 = -533.3 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{1.5}{0.78} \approx -1.026$$ **Step 4 — Normalise to sum to unity.** $$\sum \vert \overline{S}_i \vert = 1.539 + 0.769 + 1.026 = 3.334$$ $$\overline{S}_1^{norm} = \frac{1.539}{3.334} \approx 0.462 \quad (46.2\%)$$ $$\overline{S}_2^{norm} = \frac{0.769}{3.334} \approx 0.231 \quad (23.1\%)$$ $$\overline{S}_3^{norm} = \frac{1.026}{3.334} \approx 0.308 \quad (30.8\%)$$ **Step 5 — Rank and decide.** | Rank | Variable | Normalised Sensitivity | Decision | |------|----------|------------------------|----------| | 1 | $T_{oven}$ | 0.462 | **Retain** | | 2 | $r_{cool}$ | 0.308 | **Retain** | | 3 | $t_{heat}$ | 0.231 | Retain (just above threshold) | All three variables have $\overline{S}_i^{norm} > 0.05$, so **all three are retained** for the design-optimisation study. The dominant variable is the oven temperature, contributing ~46 % of the variance in the sintering degree. The cooling rate has a *negative* effect (faster cooling → less time for sintering → lower $D_s$), which is physically consistent. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic — KTU 2024 examiner notes:** > > 1. **Confusing design variables with responses.** Design variables are *inputs you choose*; responses are *outputs you measure*. Marks are routinely docked when a student lists tensile strength as a design variable. > > 2. **Forgetting to specify units AND bounds.** A design variable without explicit bounds $X_i^L, X_i^U$ is incomplete. Always state both. > > 3. **Writing $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \geq 0$ when the standard convention is $g_j(\mathbf{X}) \leq 0$.** KTU examiners follow the canonical NLP convention strictly. > > 4. **Not stating Carathéodory's existence condition** when justifying the number of design variables selected. This is a frequent 1-mark differentiator. > > 5. **Mixing material, process, geometric, and microstructural variables into one undifferentiated list.** Always classify them — examiners award marks for the classification logic itself. > > 6. **Skipping the sensitivity analysis step.** Even an approximate ranking earns at least 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. > > 7. **Dimensional inconsistency.** Check that $f(\mathbf{X})$ and $g_j(\mathbf{X})$ carry well-defined units (Pa, °C, MPa, dimensionless, etc.). A 1-mark penalty is applied per inconsistency. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** A design variable is an independent, controllable input to a polymer model. The set of all such variables forms the design vector $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. - **Four classes:** Material, process, geometric, microstructural — classify every variable you list. - **Three mathematical types:** Continuous (most common), discrete, integer. - **Bounds:** Every $X_i$ must have $X_i^L \leq X_i \leq X_i^U$ from physics and specifications. - **Standard NLP form:** $\min f(\mathbf{X})$ subject to $g_j \leq 0$, $h_k = 0$, and side bounds. - **Sensitivity ranking:** Use $\overline{S}_i = S_i \cdot X_i^{ref}/f^{ref}$ to identify dominant variables; threshold at ~0.05. - **Carathéodory's condition:** A feasible polymer design exists iff $n \geq p + q$ (number of variables ≥ number of active constraints). - **Microstructural deferral:** $M_w$, $T_g$, $\chi_c$, $\nu_e$ are usually *responses*, not decision variables — handle them in Stage-2 modelling. - **Polymer examples to memorise:** Injection moulding (PP, ABS), extrusion (LDPE, PVC), composites (CF/epoxy), rotational moulding (PE powder), membranes (sulfonated PEEK). - **Units to remember:** $T$ in °C or K, $M_w$ in g/mol, $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $\alpha$ and $\chi_c$ dimensionless. - **Examiner's mantra:** *"Variables must be independent, controllable, bounded, and physically meaningful — and the design problem must be dimensionally consistent."*

topics.title: Review of numerical techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of numerical techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Techniques** are a set of computational algorithms and mathematical procedures used to obtain approximate solutions to mathematical problems that cannot be solved exactly by analytical (closed-form) methods. In the context of **Modelling and Simulation of Polymer Materials**, numerical techniques provide the computational backbone for solving governing equations describing polymerization kinetics, viscoelastic behavior, heat and mass transfer in polymer processing, diffusion in membranes, and molecular dynamics simulations.

The review of numerical techniques in this module establishes the foundation for the simulation tasks encountered later in polymer engineering, including:

- **Root-finding** for equilibrium equations (e.g., Flory-Huggins interaction parameter estimation).
- **Numerical integration** for processing throughput and molecular weight distribution calculations.
- **Solving systems of linear/non-linear algebraic equations** arising from discretized transport equations.
- **Numerical differentiation** for evaluating strain rates and stress gradients.
- **Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) solvers** for polymerization kinetics and cure kinetics.
- **Partial Differential Equation (PDE) solvers** for heat transfer in extrusion or injection molding.
- **Curve fitting and regression** for experimental rheological data (viscosity vs. shear rate).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module "Review of numerical techniques" is intended as a refresher/bridge module to ensure students possess adequate computational fluency before tackling advanced polymer simulation topics such as Monte Carlo methods, Finite Element Analysis (FEA), and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) of polymer flows.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **numerical techniques as a GPS navigating a complex terrain**:

When you drive from point A (initial guess) to point B (exact solution), an analytical solution would be like having a straight highway with a signpost showing exactly how far you are and when you'll arrive. But in polymer modelling, the terrain is full of hills, valleys, and curves — there's no straight highway. A numerical method is like a GPS that:

1. **Takes your current position** (the current estimate of the solution).
2. **Checks the gradient of the terrain** (the slope of the function).
3. **Tells you which direction to move** (next iteration step).
4. **Repeats until you reach your destination** (convergence to a tolerance).

Just as different GPS algorithms (shortest path, fastest, scenic route) suit different travellers, different numerical methods (Bisection, Newton-Raphson, Runge-Kutta, etc.) suit different types of mathematical problems encountered in polymer engineering.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Polymer systems rarely yield closed-form solutions because they involve complex non-linear viscoelastic constitutive equations (e.g., the Giesekus, Phan-Thien Tanner, or Oldroyd-B models) coupled with momentum and energy equations. Numerical techniques are therefore indispensable in polymer processing simulations.

---

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Definitions

- **Convergence:** The iterative process approaches the true solution as $n \to \infty$.
- **Error:** The difference between the numerical approximation and the exact solution.
  - **Absolute error:** $E_a = \vert x_{\text{approx}} - x_{\text{exact}} \vert$
  - **Relative error:** $E_r = \dfrac{\vert x_{\text{approx}} - x_{\text{exact}} \vert}{\vert x_{\text{exact}} \vert}$
- **Tolerance (ε):** A pre-defined small value (e.g., $10^{-6}$) used to stop iteration when the error falls below this threshold.
- **Order of accuracy ($p$):** The exponent $p$ such that the error is proportional to $h^p$, where $h$ is the step size.
- **Iteration:** A single execution of the numerical scheme producing a new approximation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values used in polymer simulations:**
> - Typical convergence tolerance: **$\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$**
> - Time-step size in kinetic simulations: **$\Delta t = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ s**
> - Mesh size in CFD of polymer flow: **$h = 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Numerical Techniques

Numerical techniques relevant to polymer modelling can be classified as follows:

1. **Solution of Algebraic Equations**
   - Single variable (non-linear): Bisection, Newton-Raphson, Secant, Fixed-Point Iteration.
   - Multiple variables: Gauss elimination, Gauss-Seidel, LU decomposition, Newton's method for systems.

2. **Numerical Integration**
   - Newton-Cotes formulas: Trapezoidal rule, Simpson's 1/3 rule, Simpson's 3/8 rule.
   - Gauss quadrature: Gauss-Legendre, Gauss-Chebyshev.

3. **Numerical Differentiation**
   - Forward, backward, and central difference formulas.

4. **Interpolation**
   - Newton's forward/backward difference, Lagrange interpolation, cubic splines.

5. **Regression & Curve Fitting**
   - Linear regression, non-linear least squares (used for fitting Carreau, Cross, and Power-law viscosity models to rheological data).

6. **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)**
   - Single-step: Euler's method, Modified Euler, Runge-Kutta (RK2, RK4).
   - Multi-step: Adams-Bashforth, Adams-Moulton.

7. **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)**
   - Finite Difference Method (FDM), Finite Element Method (FEM), Finite Volume Method (FVM).

8. **Stochastic Methods**
   - Monte Carlo simulation (for polymer chain statistics and molecular simulations).

---

### 2.2 Logical Steps for the Bisection Method (Root Finding)

**Why this matters for polymers:** Determining the equilibrium conversion in step-growth polymerization where the Carothers equation is non-linear.

**Procedure:**
1. Locate an interval $[a, b]$ such that $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ (Intermediate Value Theorem).
2. Compute the midpoint: $c = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$.
3. Evaluate $f(c)$.
4. If $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, set $b = c$; otherwise, set $a = c$.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$.

**Convergence:** Linear (order $p = 1$); guaranteed but slow.

---

### 2.3 Logical Steps for the Newton-Raphson Method

**Why this matters:** Used for solving non-linear equations arising from Flory-Huggins theory and free-volume theories of polymer diffusion.

**Procedure:**
1. Start with an initial guess $x_0$.
2. Iterate using:
   $$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$
3. Stop when $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$.

**Convergence:** Quadratic (order $p = 2$); fast but requires good initial guess and derivative computation.

---

### 2.4 Numerical Integration — Trapezoidal Rule & Simpson's Rules

**Why this matters:** Computing cumulative molecular weight distribution $\int_0^\infty M \cdot p(M)\, dM$ where analytical integration is impossible.

- **Trapezoidal Rule (composite):**
  $$\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \approx \dfrac{h}{2}\left[f(x_0) + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\right]$$
  where $h = \dfrac{b-a}{n}$.

- **Simpson's 1/3 Rule:**
  $$\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \approx \dfrac{h}{3}\left[f(x_0) + 4\sum_{\text{odd}} f(x_i) + 2\sum_{\text{even}} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\right]$$
  Requires $n$ to be even.

- **Simpson's 3/8 Rule:**
  $$\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \approx \dfrac{3h}{8}\left[f(x_0) + 3f(x_1) + 3f(x_2) + f(x_3)\right]$$
  Used when $n$ is a multiple of 3.

---

### 2.5 Euler's Method for ODEs (Polymerization Kinetics)

For an ODE: $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = f(t, y)$, with initial condition $y(t_0) = y_0$.

**Euler's explicit method:**
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(t_n, y_n)$$

**Modified Euler (Heun's method):**
$$\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= f(t_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= f(t_n + h, y_n + h \cdot k_1) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \dfrac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**4th-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4) — Industry Standard:**
$$\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= f(t_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= f(t_n + h/2, \; y_n + h \cdot k_1/2) \\
k_3 &= f(t_n + h/2, \; y_n + h \cdot k_2/2) \\
k_4 &= f(t_n + h, \; y_n + h \cdot k_3) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \dfrac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Method** | **Formula / Update Rule** | **Order (p)** | **Polymer Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bisection | $c = (a+b)/2$, halve interval each step | 1 | Equilibrium conversion in step-growth polymerization |
| Newton-Raphson | $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$ | 2 | Flory-Huggins interaction parameter |
| Secant | $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n) \cdot (x_n - x_{n-1}) / [f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})]$ | ~1.618 | Rheological model parameter fitting |
