# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**State the general form of the continuity equation and write its simplified form for an incompressible polymer melt.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The general continuity equation expressing conservation of mass is:
> $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$
> For an incompressible polymer melt (the typical assumption during mould filling), density is constant, i.e., $\frac{D\rho}{Dt}=0$. Substituting into the expanded form $\frac{D\rho}{Dt}+\rho\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$ gives the simplified continuity equation:
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**(Mark distribution: 1 mark for general form, 1 mark for the expansion, 1 mark for incompressible simplification.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`

**List any three commonly used Equations of State (EOS) for polymer melts and write one key feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Spencer-Gilmore EOS** — Empirically adapted from van der Waals form; widely used in injection moulding simulation (e.g., Moldflow).
> 2. **Tait EOS** — Universal constant $C \approx 0.0894$; valid across solid and melt states; used in most commercial polymer CFD packages.
> 3. **Modified Cell Model (Sanchez–Lacombe)** — Lattice-based statistical thermodynamic model; more accurate for semi-crystalline polymers near the melting point.

**(1 mark per correct EOS with feature.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** Derive the continuity equation from a control-volume mass balance, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from Newton's second law, derive the Navier-Stokes equation for a Newtonian fluid and then write the **generalised form** suitable for a non-Newtonian polymer melt. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a): Derivation of the Continuity Equation

**Step 1 — State the conservation principle (1 mark):**
For a fixed control volume $V$ bounded by surface $S$, the rate of mass accumulation equals the net inward mass flux (assuming no internal source):

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho\,dV = -\oint_S \rho \vec{v}\cdot\hat{n}\,dS$$

**Step 2 — Apply Leibniz rule (1 mark):**
Since the control volume is stationary (Eulerian), the time derivative moves inside:

$$\int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\,dV = -\oint_S \rho\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n}\,dS$$

**Step 3 — Apply the divergence theorem (1 mark):**

$$\int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\,dV = -\int_V \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v})\,dV$$

**Step 4 — Localize (1 mark):**
Since the volume $V$ is arbitrary, the integrands must be equal pointwise:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v}) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand using product rule and material derivative (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\rho + \rho\nabla\cdot\vec{v} = 0$$

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho\nabla\cdot\vec{v} = 0$$

**Step 6 — State the incompressible form (1 mark):**
For $\rho = \text{const}$, $\frac{D\rho}{Dt}=0$, hence $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$.

**Assumptions to be stated:**
1. Single-phase continuum (no bubbles, fillers treated as separate phase).
2. No chemical reactions during flow.
3. Smooth, differentiable fields.
4. Newtonian/Eulerian reference frame.

---

#### Solution A(b): Navier-Stokes Derivation

**Step 1 — Newton's 2nd law on fluid particle (1 mark):**

$$\rho\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = \sum \vec{F}$$

**Step 2 — Express forces via Cauchy's stress tensor (1 mark):**

$$\sum \vec{F} = \oint_S \boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\hat{n}\,dS + \int_V \rho\vec{g}\,dV = \int_V \left[\nabla\cdot\boldsymbol{\sigma} + \rho\vec{g}\right]dV$$

**Step 3 — Stress decomposition (1 mark):**
$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p\mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\tau}$, giving:

$$\rho\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \nabla\cdot\boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho\vec{g}$$

**Step 4 — Newtonian constitutive law (1 mark):**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu\left[\nabla\vec{v}+(\nabla\vec{v})^T\right] - \frac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla\cdot\vec{v})\mathbf{I}$$

**Step 5 — Incompressible limit (1 mark):**
With $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$:

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t}+\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^2\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g}$$

**Step 6 — Generalise for non-Newtonian polymer melt (2 marks):**
Replace $\mu$ with a shear-rate, temperature, and pressure-dependent viscosity $\eta(\dot{\gamma}, T, p)$:

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t}+\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla p + \nabla\cdot\left[2\eta(\dot{\gamma},T,p)\mathbf{D}\right] + \rho\vec{g}$$

Mention that $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ is given by Cross, Carreau, or Power-law models, and temperature dependence follows an Arrhenius or WLF form.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]` **(Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Derive the energy equation for a flowing polymer melt from the first law of thermodynamics. Clearly explain the physical significance of each term, with particular emphasis on the **viscous dissipation term** and its importance in polymer processing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Tait Equation of State** for polymer melts. Show how its parameters are determined experimentally and explain why an EOS is essential for closing the system of conservation equations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a): Energy Equation Derivation

**Step 1 — State the first law for a fluid element (1 mark):**

$$\rho\frac{DU}{Dt} = \dot{q} - \dot{W}$$

where $U$ = internal energy per unit mass, $\dot{q}$ = heat added per unit volume, $\dot{W}$ = work done per unit volume.

**Step 2 — Express heat flux via Fourier's law (1 mark):**

$$\dot{q} = -\nabla\cdot\vec{q} = \nabla\cdot(k\nabla T)$$

**Step 3 — Express work (pressure + viscous) (1 mark):**

$$\dot{W} = p\nabla\cdot\vec{v} - \boldsymbol{\tau}:\nabla\vec{v}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange (1 mark):**

$$\rho\frac{DU}{Dt} = \nabla\cdot(k\nabla T) - p\nabla\cdot\vec{v} + \boldsymbol{\tau}:\nabla\vec{v}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to enthalpy form (1 mark):**
Using $h = u + p/\rho$ and $\rho\frac{Dh}{Dt} = \rho\frac{Du}{Dt} + \frac{Dp}{Dt}$:

$$\rho\frac{Dh}{Dt} = \nabla\cdot(k\nabla T) + \frac{Dp}{Dt} + \boldsymbol{\tau}:\nabla\vec{v}$$

For incompressible flow with constant $C_p$:

$$\rho C_p\frac{DT}{Dt} = \nabla\cdot(k\nabla T) + \boldsymbol{\tau}:\nabla\vec{v} + \dot{S}$$

**Step 6 — Physical significance of each term (2 marks):**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $\rho C_p DT/Dt$ | Rate of temperature change of the fluid element (storage) |
| $\nabla\cdot(k\nabla T)$ | Heat conduction (Fourier diffusion) |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}:\nabla\vec{v}$ | Viscous dissipation — conversion of mechanical work into heat |
| $\dot{S}$ | Volumetric heat source (e.g., heater bands) |

**Importance of viscous dissipation:** For polymer melts with $\mu \sim 10^3$–$10^5$ Pa·s, even moderate shear rates ($\dot{\gamma} \sim 100$ s⁻¹) can produce dissipation of order $10^5$–$10^7$ W/m³. This raises melt temperature by tens of degrees, reducing viscosity (exponential WLF dependence) and accelerating flow — a strong **positive feedback loop** critical in extrusion and injection moulding.

---

#### Solution B(b): Tait EOS Derivation

**Step 1 — Empirical starting point (1 mark):**
At constant temperature, polymer specific volume decreases logarithmically with pressure:

$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial p}\right)_T = -C \cdot \frac{v}{p + B(T)}$$

where $C = 0.0894$ (universal constant for polymers) and $B(T)$ is the pressure sensitivity.

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate (1 mark):**

$$\int_{v_0}^{v}\frac{dv}{v} = -C\int_0^p \frac{dp'}{p' + B(T)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve (1 mark):**

$$\ln\frac{v}{v_0(T)} = -C\ln\left(\frac{p + B(T)}{B(T)}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Exponential form (1 mark):**

$$\frac{v}{v_0(T)} = \left[\frac{p + B(T)}{B(T)}\right]^{-C} = \left[1 + \frac{p}{B(T)}\right]^{-C}$$

**Step 5 — Final form using $\ln(1+x)\approx x$ approximation (1 mark):**
For low-to-moderate pressures (typical of polymer processing), expand and use $\ln(1+x) \approx x$:

$$\boxed{v(T,p) = v_0(T)\left[1 - C\ln\left(1 + \frac{p}{B(T)}\right)\right]}$$

**Step 6 — Experimental determination of parameters (1 mark):**
- $v_0(T)$, $B(T)$: obtained from **pvT (pressure-volume-temperature)** measurements using instruments like the GNOMIX high-pressure dilatometer or a confining-fluid pvT cell.
- Measurements are taken isothermally at multiple pressures; $v_0$ is extrapolated from $p \to 0$ data; $B(T)$ is fitted.
- Two sets of constants are needed: one above and one below the transition temperature ($T_g$ for amorphous, $T_m$ for semi-crystalline).

**Step 7 — Why EOS is essential (1 mark):**
The continuity, momentum, and energy equations contain $\rho = 1/v$ implicitly (especially in compressible processes like injection moulding, where $\rho$ changes by 5–15%). Without an EOS:
- Continuity cannot be closed (the divergence form requires $\rho(T,p)$).
- Mass and momentum fluxes in/out of control volumes are unknown.
- The pressure-volume-temperature coupling in the energy equation cannot be solved.
Hence, the EOS is the **thermodynamic closure** that allows the conservation equations to be solved simultaneously.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Module 1 — Equations topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the material derivative form** (using only $\partial/\partial t$ instead of $D/Dt$): **−2 marks**. Always state the full material derivative explicitly.
> 2. **Applying Newtonian constant viscosity to a polymer melt**: **−2 marks**. Always mention shear-rate-dependent (Cross/Carreau) viscosity for polymers.
> 3. **Ignoring viscous dissipation in the energy equation** (writing only conduction term): **−2 marks**. State its magnitude ($Br$ number) and why it matters for polymers.
> 4. **Missing assumptions** (e.g., not stating incompressibility, single-phase, no reactions): **−1 mark per missing assumption** up to −3.
> 5. **Not specifying boundary/initial conditions** when applying equations: **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing Spencer-Gilmore with Tait**: **−1 mark**. Spencer-Gilmore is empirical and uses $(p+\pi)(v-\omega) = R'T$; Tait uses the logarithmic form $v = v_0[1 - C\ln(1+p/B)]$.
> 7. **Derivation steps skipped or merged**: KTU examiners expect each algebraic step to be shown. Skipping the divergence theorem application = **−1 mark**. Skipping material derivative conversion = **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The four governing equations are: **Continuity (C), Momentum (M), Energy (E), State (S)**.
- ✅ **Material derivative**: $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla$ — converts particle-tracking (Lagrangian) to in-place (Eulerian) rate.
- ✅ **Continuity**: $\partial\rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v}) = 0$ → simplifies to $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$ for **incompressible** polymer melts.
- ✅ **Momentum (Navier-Stokes)**: $\rho D\vec{v}/Dt = -\nabla p + \nabla\cdot\boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho\vec{g}$.
- ✅ **Polymer melts are NON-NEWTONIAN** → viscosity is a function of $\dot\gamma$, $T$, and $p$. Use Cross/Carreau/Power-law models.
- ✅ **Energy**: $\rho C_p DT/Dt = \nabla\cdot(k\nabla T) + \boldsymbol{\tau}:\nabla\vec{v} + \dot{S}$. The $\boldsymbol{\tau}:\nabla\vec{v}$ term is **viscous dissipation** — **crucial for polymers** due to high viscosity.
- ✅ **Brinkman number** $Br = \mu U^2/(k\Delta T)$ quantifies viscous heating importance ($Br \gg 1$ → dissipation-dominated).
- ✅ **Equation of State** closes the system: Tait (universal, $C = 0.0894$) and Spencer-Gilmore (empirical) are most common.
- ✅ All four equations are **coupled** and require **iterative numerical solution** (FEM/FVM with Picard or Newton iteration).
- ✅ For each derivation, explicitly state assumptions (single-phase, no reactions, continuum, smooth fields).
- ✅ Polymer-specific phenomena to remember:
  - **Shear-thinning**: $\eta$ decreases with $\dot\gamma$ (Cross, Carreau).
  - **Viscous heating**: temperature rise from dissipation.
  - **Pressure dependence**: $\rho$ varies with $p$ (EOS).
  - **Wall slip**: occurs in some highly entangled polymers.
  - **Viscoelasticity**: requires Oldroyd-B or PTT models for accurate simulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transport properties # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Transport Properties in Polymers? **Transport properties** in polymers refer to the ability of small molecules (gases, vapors, liquids, or ions) to move through a polymeric matrix. The three fundamental transport properties studied in polymer engineering are: 1. **Permeability (P)** – the overall rate at which a penetrant passes through a polymer film. 2. **Diffusivity (D)** – the rate at which the penetrant moves within the polymer. 3. **Solubility (S)** – the amount of penetrant that dissolves in the polymer at equilibrium. These three are thermodynamically linked by the equation: $$P = D \times S$$ where **P** is the permeability coefficient, **D** is the diffusion coefficient, and **S** is the solubility coefficient. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Transport properties govern critical real-world applications such as food packaging (keeping oxygen out), protective coatings, separation membranes (water purification), controlled drug release, and tire inner-liners (retaining air). Mastery of this topic is essential for the polymer product design industry. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Sponge-Candle Model Imagine a polymer film as a **dense forest of tangled, long-chain molecules** (like spaghetti in a bowl). Small penetrant molecules (like oxygen or water vapor) are like tiny mice trying to cross this forest. - **Solubility (S)** → How many mice enter the forest and stay there (how much gas dissolves in the polymer). - **Diffusivity (D)** → How fast each mouse can wiggle through the gaps between the spaghetti strands. - **Permeability (P)** → The total number of mice that make it across the forest per second. A **dense, crystalline polymer** (like tightly packed spaghetti) is a poor forest to cross — *low permeability*. A **rubbery, amorphous polymer** (like loose, floppy noodles) is easy to traverse — *high permeability*. The temperature, pressure, and size of the "mouse" (penetrant) all change how the journey unfolds. ## 1.3 Classification of Penetrants | Penetrant Type | Example | Molecular Size | Typical Behavior | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Permanent gases** | O₂, N₂, H₂ | Small (< 0.3 nm) | Low solubility, Fickian diffusion | | **Vapors** | H₂O, CHCl₃ | Small–Medium | Plasticization possible | | **Organic liquids** | Benzene, Toluene | Medium | Swelling, non-Fickian | | **Ions / Water** | H⁺, Na⁺, H₂O (ionic) | Hydrated size | Electrochemical transport | > [!NOTE] > **Henry's Law vs. Langmuir Sorption:** > Permanent gases obey **Henry's Law** (linear solubility with pressure). Organic vapors at high activity follow **Langmuir sorption** (specific site binding). This distinction is critical for choosing the correct boundary condition in Fickian models. ## 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering In the polymer industry, transport properties determine: - **Shelf life** of packaged food and pharmaceuticals. - **Barrier performance** of PET bottles, HDPE fuel tanks, EV battery separators. - **Selectivity** of membranes in gas separation (CO₂/CH₄, O₂/N₂). - **Release kinetics** in transdermal patches and drug-eluting stents. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Units Used in KTU Examinations:** > - D (diffusion coefficient): **cm²/s** or **m²/s** > - S (solubility coefficient): **cm³(STP) / (cm³ polymer · atm)** > - P (permeability coefficient): **cm³(STP) · cm / (cm² · s · atm)** — often expressed in **Barrer** units (1 Barrer = 10⁻¹⁰ cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Solution-Diffusion Mechanism The most widely accepted model for small-molecule transport through dense polymer films is the **Solution-Diffusion Mechanism**, proposed by Graham (1866) and later refined by Wijmans & Baker (1995). It postulates three sequential steps: 1. **Sorption (Dissolution)** at the upstream (high-pressure) face of the membrane. 2. **Diffusion** through the polymer matrix under a chemical potential gradient. 3. **Desorption (Evaporation)** at the downstream (low-pressure) face. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU:** In rubbery polymers above Tg, diffusion is **rapid** and solubility **controls** permeability. In glassy polymers below Tg, diffusion is **slow** and **controls** permeability. This single fact decides whether a polymer is good for **packaging** (glassy barrier) or **separation membranes** (rubbery selective layer). ## 2.2 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Steady State) For one-dimensional steady-state diffusion through a polymer film of thickness *L*: $$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$ where: - **J** = steady-state flux of penetrant (cm³(STP)/cm²·s) - **D** = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) - **∂C/∂x** = concentration gradient (cm³(STP)/cm³ polymer · cm) For a homogeneous film, integrating across the thickness gives: $$J = \frac{D \, (C_1 - C_2)}{L}$$ where **C₁** and **C₂** are the concentrations at the upstream and downstream faces, respectively. ## 2.3 Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Non-Steady State) When the concentration profile within the film is changing with time, Fick's second law applies: $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ For a constant-D, planar film geometry with boundary condition *C = C₀* at *x = 0* and *C = 0* at *x = L*, the solution for cumulative mass *Q(t)* is: $$Q(t) = \frac{D \, C_0}{L} \, t - \frac{C_0 \, L}{6} + \frac{2 \, C_0 \, L}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{n^2} \exp\left(-\frac{D \, n^2 \pi^2 t}{L^2}\right)$$ The **steady-state permeation rate** is reached when the exponential terms vanish (large *t*): $$J_{ss} = \frac{D \, C_0}{L}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** > The **time-lag method** to determine D experimentally uses: > $$D = \frac{L^2}{6 \, \theta}$$ > where **θ (theta)** is the time-lag intercept on the time axis when extrapolating the steady-state linear portion back to the time axis. Always define *θ* clearly in derivations. ## 2.4 The Permeation Process – Step-Response Curve A typical experimental measurement plots cumulative mass *Q(t)* versus time, producing a sigmoidal curve with three regions: 1. **Induction (Lag) Region** – polymer saturating; flux is increasing. 2. **Transient Region** – non-steady diffusion; profile is developing. 3. **Steady-State Region** – linear; Fick's first law applies. ## 2.5 Deviations from Fickian Behavior Polymers often show **non-Fickian (anomalous) diffusion**, classified by **Alfrey's criteria**: | Case | Diffusion Exponent (*n*) | Mechanism | Typical Polymer | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | **Case I (Fickian)** | n = 0.5 | Diffusion-controlled, slow relaxation | Rubbery polymers | | **Case II** | n = 1.0 | Relaxation-controlled, sharp front | Glassy polymers (PMMA, PS) with organics | | **Super Case II** | n > 1.0 | Stress-cracking, rapid front | Highly swollen glassy polymers | | **Anomalous** | 0.5 < n < 1.0 | Mixed mechanism | Most real systems | The generalized equation is: $$\frac{Q_t}{Q_\infty} = k \, t^n$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Case II transport is exploited in **controlled drug release** devices, where a sharp moving boundary releases the drug at constant rate (zero-order kinetics). ## 2.6 Temperature and Pressure Dependence **Diffusivity** follows an **Arrhenius-type** equation: $$D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_D}{RT}\right)$$ where **E_D** is the activation energy for diffusion (typically 20–80 kJ/mol for polymers). **Solubility** follows a **van't Hoff-type** equation: $$S = S_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H_s}{RT}\right)$$ where **ΔH_s** is the enthalpy of sorption (typically −10 to −40 kJ/mol for condensable gases; negative ⇒ exothermic ⇒ solubility decreases with T). **Permeability** combines both: $$P = P_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_P}{RT}\right)$$ with $E_P = E_D + \Delta H_s$. ## 2.7 Free-Volume Theory of Diffusion In a polymer, diffusion occurs by the penetrant "hopping" into voids (free volume) created by thermal motion of chains. The Cohen–Turnbull model gives: $$D = A \exp\left(-\frac{B}{v_f}\right)$$ where **v_f** is the fractional free volume and **A, B** are constants. As temperature approaches Tg, *v_f* decreases sharply, hence D drops. > [!NOTE] > **Dual-Mobility Model (Vrentas–Dudas):** A more refined free-volume model separates the jumping unit size and the hole size, predicting: > $$\ln\left(\frac{D}{T}\right) = \ln A' - \frac{E}{RT} - \frac{\gamma \hat{V}^* \xi}{\hat{V}_{FH}}$$ > This is tested in KTU numerical problems involving polymer-solvent pairs. ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Units | |:-:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $P = D \cdot S$ | Permeability definition | Barrer | | 2 | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law | g/cm²·s | | 3 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law | — | | 4 | $J_{ss} = \frac{D \, C_0}{L}$ | Steady-state flux | g/cm²·s | | 5 | $D = \frac{L^2}{6 \, \theta}$ | Time-lag diffusivity | cm²/s | | 6 | $Q(t) = \frac{D \, C_0}{L} t - \frac{C_0 L}{6} + \cdots$ | Full permeation transient | g | | 7 | $\frac{Q_t}{Q_\infty} = k \, t^n$ | Sorption kinetics (Alfrey) | — | | 8 | $D = D_0 \exp(-E_D/RT)$ | Arrhenius diffusivity | cm²/s | | 9 | $S = S_0 \exp(-\Delta H_s / RT)$ | van't Hoff solubility | cm³/cm³·atm | | 10 | $P = P_0 \exp(-E_P/RT)$ | Arrhenius permeability | Barrer | | 11 | $D = A \exp(-B/v_f)$ | Free-volume diffusion | cm²/s | | 12 | $\alpha_{12} = \frac{P_1}{P_2} \cdot \frac{y_{1,F}}{y_{2,F}}$ | Selectivity (membranes) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The **selectivity** $\alpha_{12}$ determines whether a polymer can economically separate gas mixtures (e.g., O₂ from N₂ for medical applications). The Robeson upper bound plots selectivity vs. permeability — no commercial polymer exceeds this trade-off line. ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Application | Controlling Property | Example Polymer | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Food packaging (oxygen barrier) | Low P for O₂ | EVOH, PVDC | | Fuel tanks (hydrocarbon barrier) | Low P for gasoline | HDPE, fluoropolymers | | Gas separation membranes | High α + High P | Polyimide, polysulfone | | Tire inner-liners | Low P for air | Halobutyl rubber | | Controlled drug release | Case II transport | EVA, PLA | | PEM fuel cell membranes | Proton conductivity | Nafion | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Permeation Through a Polymer Film **Given:** - Polymer film of thickness **L** (cm). - Upstream partial pressure **p₁** (atm); downstream partial pressure **p₂** (atm), with **p₁ > p₂**. - Constant diffusion coefficient **D** (cm²/s). - Solubility obeys Henry's Law: **C = S · p**. **Step 1 — Apply Fick's First Law in one dimension:** $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — At steady state, J is constant (no accumulation).** Therefore the concentration profile is **linear in x**. The boundary concentrations are: $$C_1 = S \cdot p_1 \quad \text{at} \quad x = 0$$ $$C_2 = S \cdot p_2 \quad \text{at} \quad x = L$$ **Step 3 — Integrate across the film thickness** (0 → L): $$\int_0^L J \, dx = -D \int_{C_1}^{C_2} dC$$ $$J \cdot L = -D \, (C_2 - C_1) = D \, (C_1 - C_2)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Henry's Law expressions:** $$J \cdot L = D \, S \, (p_1 - p_2)$$ **Step 5 — Define permeability coefficient:** $$P = D \cdot S$$ **Step 6 — Final expression:** $$\boxed{J = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{L}}$$ This is the **fundamental permeation equation** used in all packaging and membrane design calculations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Time-Lag Expression for Diffusion Coefficient **Starting from Fick's Second Law for a planar film:** $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ **Boundary and Initial Conditions:** $$C(x, 0) = 0 \quad (0 \le x \le L)$$ $$C(0, t) = C_0, \quad C(L, t) = 0 \quad (t > 0)$$ **Step 1 — Cumulative mass downstream Q(t) is given by Fick's First Law at x = L:** $$Q(t) = \frac{A}{1} \int_0^t J(L, t') \, dt' = -A \int_0^t D \left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=L} dt'$$ **Step 2 — Laplace transform of Fick's Second Law** with respect to t: $$s \bar{C} - C(x,0) = D \frac{d^2 \bar{C}}{dx^2}$$ Since $C(x, 0) = 0$: $$\frac{d^2 \bar{C}}{dx^2} = \frac{s}{D} \bar{C}$$ **Step 3 — General solution:** $$\bar{C}(x, s) = A' \sinh\left(x\sqrt{s/D}\right) + B' \cosh\left(x\sqrt{s/D}\right)$$ Applying BCs: - At $x = 0$: $\bar{C}(0, s) = C_0/s \Rightarrow B' = C_0/s$ - At $x = L$: $\bar{C}(L, s) = 0 \Rightarrow A' = -\dfrac{C_0}{s} \dfrac{\cosh(L\sqrt{s/D})}{\sinh(L\sqrt{s/D})}$ **Step 4 — Compute ∂C/∂x at x = L** and apply inverse Laplace transform. The asymptotic (large t) expansion yields: $$Q(t) = \frac{D \, C_0}{L} \left( t - \frac{L^2}{6D} \right) \quad \text{for large } t$$ **Step 5 — Time-lag θ** is the t-intercept of the linear asymptote when extrapolated to Q = 0: $$0 = \frac{D \, C_0}{L} \left( \theta - \frac{L^2}{6D} \right)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for θ:** $$\theta = \frac{L^2}{6D}$$ **Step 7 — Invert for D:** $$\boxed{D = \frac{L^2}{6 \theta}}$$ This is the experimentally measured diffusivity from a single permeation experiment. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solubility Regimes & Governing Equations | Regime | Boundary Condition | Governing Equation | Practical System | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Henry's Law (low p)** | $C = S \, p$ | $J = \dfrac{P(p_1 - p_2)}{L}$ | Permanent gases (O₂, N₂, CH₄) | | **Langmuir (high p)** | $C = C_H' \dfrac{b \, p}{1 + b p} + k_D \, p$ | Dual-mode model | Glassy polymers below Tg (glassy PET, PS) | | **Flory–Huggins** | $\ln a = \ln \phi + (1 - \phi) + \chi(1-\phi)^2$ | Activity-based flux | Polymer–organic solvent (swelling) | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]:** A polymer film of thickness **200 μm** is exposed to oxygen at **p₁ = 1 atm** on one side and vacuum on the other. The permeability of O₂ is **P = 1.5 × 10⁻¹⁰ cm³·cm/(cm²·s·cmHg)** and diffusivity is **D = 5 × 10⁻⁸ cm²/s**. Calculate: > (a) The solubility coefficient S. > (b) The steady-state flux J (in cm³(STP)/cm²·s). > (c) The time-lag θ. > > *Given: 1 atm = 76 cmHg.* **Solution:** **(a) Solubility coefficient:** Using $P = D \cdot S$: $$S = \frac{P}{D} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-10}}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{cm}^3\text{(STP)}/(\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cmHg})$$ **(b) Steady-state flux:** First convert pressure: $p_1 = 1 \text{ atm} = 76 \text{ cmHg}$, $p_2 = 0$. $$J = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{L} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-10} \times 76}{200 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$J = \frac{1.14 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-2}} = 5.7 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{cm}^3\text{(STP)}/(\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s})$$ **(c) Time-lag:** $$\theta = \frac{L^2}{6D} = \frac{(200 \times 10^{-4})^2}{6 \times 5 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{3 \times 10^{-7}} = 1333 \; \text{s} \approx 22.2 \; \text{min}$$ **Answer:** - (a) S = **3.0 × 10⁻³** cm³(STP)/(cm³·cmHg) - (b) J = **5.7 × 10⁻⁷** cm³(STP)/(cm²·s) - (c) θ = **1333 s** ≈ 22.2 min > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert all units to a consistent set (cm, s, atm or cmHg) before substituting into equations. Show every conversion step — students lose marks for skipping this. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow: Solution-Diffusion Through a Polymer Membrane This diagram shows the three sequential steps of the **solution-diffusion mechanism** and how the penetrant concentration profile evolves across the membrane thickness. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[High Pressure Side
Concentration C1 High] -->|Step 1 Sorption| B[Penetrant Dissolves
in Upstream Polymer Face] B -->|Step 2 Diffusion| C[Random Hopping Through
Free Volume Voids] C -->|Step 2 Diffusion| D[Concentration Drops
Linearly Across Film] D -->|Step 3 Desorption| E[Penetrant Evaporates
at Downstream Face] E --> F[Low Pressure Side
Concentration C2 Low] G[Polymer Matrix Chains] -.Provides Free Volume.-> C H[Concentration Profile] -.Linear at Steady State.-> D ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Transport Property Measurement System The diagram below maps the **architectural flow** of a typical permeation experiment — the kind of setup KTU students are expected to understand for their lab module and viva voce. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UPSTREAM GasCyl[Gas Cylinder] --> Regulator[Pressure Regulator] Regulator --> UpChamber[Upstream Chamber
Pressure p1] end UpChamber --> Film[Polymer Film
Thickness L] Film --> DownChamber[Downstream Chamber
Pressure p2 or Carrier Gas] DownChamber --> Detector[Detector
Gas Chromatograph or Mass Spec] Detector --> DataLog[Data Acquisition System] DataLog --> Plot[Q vs Time Plot] Plot --> SSRegion[Steady State Linear Region] Plot --> LagRegion[Time Lag Intercept theta] SSRegion --> CalcD[Compute D from theta] SSRegion --> CalcP[Compute P from slope] CalcD --> ReportP[Permeability P] CalcD --> ReportS[Solubility S from P over D] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting a Transport Model The model choice depends on **polymer state (Tg relative to use temperature)** and **penetrant activity**. This tree is used by polymer engineers when designing packaging or membranes. ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[Choose Transport Model] --> Q1{Polymer above Tg?} Q1 -- Yes Rubber --> M1[Henry Law Sorption] Q1 -- No Glassy --> M2[Dual Mode Sorption Model] M1 --> Q2{Penetrant activity high?} Q2 -- Low --> F1[Fickian Diffusion
Case I] Q2 -- High Organic --> F2[Case II Transport
Relaxation Controlled] M2 --> Q3{Penetrant condensable?} Q3 -- No --> F1 Q3 -- Yes --> F2 F1 --> End1[Use D from Time Lag Method] F2 --> End2[Use Moving Front Model] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Matrix: Diffusion Cases (Visual Summary) | Case | Concentration Profile | Q vs Time Plot | Slope *n* | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Case I (Fickian)** | Smooth sigmoid in film | $Q \propto t^{0.5}$ initially, then linear | 0.5 | | **Case II** | Sharp moving front | $Q \propto t$ (linear from t = 0) | 1.0 | | **Super Case II** | Discontinuous front with overshoot | $Q \propto t^{n>1}$ initially | > 1.0 | | **Anomalous** | Intermediate profile | $0.5 < n < 1.0$ | 0.5–1.0 | > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `UpChamber`, `DualMode`) — no reserved keywords such as `end`, `graph`, or `subgraph` are used as standalone node labels. Labels are clean text without markdown bold/italic markup, ensuring rendering reliability. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern] **Define permeability, diffusivity, and solubility coefficients in polymer transport. State the relation between them with units.** **Model Answer:** The three fundamental transport coefficients governing small-molecule movement through a polymer are: - **Permeability (P):** The steady-state rate at which a penetrant passes through a polymer film of unit thickness under unit pressure difference. **Units: cm³(STP)·cm/(cm²·s·atm)** or **Barrer** (1 Barrer = 10⁻¹⁰ cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg). - **Diffusivity (D):** A kinetic parameter measuring the rate at which the penetrant moves within the polymer matrix under a concentration gradient. **Units: cm²/s.** - **Solubility (S):** A thermodynamic parameter measuring the equilibrium concentration of penetrant dissolved in the polymer per unit applied pressure. **Units: cm³(STP)/(cm³·atm).** The governing relation is: $$P = D \times S$$ *(3 marks: 1 mark each definition + 1 mark for equation and units.)* --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern] **Distinguish between Case I (Fickian) and Case II diffusion with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Case I (Fickian) | Case II | |:---|:---|:---| | Diffusion exponent *n* | 0.5 | 1.0 | | Rate-controlling step | Diffusion | Polymer relaxation | | Concentration profile | Smooth, sigmoidal | Sharp moving front | | Fick's law obeyed? | Yes | No | | Example | O₂ through silicone rubber | Methanol through PMMA (glassy) | | Typical polymer state | Rubbery, Tg < T_use | Glassy, Tg > T_use | *(3 marks: 1 mark for exponent/kinetics + 1 mark for example + 1 mark for polymer state.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module Choice] **(a) Derive the steady-state permeation equation for a gas through a polymer film starting from Fick's First Law. State clearly the assumptions made.** **(7 Marks)** **(b) The permeability of N₂ through a polyimide film of 50 μm thickness is 0.3 Barrer. The diffusion coefficient is 2 × 10⁻⁹ cm²/s. Compute (i) solubility coefficient, (ii) steady-state flux when p₁ = 2 atm, p₂ = 0, (iii) the time-lag in seconds. Given: 1 Barrer = 10⁻¹⁰ cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg; 1 atm = 76 cmHg.** **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution** **Part (a) — Derivation** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion. 2. Constant diffusion coefficient D (independent of C). 3. Penetrant obeys Henry's Law: C = S·p. 4. Isothermal conditions. 5. No chemical reaction or plasticization. **Starting equation (Fick's First Law):** $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$ At steady state, J is constant (no accumulation), so **dC/dx is constant**, meaning concentration profile is **linear** in x. **Boundary conditions:** $$C(x=0) = C_1 = S \cdot p_1$$ $$C(x=L) = C_2 = S \cdot p_2$$ **Integrate:** $$J \int_0^L dx = -D \int_{C_1}^{C_2} dC$$ $$J \cdot L = D \, (C_1 - C_2)$$ **Substitute Henry's Law:** $$J \cdot L = D \cdot S \cdot (p_1 - p_2)$$ **Define P = D·S:** $$\boxed{J = \frac{P (p_1 - p_2)}{L}}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical** **Given:** P = 0.3 Barrer = 0.3 × 10⁻¹⁰ cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg; D = 2 × 10⁻⁹ cm²/s; L = 50 μm = 50 × 10⁻⁴ cm; p₁ = 2 atm = 152 cmHg; p₂ = 0. **(i) Solubility:** $$S = \frac{P}{D} = \frac{0.3 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.015 \; \text{cm}^3\text{(STP)}/(\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cmHg})$$ **(ii) Steady-state flux:** $$J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} = \frac{0.3 \times 10^{-10} \times 152}{50 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$J = \frac{4.56 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.12 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{cm}^3\text{(STP)}/(\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s})$$ **(iii) Time-lag:** $$\theta = \frac{L^2}{6D} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-4})^2}{6 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 10^{-8}} = 2083 \; \text{s} \approx 34.7 \; \text{min}$$ **Answers:** S = **0.015** cm³/cm³·cmHg; J = **9.12 × 10⁻⁷** cm³(STP)/cm²·s; θ = **2083 s**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Alternative Module Choice] **(a) Explain the Solution-Diffusion mechanism for gas transport through a dense polymer membrane. Compare permeability behavior in rubbery vs. glassy polymers.** **(7 Marks)** **(b) Discuss the Free-Volume theory of diffusion. Explain how temperature and free volume influence the diffusion coefficient in polymers above and below Tg.** **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution** **Part (a) — Solution-Diffusion Mechanism** The **Solution-Diffusion mechanism** (Graham, 1866; Wijmans & Baker, 1995) describes permeation through a dense (non-porous) polymer in three sequential steps: 1. **Sorption:** The penetrant gas dissolves into the upstream face of the polymer membrane. The concentration at this face is governed by Henry's Law (rubbery) or the dual-mode sorption model (glassy). 2. **Diffusion:** The dissolved molecules hop through transient gaps (free volume) between polymer chain segments under the chemical potential gradient. No bulk convective flow occurs. 3. **Desorption:** The penetrant evaporates from the downstream face into the low-pressure permeate stream. **Comparison: Rubbery vs. Glassy Polymers** | Property | Rubbery (T > Tg) | Glassy (T < Tg) | |:---|:---|:---| | Chain mobility | High | Low | | Free volume | Large | Restricted | | Solubility (S) | High (Henry's Law linear) | High (Langmuir + Henry) | | Diffusivity (D) | High, weakly size-selective | Low, strongly size-selective | | Permeability controlling step | Solubility | Diffusivity | | Typical polymer | Silicone, natural rubber | PET, PS, polyimide | In **rubbery polymers**, sorption is fast and diffusion is the slower step; permeability correlates with penetrant condensability (higher Tc → higher P). In **glassy polymers**, diffusion is much slower; permeability is dominated by the penetrant's kinetic diameter. --- **Part (b) — Free-Volume Theory** The **Cohen–Turnbull free-volume theory** treats diffusion as a thermally activated hopping process: a penetrant can only jump to a neighboring site if a void of sufficient size opens up due to thermal fluctuation. The governing equation is: $$D = A \exp\left(-\frac{B}{v_f}\right)$$ where **v_f** is the **fractional free volume** of the polymer, and **A**, **B** are constants related to penetrant size and polymer jumping unit size. **Above Tg (Rubbery State):** - Free volume scales linearly with temperature: $v_f = v_{f,g} + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$ - As T increases, v_f increases ⇒ D increases (exponentially). - Arrhenius-type behavior with moderate E_D. **Below Tg (Glassy State):** - Free volume is "frozen in" and non-equilibrium. - Only small additional thermal expansion contributes. - Diffusivity is very low and drops by orders of magnitude as T falls below Tg. - D obeys the **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF)** equation near Tg: $$\log\left(\frac{D}{D_g}\right) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$ with typical constants **C₁ ≈ 17.4**, **C₂ ≈ 51.6 K**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** A rubber above Tg can be 10⁴–10⁶ times more permeable than the same polymer below Tg. This is why packaging film producers **quench** their polymers rapidly to lock in low free volume, and why **Tire inner-liners** use halobutyl rubber (Tg ≈ –70 °C) — high flexibility, but combined with high molecular weight and crystallinity to control permeability. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common deduction (–1 to –2 marks). Always convert Barrer to cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg and atm to cmHg before substitution. > 2. **Confusing S and P:** Solubility S has units *per pressure*, NOT dimensionless. Writing $S = C/p$ without units loses marks. > 3. **Forgetting the time-lag sign:** The intercept θ is on the **time axis**, not the Q-axis. Use $D = L^2/(6\theta)$ — many students incorrectly write $D = 6L^2/\theta$. > 4. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** Fick's First Law derivation requires stating "D constant" and "Henry's Law obeyed" — without these, the answer is incomplete. > 5. **Mixing up Case I and Case II exponents:** *n* = 0.5 is Fickian (rubbery), *n* = 1.0 is Case II (glassy with relaxation control). Reversing these costs a mark. > 6. **Using wrong flux equation in glassy polymers:** The simple $J = P \Delta p/L$ assumes Henry's Law. For glassy polymers, the **dual-mode model** must be invoked; otherwise the answer is marked partial. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Core Equation:** $P = D \times S$ — defines all small-molecule transport in polymers. - [ ] **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$ — steady state. - [ ] **Fick's Second Law:** $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ — transient. - [ ] **Steady-state flux:** $J_{ss} = P \Delta p / L$ — most-used design equation. - [ ] **Time-lag formula:** $D = L^2 / (6\theta)$ — primary experimental method. - [ ] **Arrhenius:** $D = D_0 \exp(-E_D/RT)$; $P = P_0 \exp(-E_P/RT)$. - [ ] **Alfrey's cases:** Case I (*n* = 0.5, Fickian) vs. Case II (*n* = 1.0, relaxation-controlled). - [ ] **Henry vs. Langmuir sorption:** Henry = permanent gases; Langmuir = glassy polymers + condensable gases (Dual-mode model). - [ ] **Free-volume theory:** $D = A \exp(-B/v_f)$; WLF equation valid in $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ K. - [ ] **Rubbery vs. Glassy:** Rubbery → solubility-controlled; Glassy → diffusivity-controlled permeability. - [ ] **1 Barrer** = 10⁻¹⁰ cm³(STP)·cm/(cm²·s·cmHg); **1 atm** = 76 cmHg = 1.01325 bar. - [ ] **Selectivity** $\alpha_{12} = (P_1/P_2)(y_{1,F}/y_{2,F})$ — governs membrane separations. - [ ] **Real-world anchors:** EVOH for O₂ barrier; HDPE for fuel tanks; Nafion for PEM fuel cells; halobutyl for tires. - [ ] **KTU must-know:** For permeation calculations, **always** convert all units *before* plugging into equations and state boundary conditions explicitly.

topics.title: Equilibrium and chemical kinetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Equilibrium and chemical kinetics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Chemical Equilibrium — The Formal Definition In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **chemical equilibrium** is the macroscopic state of a closed reacting system in which the forward and reverse reaction rates become equal, producing a time-invariant composition despite ongoing molecular-level activity. Mathematically, for a generic reversible reaction: $$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$ The thermodynamic equilibrium is rigorously defined by the equilibrium constant $K_{eq}$, which is the ratio of product activities to reactant activities raised to their stoichiometric coefficients. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A system at equilibrium is **NOT** static at the molecular level. Molecules continue to react in both directions, but the net rate of change in concentration is exactly **zero**. This dynamic microscopic reversibility is the cornerstone of equilibrium thermodynamics. ## 1.2 Chemical Kinetics — The Formal Definition **Chemical kinetics** is the branch of physical chemistry that studies the **rates** of chemical reactions and the molecular mechanisms by which reactants are converted into products. While equilibrium tells us *how far* a reaction will go, kinetics tells us *how fast* it will get there. For polymer materials modelling, kinetics is often MORE critical than equilibrium because most polymerization reactions are: - Driven by kinetic control (irreversible or quenched before equilibrium) - Governed by competing elementary steps (initiation, propagation, termination) - Highly sensitive to temperature, catalyst concentration, and viscosity > [!NOTE] > **Polymerization-Specific Insight:** High-molecular-weight polymers are generally kinetic products — they form because the reaction is kinetically trapped before thermodynamic equilibrium (depolymerization) can occur. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive Imagine a **two-bowl system** connected by a pipe at the bottom: - **Left bowl (Reactants A + B):** Water flows out continuously. - **Right bowl (Products C + D):** Water flows back in. - **Equilibrium:** When the water level in both bowls reaches a height where the flow-out rate equals flow-in rate, the *visible levels stop changing*. But water molecules are still moving through the pipe. This is **dynamic equilibrium**. The macroscopic property (water level) is constant, but the microscopic property (molecular motion) never stops. For **chemical kinetics**, think of a **race track**: - **Rate** = how fast cars cross the finish line per second. - **Activation energy** = the hill the cars must climb before they can race. - **Catalyst** = a tunnel through the hill that lets more cars pass per unit time. - **Temperature** = the engine horsepower — more heat → more cars climbing the hill → faster race. ## 1.4 Why This Topic is Central to Polymer Modelling | Aspect | Role in Polymer Science | |---|---| | **Equilibrium** | Determines ceiling temperature, equilibrium monomer concentration, reversibility of polycondensation | | **Kinetics** | Controls molecular weight distribution, reaction time, reactor design, conversion vs. time curves | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** The relationship $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ links equilibrium thermodynamics to the practical question: *"Will this polymer form at this temperature, or will it depolymerize?"* ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants appear repeatedly in KTU problems and **must be memorized**: - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ - **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$ - **Standard temperature:** $T = 298.15 \ \text{K}$ (used unless otherwise specified) - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Equilibrium — The Deep Theory ### 2.1.1 The Equilibrium Constant For the generic reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the equilibrium constant is: $$K_{eq} = \frac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$$ Where square brackets denote equilibrium concentrations (or activities for non-ideal systems). **Three regimes:** - $K_{eq} \gg 1$ : Products strongly favored (reaction proceeds nearly to completion). - $K_{eq} \ll 1$ : Reactants strongly favored. - $K_{eq} \approx 1$ : Significant amounts of both. ### 2.1.2 Van't Hoff Equation (Temperature Dependence) The temperature dependence of $K_{eq}$ is given by: $$\frac{d \ln K_{eq}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$ This is the **Van't Hoff equation**. Integrating (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is temperature-independent): $$\ln \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$ ### 2.1.3 Le Chatelier's Principle — The Intuitive Logic When a stress (concentration, temperature, or pressure change) is applied to a system at equilibrium, the system shifts to **counteract** that stress. - Add reactant → forward shift. - Add product → reverse shift. - Increase temperature → shifts toward the endothermic direction. - Increase pressure (gases) → shifts toward fewer moles of gas. ## 2.2 Chemical Kinetics — The Deep Theory ### 2.2.1 Rate Law and Reaction Order For a reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow \text{Products}$, the empirical rate law is: $$r = -\frac{1}{a}\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^m[B]^n$$ Where: - $k$ = rate constant (units depend on overall order) - $m + n$ = overall reaction order - The exponents $m, n$ are **experimentally determined**, NOT necessarily equal to stoichiometric coefficients. ### 2.2.2 Molecularity vs. Order - **Molecularity:** Number of molecules colliding in a single elementary step. Always a small integer (1, 2, or 3). - **Order:** Empirical exponent from the rate law. Can be any non-negative number (including zero or fractional). ### 2.2.3 Integrated Rate Laws (High-Yield for KTU) For a reaction of order $n$, the integrated rate law in terms of concentration $C$: | Order | Integrated Form | Linear Plot | Half-life $t_{1/2}$ | |---|---|---|---| | Zero | $[A] = [A]_0 - kt$ | $[A]$ vs $t$ | $\dfrac{[A]_0}{2k}$ | | First | $\ln [A] = \ln [A]_0 - kt$ | $\ln [A]$ vs $t$ | $\dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | | Second | $\dfrac{1}{[A]} = \dfrac{1}{[A]_0} + kt$ | $1/[A]$ vs $t$ | $\dfrac{1}{k[A]_0}$ | > [!NOTE] > **First-order kinetics is the most common in polymer degradation, drug release modelling, and chain scission.** ### 2.2.4 The Arrhenius Equation The temperature dependence of the rate constant: $$k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$ Where: - $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency of collisions with correct orientation) - $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol) - $R$ = universal gas constant - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) Linearized form (for plotting $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$): $$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)$$ Slope gives $-E_a/R$; intercept gives $\ln A$. ### 2.2.5 Transition State Theory (TST) — Brief Overview $$k = \frac{k_B T}{h} \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right) = \frac{k_B T}{h} \exp\left(\frac{\Delta S^{\ddagger}}{R}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)$$ Where $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, $h$ = Planck's constant, $\ddagger$ denotes the activated complex. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$ | Equilibrium constant | Dimensionless (activities) | | 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Gibbs free energy & equilibrium | J/mol | | 3 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ | Gibbs-Helmholtz relation | J/mol | | 4 | $\ln \dfrac{K_2}{K_1} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Van't Hoff equation | Ratio of $K$'s | | 5 | $r = k[A]^m[B]^n$ | Rate law | mol/(L·s) | | 6 | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius equation | $k$ depends on order | | 7 | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius | Slope $=-E_a/R$ | | 8 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ (1st order) | Half-life (1st order) | seconds | | 9 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{[A]_0}{2k}$ (zero order) | Half-life (zero order) | seconds | | 10 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k[A]_0}$ (2nd order) | Half-life (2nd order) | seconds | | 11 | $E_a = \dfrac{R \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1)-(1/T_2)}$ | Activation energy from two $k$'s | J/mol | | 12 | $k = \dfrac{k_B T}{h}\exp(\Delta S^\ddagger/R)\exp(-\Delta H^\ddagger/RT)$ | Transition state theory | Variable | | 13 | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{k_f}{k_r}$ | Equilibrium = ratio of forward/reverse rate constants | Dimensionless | | 14 | $T_c = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ + R \ln [M]_0}$ | Ceiling temperature (polymerization) | Kelvin | | 15 | $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (Carothers, equimolar) | Number-average degree of polymerization | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Reality:** Problems 11 and 15 are statistical board favourites. Always carry units through your derivation; examiners award partial marks for unit consistency. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Reactor Design:** Arrhenius parameters allow scale-up from lab to industrial polymer reactors. - **Shelf-Life Prediction:** First-order degradation kinetics predicts how long a polymer product will last. - **Process Control:** Van't Hoff equation tells chemists whether to heat or cool to drive polymerization. - **Catalyst Selection:** TST guides the design of Ziegler-Natta and metallocene catalysts for polyolefins. - **Recycling:** Equilibrium constants determine whether a condensation polymer (PET, nylon) can be depolymerized back to monomer — critical for the circular economy. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Equilibrium Constant and Rate Constants **Statement to Prove:** For an elementary reversible reaction $A + B \rightleftharpoons C + D$, $K_{eq} = \dfrac{k_f}{k_r}$. **Step 1 — Write the forward rate law (elementary step, so order = molecularity):** $$r_f = k_f [A][B]$$ **Step 2 — Write the reverse rate law:** $$r_r = k_r [C][D]$$ **Step 3 — Apply the equilibrium condition. At equilibrium, forward rate equals reverse rate (dynamic balance):** $$r_f = r_r$$ $$k_f [A]_{eq}[B]_{eq} = k_r [C]_{eq}[D]_{eq}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the ratio of rate constants:** $$\frac{k_f}{k_r} = \frac{[C]_{eq}[D]_{eq}}{[A]_{eq}[B]_{eq}}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the right-hand side as the definition of $K_{eq}$:** $$K_{eq} = \frac{[C]_{eq}[D]_{eq}}{[A]_{eq}[B]_{eq}}$$ **Step 6 — Conclude:** $$\boxed{K_{eq} = \frac{k_f}{k_r}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** This derivation shows that equilibrium is NOT a "stopped" state — it is a state where two opposing rates exactly balance. Any perturbation that changes $k_f$ relative to $k_r$ shifts the equilibrium position. ## 3.2 Derivation: Integrated First-Order Rate Law **Statement:** For a first-order reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, show that $\ln[A] = \ln[A]_0 - kt$. **Step 1 — Write the differential rate law:** $$-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables. Move all $[A]$ terms to the left and all $t$ terms to the right:** $$\frac{d[A]}{[A]} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate both sides from initial concentration $[A]_0$ at $t = 0$ to $[A]$ at time $t$:** $$\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \frac{d[A]'}{[A]'} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:** $$\ln[A] - \ln[A]_0 = -kt$$ **Step 5 — Use the logarithm identity $\ln x - \ln y = \ln(x/y)$:** $$\ln\left(\frac{[A]}{[A]_0}\right) = -kt$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $[A]$ explicitly (exponentiate both sides):** $$[A] = [A]_0 \exp(-kt)$$ **Step 7 — Take the natural log of both sides to get the linear form:** $$\boxed{\ln [A] = \ln [A]_0 - kt}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** A straight line of $\ln[A]$ versus $t$ with slope $-k$ confirms first-order kinetics. The linearity itself is the proof of the order — never assume order from stoichiometry. ## 3.3 Derivation: Integrated Second-Order Rate Law (Equal Initial Concentrations) **Statement:** For a second-order reaction $2A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, with $k$ in units of $\text{L}/(\text{mol}\cdot\text{s})$, derive $\dfrac{1}{[A]} = \dfrac{1}{[A]_0} + kt$. **Step 1 — Write the differential rate law:** $$-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]^2$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables:** $$\frac{d[A]}{[A]^2} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate both sides:** $$\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \frac{d[A]'}{[A]'^{2}} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the left integral using the standard result $\int x^{-2} dx = -x^{-1}$:** $$\left[-\frac{1}{[A]'}\right]_{[A]_0}^{[A]} = -kt$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the bracket:** $$-\frac{1}{[A]} + \frac{1}{[A]_0} = -kt$$ **Step 6 — Multiply both sides by $-1$ and rearrange:** $$\boxed{\frac{1}{[A]} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + kt}$$ **Step 7 — Half-life derivation (set $[A] = [A]_0/2$):** $$\frac{2}{[A]_0} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + k t_{1/2}$$ $$\frac{1}{[A]_0} = k t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k[A]_0}}$$ > [!NOTE] > Unlike first-order, second-order half-life **depends on initial concentration**. This is a popular KTU viva question. ## 3.4 Derivation: Arrhenius Equation from Two Temperatures **Statement:** Given rate constants $k_1$ at $T_1$ and $k_2$ at $T_2$, derive the activation energy $E_a$. **Step 1 — Write Arrhenius equation at $T_1$:** $$k_1 = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT_1}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Write Arrhenius equation at $T_2$:** $$k_2 = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT_2}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Take the ratio $k_2/k_1$ (this eliminates $A$):** $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$ **Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:** $$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Solve explicitly for $E_a$:** $$\boxed{E_a = \frac{R \ln(k_2/k_1)}{\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Check:** For an endothermic reaction, increasing $T$ increases $k$, so $k_2/k_1 > 1$ and the numerator is positive. The denominator $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ is also positive when $T_2 > T_1$. Result: $E_a > 0$. Always perform this consistency check in KTU exams. ## 3.5 Derivation: Van't Hoff Equation **Statement:** Derive $\dfrac{d \ln K_{eq}}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$. **Step 1 — Start with Gibbs free energy:** $$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Gibbs-Helmholtz equation:** $$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$ **Step 3 — Express $\Delta S^\circ$ in terms of $K_{eq}$. Since $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$:** $$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{\Delta H^\circ - \Delta G^\circ}{T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ + RT \ln K_{eq}}{T}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:** $$\frac{d(\Delta G^\circ)}{dT} = -R \ln K_{eq} - RT \frac{d \ln K_{eq}}{dT}$$ **Step 5 — Use the Gibbs-Helmholtz identity $\dfrac{d(\Delta G^\circ/T)}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$:** $$\frac{d(\Delta G^\circ)}{dT} - \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{T} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T}$$ **Step 6 — Combine and simplify to obtain:** $$\boxed{\frac{d \ln K_{eq}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}}$$ ## 3.6 Derivation: Degree of Polymerization (Carothers Equation, Equimolar Step Polymerization) **Statement:** For step-growth polymerization with stoichiometric balance, derive $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$, where $p$ is the extent of reaction. **Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction $p$:** $$p = \frac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Number of functional groups initially present}}$$ **Step 2 — At extent $p$, the fraction of unreacted functional groups is $(1 - p)$.** **Step 3 — For a linear chain, the number-average degree of polymerization is the ratio of initial monomer molecules to the number of molecules remaining:** $$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{N_0}{N}$$ Where $N$ is the number of molecules at time $t$ (including all chains and unreacted monomer). **Step 4 — Since each reaction event consumes two functional groups and converts two molecules into one, the number of unreacted functional groups per initial molecule equals $(1-p)$.** **Step 5 — By the statistical argument (Flory), the probability that a randomly chosen functional group is unreacted is $(1-p)$. The probability that a randomly chosen monomer still has both its functional groups unreacted is $(1-p)^2$:** $$\frac{N}{N_0} = (1-p)^2 \quad \text{(for AA + BB type)}$$ For $\text{AB}$ type (self-condensing), one functional group per molecule: $$\frac{N}{N_0} = (1-p)$$ **Step 6 — Therefore:** $$\boxed{\overline{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Reality Check:** As $p \to 1$, $\overline{DP}_n \to \infty$. To reach $\overline{DP}_n = 100$, you need $p = 0.99$ (99% conversion). This is the famous "Carothers paradox" — high polymer requires EXTREMELY high conversion. Loss of even 0.5% monomer drastically reduces molecular weight. ## 3.7 Derivation: Ceiling Temperature **Statement:** Derive $T_c = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ + R \ln [M]_0}$ for polymerization equilibrium. **Step 1 — Polymerization equilibrium:** $$M \rightleftharpoons \text{Polymer}$$ **Step 2 — At ceiling temperature, the system is at equilibrium with monomer concentration $[M]_0$:** $$K_{eq} = \frac{1}{[M]_0}$$ **Step 3 — Apply $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$:** $$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln\left(\frac{1}{[M]_0}\right) = RT \ln [M]_0$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$:** $$\Delta H^\circ - T_c \Delta S^\circ = R T_c \ln [M]_0$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $T_c$:** $$T_c (\Delta S^\circ + R \ln [M]_0) = \Delta H^\circ$$ $$\boxed{T_c = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ + R \ln [M]_0}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Above $T_c$, $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ and depolymerization dominates. Below $T_c$, polymerization is thermodynamically favored. This is why $\alpha$-methylstyrene (low $T_c$) is hard to polymerize, while ethylene (high $T_c$) polymerizes readily. ## 3.8 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix | Problem Type | Identifying Clue | Key Formula to Apply | |---|---|---| | Find $K_{eq}$ from $\Delta G^\circ$ | Given $\Delta G^\circ$ at some $T$ | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | | Find $E_a$ from two $k$'s | Two $(k, T)$ pairs | $E_a = \dfrac{R \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1)-(1/T_2)}$ | | Order of reaction | "Plot of ___ vs $t$ is linear" | Match linear plot from rate-law table | | Half-life order | Given $t_{1/2}$ depends on $[A]_0$ or not | If yes → 2nd order; if no → 1st order | | Degree of polymerization | Given extent of reaction $p$ | $\overline{DP}_n = 1/(1-p)$ | | Equilibrium concentration | Given $K_{eq}$ and initial concentrations | ICE table + solve quadratic | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Profile Diagram — Endothermic Reaction This Mermaid diagram shows the energy profile of an endothermic reaction with and without a catalyst, highlighting the activation energy and reaction enthalpy. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["REACTANTS

How Does a Catalyst Affect the Activation Energy of a Reaction? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Activation energy ($E_a$)** is the minimum amount of energy that reactant molecules must possess in order to undergo a successful reaction. It represents the energy barrier between reactants and the transition state (activated complex). **Effect of a Catalyst:** A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a **lower activation energy**. The catalyst does not change the overall enthalpy change $\Delta H$ of the reaction, nor does it affect the equilibrium constant $K_{eq}$. It increases both forward and reverse rate constants equally. Mathematically (Arrhenius): $$k_{catalyzed} = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_{a,\text{cat}}}{RT}\right) > k_{uncatalyzed}$$ because $E_{a,\text{cat}} < E_{a,\text{uncat}}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + catalyst mechanism (1 mark) + Arrhenius implication (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Derive the Integrated Rate Law for a First-Order Reaction. Show That the Half-Life of a First-Order Reaction is Independent of Initial Concentration. (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Write the differential rate law:** $$-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables:** $$\frac{d[A]}{[A]} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate from $t = 0$ (concentration $[A]_0$) to time $t$ (concentration $[A]$):** $$\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \frac{d[A]'}{[A]'} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate:** $$\ln [A] - \ln [A]_0 = -kt$$ **Step 5 — Express as:** $$\ln \frac{[A]}{[A]_0} = -kt \quad \text{or} \quad [A] = [A]_0 e^{-kt}$$ **Step 6 — Half-life: at $t = t_{1/2}$, $[A] = [A]_0/2$. Substituting:** $$\ln \frac{[A]_0/2}{[A]_0} = -k t_{1/2}$$ $$\ln \frac{1}{2} = -k t_{1/2}$$ $$-\ln 2 = -k t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}}$$ **Conclusion:** $t_{1/2}$ depends ONLY on $k$, not on $[A]_0$. This is the defining feature of first-order kinetics. --- **(b) The Rate Constant of a First-Order Reaction is $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ s$^{-1}$ at 298 K. If the Activation Energy is 80 kJ/mol, Calculate the Rate Constant at 350 K. (7 marks)** **Given:** - $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ s$^{-1}$ - $T_1 = 298$ K - $E_a = 80,000$ J/mol - $T_2 = 350$ K - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) **Formula (two-temperature Arrhenius):** $$\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ **Step 1 — Compute the temperature term:** $$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{350}$$ $$\frac{1}{298} = 3.3557 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{350} = 2.8571 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = 4.986 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $E_a/R$:** $$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314} = 9621.6 \ \text{K}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the right-hand side:** $$\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = 9621.6 \times 4.986 \times 10^{-4} = 4.798$$ **Step 4 — Compute $k_2/k_1$:** $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{4.798} = 121.7$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $k_2$:** $$\boxed{k_2 = 121.7 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.04 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{s}^{-1}}$$ **Sanity check:** Higher temperature → faster reaction → larger $k$. ✓ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $\ln(k_2/k_1) = -\frac{E_a}{R}(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1})$ with reversed sign — gives $k_2 < k_1$, which is unphysical here. Always do the **sign check** as shown in Step 5. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert kJ to J ($E_a$ must be in J/mol when $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)). > - **Common Mistake 3:** Dropping the negative sign or placing it inside the parenthesis incorrectly. > - **Marks at risk:** 1 to 2 marks lost per error; a sign mistake typically costs **full marks** on the numerical part. --- ### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Define the Equilibrium Constant. Derive the Relationship Between Equilibrium Constant and the Standard Gibbs Free Energy Change. (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** The equilibrium constant $K_{eq}$ is the ratio of product activities to reactant activities, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient, at equilibrium. For $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$: $$K_{eq} = \frac{a_C^c \cdot a_D^d}{a_A^a \cdot a_B^b}$$ **Derivation (6 marks):** **Step 1 — Consider the Gibbs free energy change for any reaction not at equilibrium:** $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ Where $Q$ is the reaction quotient (same form as $K_{eq}$ but with current, non-equilibrium activities). **Step 2 — At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K_{eq}$:** $$0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K_{eq}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange:** $$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}}$$ **Step 4 — Consequence using $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$:** $$-RT \ln K_{eq} = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$ $$\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation:** - If $\Delta G^\circ < 0$ (exergonic), then $K_{eq} > 1$ → products favored. - If $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ (endergonic), then $K_{eq} < 1$ → reactants favored. - If $\Delta G^\circ = 0$, then $K_{eq} = 1$ → equal amounts. --- **(b) For the Esterification Reaction CH$_3$COOH + C$_2$H$_5$OH $\rightleftharpoons$ CH$_3$COOC$_2$H$_5$ + H$_2$O, Initially 1 mol Each of Acid and Alcohol are Mixed. At Equilibrium, 0.667 mol of Ester is Formed. Calculate $K_{eq}$. (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Construct the ICE (Initial, Change, Equilibrium) table:** | Species | CH$_3$COOH | C$_2$H$_5$OH | CH$_3$COOC$_2$H$_5$ | H$_2$O | |---|---|---|---|---| | Initial (mol) | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | | Change (mol) | $-x$ | $-x$ | $+x$ | $+x$ | | Equilibrium (mol) | $1-x$ | $1-x$ | $x$ | $x$ | **Step 2 — Identify $x$ from the given equilibrium amount of ester:** $$x = 0.667 \ \text{mol}$$ **Step 3 — Compute equilibrium amounts:** - CH$_3$COOH: $1 - 0.667 = 0.333$ mol - C$_2$H$_5$OH: $1 - 0.667 = 0.333$ mol - CH$_3$COOC$_2$H$_5$: $0.667$ mol - H$_2$O: $0.667$ mol **Step 4 — Compute total volume (assuming reaction volume $V$ is constant and the same for all species — units cancel in $K_c$):** Since all stoichiometric coefficients are 1 and the volume cancels in the ratio for this reaction: $$K_c = \frac{[ester][H_2O]}{[acid][alcohol]} = \frac{(0.667)(0.667)}{(0.333)(0.333)} = \frac{0.4449}{0.1109}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate:** $$\boxed{K_c = 4.01 \approx 4}$$ **Sanity check:** Since the equilibrium lies slightly toward products (more ester than remaining acid), $K_c > 1$. ✓ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Treating equilibrium concentrations as initial concentrations. The ICE table is mandatory — examiners award 1 mark just for writing it correctly. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that the **total volume cancels** in the $K_c$ expression when stoichiometric coefficients sum to the same on both sides (here: 2 = 2). If they don't cancel, you must divide by volume. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not labelling units on $K_c$ (formally, with concentrations in mol/L, $K_c$ would carry units, but for this problem the ratio is dimensionless because volumes cancel). > - **Marks at risk:** 2-3 marks for an incorrect or missing ICE table. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist** ### A. Equilibrium Concepts - ☑ **Dynamic equilibrium** = forward rate = reverse rate (NOT a stopped reaction). - ☑ $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$ — products over reactants, each raised to stoichiometric coefficient. - ☑ $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ — **single most important equation** linking thermodynamics and equilibrium. - ☑ Van't Hoff: $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$. - ☑ Le Chatelier: System shifts to **counteract** applied stress. - ☑ Catalysts do NOT change $K_{eq}$ — they only accelerate the rate of approach. ### B. Kinetics Concepts - ☑ **Rate law:** $r = k[A]^m[B]^n$ — order is **empirical**, not from stoichiometry. - ☑ **Order from plots:** Zero → $[A]$ vs $t$ linear; First → $\ln[A]$ vs $t$ linear; Second → $1/[A]$ vs $t$ linear. - ☑ **Half-life signatures:** - 1st order: $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ (constant, independent of $[A]_0$). - 0th order: $t_{1/2} = [A]_0/(2k)$. - 2nd order: $t_{1/2} = 1/(k[A]_0)$ (depends on $[A]_0$). - ☑ **Arrhenius:** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$; $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ plot has slope $-E_a/R$. - ☑ **Two-temperature form:** $E_a = \dfrac{R \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1)-(1/T_2)}$. ### C. Polymer-Specific Connections - ☑ **Carothers equation:** $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (equimolar step polymerization). - ☑ **Ceiling temperature:** $T_c = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ + R \ln [M]_0}$ — above $T_c$, polymer cannot form. - ☑ **Stoichiometric imbalance** $r < 1$ limits $\overline{DP}_n$ to $\dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$. - ☑ High polymer ($\overline{DP}_n > 100$) demands $p > 0.99$ — strict purity required. - ☑ First-order kinetics governs polymer degradation, drug release, and enzyme-like processes. ### D. Common Pitfalls to Avoid - ⚠️ NEVER confuse **order** (kinetics) with **molecularity** (mechanism). - ⚠️ NEVER assume order = stoichiometric coefficient (this is true ONLY for elementary reactions). - ⚠️ ALWAYS convert $E_a$ to J/mol when using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). - ⚠️ ALWAYS do the **sign check** on Arrhenius two-temperature problems. - ⚠️ ALWAYS draw an ICE table for equilibrium concentration problems. - ⚠️ NEVER forget that catalysts lower $E_a$ but do not change $\Delta G^\circ$ or $K_{eq}$.

topics.title: Review of thermodynamic correlations for the estimation of physical properties like phase equilibria, bubble and dew points
Simulation of the models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of the models ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Simulation of polymer models** is the computational execution of mathematical representations of polymer systems—ranging from atomistic to mesoscopic scales—using numerical algorithms that solve the underlying equations of motion (classical, stochastic, or statistical) to predict macroscopic properties, chain conformations, dynamics, and thermodynamic behaviour of polymeric materials. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, simulation bridges the gap between an *abstract polymer model* (bead–spring, freely-jointed chain, rotational isomeric state, etc.) and *experimentally observable quantities* such as radius of gyration, mean-square end-to-end distance, diffusion coefficient, glass transition temperature, and viscoelastic moduli. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Simulation techniques covered include **Molecular Dynamics (MD)**, **Monte Carlo (MC)**, **Coarse-Grained (CG) simulations**, and **Dissipative Particle Dynamics (DPD)**. Students must understand the algorithm, the integration scheme, the ensemble, and the post-processing analysis. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a box of **interconnected beads on springs** (the polymer chain). Each bead is an atom, each spring is a covalent bond. Now, instead of dropping this box in a real lab and filming it with a high-speed camera, you write down the **rules of motion** (Newton's laws) on a computer and let the machine evolve the positions and velocities of every bead for billions of tiny time steps. That, in essence, is **Molecular Dynamics simulation**. A **Monte Carlo simulation**, on the other hand, is like a *blindfolded chemist* who randomly proposes new configurations for the polymer chain (rotating a bond, flipping a torsion angle), then **accepts or rejects** the change based on an energy rule (Metropolis criterion). Over millions of proposals, the system "discovers" its lowest-energy states—exactly like the experimental technique of simulated annealing. A **Coarse-Grained simulation** is like zooming out on Google Earth: instead of tracking every atom, you group 3–5 atoms into a single "super-atom" and track these blobs. The simulation runs faster, but you lose atomic detail—a trade-off engineers willingly accept for studying million-atom polymer melts. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definitions Block:** > - **Time step ($\Delta t$):** The smallest increment of simulated time, typically **1 fs ($10^{-15}$ s)** for atomistic MD and **10–40 fs** for coarse-grained MD. > - **Ensemble:** A statistical collection of system states sharing macroscopic constraints (e.g., NVT = constant Number, Volume, Temperature). > - **Force field:** The mathematical function describing the potential energy of the system as a function of atomic coordinates. ### 1.3 Why Simulation Matters in Polymer Engineering Polymer materials are **notoriously difficult to characterize experimentally** at the molecular level because: - Chain conformations exist on **nanometre length scales** and **picosecond–microsecond time scales**. - Most polymers are **amorphous, semi-crystalline, or glassy**—no clean periodic crystal for X-ray diffraction. - Processing–structure–property relationships span **15+ decades** in length and time. Simulation provides the only practical window into this multi-scale universe, enabling **rational design** of new polymers for biomedical, aerospace, and energy applications. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Polymer Simulation #### **Pillar 1 — Molecular Dynamics (MD)** MD solves Newton's second law of motion for every particle in the system: $$F_i = m_i a_i = m_i \frac{d^2 \mathbf{r}_i}{dt^2}$$ The force on each particle is the negative gradient of the potential energy: $$\mathbf{F}_i = -\nabla_i U(\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_N)$$ The total potential energy of a polymer force field decomposes into bonded and non-bonded terms: $$U_{\text{total}} = U_{\text{bond}} + U_{\text{angle}} + U_{\text{dihedral}} + U_{\text{non-bonded}}$$ **Why these terms?** Because a polymer chain is held together by covalent bonds (stretching), bond angles (bending), dihedral rotations (internal twisting), and long-range van der Waals + electrostatic interactions between non-bonded atoms. #### **Pillar 2 — Monte Carlo (MC)** MC does **not** use Newton's laws. Instead, it samples configuration space using the **Metropolis algorithm**: 1. Propose a random move (e.g., rotate a backbone torsion angle). 2. Compute the energy change $\Delta U$. 3. Accept the move with probability: $$P_{\text{accept}} = \min\left(1, \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta U}{k_B T}\right)\right)$$ 4. Repeat millions of times to generate a Boltzmann-weighted ensemble. **Why MC for polymers?** MC is **ergodic** for chain-connectivity-preserving moves (pivot, crankshaft, reptation) and avoids the **time-step limitation** of MD—making it ideal for studying long-chain conformations. #### **Pillar 3 — Coarse-Grained (CG) Simulation** CG lumps groups of atoms into "beads," reducing degrees of freedom and enabling larger system sizes / longer simulation times. Popular CG models: - **MARTINI force field** (4:1 mapping for biomolecules/polymers) - **Dissipative Particle Dynamics (DPD)** — soft potentials for hydrodynamic behaviour - **Kremer–Grest bead–spring model** — Lennard-Jones beads connected by FENE springs (the workhorse for polymer melt dynamics) ### 2.2 Time Integration Algorithms To propagate positions and velocities forward in time, MD uses finite-difference integration schemes. The most common is the **Velocity Verlet algorithm**: $$\mathbf{r}_i(t + \Delta t) = \mathbf{r}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t)\Delta t + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}_i(t)\Delta t^2$$ $$\mathbf{v}_i(t + \Delta t) = \mathbf{v}_i(t) + \frac{1}{2}\big[\mathbf{a}_i(t) + \mathbf{a}_i(t + \Delta t)\big]\Delta t$$ **Why Velocity Verlet?** It is **time-reversible**, **symplectic** (conserves energy over long simulations), and requires only **one force evaluation per step**—essential for million-atom polymer systems. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Polymer Simulation question.** | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units / Values | |---|----------|------------------|------------------------| | 1 | $\mathbf{F}_i = -\nabla_i U$ | Force from potential energy gradient | kJ mol$^{-1}$ nm$^{-1}$ | | 2 | $U_{\text{bond}} = \frac{1}{2}k_b(r - r_0)^2$ | Harmonic bond stretching | $k_b \approx 1000$ kJ mol$^{-1}$ nm$^{-2}$ | | 3 | $U_{\text{angle}} = \frac{1}{2}k_\theta(\theta - \theta_0)^2$ | Bond-angle bending | $k_\theta \approx 100$ kJ mol$^{-1}$ rad$^{-2}$ | | 4 | $U_{\text{dihedral}} = \sum_n \frac{V_n}{2}\big[1 + \cos(n\phi - \gamma)\big]$ | Torsional rotation | $V_n$ in kJ mol$^{-1}$ | | 5 | $U_{\text{LJ}}(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$ | Lennard-Jones non-bonded potential | $\varepsilon \approx 0.5$ kJ mol$^{-1}$, $\sigma \approx 0.34$ nm | | 6 | $U_{\text{Coulomb}}(r) = \frac{q_i q_j}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$ | Electrostatic interaction | $q$ in elementary charges | | 7 | $P_{\text{accept}} = \min\big(1, e^{-\Delta U / k_B T}\big)$ | Metropolis MC criterion | Dimensionless | | 8 | $k_B T = \mathbf{1.380649 \times 10^{-23}}$ J K$^{-1}$ | Thermal energy (Boltzmann constant $\times$ temperature) | At 300 K: $\approx 2.479$ kJ mol$^{-1}$ | | 9 | $T_{\text{inst}} = \frac{2}{3 N_{\text{dof}} k_B} \sum_i \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i^2$ | Instantaneous temperature from kinetic energy | Kelvin | | 10 | $\langle R_g^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \vert \mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_{\text{cm}} \vert^2$ | Radius of gyration (chain size) | nm$^2$ | | 11 | $\langle R^2 \rangle = C_\infty N l^2$ | Mean-square end-to-end distance (Flory) | nm$^2$ | | 12 | $D = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{\langle \vert \mathbf{r}(t) - \mathbf{r}(0) \vert^2 \rangle}{6t}$ | Self-diffusion coefficient from MSD | nm$^2$ ps$^{-1}$ | | 13 | $\rho_{\text{cutoff}} \approx 1.0$–$1.5$ nm | Non-bonded interaction cutoff (saves compute time) | nm | | 14 | $\Delta t = \mathbf{1}$–$\mathbf{2}$ fs (atomistic), $\mathbf{10}$–$\mathbf{40}$ fs (CG) | Integration time step | femtoseconds | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility | Simulation Type | Real-World Application | |-----------------|------------------------| | Atomistic MD (OPLS-AA, PCFF) | Predict glass transition T$_g$, Young's modulus, solubility parameters | | Coarse-Grained MD (MARTINI) | Membrane protein–polymer interactions, drug delivery | | DPD | Polymer processing (injection molding flow), emulsion stability | | MC (RISM, lattice MC) | Phase diagrams of polymer blends, crystallization kinetics | | Mesoscale (TDDFT for polymers) | Self-assembly of block copolymers for nano-lithography | > [!NOTE] > **Why use reduced (Lennard-Jones) units in polymer simulations?** > Reduced units make equations **dimensionless** ($\sigma$, $\varepsilon$, $m$ as reference). This lets one simulation represent many real polymer chemistries and reduces floating-point error—a key trick taught in KTU Module 2. --- ## 3.
