Always conclude with the consolidated 3-equation (or 6-equation with moments) system. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)** > > - **Definition (must memorize):** EO = simultaneous solution of the *entire* set of process equations in one unified system. > - **Mathematical form to remember:** $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}', \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{0}$ > - **Three pillars of EO models in polymers:** mass balance + energy balance + constitutive/rheological law. > - **Newton-Raphson update formula:** $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ > - **Jacobian definition:** $J_{ij} = \partial F_i / \partial x_j$ > - **Power-law viscosity with temperature:** $\eta = K \exp[E_a/R(1/T - 1/T_0)]\, \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ > - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ > - **Free-radical rate:** $R_p = k_p [M]\sqrt{2f k_d [I]/k_t}$ > - **QSSA result:** $[P^\bullet] = \sqrt{f k_d [I] / k_t}$ > - **Molecular weight averages:** $M_n = M_0 \lambda_1/\lambda_0$, $M_w = M_0 \lambda_2/\lambda_1$ > - **PDI:** $M_w / M_n = \lambda_0 \lambda_2 / \lambda_1^2$ > - **EO strength:** dynamic simulation + optimization + real-time control. > - **SM strength:** steady-state design of large flowsheets. > - **Solver type:** Newton-Raphson with sparse Jacobian; DAE integrators DASSL/DASPK for dynamics. > - **Convergence tolerance:** typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$. > - **Always include the final consolidated system** in derivations to earn full marks.

topics.title: Partitioning and tearing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partitioning and tearing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Finite Element Modelling of Polymer Materials**, the computational domain (the discretised representation of a polymer component, mould cavity, or microstructural Representative Volume Element, RVE) is rarely solved as a single monolithic block. Instead, it is decomposed into smaller, manageable pieces. Two fundamental decomposition philosophies are employed:

**Partitioning** is the systematic subdivision of the global Finite Element (FE) mesh into a set of non-overlapping **subdomains** (or *partitions*) such that the total number of degrees of freedom (DOF), element count, or computational workload is balanced across processors/regions, while communication across partition boundaries is minimised.

**Tearing** (also called *mesh tearing* or *element tearing*) is the local geometric subdivision of a single large element (or a cluster of elements) into smaller, simpler elements by introducing internal **hanging nodes** or **internal boundaries**, typically to capture steep solution gradients such as crack tips, phase boundaries in polymer blends, or stress singularities in elastomers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Partitioning is a *global* operation (operates on the entire mesh graph), while tearing is a *local* operation (operates on one element or a small patch). Together, they form the foundation of **adaptive mesh refinement** and **parallel FE solvers** used in modern polymer simulation packages (ABAQUS, ANSYS Polyflow, COMSOL, OpenFOAM).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **large chocolate bar** (the polymer moulding) that you want to share among **8 friends** (the parallel processors).

- **Partitioning** is like breaking the bar into 8 pieces so that each friend gets roughly the same amount of chocolate, and you minimise the number of breaks that go all the way across the bar (because those breaks represent costly inter-processor communication).
- **Tearing** is when you discover a small, hairline crack right in the middle of one piece. You then *tear* that piece along the crack to expose the damage so you can study it in finer detail.

In a real polymer simulation:
- **Partitioning** allows a million-element simulation of an injection-moulded part to be split across 64 CPU cores.
- **Tearing** allows a tiny tear-tip in a rubber tyre to be resolved with **µm-scale elements** while the rest of the tyre uses **mm-scale elements** — saving enormous computational cost.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Polymer FE) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Global partition count | $p$ | 2 – 1024 | — |
| Load imbalance factor | $\varepsilon$ | 0.01 – 0.10 | dimensionless |
| Edge-cut ratio | $\eta_{cut}$ | 0.001 – 0.05 | dimensionless |
| Hanging-node ratio | $\rho_{hn}$ | 0 – 0.30 | dimensionless |
| Critical tearing energy (natural rubber) | $T_c$ | 10 000 – 100 000 | J·m⁻² |
| Threshold tearing energy (GPC, PE) | $T_c$ | 1000 – 10 000 | J·m⁻² |
| Plane-strain fracture toughness | $K_{Ic}$ | 0.5 – 5 | MPa·m^½ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical tearing energy $T_c$** (also called *fracture energy* or *essential work of fracture*) is a fundamental polymer property introduced by Rivlin & Thomas (1953). It is the energy required to propagate a tear by unit area and governs **slow crack growth** in polyethylene pipes, rubber seals, and packaging films.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Decompose the Mesh?

Three engineering motivations justify mesh decomposition in polymer simulation:

1. **Parallel Computability:** A single 8-core workstation cannot realistically invert the global stiffness matrix of a 10-million-DOF model of an automotive bumper in acceptable time. Partitioning decomposes the linear system into $p$ smaller subsystems solved concurrently.
2. **Memory Scalability:** The peak RAM per core (typically **32 – 256 GB**) limits the local stiffness matrix size. Partitioning keeps each subdomain below this ceiling.
3. **Local Solution Adaptivity:** Polymer fields (stress in a notch root, concentration gradient in a diffusion couple, crystallinity front in a cooling mould) are *locally* steep. Tearing concentrates resolution where the physics demands it.

### 2.2 The Partitioning Problem — Formal Statement

Let $G = (V, E)$ be the **element-adjacency graph** of the FE mesh, where each vertex $v_i \in V$ represents one element and each edge $e_{ij} \in E$ exists iff elements $i$ and $j$ share a face (in 3D) or an edge (in 2D). The graph has $|V| = N$ elements.

**Objective:** Partition $V$ into $k$ disjoint subsets $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_k$ such that:

$$\bigcup_{a=1}^{k} V_a = V \quad \text{and} \quad V_a \cap V_b = \emptyset \;\; \forall\, a \neq b$$

**Subject to the load-balance constraint:**

$$|V_a| \leq (1+\varepsilon)\,\frac{N}{k} \quad \forall a \in \{1, \dots, k\}$$

**Minimising the edge-cut cost function:**

$$\Phi(V_1, \dots, V_k) = \sum_{a<b} \; \sum_{\substack{e_{ij} \in E \\ v_i \in V_a, \, v_j \in V_b}} w(e_{ij})$$

where $w(e_{ij})$ is the **edge weight** (often the number of shared DOFs between elements $i$ and $j$, or the communication cost per iteration).

### 2.3 Tearing — Formal Statement

Given a parent element $\Omega_p$ (typically a quadrilateral in 2D or hexahedron in 3D) and a marked region $R \subset \Omega_p$ requiring refinement, **tearing** constructs a sub-mesh $\{\Omega_c\}$ such that:

$$\bigcup_c \bar{\Omega}_c = \bar{\Omega}_p, \quad \Omega_c \cap \Omega_d = \emptyset \;\; (c \neq d)$$

and at least one new vertex $v^*$ (a **hanging node** or **tearing node**) is introduced on an existing edge or face. The conformity condition (in the $C^0$ sense) is enforced by constraining the DOFs of $v^*$ via the parent element's shape functions:

$$\mathbf{u}(v^*) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_p} N_i(v^*)\,\mathbf{u}_i$$

where $N_i$ are the parent element's shape functions evaluated at the new node location $v^*$ and $n_p$ is the number of parent corner nodes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarises every equation you must know for partitioning-and-tearing questions in the KTU exam. **Memorise these before attempting any numerical.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Edge-cut objective | $\Phi = \sum_{a<b}\sum_{e_{ij}\in E_{cut}} w(e_{ij})$ | Total communication volume across partition boundaries |
| 2 | Load-balance bound | $\vert V_a\vert \leq (1+\varepsilon)N/k$ | No partition exceeds $(1+\varepsilon)$ times the average |
| 3 | Isoperimetric ratio | $\rho = \frac{\text{edge-cut}}{N^{1-1/d}}$ | Quality metric; lower is better, $d$ = spatial dimension |
| 4 | RCB bisection cost | $\Phi_{RCB} \approx N^{(d-1)/d}$ | Recursive Coordinate Bisection upper bound |
| 5 | Spectral gap | $\lambda_2 / \lambda_1$ | Quality of spectral bisection (larger = cleaner cut) |
| 6 | Tearing energy (Rivlin–Thomas) | $T = -\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial A}$ | Energy released per unit increase of crack area |
| 7 | Pure-shear tearing energy | $T = 2\,k\,W\,c$ | Pure-shear specimen, $k$ = geometric factor, $W$ = strain energy density, $c$ = crack length |
| 8 | Trousers-tear formula | $T = \dfrac{2 F \lambda}{t} - \dfrac{\partial U_{el}}{\partial A}$ | Force-based measurement, $F$ = tear force, $\lambda$ = extension ratio, $t$ = thickness |
| 9 | Hanging-node constraint | $\mathbf{u}(v^*) = \sum N_i(v^*)\,\mathbf{u}_i$ | Conformity constraint tying hanging node to parents |
| 10 | Subdivision ratio (quaternary) | $r = \dfrac{h_{\text{parent}}}{h_{\text{child}}}$ | Each tear step divides element size by $r$ |
| 11 | DOF growth after $L$ tear levels | $N_{DOF}(L) = N_0 + \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \Delta N_\ell$ | Track computational cost of adaptive tearing |
| 12 | Fracture-mechanics link | $T = \dfrac{K_{Ic}^2}{E'}$ | Plane strain: $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$; plane stress: $E' = E$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Partitioning** is used by every modern solver: ABAQUS uses **METIS/ScOTCH** for MPI-domain decomposition; ANSYS Mechanical uses the **RCM (Reverse Cuthill–McKee)** bandwidth reducer plus partition-by-region; OpenFOAM uses **simple/topoSet** decomposition. Without partitioning, simulating a full car bumper in glass-fibre-reinforced PP would be infeasible.
- **Tearing** is the engine of **adaptive crack-propagation studies** for rubber gaskets (used in FKM/EPDM sealing analysis), **short-fibre composites**, and **microcellular foam** crushing simulations. The classic Rivlin–Thomas tearing energy $T_c$ is the design parameter in **tyre durability** standards (e.g., ISO 34-1, ASTM D624) and **geomembrane liner** selection for landfills (HDPE, LLDPE).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Edge-Cut Lower Bound via Isoperimetric Inequality

We derive a theoretical lower bound on the edge-cut for any bisection of a 2D FE mesh with $N$ elements.

**Step 1 — Define the boundary of a subset.** For a subset $V_a \subseteq V$, let $\partial V_a$ be the set of edges connecting $V_a$ to $V \setminus V_a$:

$$\partial V_a = \{e_{ij} \in E \mid v_i \in V_a, \, v_j \notin V_a\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the graph isoperimetric inequality.** For a planar graph with $N$ vertices and unit edge weights, the isoperimetric inequality states:

$$|\partial V_a| \;\geq\; c_d \cdot |V_a|^{(d-1)/d}$$

For $d = 2$, $c_2 = 1$, giving:

$$\begin{aligned}
|\partial V_a| \;\geq\; |V_a|^{1/2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Sum over both sides of a bisection.** A bisection $(V_1, V_2)$ cuts at least:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi &\geq |\partial V_1| + |\partial V_2| \\
&\geq |V_1|^{1/2} + |V_2|^{1/2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Minimise subject to $|V_1| + |V_2| = N$.** By symmetry and concavity of $\sqrt{\cdot}$, the minimum occurs at $|V_1| = |V_2| = N/2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{\min}^{(2)} &\geq 2\left(\frac{N}{2}\right)^{1/2} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{N^{1/2}}{2^{1/2}} \\
&= \sqrt{2\,N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Any 2-way partition of an $N$-element mesh must cut at least $\sqrt{2N}$ edges. This is the *theoretical floor* against which practical algorithms (METIS, RCB) are benchmarked.

### 3.2 Derivation: Rivlin–Thomas Tearing Energy from Energy Release Rate

We derive the classical Rivlin–Thomas formula starting from first principles of fracture mechanics.

**Step 1 — Define tearing energy.** Tearing energy $T$ is the energy released per unit area of new crack surface:

$$\begin{aligned}
T = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial A}
\end{aligned}$$

where $U$ is the elastic strain energy stored in the body and $A$ is the crack surface area.

**Step 2 — Geometry of pure-shear specimen.** A pure-shear test piece is a long, narrow strip of height $h$ containing an edge crack of length $c$. The crack area is $A = t \cdot c$, where $t$ is the specimen thickness.

**Step 3 — Express strain energy in terms of $c$.** In the pure-shear region, the strain energy density $W$ is approximately uniform. The total elastic energy is:

$$\begin{aligned}
U = W \cdot V = W \cdot t \cdot h \cdot L_{\text{eff}}(c)
\end{aligned}$$

where $L_{\text{eff}}(c)$ is the effective gauge length over which $W$ acts. For a crack growing from $c$ to $c + dc$, the *new* un-cracked material entering the high-energy zone has gauge length $dc$. Thus $L_{\text{eff}}(c) \approx k \cdot c$ for some geometric factor $k$ (typically $k = 1$ for a long strip with end-clamping far from the crack tip).

**Step 4 — Substitute and differentiate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= W \cdot t \cdot h \cdot k \cdot c \\
\frac{\partial U}{\partial c} &= W \cdot t \cdot h \cdot k
\end{aligned}$$

Since $A = t \cdot c$, we have $\partial A = t \cdot \partial c$, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= -\frac{1}{t}\frac{\partial U}{\partial c} \\
  &= -\frac{1}{t}\cdot W \cdot t \cdot h \cdot k \\
  &= W \cdot h \cdot k
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the pure-shear condition.** In pure shear, the stored energy density in the uncracked ligament is $W = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{22}\,\varepsilon_{22}$ (no other stress components act). For a strip under displacement control, classical elasticity gives $W \cdot h = $ const, and the geometric factor $k = 1$ for the standard pure-shear geometry. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,T = 2\,W\,c\,}
\end{aligned}$$

after accounting for the factor of 2 that arises because both sides of the crack release energy. This is the **Rivlin–Thomas equation** for the pure-shear tearing test.

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Adaptive Tearing of a Notched Polymer Bar

**Problem:** A notched polymer tensile specimen has an initial mesh of 800 quadrilateral elements. Adaptive tearing is applied near the notch root for 3 levels, each level quartering the element size locally (subdivision ratio $r = 2$ per side, generating 4 children per parent element). After tearing, 25% of the original elements are subdivided at level 1, 10% at level 2 (of the original), and 4% at level 3 (of the original). Compute the final element count.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Count level-1 children.** Each torn parent produces 4 children. Number of level-1 parents $= 0.25 \times 800 = 200$ elements. New children generated $= 200 \times 4 = 800$. The 200 original parents are *replaced*, not added.

Net level-1 contribution: $-200 + 800 = +600$ elements.

**Step 2 — Count level-2 children.** Number of level-2 parents $= 0.10 \times 800 = 80$ elements (these are *original* elements, not level-1 children, in this simplified count). New children $= 80 \times 4 = 320$.

Net level-2 contribution: $-80 + 320 = +240$ elements.

**Step 3 — Count level-3 children.** Number of level-3 parents $= 0.04 \times 800 = 32$ elements. New children $= 32 \times 4 = 128$.

Net level-3 contribution: $-32 + 128 = +96$ elements.

**Step 4 — Total.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{final}} &= N_0 + \Delta N_1 + \Delta N_2 + \Delta N_3 \\
&= 800 + 600 + 240 + 96 \\
&= \boxed{1736 \text{ elements}}
\end{aligned}$$

This represents a **2.17× growth** in element count, concentrated near the notch root — exactly the strategy needed to capture the steep stress gradient there.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Mesh Partitioning and Tearing in a Polymer FE Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT CAD Geometry of Polymer Part"]
    nodeB["Stage 1 Mesh Generation"]
    nodeC["Stage 2 Global Mesh Graph G of V E"]
    nodeD{"Is Adaptive Refinement Required?"}
    nodeE["Stage 3 Partitioning Algorithm METIS RCB Spectral"]
    nodeF["Stage 4 Load Balance Check epsilon constraint"]
    nodeG["Stage 3b Local Tearing Subdivision near singularity"]
    nodeH["Stage 4b Hanging Node Constraint Application"]
    nodeI["Stage 5 Assembly of Subdomain Stiffness Matrices"]
    nodeJ["Stage 6 Parallel Solver MPI CG GMRES"]
    nodeK["Stage 7 Post Processing Stress Strain Field in Polymer"]
    nodeL["Output Crack propagation path Tearing energy Tc"]
    nodeM["Recursion Check Compute error indicator eta"]
    nodeN["Refine further or accept solution"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeM -- High eta --> nodeD
    nodeM -- Low eta --> nodeN
```

### 4.2 Tearing Hierarchy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    parentA["Level 0 Parent Quadrilateral"]
    split1["Introduce Hanging Node on Edge"]
    c1A["Child Quadrilateral 1 of 4"]
    c1B["Child Quadrilateral 2 of 4"]
    c1C["Child Quadrilateral 3 of 4"]
    c1D["Child Quadrilateral 4 of 4"]
    split2["Identify Singularity Region"]
    c2A["Level 1 Tearing of Child 1"]
    c2B["Level 1 Tearing of Child 2"]
    sA["Sub Child 1A"]
    sB["Sub Child 1B"]
    sC["Sub Child 1C"]
    sD["Sub Child 1D"]

    parentA --> split1
    split1 --> c1A
    split1 --> c1B
    split1 --> c1C
    split1 --> c1D
    c1A --> split2
    c1B --> split2
    split2 --> c2A
    split2 --> c2B
    c2A --> sA
    c2A --> sB
    c2A --> sC
    c2A --> sD
```

### 4.3 Partitioning Communication Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0["Partition 0 Core 0"]
    p1["Partition 1 Core 1"]
    p2["Partition 2 Core 2"]
    p3["Partition 3 Core 3"]
    m01["Ghost Elements Shared Boundary"]
    m12["Ghost Elements Shared Boundary"]
    m23["Ghost Elements Shared Boundary"]
    m03["Ghost Elements Shared Boundary"]
    halo["Halo Update MPI Allreduce"]

    p0 --- m01
    p1 --- m01
    p1 --- m12
    p2 --- m12
    p2 --- m23
    p3 --- m23
    p0 --- m03
    p3 --- m03
    p0 -.-> halo
    p1 -.-> halo
    p2 -.-> halo
    p3 -.-> halo
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **halo / ghost-layer** updates shown above are the dominant communication cost in partitioned FE solves. The objective $\Phi$ in the formula sheet is precisely the total volume of these ghost layers, which is why **partitioning minimises $\Phi$** — and tearing adds to $\Phi$ locally whenever a hanging node sits on a partition boundary.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** Define *mesh partitioning* in the context of finite element simulation of polymer components. List any two objectives of partitioning.

**Model Answer:**
*Mesh partitioning* is the decomposition of the global finite element mesh of a polymer component into a set of non-overlapping subdomains such that each subdomain can be assigned to a separate processor core or solver partition.
The two primary objectives are:
1. **Parallel computation:** distributing the workload across multiple cores to reduce wall-clock solution time.
2. **Memory scalability:** ensuring the local stiffness matrix of each subdomain fits within the per-core RAM budget.
*(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for objectives.)*

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** State the Rivlin–Thomas equation for tearing energy in a pure-shear specimen. Define each term and indicate the units.

**Model Answer:**
The Rivlin–Thomas equation is:

$$T = 2\,W\,c$$

where $T$ is the *tearing energy* in J·m⁻², $W$ is the *elastic strain energy density* stored in the uncracked ligament in J·m⁻³, and $c$ is the *crack length* in m. The product $W \cdot c$ has units J·m⁻², consistent with an energy per unit area of new crack surface.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the difference between *partitioning* and *tearing* in FE mesh pre-processing for polymer simulations. With the help of a labelled sketch, describe how a single quadrilateral element can be *torn* into four child elements with hanging nodes, and write the constraint equation that enforces $C^0$ conformity at the hanging node.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A pure-shear rubber specimen (natural rubber, $W = 2.5 \times 10^{6}\;\text{J·m}^{-3}$ at the test extension ratio) has an edge crack of length $c = 12\;\text{mm}$. Compute the tearing energy $T$. If the critical tearing energy for this rubber grade is $T_c = 50\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}$, determine whether the crack will propagate. State the governing criterion in words.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**
- **Partitioning** is a *global*, graph-theoretic decomposition of the entire mesh into subdomains, optimised for parallel processing and load balance. Its objective function is the edge-cut $\Phi$, and typical algorithms include RCB, METIS, and spectral bisection. *(2 marks)*
- **Tearing** is a *local*, geometric subdivision of one element (or a small patch) into smaller elements, optimised for solution adaptivity in regions with steep gradients such as crack tips, weld lines, or phase boundaries. Hanging nodes are introduced on the parent element's edges. *(2 marks)*

**Sketch description (text rendering):** A parent quadrilateral with corners labelled 1, 2, 3, 4 (counter-clockwise). A new node $v^*$ is created at the midpoint of edge 1–2 (hanging node). Three additional edges are drawn from $v^*$ to the midpoints of edges 1–4 and 2–3 and across to corner 3, producing 4 child quadrilaterals. *(2 marks for description and labelled sketch)*

**Constraint equation:** For $C^0$ conformity, the displacement at the hanging node is interpolated from the parent corners:

$$\mathbf{u}(v^*) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(v^*)\,\mathbf{u}_i$$

where $N_i$ are the bilinear shape functions of the parent element evaluated at $v^*$. For a hanging node at the midpoint of edge 1–2, $N_1 = N_2 = 1/2$ and $N_3 = N_4 = 0$, so $\mathbf{u}(v^*) = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{u}_1 + \mathbf{u}_2)$. *(1 mark)*

**(b)**
Apply the Rivlin–Thomas pure-shear equation:

$$T = 2\,W\,c$$

Substitute:

$$T = 2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{6}\;\text{J·m}^{-3}) \times (12 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= 2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{6} \times 0.012\;\text{J·m}^{-2} \\
  &= 60\,000\;\text{J·m}^{-2} \\
  &= 60\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

*(4 marks for substitution and arithmetic.)*

**Comparison with $T_c$:**

$$T = 60\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2} \;>\; T_c = 50\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}$$

**Governing criterion (in words):** A crack in a polymer will propagate *slowly* (or in a stick-slip manner) when the applied tearing energy $T$ *exceeds* the critical material property $T_c$. *(1 mark)*

**Conclusion:** Since $T > T_c$, the crack **will propagate** in this specimen. *(1 mark for the verdict)*

**Safety factor:** $SF = T_c / T = 50/60 = 0.83$ — the design is unsafe at this extension ratio; either reduce strain energy density (lower extension ratio) or select a rubber grade with $T_c \geq 60\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Formulate the **graph-partitioning problem** for a finite element mesh with $N = 10\,000$ elements to be divided into $k = 8$ subdomains. State the load-balance constraint with $\varepsilon = 0.05$. An RCB (Recursive Coordinate Bisection) algorithm produces the cut shown below (in terms of edges cut between adjacent partitions): Partition cut edges: (0–1)=120, (0–4)=80, (1–2)=110, (2–3)=95, (3–7)=130, (4–5)=70, (5–6)=100, (6–7)=85. Compute the total edge-cut $\Phi$. If a METIS-based partitioner achieves $\Phi = 540$, calculate the percentage improvement of METIS over RCB.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A polyethylene pipe (PE 100 grade) is subjected to a constant internal pressure that drives slow crack growth. The critical tearing energy is $T_c = 8.5\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}$ and the strain energy density in the crack-tip process zone is $W = 350\;\text{kJ·m}^{-3}$. Using the simplified relation $T \approx W \cdot h$ for a pipe wall of thickness $h = 22\;\text{mm}$, evaluate $T$ and decide whether the pipe will fail by slow crack growth. If the pipe wall thickness is reduced by 25% in a redesigned version, by what percentage does the driving tearing energy change?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

**Formulation:** Given the element-adjacency graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = N = 10\,000$ vertices, partition $V$ into $k = 8$ subsets $V_0, V_1, \dots, V_7$ such that:

$$|V_a| \leq (1+\varepsilon)\,\frac{N}{k} = (1.05) \times \frac{10\,000}{8} = 1312.5 \;\;\text{elements per partition}$$

*(1 mark for formulation, 1 mark for the bound)*

**Minimise:**

$$\Phi = \sum_{a<b}\; \sum_{\substack{e_{ij} \in E \\ v_i \in V_a,\; v_j \in V_b}} w(e_{ij})$$

*(1 mark)*

**Total edge-cut from RCB:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{RCB} &= 120 + 80 + 110 + 95 + 130 + 70 + 100 + 85 \\
          &= 790
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks for summing)*

**Percentage improvement of METIS over RCB:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ improvement} &= \frac{\Phi_{RCB} - \Phi_{METIS}}{\Phi_{RCB}} \times 100 \\
                     &= \frac{790 - 540}{790} \times 100 \\
                     &= \frac{250}{790} \times 100 \\
                     &\approx 31.65\%
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks for formula and answer)*

**Comment:** A 31.65% reduction in edge-cut translates roughly to a 25–35% reduction in MPI communication time, often a significant fraction of total wall-clock time in large parallel FE runs.

**(b)**

**Compute $T$:**

$$T = W \cdot h = (350 \times 10^{3}\;\text{J·m}^{-3}) \times (22 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= 350\,000 \times 0.022 \\
  &= 7700\;\text{J·m}^{-2} \\
  &= 7.7\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Comparison:** $T = 7.7\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2} < T_c = 8.5\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}$.

**Verdict:** Since $T < T_c$, the crack will **not** propagate under this loading — the design is currently **safe** (margin: $T/T_c \approx 0.91$). *(1 mark)*

**Redesign with 25% thinner wall:**

New thickness: $h_{\text{new}} = 0.75 \times 22 = 16.5\;\text{mm}$.

New tearing energy (assuming $W$ unchanged — valid if internal pressure is held constant and only wall is thinned):

$$T_{\text{new}} = W \cdot h_{\text{new}} = 350\,000 \times 0.0165 = 5775\;\text{J·m}^{-2} = 5.775\;\text{kJ·m}^{-2}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Percentage change:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta T &= \frac{T_{\text{new}} - T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \times 100 \\
          &= \frac{5775 - 7700}{7700} \times 100 \\
          &= \frac{-1925}{7700} \times 100 \\
          &\approx -25\%
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Note for the examiner:** Some students may incorrectly write "$T$ also drops by 25% because it is linear in $h$" — this is *exactly* correct here because $W$ is held fixed by the constant-pressure boundary condition. Award full marks for stating that $T$ scales linearly with $h$, hence $T$ drops by exactly 25%.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing partitioning and tearing (–3 marks):** Partitioning is *global* (entire mesh into subdomains); tearing is *local* (one element into smaller elements). Examiners *will* deduct for treating them as synonyms.
> 2. **Wrong units in Rivlin–Thomas (–2 marks):** $T$ must be in **J·m⁻²**, $W$ in **J·m⁻³**, $c$ in **m**. A common error is writing $T = W \cdot c$ (off by the factor of 2) without explaining why the factor 2 appears — state that *both* sides of the crack release energy.
> 3. **Forgetting the hanging-node constraint (–2 marks):** When you tear an element, the hanging node is *not* an independent DOF. You must write the bilinear-interpolation constraint or you will lose marks on the conformity condition.
> 4. **Sign error in tearing-energy derivative (–1 mark):** Some students write $T = +\partial U/\partial A$. The correct sign is *negative* because increasing $A$ releases stored energy (i.e., $U$ decreases).
> 5. **Not stating the propagation criterion explicitly (–1 mark):** Always end with "**$T > T_c$ ⇒ crack propagates**" or its negation — never just quote a number.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Partitioning** is the global decomposition of a polymer FE mesh into $k$ subdomains, governed by the edge-cut objective $\Phi$ and load-balance bound $(1+\varepsilon)N/k$. Standard algorithms: **RCB, Spectral Bisection, METIS, Scotch, KaHIP**.
- **Tearing** is the local subdivision of one element into smaller children using **hanging nodes**, governed by the bilinear constraint $\mathbf{u}(v^*) = \sum N_i(v^*)\,\mathbf{u}_i$.
- **Rivlin–Thomas tearing energy** for pure shear: $\boxed{T = 2\,W\,c}$. **Propagation criterion:** $T > T_c$ ⇒ slow crack growth.
- **Trouser-tear formula:** $T = 2F\lambda/t - \partial U_{el}/\partial A$ — used in **ASTM D624** and **ISO 34-1** standards.
- **Fracture-mechanics link:** $T = K_{Ic}^2/E'$, with $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$ in plane strain — connects tearing energy to linear-elastic $K$-field.
- **Adaptive tearing** grows element count by a factor of approximately $r^{d \cdot L}$ after $L$ refinement levels with subdivision ratio $r$ in dimension $d$.
- **Typical polymer $T_c$ values to memorise:** NR ≈ 50 kJ·m⁻²; PE 100 ≈ 8 kJ·m⁻²; PP homopolymer ≈ 3 kJ·m⁻²; PET ≈ 5 kJ·m⁻².
- **Hanging nodes** must be eliminated (via constraint equations) before the global stiffness assembly, or the solver will report a singular matrix.
- **Edge-cut** in partitioning maps directly onto **MPI communication volume** — minimising $\Phi$ is minimising wall-clock time on a cluster.
- **Isoperimetric lower bound** for 2D bisection: $\Phi_{\min} \geq \sqrt{2N}$ — your partitioner should approach this for regular meshes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction and use of process simulation software for flow sheet simulation
Transporting of polymer particulate solid
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transporting of polymer particulate solid

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Polymer particulate solids** are discrete, non-continuous solid phases of polymeric matter (pellets, granules, flakes, powders, or regrinds) that must be moved from one processing station to another while preserving their physical integrity (size distribution, shape, moisture, crystallinity). **Transporting of polymer particulate solids** is defined as the controlled conveyance of these bulk solids through process equipment (hoppers, screw feeders, pneumatic lines, extruder solids-conveying zones, rotary valves) governed by inter-particle forces, gravity, drag, and applied mechanical work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Anchor Definition**
> Transport of polymer particulates is modelled as a **continuum** (using bulk density $\rho_b$ and stress tensors) for macroscopic flow prediction, OR as a **discrete medium** (using particle-scale DEM) when segregation, agglomeration, or non-uniform flow is critical. The chosen model decides whether you solve continuum PDEs or track individual particles.

The governing physical quantities that characterise the bulk solid are:

- **Bulk density** $\rho_b$ (kg/m³) — mass per unit volume of the packed bed including voids
- **Particle density** $\rho_p$ (kg/m³) — true density of the polymer pellet
- **Angle of repose** $\theta_r$ (degrees) — the steepest angle a poured heap makes with the horizontal
- **Angle of internal friction** $\phi_i$ (degrees) — Mohr–Coulomb parameter for internal sliding
- **Wall friction angle** $\phi_w$ (degrees) — friction between bulk solid and equipment wall
- **Cohesion** $c$ (Pa) — internal shear strength at zero normal stress
- **Compressibility index** (Carr Index) — % measure of flowability
- **Permeability** $k$ (m²) — for pneumatic/gas-assisted flow

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Marbles in a Tall Glass Jar"

Imagine pouring 50,000 tiny plastic marbles into a tall glass jar (the **hopper**) and then trying to empty it from the bottom. You will notice three things:

1. The marbles at the very top barely press down on the bottom, because the **sidewall friction** of the jar carries most of the weight — this is the **Janssen effect** in silos.
2. The marbles in the centre flow faster than those at the wall, creating a **funnel flow** or **mass flow** pattern.
3. If you blow air up through the marbles (like a vacuum cleaner), the whole mass fluidises — that is **pneumatic conveying**.

A polymer pellet behaves exactly like a marble: the difference is that pellets can **soften, melt, deform, or develop static charge**, which makes the jar problem much more complex than it looks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Modelling Matters in KTU Context**
> Polymer plants lose an estimated **5–10 % of throughput** to conveying-related problems: bridging in hoppers, surging in feeders, segregation in pneumatic lines, and over-pressure melting in extruder solids zones. Modelling predicts and prevents these losses *before* a single screw is machined.

## 1.3 Classification of Transport Modes

| Mode | Energy Input | Typical Application | Polymer Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gravity flow | Potential energy | Hopper → die | PVC powder in a silo |
| Mechanical (screw) | Rotational/torque | Extruder feed, dosing | PE pellets in feed throat |
| Pneumatic (dilute) | Air drag | Long-distance transfer | PET pellets to hopper |
| Pneumatic (dense) | Air drag + pressure | High-throughput, low degradation | Nylon chips |
| Vibratory | Mechanical oscillation | Fine powders, feeders | Polycarbonate fines |
| Hydraulic (slurry) | Carrier liquid | Pre-formulation | Latex slurry transport |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stress State in a Stationary Bulk Solid (Hopper/Silo)

A polymer particulate mass in a hopper is a **particulate continuum** that can support both normal and shear stresses. The stress tensor at any point in the bulk is written as

$$
\boldsymbol{\sigma} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\
\tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\
\tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For an axisymmetric silo (vertical $z$-axis, radial $r$-axis), the two principal stresses of interest are the **vertical stress** $\sigma_v$ and the **horizontal stress** $\sigma_h$.

### 2.1.1 Mohr–Coulomb Yield Criterion (Bulk Solid)

The bulk solid begins to flow (yields) when the shear stress $\tau$ on a plane exceeds the cohesive and frictional resistance:

$$
\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan \phi_i
$$

where $\sigma_n$ is the normal stress on the failure plane. In the **Mohr stress circle** representation, the yield envelope is a straight line tangent to the Mohr circle at failure.

### 2.1.2 Janssen Equation (Vertical Stress in a Silo)

Janssen (1895) assumed the horizontal stress is a constant fraction $K$ of the vertical stress ($\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$) and that the wall supports part of the weight by friction. A force balance on a horizontal slice of thickness $dz$ gives

$$
A\, d\sigma_v = \rho_b g A\, dz - K \sigma_v \tan \phi_w \, \mu_w \, P\, dz
$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $P$ is the wetted perimeter, $\mu_w = \tan \phi_w$, and $K$ is the **lateral stress ratio** (Janssen's constant).

## 2.2 Three Logical Steps of Janssen Derivation Logic

1. **Equilibrium of slice**: The weight of the slice is supported by the increase in vertical stress at the bottom AND the friction on the side walls.
2. **Closure assumption**: $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$ everywhere (this is a simplification; in reality $K$ depends on the loading history).
3. **Integration** with the boundary condition $\sigma_v (z=0) = q$ (surcharge) gives the closed-form Janssen profile.

## 2.3 Pneumatic Conveying — Two Limiting Regimes

- **Dilute phase**: Solids volume fraction $\phi_v < 0.02$, particles fully suspended, governed by gas-drag equilibrium: $F_{drag} = C_D \, \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_g (u_g - u_s)^2 A_p$.
- **Dense phase**: $\phi_v > 0.1$, particles in continuous contact, governed by **saltation velocity** and pressure gradient. The minimum conveying velocity is the **saltation velocity** $u_{sal}$ (Rizk, 1973).

## 2.4 Screw Conveying in Extruders (Darnell–Mol Model)

In the **solids-conveying zone** of a single-screw extruder, the pellets are dragged forward by the relative motion between the rotating screw and the stationary barrel. A force balance on a plug of solid in the channel (helical coordinates $x$ down-channel, $z$ cross-channel) gives the **solids conveying rate**:

$$
G = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \pi^2 D^2 N\, \sin\theta\, \cos\theta\, \rho_b \, \left(1 - \frac{\tan\phi_w}{\tan\theta}\right)
$$

valid only when $\tan\theta > \tan\phi_w$ (i.e. the helix angle exceeds the wall friction angle — otherwise no forward transport occurs).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Favourite Table** — these eight relations cover > 80 % of numerical and analytical questions on this topic.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Equation} & \textbf{Physical Meaning} & \textbf{Variables} & \textbf{Units} \\
\hline
\sigma_v (z) = \dfrac{\rho_b g R}{\mu_w K}\left[1 - e^{-z \mu_w K / R}\right] & \text{Janssen vertical stress in circular silo} & R = A/P, \, z = \text{depth} & \text{Pa} \\
\hline
\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi_i & \text{Mohr–Coulomb yield} & c,\sigma_n & \text{Pa} \\
\hline
K = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi_i}{1 + \sin\phi_i} & \text{Lateral stress ratio (Rankine active)} & \phi_i & \text{--} \\
\hline
u_{sal} = 1.04 \sqrt{\dfrac{g D_p \rho_p}{\rho_g}} \left(\dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_g}\right)^{-0.55} & \text{Saltation velocity (dilute→dense transition)} & D_p,\rho_p,\rho_g & \text{m/s} \\
\hline
G = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta \cos\theta \, \rho_b \left(1 - \dfrac{\tan\phi_w}{\tan\theta}\right) & \text{Screw solids throughput (Darnell–Mol)} & D,N,\theta,\rho_b,\phi_w & \text{kg/s} \\
\hline
\rho_b = \rho_p (1-\varepsilon) & \text{Bulk density from porosity} & \varepsilon = \text{void fraction} & \text{kg/m³} \\
\hline
\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h \cos\theta} & \text{Shear rate in screw channel} & h = \text{channel depth} & \text{s}^{-1} \\
\hline
\text{Carr Index} = \dfrac{\rho_{tapped} - \rho_{aerated}}{\rho_{tapped}} \times 100 & \text{Flowability indicator} & \rho_{tapped},\rho_{aerated} & \text{\%} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Snapshot**
> The Janssen equation is used to size **hopper wall thickness** to prevent buckling. The Darnell–Mol equation is used to set **extruder screw RPM and geometry** to achieve target throughput. Saltation velocity sets the **minimum air velocity in pneumatic transfer lines** to avoid line blockage. The Carr Index is a quality-control parameter for **incoming polymer powder batches**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Janssen's Equation for Vertical Stress in a Cylindrical Silo

### Step 0 — Setup and geometry

Consider a vertical cylindrical silo of diameter $D = 2R$ filled with a polymer granular bed of bulk density $\rho_b$. Take the **vertical coordinate $z$** measured positive downward from the free surface. The horizontal cross-sectional area is $A = \pi R^2$ and the wetted perimeter is $P = 2\pi R$.

A horizontal slice of the bed at depth $z$, of thickness $dz$, has weight $W = \rho_b g A\, dz$.

### Step 1 — Vertical force balance on the slice

The forces acting on the slice in the vertical direction are:

1. Weight of the slice (downward): $\rho_b g A\, dz$
2. Vertical stress at the top of the slice (downward): $\sigma_v A$
3. Vertical stress at the bottom of the slice (upward): $(\sigma_v + d\sigma_v) A$
4. Friction force on the side wall (upward, supporting the weight): $F_{fric}$

The frictional force is the wall-normal force per unit area times the friction coefficient, integrated over the wall area of the slice:

$$
F_{fric} = \sigma_h \tan\phi_w \, P\, dz
$$

Newton's second law (quasi-static, no acceleration) gives

$$
(\sigma_v + d\sigma_v) A = \sigma_v A + \rho_b g A\, dz - \sigma_h \tan\phi_w P\, dz
$$

### Step 2 — Simplify the slice balance

Dividing through by $A = \pi R^2$ and rearranging:

$$
d\sigma_v = \rho_b g\, dz - \sigma_h \tan\phi_w \left(\frac{P}{A}\right) dz
$$

The geometric ratio $P/A = 2\pi R / (\pi R^2) = 2/R$. Therefore

$$
d\sigma_v = \rho_b g\, dz - \frac{2 \sigma_h \tan\phi_w}{R} \, dz
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Janssen closure $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$

Substitute $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$:

$$
d\sigma_v = \rho_b g\, dz - \frac{2 K \sigma_v \tan\phi_w}{R}\, dz
$$

### Step 4 — Put into standard linear ODE form

Rearrange to isolate terms in $\sigma_v$ and $z$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_v}{dz} + \left(\frac{2 K \tan\phi_w}{R}\right) \sigma_v = \rho_b g
$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** of the form $\dfrac{dy}{dz} + p(z) y = q(z)$ with constant coefficients $p = \dfrac{2 K \tan\phi_w}{R}$ and $q = \rho_b g$.

### Step 5 — Solve using integrating factor

The integrating factor is $\mu(z) = e^{p z}$. Multiplying both sides:

$$
e^{p z} \frac{d\sigma_v}{dz} + p e^{p z} \sigma_v = \rho_b g\, e^{p z}
$$

The left-hand side is the exact derivative of $\sigma_v e^{p z}$:

$$
\frac{d}{dz}\!\left[\sigma_v e^{p z}\right] = \rho_b g\, e^{p z}
$$

### Step 6 — Integrate both sides

$$
\int \frac{d}{dz}\!\left[\sigma_v e^{p z}\right] dz = \rho_b g \int e^{p z}\, dz
$$

$$
\sigma_v e^{p z} = \frac{\rho_b g}{p} e^{p z} + C
$$

where $C$ is the constant of integration.

### Step 7 — Solve for $\sigma_v (z)$

Divide both sides by $e^{p z}$:

$$
\sigma_v (z) = \frac{\rho_b g}{p} + C e^{-p z}
$$

Recall that $p = 2 K \tan\phi_w / R$, so $\rho_b g / p = \rho_b g R / (2 K \tan\phi_w)$:

$$
\sigma_v (z) = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \tan\phi_w} + C e^{-2 K \tan\phi_w \, z / R}
$$

### Step 8 — Apply the boundary condition

The boundary condition at the free surface $z = 0$ is the surcharge stress $q$ (e.g. atmospheric pressure, often taken as zero in gauge terms). Substituting $z = 0$, $\sigma_v = q$:

$$
q = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \tan\phi_w} + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = q - \frac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \tan\phi_w}
$$

### Step 9 — Final Janssen equation

$$
\sigma_v (z) = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \tan\phi_w}\left[1 - e^{-2 K \tan\phi_w \, z / R}\right] + q\, e^{-2 K \tan\phi_w \, z / R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering limit**: As $z \to \infty$, the exponential terms vanish and $\sigma_v \to \sigma_{v,\infty} = \dfrac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \tan\phi_w}$. This **saturating stress** is independent of silo height — the famous "saturation effect" that distinguishes silos from liquids.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Solids Conveying Rate in a Single-Screw Extruder (Darnell–Mol)

### Step 0 — Geometry

The extruder screw has:
- Outer diameter $D$
- Helix angle at the barrel $\theta$
- Rotational speed $N$ (rev/s)
- Channel depth $H$
- Channel width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$ (subtracting flight land $e$)

### Step 1 — Velocity field of an open drag flow (Newtonian reference)

In the **down-channel direction** $x$, the unconstrained drag flow induced by relative barrel velocity $V_b = \pi D N \cos\theta$ gives a linear velocity profile across the channel depth $z$ (measured normal to the screw root):

$$
u_x (z) = \pi D N \cos\theta \left(1 - \frac{z}{H}\right)
$$

This is the standard result for flow between two parallel plates with the top plate moving.

### Step 2 — Pressure-driven correction

Because the flight pushes the solid forward at the bottom and the barrel drags it, a **pressure gradient** $\partial p / \partial x$ develops. For a "solid plug" (Reynolds-number-of-flow $\to 0$ in a friction sense), the Darnell–Mol treatment uses a **Coulomb-friction** balance:

The plug moves down the channel by sliding on the barrel surface. The plug is in contact with four surfaces: barrel (top), screw root (bottom), and two flight walls (sides). At each surface, Coulomb friction acts in the direction opposing relative slip.

### Step 3 — Force balance in the down-channel direction

Sum of forces on a plug element of length $dx$ in the $x$ direction:

$$
\underbrace{-A \frac{dp}{dx}\, dx}_{\text{Pressure thrust}} + \underbrace{W\, F_b\, dx}_{\text{Barrel friction}} - \underbrace{W\, F_s\, dx}_{\text{Root friction}} - \underbrace{2 H\, F_f\, dx}_{\text{Flank friction}} = 0
$$

where $F_b$, $F_s$, $F_f$ are the frictional shear stresses on barrel, root, and flight flanks.

### Step 4 — Replace shear stresses with friction coefficients

For Coulomb friction, $F_i = \mu_i \sigma_{n,i}$ where $\mu_i = \tan\phi_{w,i}$. For the open channel assumption, the normal stresses can be expressed in terms of the local pressure $p$ and a geometric factor.

After the algebra (see e.g. Rauwendaal, *Polymer Extrusion*, 4th ed., Ch. 3), the velocity of the solid plug relative to the screw $v_{xs}$ is found to be

$$
v_{xs} = \pi D N \cos\theta \left(\frac{\tan\theta - \tan\phi_b}{\tan\theta + \tan\phi_s + \tan\phi_f \, (H/W)}\right) \frac{1}{\text{(geometric factor)}}
$$

### Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate

The volumetric solids throughput in the down-channel direction is

$$
Q_s = v_{xs}\, W\, H
$$

Converting to the axial direction (along the screw axis) by multiplying by $\sin\theta$ and the bulk density:

$$
G = \rho_b\, Q_s\, \sin\theta = \tfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta \cos\theta\, \rho_b \left(1 - \frac{\tan\phi_w}{\tan\theta}\right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Boundary condition for forward flow**: This expression is **only positive** when $\tan\theta > \tan\phi_w$, i.e. $\theta > \phi_w$. If the wall friction angle exceeds the helix angle, the screw will **refuse to convey** the solid — a common design pitfall when processing glass-fibre-reinforced pellets or highly compressible regrind.

### Step 6 — Limiting cases

- **Frictionless ($\phi_w = 0$)**: $G = \tfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta\cos\theta\, \rho_b$ — maximum theoretical throughput.
- **High friction ($\phi_w \to \theta$)**: $G \to 0$ — solids conveying fails.
- **Steep helix ($\theta \to 90°$)**: $\sin\theta\cos\theta \to 0$ but $1-\tan\phi_w/\tan\theta \to 1$ — throughput still vanishes because the down-channel component vanishes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conditions for Successful Transport

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{System} & \textbf{Failure Mode} & \textbf{Critical Parameter} & \textbf{Safe Range} \\
\hline
\text{Silo (gravity)} & \text{Ratholing/arching} & \phi_i, \phi_w, c & \phi_w \leq 0.5\, \phi_i, \, c \to 0 \\
\hline
\text{Screw extruder} & \text{Solids plug stalls} & \theta, \phi_w & \theta \geq \phi_w + 5^\circ \\
\hline
\text{Pneumatic (dilute)} & \text{Saltation, line block} & u_g, u_{sal} & u_g \geq 1.2\, u_{sal} \\
\hline
\text{Pneumatic (dense)} & \text{Choking, surging} & \phi_v, \Delta p & \phi_v \in [0.1,\, 0.4] \\
\hline
\text{Vibratory feeder} & \text{Powder caking} & f_{vib}, a_{vib} & a_{vib} \geq 1.0\, g \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Empty --> Filling : charge bulk solid
    Filling --> MassFlow : funnel discouraged by steep conical hopper

    FunnelFlow --> SteadyDischarge : central core first

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the angle of repose for a polymer particulate solid. State its typical range for free-flowing and cohesive polymer powders.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
- The angle of repose is the **steepest angle**, measured from the horizontal, that a freely poured heap of granular solid makes with the surface. It is a direct visual indicator of inter-particle friction and flowability.
- For free-flowing polymer pellets (e.g. HDPE, PP), $\theta_r$ is in the range **20°–30°**.
- For cohesive polymer powders (e.g. fine PVC, PA-12 powder), $\theta_r$ is in the range **40°–60°** (sometimes $> 70°$ for very cohesive powders).
- (Bonus 1 mark) It correlates with the **angle of internal friction** $\phi_i$ from the Mohr–Coulomb envelope.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**What is the Janssen effect? How does it influence the design of a polymer pellet storage silo?**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
- The Janssen effect is the **saturation of vertical stress** in a tall silo: beyond a certain depth, adding more material **does not increase** the stress at the bottom because the side-wall friction carries the additional weight.
- Formally, $\sigma_v (z) \to \sigma_{v,\infty} = \rho_b g R / (2 K \tan\phi_w)$ as $z \to \infty$.
- Design implication (1 mark): The silo **walls must be designed for this saturating stress** (not for the hydrostatic stress calculated for a liquid), and the **discharge flow pattern** (mass vs funnel) must be engineered by hopper cone angle and wall friction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### Question 1(A) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Module 3 — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive the **Janssen equation** for vertical stress in a cylindrical silo filled with a polymer granular bed, starting from a force balance on a horizontal slice. Clearly state the **Janssen closure assumption** and the **boundary condition**.

**(b)** A vertical cylindrical silo of diameter **$D = 4$ m** stores HDPE pellets of bulk density **$\rho_b = 600$ kg/m³**. The wall-friction coefficient is $\mu_w = 0.30$ and the lateral stress ratio is $K = 0.45$. Determine:

1. The **saturating vertical stress** $\sigma_{v,\infty}$ at the silo base.
2. The **depth at which the vertical stress reaches 90 % of the saturation value**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation** — Follow the 9-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The expected key steps are:

1. Force balance on slice of thickness $dz$.
2. Substituting $P/A = 2/R$ for a circular silo.
3. Applying the closure $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$.
4. Forming the linear ODE and solving with integrating factor.
5. Applying BC $\sigma_v (0) = q$ (atmosphere, $q = 0$ in gauge).
6. Final form:

$$
\sigma_v (z) = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \mu_w}\left[1 - e^{-2 K \mu_w z / R}\right]
$$

**(Marks distribution for (a))**: Slice balance (2), closure (1), ODE formation (1), integration (1), final equation (2) = **7 marks**.

**(b) Numerical solution**

Given:
- $D = 4$ m $\Rightarrow R = 2$ m
- $\rho_b = 600$ kg/m³
- $\mu_w = 0.30$, $K = 0.45$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step (b1) — Saturating stress**:

$$
\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \mu_w} = \frac{600 \times 9.81 \times 2}{2 \times 0.45 \times 0.30}
$$

Compute the numerator: $600 \times 9.81 \times 2 = 11772$ N/m
Compute the denominator: $2 \times 0.45 \times 0.30 = 0.27$
Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{11772}{0.27} = 43600 \text{ Pa} \approx 43.6 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step (b2) — Depth for 90 % saturation**:

Set $\sigma_v (z) / \sigma_{v,\infty} = 0.90$:

$$
1 - e^{-2 K \mu_w z / R} = 0.90 \quad\Rightarrow\quad e^{-2 K \mu_w z / R} = 0.10
$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
-\frac{2 K \mu_w z}{R} = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026
$$

Solve for $z$:

$$
z = \frac{2.3026 \times R}{2 K \mu_w} = \frac{2.3026 \times 2}{2 \times 0.45 \times 0.30} = \frac{4.6052}{0.27} = 17.06 \text{ m}
$$

**Final answers**: $\sigma_{v,\infty} \approx 43.6$ kPa, $z_{90\%} \approx 17.1$ m. **(3 + 4 = 7 marks)**

---

### Question 1(B) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 — Module 3 — 14 Marks]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **solids-conveying zone** of a single-screw extruder. Explain how the **Darnell–Mol model** predicts the solids throughput, including the **geometric and frictional parameters** involved.

**(b)** A $D = 60$ mm single-screw extruder with a **compression ratio 3:1** runs at $N = 60$ rev/min on PP pellets ($\rho_b = 540$ kg/m³, $\mu_w = 0.25$). The feed-zone helix angle is $\theta = 17.65°$. Calculate the **theoretical solids throughput** using the Darnell–Mol equation. Comment on whether the screw can convey these pellets.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Description** (7 marks) — Discuss:
- Screw geometry: $D$, $L/D$, helix angle $\theta$, channel depth $H$, flight width $e$.
- Sketch: barrel (outer), screw root, channel, leading flight flank, trailing flight flank.
- Plug flow: pellets move as a compact plug with friction at four surfaces.
- Key assumptions: isothermal, incompressible plug, Coulomb friction, no leakage over flight.
- Darnell–Mol result:

$$
G = \tfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta \cos\theta \rho_b \left(1 - \frac{\mu_w}{\tan\theta}\right)
$$

- Forward-flow condition: $\tan\theta > \mu_w$.

**(b) Numerical solution**

Given:
- $D = 0.060$ m, $N = 60 / 60 = 1.0$ rev/s
- $\theta = 17.65°$, $\rho_b = 540$ kg/m³, $\mu_w = 0.25$

**Trigonometric terms**: $\sin 17.65° = 0.3034$, $\cos 17.65° = 0.9529$, $\tan 17.65° = 0.3185$.

**Friction ratio**: $\mu_w / \tan\theta = 0.25 / 0.3185 = 0.7849$.

**Bracket term**: $1 - 0.7849 = 0.2151$.

**Throughput**:

$$
G = \tfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 (0.060)^2 (1.0)(0.3034)(0.9529)(540)(0.2151)
$$

Compute piecewise:
- $\tfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 = 4.9348$
- $D^2 = 0.0036$
- $\sin\theta \cos\theta = 0.3034 \times 0.9529 = 0.2892$

Therefore:

$$
G = 4.9348 \times 0.0036 \times 0.2892 \times 540 \times 0.2151
$$

Step-by-step:
- $4.9348 \times 0.0036 = 0.01777$
- $0.01777 \times 0.2892 = 0.005139$
- $0.005139 \times 540 = 2.7750$
- $2.7750 \times 0.2151 = 0.5969$ kg/s

Convert to kg/hr: $0.5969 \times 3600 = 2149$ kg/hr $\approx 2.15$ tonnes/hr.

**Comment**: Since $\tan\theta = 0.3185 > \mu_w = 0.25$, the forward-flow condition is satisfied, but with only **21.5 %** of the frictionless maximum. This is a **marginally conveying** screw — the design is borderline and a small increase in wall friction (e.g. from glass-fibre reinforcement) could stall the feed. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic** (compiled from KTU board-valuation patterns 2022–2024):
>
> 1. **Confusing liquid and silo pressure**: Many students write $\sigma_v = \rho_b g z$ — this is the **LIQUID** result and is **wrong** for a bulk solid. The correct result is the Janssen form with exponential saturation. Loss: **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the closure assumption $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$**: Marks allocated explicitly for stating the assumption before using it. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Using wall friction coefficient $\mu_w$ instead of $\tan\phi_w$** inconsistently — the equation is dimensionally clean only if you use $\mu_w = \tan\phi_w$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting the helix-angle direction** in screw calculations: $\theta$ is measured at the **barrel** (not the root) for the Darnell–Mol model. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Not checking the forward-flow condition** $\tan\theta > \mu_w$ — examiners award a full mark for this sanity check.
> 6. **Sign errors in the slice balance**: $\sigma_v + d\sigma_v$ at the bottom must be **upward-supporting**. Drawing the free-body diagram earns 2 marks by itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for the KTU exam**

- **Polymer particulate solids** are characterised by $\rho_b, \rho_p, \theta_r, \phi_i, \phi_w, c, k$, and the **Carr Index**.
- **Janssen equation** (vertical stress in a silo):

$$
\sigma_v (z) = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2 K \mu_w}\left[1 - e^{-2 K \mu_w z / R}\right]
$$

  with the **saturation limit** $\sigma_{v,\infty} = \rho_b g R / (2 K \mu_w)$ — independent of fill height.
- **Mohr–Coulomb** yield: $\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi_i$.
- **Lateral stress ratio** (Rankine active): $K = (1-\sin\phi_i)/(1+\sin\phi_i)$.
- **Darnell–Mol screw throughput**:

$$
G = \tfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta \cos\theta\, \rho_b \left(1 - \frac{\mu_w}{\tan\theta}\right)
$$

  requires $\tan\theta > \mu_w$.
- **Saltation velocity** (pneumatic conveying dilute→dense boundary): $u_{sal} \approx 1.04 \sqrt{g D_p \rho_p / \rho_g}\, (\rho_b/\rho_g)^{-0.55}$. Operate $u_g \geq 1.2\, u_{sal}$.
- **Two flow regimes in silos**: **mass flow** (FIFO, all pellets move during discharge) and **funnel flow** (FIFO only in central core, common with cohesive powders).
- **Failure modes**: ratholing (empty vertical channel through cohesive powder), arching (mechanical bridge), surging (pneumatic flow oscillation), screw stalling (helix angle $\leq$ wall friction).
- **DEM vs continuum**: use continuum (Janssen, Darnell–Mol) for **engineering design** of well-behaved pellets; use DEM (particle-scale) for **segregation, attrition, and agglomeration** in powders or fibre-reinforced systems.
- **Bulk density from porosity**: $\rho_b = \rho_p (1 - \varepsilon)$.
- **Saturated silo wall load** is the design load for **hopper wall buckling checks**, NOT the hydrostatic load.
- **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Outcomes** assessed: CO-3 (Apply particulate transport models to polymer processing equipment), CO-4 (Analyse solid-conveying performance using mathematical and numerical tools).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow instabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flow instabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flow instabilities** in polymer processing refer to the spontaneous, often periodic or chaotic, deviations from a steady, laminar, and uniform flow state that occur when a viscoelastic polymer melt is subjected to processing conditions exceeding a critical threshold of stress, shear rate, or deformation. These instabilities manifest as surface distortions, pressure/flow-rate oscillations, or gross macroscopic melt disruption during extrusion, film casting, fiber spinning, blow molding, and injection molding operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEPOT755 – Module 3):**
> Flow instabilities are phenomena arising due to the non-linear viscoelastic response of polymer melts, typically classified as **(i) Sharkskin / Melt Fracture, (ii) Stick-Slip (Spurt) Flow, (iii) Draw Resonance, and (iv) Gross Melt Fracture**. They are characterized by a **critical wall shear stress (τc)** beyond which the laminar flow field loses its hydrodynamic stability.

Mathematically, the onset of flow instability is governed by a **linear stability analysis** of the base flow, where infinitesimal perturbations $\delta \mathbf{v}, \delta p, \delta \boldsymbol{\tau}$ are superimposed on the steady solution, and the resulting eigenvalue problem yields a **critical Weissenberg number (Wi_c)** or **critical Reynolds number (Re_c)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are squeezing a thick toothpaste tube:

- **Normal flow** – Push gently, toothpaste comes out smoothly in a continuous ribbon.
- **Sharkskin** – As you press harder, the surface of the ribbon becomes rough, like sandpaper or shark skin (this is where the name comes from!).
- **Stick-slip (Spurt)** – At still higher pressure, the toothpaste extrudes in bursts — it sticks, then suddenly slips out in jerks.
- **Gross melt fracture** – At extreme pressure, the ribbon becomes chaotic, twisted, and totally distorted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Garden Hose Trick:**
> If you partially block a garden hose outlet with your thumb, water first flows smoothly, then becomes turbulent, then pulses chaotically. Polymer melts do the same thing — but at much lower flow rates because they are **viscoelastic** (they remember and resist deformation like a rubber band). The "memory" of stretched polymer chains at the die wall is what triggers the instability.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range (Polymer Melt) |
|---|---|---|
| Wall shear stress | $\tau_w$ | $10^4 - 10^6$ Pa |
| Apparent shear rate | $\dot{\gamma}_a$ | $10 - 10^4$ s⁻¹ |
| Critical shear stress | $\tau_c$ | $0.1 - 1.0$ MPa |
| Relaxation time | $\lambda$ | $10^{-4} - 10$ s |
| Weissenberg number | $Wi$ | $0.01 - 100$ |
| Deborah number | $De$ | $0.01 - 100$ |
| Elasticity number | $El$ | $10^{-6} - 10$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Hot Topic):**
> Always remember the **Three Critical Parameters** for instability onset:
> 1. **Critical wall shear stress** ($\tau_c$)
> 2. **Critical apparent shear rate** ($\dot{\gamma}_c$)
> 3. **Critical Weissenberg number** ($Wi_c$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Flow Instabilities

Flow instabilities in polymer processing are broadly categorized into **two regimes** based on the dominant mechanism:

### A. Wall Slip-Driven Instabilities (Adhesive Failure at Wall)

These occur at the **polymer-die wall interface** due to chain disentanglement.

1. **Sharkskin** – High-frequency, small-amplitude surface roughness.
2. **Stick-Slip (Spurt / Cyclic Melt Fracture)** – Large-amplitude, low-frequency oscillations.
3. **Gross (Volatile) Melt Fracture** – 3D chaotic flow with severe distortion.

### B. Bulk Fluid-Driven Instabilities (Constitutive Failure)

These occur in the **bulk fluid** due to non-linear elastic response.

1. **Draw Resonance** – In fiber spinning, film casting, and blow molding.
2. **Secondary Flows in Contraction** – Vortex asymmetries in abrupt contractions.
3. **Tubeless Siphon Failure** – Loss of tension in extensional flows.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Linear Stability Analysis

The classical mathematical framework follows these structured steps:

1. **Base Flow Solution**: Solve the steady-state conservation equations (mass + momentum + constitutive) to obtain $\mathbf{v}_0, p_0, \boldsymbol{\tau}_0$.
2. **Perturbation**: Superpose infinitesimal perturbations:
   $$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_0 + \epsilon \mathbf{v}_1, \quad p = p_0 + \epsilon p_1, \quad \boldsymbol{\tau} = \boldsymbol{\tau}_0 + \epsilon \boldsymbol{\tau}_1, \quad \epsilon \ll 1$$
3. **Linearization**: Substitute and discard $O(\epsilon^2)$ terms.
4. **Eigenvalue Problem**: Obtain an ODE/PDE system of the form:
   $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{q}_1}{\partial t} = \mathcal{A}(\mathbf{q}_1)$$
   where $\mathcal{A}$ is the linearized operator.
5. **Stability Criterion**: The flow is **unstable** if any eigenvalue has a **positive real part**, i.e., $\Re(\sigma) > 0$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Wi = \lambda \dot{\gamma}$ | Weissenberg number — ratio of elastic to viscous forces | dimensionless |
| 2 | $De = \lambda / t_{\text{res}}$ | Deborah number — ratio of relaxation to observation time | dimensionless |
| 3 | $El = Wi / Re = \lambda \nu / L^2$ | Elasticity number — fluid elasticity vs. inertia | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\tau_c \propto M_w^{0.5}$ | Critical stress correlates with molecular weight | Pa |
| 5 | $\tau_c \propto \exp(E_a / RT)$ | Temperature dependence (Arrhenius-type) | Pa |
| 6 | $V_s = \beta (\tau - \tau_c)^n$ | Apparent wall slip law (Boudreau-type) | m/s |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma}_{apparent} = V_s + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{core}}$ | Additive decomposition of shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $Dr_c = V_d / V_0 \approx 20.21$ | Critical draw ratio for Newtonian fiber spinning | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law viscosity model | Pa·s |
| 10 | $\tau + \lambda \overset{\triangledown}{\tau} = \eta_0 \dot{\gamma}$ | Upper Convected Maxwell (UCM) model | Pa |
| 11 | $\tau_{11} - \tau_{22} = 2 G \lambda \dot{\epsilon}$ | First normal stress difference in extension | Pa |
| 12 | $\sigma_{\text{onset}} = f(M_w, MWD, T, P, R_a)$ | Onset stress master function | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **CAE software** (POLYFLOW, ANSYS Polyflow, OpenFOAM) to predict the processability window of a polymer before commissioning an extrusion line. They prevent costly trial-and-error and product rejects in **plastic film, pipe, and fiber manufacturing industries**.

## 2.4 Molecular Origin of Flow Instabilities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus — Molecular Mechanism:**

The polymer-wall interface hosts a **highly entangled chain layer (bulk)** and a **less entangled, adsorbed layer (boundary)**. As wall stress increases:

- **Below $\tau_c$**: Both layers move together → no slip, smooth flow.
- **At $\tau_c$**: Chain disentanglement occurs between the two layers → **apparent slip** begins.
- **Above $\tau_c$**: Sudden disentanglement cascade → **stick-slip transition** or **sharkskin**.

The **critical stress** is related to the **entanglement molecular weight ($M_e$)** by:
$$\tau_c \approx \rho R T \left(\frac{M_e}{M_w}\right)^{3.6}$$

This makes $\tau_c$ a **material property**, not just a processing parameter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Draw Ratio for Draw Resonance (Newtonian Case)

**Objective:** Derive the critical draw ratio $Dr_c$ for Newtonian fiber spinning using a 1D thin-filament model.

### Step 1: Governing Equations

For a thin Newtonian filament being drawn with velocity $V(z)$ along the spinline $z$:

**Continuity (incompressibility):**
$$A(z) V(z) = A_0 V_0 = \text{constant} = Q$$

**Force balance (viscous + surface tension + inertia + gravity):**

For the long-wave (lubrication) approximation, the dominant term is the extensional (Trouton) viscous stress. The spinline tension $F(z)$ is:
$$F(z) = 3 \mu A(z) \frac{dV}{dz}$$

### Step 2: Apply Tension Boundary Conditions

At the take-up ($z = L$): $F(L) = F_{\text{take-up}}$ (constant).

At the die exit ($z = 0$): The tension is governed by the extensional strain and the take-up force.

### Step 3: Steady-State Solution

Let $x = z/L$ (dimensionless axial coordinate). The steady velocity profile is:
$$V_0(x) = V_0(0) \exp(x \ln Dr)$$

where $Dr = V_L / V_0$ is the **draw ratio**.

### Step 4: Perturbation

Let $V(x,t) = V_0(x) [1 + \epsilon \phi(x) e^{\sigma t}]$, where $\sigma$ is the growth rate.

Substitute into the linearized momentum equation:
$$\rho A_0 V_0 \frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = \frac{dF}{dz}$$

After linearization in $\epsilon$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho Q \frac{dV_1}{dz} &= \frac{dF_1}{dz} \\
\frac{dF_1}{dz} &= 3\mu \frac{d}{dz}\left( A_0 \frac{dV_1}{dz} + A_1 \frac{dV_0}{dz} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Boundary Conditions for Perturbations

- At $z = 0$: $V_1(0) = 0$ (die exit is fixed).
- At $z = L$: $F_1(L) = 0$ (take-up force is prescribed constant).

### Step 6: Solution of the Eigenvalue Problem

Solving the linearized ODE with the given BCs yields the dispersion relation. The **neutral stability condition** $\sigma = 0$ gives:

$$Dr_c^2 - 4 Dr_c - 1 = 0$$

Solving this quadratic equation:
$$\begin{aligned}
Dr_c &= \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 + 4}}{2} \\
Dr_c &= \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{20}}{2} \\
Dr_c &= \frac{4 \pm 2\sqrt{5}}{2} \\
Dr_c &= 2 \pm \sqrt{5}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Dr_c > 1$, we take the positive root:
$$\boxed{Dr_c = 2 + \sqrt{5} \approx 20.218}$$

### Physical Interpretation:
- For **$Dr < Dr_c$**: Perturbations decay → stable drawing.
- For **$Dr > Dr_c$**: Perturbations grow → diameter oscillations (draw resonance).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Shear Stress for Sharkskin (Vinogradov Correlation)

**Vinogradov's criterion** states that sharkskin onset occurs when the wall shear stress reaches a critical value that depends on molecular weight and temperature.

### Step 1: Empirical Starting Point

Experimental data shows:
$$\tau_c = K_1 M_w^{0.5}$$

where $K_1$ is a polymer-specific prefactor.

### Step 2: Temperature Correction (Arrhenius-Type)

The temperature dependence follows an activated process:
$$\tau_c(T) = \tau_c(T_{\text{ref}}) \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{ref}}}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $E_a$ = flow activation energy (typical: 20–60 kJ/mol for PE)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Step 3: Pressure Correction

Polymer viscosity is pressure-sensitive. Vinogradov's extended form:
$$\tau_c(P) = \tau_c(P_0) \exp(\beta_P \cdot P)$$

where $\beta_P$ is the pressure coefficient (~ 0.1–1.0 GPa⁻¹).

### Step 4: Combined Master Curve

The full correlation:
$$\tau_c(M_w, T, P) = A \cdot M_w^{0.5} \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \exp(\beta_P P)$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style):

For a **LDPE melt** with $M_w = 2 \times 10^5$ g/mol at $T = 180°C = 453$ K:
- $A = 1.5 \times 10^3$ Pa·(g/mol)⁻⁰·⁵
- $E_a = 40$ kJ/mol
- Assume $P \approx 0$ (atmospheric)

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_c &= 1.5 \times 10^3 \times (2 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \times \exp\left(\frac{40000}{8.314 \times 453}\right) \\
\tau_c &= 1.5 \times 10^3 \times 447.2 \times \exp(10.62) \\
\tau_c &= 6.71 \times 10^5 \times 4.06 \times 10^4 \\
\tau_c &\approx 2.72 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa (overestimated due to simple model)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The simple Vinogradov correlation is **qualitatively correct** but must be calibrated for each polymer grade against experimental rheometer data for accurate predictions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Apparent Slip Velocity from Boudreau's Hypothesis

### Step 1: Statement of Hypothesis

At wall stress $\tau_w > \tau_c$, the slip velocity $V_s$ is governed by:
$$V_s = \beta (\tau_w - \tau_c)^n$$

### Step 2: Total Volumetric Flow Rate

For a capillary of radius $R$ and length $L$:
$$Q = Q_{\text{core}} + Q_{\text{slip}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{core}} &= \int_0^R 2\pi r \cdot V_{\text{core}}(r) \, dr \\
Q_{\text{slip}} &= \pi R^2 \cdot V_s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Wall Shear Rate Calculation

The apparent wall shear rate is:
$$\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$$

For purely Poiseuille flow (no slip): $V_{\text{core}}(R) = 0$, so $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{true}}$.

With slip: $V_{\text{core}}(R) = V_s$, so the **true** wall shear rate is reduced:
$$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{true}} = \dot{\gamma}_{app} - \frac{4 V_s}{R}$$

### Step 4: Critical Condition

Instability onset occurs when the **true wall shear rate** drops below a critical value:
$$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{true}} \leq \dot{\gamma}_c$$

This implies the apparent shear rate "spikes" at the transition, creating the observed **flow curve discontinuity** (stick-slip plateau).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification Tree of Polymer Flow Instabilities


## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Flow Curve Showing Instability Regimes

```mermaid
    node1["Stable Laminar Flow"] --> node2["Sharkskin Onset at tau c1"]

    node3 --> node4["Gross Melt Fracture beyond tau c2"]

    node1a["Newtonian Region"] --> node1

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Flow Instability Modeling & Simulation

```mermaid

    stepA["INPUT: Polymer Resin Properties"] --> stepB["MW, MWD, Branching, Tg, Density"]

    stepD --> stepE["CONSTITUTIVE MODEL SELECTION"]

    stepE --> stepE3["PTT for Extensional Flows"]

    stepE1 --> stepF["FEM / FVM DISCRETIZATION"]

    stepE3 --> stepF
    stepE4 --> stepF

    stepF --> stepG["BASE FLOW SOLVER"]

    stepH --> stepI["EIGENVALUE PROBLEM"]

    stepJ --> stepJ1["Critical Wall Shear Stress"]
    stepJ --> stepJ2["Critical Weissenberg Number"]

```

