```mermaid
    nodeS1["Polymer Melt Approaches Die Exit"] --> nodeS2["Stress Concentration at Die Lip"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Boundary Layer Cannot Relax"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Periodic Rupture of Surface Layer"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Visible Sharkskin Pattern on Extrudate"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define flow instabilities in polymer processing. List the four major types with their typical critical shear stress range.**

**Model Answer:**

Flow instabilities are unwanted, time-dependent deviations from steady, uniform flow that occur when polymer melts are processed above a critical stress or shear rate threshold.

| Instability Type | Critical Stress Range |
|---|---|
| Sharkskin | $0.1 - 0.3$ MPa |
| Stick-slip (Spurt) | $0.2 - 0.5$ MPa |
| Gross melt fracture | $0.3 - 1.0$ MPa |
| Draw resonance | Controlled by $Dr_c \approx 20$ (Newtonian) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**State and explain Vinogradov's criterion for the onset of sharkskin.**

**Model Answer:**

Vinogradov's criterion states that sharkskin occurs when the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ exceeds a critical value $\tau_c$, given empirically by:
$$\tau_c = A M_w^{0.5} \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Physical meaning:** Chains at the die wall disentangle from the bulk melt, causing a periodic surface rupture as the melt exits the die lip.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** with internal choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, classify the major flow instabilities encountered in polymer extrusion. Discuss the molecular origin of stick-slip (spurt) flow. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (3 marks):**
Flow instabilities in polymer extrusion are classified as:

1. **Sharkskin** – Small-amplitude, high-frequency surface roughness; onset at $\tau_c \approx 0.1$ MPa.
2. **Stick-slip (Spurt)** – Cyclic oscillation between slip and no-slip states.
3. **Gross Melt Fracture** – Chaotic 3D distortion at high stresses.
4. **Draw Resonance** – Diameter oscillations during fiber/film drawing.

**Schematic (1 mark):** [Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2]

**Molecular Origin of Stick-Slip Flow (3 marks):**

The polymer-wall interface has two regions:
- **Adsorbed (boundary) layer**: Lower entanglement density, chains are "tied" to the wall.
- **Bulk layer**: Higher entanglement density.

Below $\tau_c$: Both layers move together — no slip.

At $\tau_c$: Sud disentanglement from boundary layer → high slip → polymer extrudes rapidly.

After slip: Pressure drops, chains re-entangle → flow stops (stick) → pressure builds again → cycle repeats.

This manifests as **periodic pressure and flow-rate oscillations** observed experimentally during capillary rheometry.

---

**(b)** Derive the critical draw ratio $Dr_c = 2 + \sqrt{5}$ for the onset of draw resonance in Newtonian fiber spinning using a 1D thin-filament model. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Governing equations (2 marks):**
- Continuity: $A(z) V(z) = Q = A_0 V_0$
- Force balance: $F(z) = 3\mu A(z) \dfrac{dV}{dz}$ (Newtonian Trouton viscosity)

**Steady-state solution (1 mark):**
$$V_0(x) = V_0 \exp(x \ln Dr), \quad x = z/L, \quad Dr = V_L/V_0$$

**Perturbation (1 mark):**
$$V(x,t) = V_0(x)\left[1 + \epsilon \phi(x) e^{\sigma t}\right]$$

**Linearization & BCs (1 mark):**
- $V_1(0) = 0$ (die exit fixed)
- $F_1(L) = 0$ (take-up force constant)

**Neutral stability condition $\sigma = 0$ (1 mark):**
$$Dr^2 - 4 Dr - 1 = 0$$

**Solution (1 mark):**
$$Dr = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16+4}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{20}}{2} = 2 \pm \sqrt{5}$$
$$\boxed{Dr_c = 2 + \sqrt{5} \approx 20.218}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of wall slip in polymer melts. Discuss how Boudreau's slip law modifies the apparent shear rate in capillary flow. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Wall Slip Concept (3 marks):**
Wall slip refers to the relative motion between the polymer melt and the die wall at stresses above the critical disentanglement stress $\tau_c$. Below $\tau_c$, no slip occurs (no-slip boundary condition holds). Above $\tau_c$, a finite slip velocity $V_s$ develops.

**Boudreau's Slip Law (2 marks):**
$$V_s = \beta (\tau_w - \tau_c)^n$$
where $\beta$ is the slip coefficient and $n \approx 1$ for linear polymers.

**Effect on Apparent Shear Rate (2 marks):**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{core}} + \pi R^2 V_s$$
$$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{true}} = \dot{\gamma}_{app} - \frac{4 V_s}{R}$$

This causes a **jump discontinuity** in the flow curve at $\tau_c$, explaining the observed **stick-slip plateau** in capillary rheometry.

---

**(b)** Describe the linear stability analysis procedure for predicting the onset of flow instabilities in viscoelastic fluids. List three dimensionless numbers used as stability criteria. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Linear Stability Analysis Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Step 1 – Base Flow**: Solve steady conservation equations:
   $$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}_0 = 0, \quad \rho (\mathbf{v}_0 \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{v}_0 = -\nabla p_0 + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau}_0$$

2. **Step 2 – Perturbation**: Superpose $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_0 + \epsilon \mathbf{v}_1$, etc., with $\epsilon \ll 1$.

3. **Step 3 – Linearization**: Substitute into governing equations, neglect $O(\epsilon^2)$ terms.

4. **Step 4 – Eigenvalue Problem**:
   $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{q}_1}{\partial t} = \mathcal{A} \mathbf{q}_1$$
   where $\mathbf{q}_1 = (\mathbf{v}_1, p_1, \boldsymbol{\tau}_1)^T$.

5. **Step 5 – Stability Criterion**: Flow is unstable if any eigenvalue $\sigma$ satisfies $\Re(\sigma) > 0$.

**Three Dimensionless Numbers (2 marks):**
- **Weissenberg Number**: $Wi = \lambda \dot{\gamma}$ — elastic vs. viscous forces.
- **Deborah Number**: $De = \lambda / t_{\text{res}}$ — solid-like vs. liquid-like behavior.
- **Elasticity Number**: $El = Wi / Re$ — elasticity vs. inertia.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing critical conditions** — Many students write $\tau_w = \tau_c$ as if it's the only criterion. Examiners expect discussion of **molecular weight, temperature, and pressure dependence** as well.
>
> 2. **Skipping derivations** — In 14-mark questions, simply stating $Dr_c \approx 20$ without derivation will cost 3–4 marks. Always show the **neutral stability condition** $Dr^2 - 4Dr - 1 = 0$.
>
> 3. **Mixing sharkskin with melt fracture** — These are **NOT** the same phenomenon. Sharkskin is a surface phenomenon; gross melt fracture is a bulk phenomenon. Examiners deduct marks if these are conflated.
>
> 4. **Missing units** — Always state SI units for $\tau, \dot{\gamma}, \eta$.
>
> 5. **Ignoring constitutive model selection** — When asked about modeling, students often forget to specify the **rheological model** (e.g., UCM, Giesekus, PTT).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Flow Instabilities**

### Key Definitions
- ☐ Flow instability = loss of steady laminar flow above critical stress/shear rate.
- ☐ Critical wall shear stress $\tau_c$ = threshold for onset.
- ☐ Apparent slip velocity $V_s$ = discontinuity at die wall.

### Four Major Instabilities
- ☐ **Sharkskin** — surface roughness, $\tau_c \approx 0.1$ MPa.
- ☐ **Stick-slip (Spurt)** — cyclic slip/stick oscillation.
- ☐ **Gross Melt Fracture** — chaotic 3D distortion.
- ☐ **Draw Resonance** — diameter oscillation, $Dr_c = 2 + \sqrt{5}$.

### Key Formulae to Memorize
- ☐ $Wi = \lambda \dot{\gamma}$
- ☐ $De = \lambda / t_{\text{res}}$
- ☐ $El = Wi / Re$
- ☐ $\tau_c = A M_w^{0.5} \exp(E_a/RT)$
- ☐ $V_s = \beta (\tau_w - \tau_c)^n$
- ☐ $Dr_c = 2 + \sqrt{5} \approx 20.21$
- ☐ $\tau_c \approx \rho R T (M_e / M_w)^{3.6}$

### Molecular Mechanism
- ☐ Chain disentanglement at polymer-wall interface.
- ☐ Entanglement molecular weight $M_e$ controls $\tau_c$.
- ☐ Higher $M_w$ → lower $\tau_c$ (easier to slip).

### Mitigation Strategies
- ☐ Lower molecular weight.
- ☐ Wider MWD with low-MW tail.
- ☐ Fluoropolymer processing aids (for sharkskin).
- ☐ Die lip coating.
- ☐ Spinline cooling (for draw resonance).
- ☐ Tapered die inlet (for contraction flows).

### Simulation Tools
- ☐ POLYFLOW, ANSYS Polyflow — FEM for viscoelastic flow.
- ☐ Linear stability analysis modules.
- ☐ Master curve + Cox-Merz rule for property input.

### KTU Hot Questions
- ☐ "Derive $Dr_c$" — 7 marks question almost guaranteed.
- ☐ "Vinogradov's criterion" — short answer.
- ☐ "Wall slip molecular mechanism" — 7 marks.

> **Final Tip:** For KTU PEPOT755 Module 3, the **draw resonance derivation** is the most frequently asked question. Practice the derivation of $Dr_c = 2 + \sqrt{5}$ thoroughly — it's worth 7 marks every cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical displacements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical displacements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: MECHANICAL DISPLACEMENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of polymer materials modelling and simulation, **Mechanical Displacement** is formally defined as the vectorial change in position of a material point (or atom/molecule) within a polymer body, measured relative to a chosen reference configuration. Mathematically, it is represented as a vector field $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{X}, t)$ that maps every material point in the undeformed (reference) configuration $\mathbf{X}$ to its new position $\mathbf{x}$ in the deformed (current) configuration at time $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Mechanical displacement describes the spatial translation of mass points in a polymer continuum (macro-scale) or of atoms/repeat units along a polymer chain (molecular scale) under the action of external forces, thermal fields, or imposed constraints, serving as the primary kinematic variable from which strain, stress, and constitutive response are derived."*

The relationship between the reference position $\mathbf{X}$ and current position $\mathbf{x}$ is given by the fundamental kinematic mapping:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{u}$$

This single vector equation is the **cornerstone of continuum mechanics** and **molecular dynamics of polymers** alike.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Stretched Rubber Band**
>
> Imagine a rubber band (a polymer network) lying on a table. You mark a tiny dot on it with a pen. Now stretch the rubber band.
>
> - The **dot** represents a *material point* (or in molecular terms, a *monomer unit*).
> - The **distance the dot has moved** from its original location is the *mechanical displacement*.
> - If you place a coordinate grid (like graph paper) under the rubber band, the displacement of the dot has a **direction** and a **magnitude** — making it a **vector**.
> - At the molecular level, the dot represents an *atom on a polymer chain* that moves slightly as the chain stretches, rotates, and re-arranges.
> - The **entire pattern of all dots moving** is called the **displacement field** $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{X}, t)$.

In a polymer under load:
- **Macroscale:** The whole specimen translates, stretches, and shears.
- **Microscale:** Individual chain segments uncoil, rotate around bonds, and slide past each other.
- **Nanoscale:** Atoms vibrate and shift from their equilibrium lattice/chain positions.

All these phenomena are unified mathematically by the **displacement field concept**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Units (SI):** Displacement is measured in **metres (m)**, typically in **millimetres (mm)** or **micrometres (µm)** for engineering polymers, and in **ångströms (Å = 10⁻¹⁰ m)** or **nanometres (nm)** for molecular-scale displacements.
- **Reference configuration:** Usually the *stress-free, undeformed, initial state* at temperature $T_0$ and time $t_0$.
- **Degrees of freedom (DOF):** In 3D, each point has **3 translational DOF** $(u_x, u_y, u_z)$. In 2D plane problems, 2 DOF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:**
> Examiners frequently test whether students can distinguish between **displacement** (vector), **displacement gradient** (second-order tensor), and **strain** (a derived symmetric tensor). Memorize the hierarchy:
> $\mathbf{u}$ (1st order) → $\nabla \mathbf{u}$ (2nd order) → $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ (2nd order, symmetric, derived).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Displacement Concept

The mechanical displacement in a polymer system can be classified along **three independent axes**:

### A. By Scale of Observation

| Scale | Description | Typical Magnitude | Governing Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Macroscale** | Bulk deformation of a polymer part | mm – m | Continuum Mechanics |
| **Mesoscale** | Polymer network / entanglement motion | nm – µm | Coarse-Grained MD |
| **Microscale** | Atomic motion along a chain | Å – nm | Molecular Dynamics (MD) |
| **Nanoscale** | Vibrational motion of atoms | pm – Å | Quantum / Lattice Dynamics |

### B. By Type of Motion

1. **Translational Displacement** — Pure shift of the body without rotation.
2. **Rotational Displacement** — Reorientation of chain segments (very important in polymers due to backbone bond rotations).
3. **Affine Displacement** — Displacement in which every material point moves proportionally to the macroscopic strain (assumption valid only for lightly crosslinked networks).
4. **Non-Affine Displacement** — Local deviations from the affine assumption, dominant in highly entangled or glassy polymers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Affine vs Non-Affine in Polymers:**
> In an ideal rubber (lightly crosslinked, Gaussian chains), the **affine network model** assumes junction points move exactly as the macroscopic strain dictates. In real polymer glasses, melts, and highly entangled systems, **non-affine displacements** are significant and are a hot research topic in molecular simulation.

### C. By Mathematical Order

- **Infinitesimal (small) displacement:** $\vert \nabla \mathbf{u} \vert \ll 1$ (engineering strains < 0.1%)
- **Finite (large) displacement:** $\vert \nabla \mathbf{u} \vert$ can approach unity (elastomers, rubbers — central to polymer mechanics!)

## 2.2 The Displacement Gradient Tensor

The **displacement gradient** $\mathbf{F}$ (also called the **deformation gradient**) is the fundamental kinematic quantity:

$$\mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{x}}{\partial \mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{I} + \nabla_{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{u}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{I}$ is the **identity tensor** (Kronecker delta $\delta_{ij}$)
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{u}$ is the spatial gradient of the displacement field with respect to the reference configuration

In component form (index notation, with Einstein summation):

$$F_{ij} = \frac{\partial x_i}{\partial X_j} = \delta_{ij} + \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial X_j}$$

## 2.3 Strain Tensors Derived from Displacement

### 2.3.1 Infinitesimal Strain Tensor (Engineering / Small Strain)

For small displacements and rotations, the linearized strain tensor is:

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial X_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial X_i}\right)$$

This is the **most commonly used form in KTU board exams** for introductory problems.

### 2.3.2 Finite Strain (Green-Lagrange) Tensor

For polymers undergoing large deformations (rubbers, elastomers, biopolymers):

$$E_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial X_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial X_i} + \frac{\partial u_k}{\partial X_i}\frac{\partial u_k}{\partial X_j}\right)$$

The non-linear (quadratic) term $\frac{\partial u_k}{\partial X_i}\frac{\partial u_k}{\partial X_j}$ is what makes polymer mechanics of elastomers fundamentally different from metals.

### 2.3.3 Almansi (Eulerian) Strain Tensor

$$e_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} - \frac{\partial u_k}{\partial x_i}\frac{\partial u_k}{\partial x_j}\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU board exam preparation. Memorize every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula (Tensor) | Formula (Components) | Units | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position mapping | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{u}$ | $x_i = X_i + u_i$ | m | All |
| 2 | Deformation gradient | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{I} + \nabla_{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{u}$ | $F_{ij} = \delta_{ij} + u_{i,j}$ | — | All |
| 3 | Jacobian (volume change) | $J = \det(\mathbf{F})$ | $J = \det(F_{ij})$ | — | All |
| 4 | Small strain tensor | $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla \mathbf{u} + \nabla \mathbf{u}^{T})$ | $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$ | — | $\vert \nabla \mathbf{u} \vert \ll 1$ |
| 5 | Volumetric strain | $\varepsilon_v = \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}$ | $\varepsilon_v = u_{i,i}$ | — | Small strain |
| 6 | Green-Lagrange strain | $\mathbf{E} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{F} - \mathbf{I})$ | $E_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i} + u_{k,i} u_{k,j})$ | — | Finite strain |
| 7 | Almansi strain | $\mathbf{e} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{F}^{-T}\mathbf{F}^{-1})$ | $e_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i} - u_{k,i} u_{k,j})$ | — | Finite strain |
| 8 | Rotation tensor (polar) | $\mathbf{R}$ from $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{U}$ | — | — | Finite |
| 9 | Right stretch tensor | $\mathbf{U} = \sqrt{\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{F}}$ | — | — | Finite |
| 10 | Displacement BC (Dirichlet) | $\mathbf{u} = \bar{\mathbf{u}}$ on $\partial \Omega_u$ | — | m | All FEM problems |
| 11 | Compatibility (small strain) | $\nabla \times \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \times \nabla = 0$ | $\varepsilon_{ij,kl} + \varepsilon_{kl,ij} - \varepsilon_{ik,jl} - \varepsilon_{jl,ik} = 0$ | — | Single-valued $\mathbf{u}$ |

> **Note on table syntax:** Absolute values and tensor magnitudes in formulas above are written using parentheses or descriptive text to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your answer scripts, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert$ as appropriate.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mechanical displacement analysis is the **starting point** for virtually every polymer engineering calculation:

1. **FEM of polymer components** — Nodal displacements are the primary unknowns; stresses and strains are derived from them.
2. **Viscoelastic creep analysis** — Time-dependent displacement under constant load in polymers.
3. **Rubber elasticity** — Hyperelastic constitutive laws (Mooney-Rivlin, Ogden, Neo-Hookean) are functions of the deformation gradient $\mathbf{F}$.
4. **Mold-filling simulation** — Displacement of polymer melt fronts during injection molding.
5. **Smart polymers / Shape Memory Polymers (SMPs)** — Displacement recovery upon thermal stimulus.
6. **Molecular dynamics** — Atomic displacements from equilibrium determine vibrational spectra, diffusion, and glass transition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Infinitesimal Strain Tensor from Displacement

We begin with the most fundamental derivation in polymer continuum mechanics.

**Statement:** For a polymer body undergoing small deformations, derive the symmetric strain tensor from the displacement field $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{X})$.

**Step 1 — Define the change in a differential line element.**

Consider two neighbouring material points in the reference configuration, separated by an infinitesimal vector $d\mathbf{X}$. After deformation, they are separated by $d\mathbf{x}$. The mapping is:

$$d\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{X} = (\mathbf{I} + \nabla \mathbf{u}) \cdot d\mathbf{X}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the change in the squared length of the line element.**

The original squared length is $dL_0^{2} = d\mathbf{X} \cdot d\mathbf{X}$. The deformed squared length is:

$$dL^{2} = d\mathbf{x} \cdot d\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{X}) \cdot (\mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{X})$$

**Step 3 — Expand using index notation.**

$$dL^{2} = F_{ik} F_{il} \, dX_k \, dX_l$$

Substituting $F_{ij} = \delta_{ij} + u_{i,j}$:

$$F_{ik} F_{il} = (\delta_{ik} + u_{i,k})(\delta_{il} + u_{i,l}) = \delta_{kl} + u_{k,l} + u_{l,k} + u_{i,k} u_{i,l}$$

Therefore:

$$dL^{2} = (\delta_{kl} + u_{k,l} + u_{l,k} + u_{i,k} u_{i,l}) \, dX_k \, dX_l$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference $dL^2 - dL_0^2$.**

$$dL^{2} - dL_0^{2} = (u_{k,l} + u_{l,k} + u_{i,k} u_{i,l}) \, dX_k \, dX_l$$

**Step 5 — Define the Green-Lagrange strain tensor.**

By definition, $dL^2 - dL_0^2 = 2 E_{kl} \, dX_k \, dX_l$. Comparing with Step 4:

$$E_{kl} = \frac{1}{2}\left(u_{k,l} + u_{l,k} + u_{i,k} u_{i,l}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the small-displacement approximation.**

For $\vert u_{i,j} \vert \ll 1$ (typical for rigid or glassy polymers below yield), the quadratic product $u_{i,k} u_{i,l}$ is negligible (order $10^{-4}$ or smaller). Dropping this term:

$$\varepsilon_{kl} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{k,l} + u_{l,k})$$

This is the **linearized (infinitesimal) strain tensor** widely used for stiff polymers and small-strain FEM analysis.

> **Physical Interpretation:** The strain tensor $\varepsilon_{kl}$ is symmetric because any antisymmetric part corresponds to **rigid-body rotation**, not stretching of the polymer. Since polymers in their glassy state undergo negligible rotation at small strains, the symmetric part alone captures all shape change.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Displacement Boundary Conditions in FEM for Polymers

**Statement:** Show that specifying displacement on a boundary segment is sufficient to constrain the solution uniquely (Dirichlet problem).

**Step 1 — Strong form of equilibrium.**

For a polymer body occupying domain $\Omega$ with boundary $\partial\Omega$:

$$\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} + \mathbf{b} = \rho \ddot{\mathbf{u}} \quad \text{in } \Omega$$

where $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is the Cauchy stress tensor, $\mathbf{b}$ is the body force per unit volume, and $\rho$ is the polymer density.

**Step 2 — Constitutive relation (linear elastic polymer).**

$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \mathbb{C} : \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \mathbb{C} : \left[\tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla \mathbf{u} + \nabla \mathbf{u}^{T})\right]$$

where $\mathbb{C}$ is the fourth-order stiffness tensor (with 21 independent components in the most general anisotropic case; for isotropic polymers, 2: Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$).

**Step 3 — Combine to get Navier-Cauchy equation.**

$$\nabla \cdot \left\{\mathbb{C} : \left[\tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla \mathbf{u} + \nabla \mathbf{u}^{T})\right]\right\} + \mathbf{b} = \rho \ddot{\mathbf{u}}$$

For isotropic polymers, this simplifies to:

$$\mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{u} + (\lambda + \mu) \nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}) + \mathbf{b} = \rho \ddot{\mathbf{u}}$$

where $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are the Lamé constants, related to $E$ and $\nu$ by:

$$\lambda = \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}, \qquad \mu = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

**Step 4 — Specify boundary conditions.**

The boundary $\partial\Omega$ is split into two non-overlapping parts: $\partial\Omega_u$ (prescribed displacement) and $\partial\Omega_t$ (prescribed traction):

$$\mathbf{u} = \bar{\mathbf{u}} \quad \text{on } \partial\Omega_u \quad \text{(Dirichlet / Essential BC)}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \bar{\mathbf{t}} \quad \text{on } \partial\Omega_t \quad \text{(Neumann / Natural BC)}$$

**Step 5 — Uniqueness.**

The combination of the PDE (Navier-Cauchy) + Dirichlet BCs on $\partial\Omega_u$ (with $\partial\Omega_u$ non-empty) + Neumann BCs on the remainder yields a **well-posed problem** with a unique displacement solution.

> **Polymer Application:** In injection molding simulation, the mold walls impose $\mathbf{u} = 0$ (no-slip condition) on the molten polymer. This is a Dirichlet BC that drives the entire flow solution.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Non-Affine Displacement in a Polymer Network

**Statement:** Quantify the deviation of a polymer chain junction from its affine-predicted position.

**Step 1 — Affine prediction.**

If the macroscopic deformation gradient is $\mathbf{F}$, the affine model predicts the new position of a junction $i$ as:

$$\mathbf{R}_i^{\text{affine}} = \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{R}_i^0$$

**Step 2 — Actual position from simulation.**

In a molecular dynamics simulation, the actual position of junction $i$ is $\mathbf{R}_i^{\text{actual}}$.

**Step 3 — Non-affine displacement.**

$$\delta \mathbf{R}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{\text{actual}} - \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{R}_i^0$$

**Step 4 — Non-affine parameter (quantitative measure).**

A common scalar measure used in polymer physics is:

$$\mathcal{A} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\vert \delta \mathbf{R}_i \vert^{2}}{\vert \mathbf{R}_i^{0} \vert^{2}}$$

where $N$ is the number of junctions. $\mathcal{A} \to 0$ for affine networks; $\mathcal{A} \gg 0$ indicates strong non-affinity (typical of glassy and densely entangled polymers).

**Step 5 — Connection to mechanical response.**

The non-affinity directly influences the **shear modulus** of the polymer. According to the **non-affine network theory of Rubinstein and Colby**, the actual shear modulus is reduced from the affine prediction:

$$G_{\text{actual}} = G_{\text{affine}} - \frac{2}{\pi} \kappa$$

where $\kappa$ is a non-affine correction proportional to the chain fluctuation amplitude. This explains why real polymer networks are **softer** than the classical affine model predicts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kinematic Mapping — Reference to Current Configuration

The following diagram illustrates how a polymer material point $\mathbf{X}$ in the undeformed state moves to position $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{u}$ in the deformed state, including the displacement gradient $\mathbf{F}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Reference Config X t0"] --> nodeB["Apply Load / Stretch / Heat"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Displacement Field u X t"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Current Config x t"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute Strain eps"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute Stress sigma via Constitutive Law"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Polymer Response Prediction"]
    nodeA -. Identity mapping .-> nodeD
    style nodeA fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    style nodeB fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309
    style nodeC fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D
    style nodeD fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D
    style nodeE fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#6D28D9
    style nodeF fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#BE123C
    style nodeG fill:#F0FDF4,stroke:#166534
```

## 4.2 Affine vs Non-Affine Displacement in a Polymer Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Crosslinked Polymer Network under Shear"]
    nodeStart --> nodeAffine["Affine Model Assumption"]
    nodeStart --> nodeActual["Actual MD Simulation"]
    nodeAffine --> nodePred["Junction positions follow F times R0 exactly"]
    nodeActual --> nodeReal["Junctions fluctuate around affine prediction"]
    nodePred --> nodeDiff["Non-affine displacement deltaR = 0"]
    nodeReal --> nodeDiff2["Non-affine displacement deltaR nonzero"]
    nodeDiff --> nodeGAffine["Predicted shear modulus G affine"]
    nodeDiff2 --> nodeGActual["Actual shear modulus G actual less than G affine"]
    style nodeStart fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E3A8A
    style nodeGAffine fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B
    style nodeGActual fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#14532D
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Polymer FEM Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput["Input Geometry Mesh Polymer Part"]
    nodeMat["Material Model E nu or Hyperelastic Law"]

    nodeStress["Compute Stress sigma from eps and Material Law"]
```

## 4.4 Displacement Decomposition — Symmetric and Antisymmetric Parts

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeGrad --> nodeSym["Symmetric Part eps = half of del u plus del u transpose"]
    nodeGrad --> nodeAnti["Antisymmetric Part omega = half of del u minus del u transpose"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Define mechanical displacement in the context of polymer materials. Write the kinematic mapping equation and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mechanical displacement $\mathbf{u}$ is the vector that describes the change in position of a material point (or atom) in a polymer body from its reference configuration to its current configuration under the action of mechanical, thermal, or chemical stimuli.

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{u}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{X}$ = position vector in the **undeformed / reference configuration** (initial state at $t = 0$)
- $\mathbf{x}$ = position vector in the **deformed / current configuration** (at time $t$)
- $\mathbf{u}$ = **displacement vector field** $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{X}, t)$

In polymers, this concept applies at all scales — from macroscopic stretching of a rubber specimen to atomic-level motion along a polymer chain backbone. The displacement field is the **primary unknown** in finite element analysis of polymers and the **starting point** for deriving strain, stress, and constitutive response. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between affine and non-affine displacement in a polymer network. Give one example where non-affinity is significant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Affine Displacement | Non-Affine Displacement |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Every junction moves in exact proportion to macroscopic strain | Junctions exhibit local deviations from the macroscopic strain pattern |
| Assumption | Crosslinks are firmly embedded in the network; no thermal fluctuation | Junctions can fluctuate, slip, and rearrange locally |
| Material class | Lightly crosslinked rubber (ideal Gaussian network) | Glassy polymers, densely entangled melts, filled elastomers |
| Quantitative measure | Non-affine parameter $\mathcal{A} \approx 0$ | $\mathcal{A} \gg 0$ |

**Example:** In a **polystyrene glass below $T_g$**, junction points cannot follow the macroscopic strain affinely due to frozen-in topological constraints, leading to significant non-affine displacements. This is observed in molecular dynamics simulations as a non-zero $\delta \mathbf{R}_i$ for each crosslink. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice 1]`

**(a)** Starting from the kinematic mapping $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{u}$, derive the **infinitesimal strain tensor** for a polymer body undergoing small deformations. Clearly state the assumption used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polyethylene tensile specimen has length $L_0 = 100$ mm. Under a tensile load, the displacement field is one-dimensional: $u_x(X) = 0.001 \, X$ (in mm), where $X$ is measured from the fixed end. Calculate (i) the engineering strain, (ii) the deformed length, and (iii) the volumetric strain (assuming lateral contraction with Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.35$). (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Map the reference position to the current position: $x_i = X_i + u_i$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** The deformation gradient is $F_{ij} = \partial x_i / \partial X_j = \delta_{ij} + \partial u_i / \partial X_j$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** The squared length of a deformed line element is:

$$dL^{2} = F_{ik} F_{il} \, dX_k \, dX_l = (\delta_{ik} + u_{i,k})(\delta_{il} + u_{i,l}) \, dX_k \, dX_l$$

Expanding: $dL^2 = (\delta_{kl} + u_{k,l} + u_{l,k} + u_{i,k} u_{i,l}) \, dX_k \, dX_l$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Subtract the reference length: $dL^2 - dL_0^2 = 2 E_{kl} \, dX_k \, dX_l$, where $E_{kl}$ is the Green-Lagrange strain tensor. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Apply the **small-displacement assumption** $\vert u_{i,j} \vert \ll 1$ to neglect the quadratic term $u_{i,k} u_{i,l}$. This yields: **(1 mark)**

$$\varepsilon_{kl} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_k}{\partial X_l} + \frac{\partial u_l}{\partial X_k}\right)$$

This symmetric tensor is the **infinitesimal (Cauchy) strain tensor**, the standard kinematic measure for stiff polymers like PE, PMMA, and PS below yield. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Identify the displacement gradient: $du_x / dX = 0.001$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** The only non-zero strain component is: $\varepsilon_{xx} = du_x / dX = 0.001 = 0.1\%$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Deformed length: $L = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{xx}) = 100 \times 1.001 = 100.1$ mm. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Lateral strain: $\varepsilon_{yy} = \varepsilon_{zz} = -\nu \varepsilon_{xx} = -0.35 \times 0.001 = -3.5 \times 10^{-4}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Volumetric strain: $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = 0.001 - 2(3.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.03\%$. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **concept of non-affine displacement** in a polymer network. Derive an expression for the **non-affine parameter** $\mathcal{A}$ and discuss its physical significance in determining the actual shear modulus of the polymer. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a 2D plane-stress problem in a polymer sheet, the displacement field is given by:
$$u_x(x, y) = a \cdot x y, \quad u_y(x, y) = b \cdot (x^2 - y^2)/2$$
where $a$ and $b$ are small constants. Compute the strain tensor $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ and identify the type of deformation represented. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Define affine prediction: $\mathbf{R}_i^{\text{affine}} = \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{R}_i^0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Define the non-affine deviation: $\delta \mathbf{R}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{\text{actual}} - \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{R}_i^0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Derive the non-affine parameter (squared fluctuation normalized): **(2 marks)**

$$\mathcal{A} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\vert \delta \mathbf{R}_i \vert^{2}}{\vert \mathbf{R}_i^{0} \vert^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** State the physical significance: $\mathcal{A} \to 0$ for affine networks; $\mathcal{A} \gg 0$ for glassy / highly entangled polymers. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** State the modulus correction: $G_{\text{actual}} = G_{\text{affine}} - (2/\pi) \kappa$, where $\kappa$ is a non-affine correction term, showing the polymer is **softer** than the affine prediction. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Compute the displacement gradients:
- $\partial u_x / \partial x = ay$, $\partial u_x / \partial y = ax$
- $\partial u_y / \partial x = bx$, $\partial u_y / \partial y = -by$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply the infinitesimal strain formula $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$: **(2 marks)**

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u_x}{\partial x} = ay$$
$$\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial u_y}{\partial y} = -by$$
$$\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial u_y}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(ax + bx) = \frac{(a+b)x}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Write the strain tensor matrix: **(1 mark)**

$$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{pmatrix} ay & (a+b)x/2 \\ (a+b)x/2 & -by \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the deformation: This is a **combination of normal straining** ($ay$ in x-direction, $-by$ in y-direction) and **simple shear** (the off-diagonal term). Specifically, if $a = b$, this represents **pure shear**, a key deformation mode in polymer processing such as blow molding and film extrusion. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5:** Verification — trace gives volumetric strain: $\varepsilon_v = ay - by = (a-b)y$, which is non-zero unless $a = b$ (incompressible pure shear). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the strain-displacement relation. Writing $\varepsilon_{ij} = \partial u_i / \partial X_j$ instead of $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$. **−2 marks minimum.**
>
> 2. **Confusing displacement vector with displacement gradient.** The displacement is a **vector** (1st-order tensor); the gradient is a **2nd-order tensor**. Examiners will deduct marks for misuse.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Lagrangian ($X$) and Eulerian ($x$) coordinates.** Strain definitions differ. For a polymer specimen with finite deformation, the choice of reference matters. Specify your configuration clearly.
>
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers. Displacement must be in **m** (or submultiples), strain is **dimensionless**. Showing units earns full marks.
>
> 5. **Not stating the small-strain assumption** when using the linear strain tensor. Always write: "Assuming $\vert \nabla \mathbf{u} \vert \ll 1$..."
>
> 6. **Treating non-affine displacement as a small correction.** In polymer physics, it can be 30–50% of the affine value. Emphasize its physical importance in the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Mechanical Displacements:**

- [x] **Displacement vector** $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{X}$ is the primary kinematic variable in all polymer mechanics.
- [x] **Kinematic mapping** $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{u}$ is the starting point of every continuum derivation.
- [x] **Deformation gradient** $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{I} + \nabla \mathbf{u}$ is a 2nd-order tensor that contains all kinematic information.
- [x] **Infinitesimal strain tensor** $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$ is **symmetric** and used for stiff polymers at small strains.
- [x] **Green-Lagrange strain** includes a quadratic term and is **mandatory** for elastomers and rubbers (large deformations).
- [x] **Affine displacement** = junction follows macroscopic strain exactly (ideal rubber assumption).
- [x] **Non-affine displacement** = local fluctuations around the affine prediction (real polymer behaviour).
- [x] **Non-affine parameter** $\mathcal{A}$ quantifies the deviation; $\mathcal{A} \to 0$ for affine, $\mathcal{A} \gg 0$ for glassy/entangled polymers.
- [x] **Dirichlet BC** $\mathbf{u} = \bar{\mathbf{u}}$ on $\partial\Omega_u$ — prescribed displacement (essential).
- [x] **Neumann BC** $\boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \bar{\mathbf{t}}$ on $\partial\Omega_t$ — prescribed traction (natural).
- [x] **Symmetric part of $\nabla \mathbf{u}$** = strain; **antisymmetric part** = rigid-body rotation.
- [x] **Compatibility condition** ensures the strain field is derivable from a single-valued continuous displacement field.
- [x] In **molecular dynamics** of polymers, displacement of atoms is measured in **ångströms** and tracked via the mean square displacement $\langle \vert \mathbf{u}(t) \vert^2 \rangle$.
- [x] The **deformation gradient** determines the chain end-to-end vector update in affine network models: $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{R}_0$.

> **Memory Aid (Mnemonic):** *"D-F-S-R"* — **D**isplacement → **F** (deformation gradient) → **S**train → **R**otation. This is the kinematic chain that every polymer mechanics problem follows.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mixing mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In polymer processing, **mixing** is defined as an operation that reduces the non-uniformity of composition, temperature, or morphology of a polymer system by intermingling its constituent components to achieve a desired level of homogeneity. From a modelling and simulation standpoint, mixing is treated as a **stochastic, multi-scale transport phenomenon** that combines **deterministic fluid mechanics** with **probabilistic dispersion theory**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, mixing mechanisms are categorised as the **fundamental physical and rheological processes** by which distinct polymeric or particulate phases are redistributed within a continuous matrix. The two principal classification axes are:

1. **Distributive (Extensive) Mixing** — Spatial re-arrangement of components without changing their physical size or morphology.
2. **Dispersive (Intensive) Mixing** — Reduction of the *physical size* of agglomerates or droplets via rupture and erosion under a stress field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT755 / Module 3):**
> Mixing mechanisms form the theoretical foundation for modelling **internal mixers, twin-screw extruders, and batch mixers**. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to differentiate *distributive* vs *dispersive* mixing and to apply the **Scale of Segregation** and **Intensity of Segregation** concepts to polymer melt blending problems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Think of mixing a drop of **red food colour** into a glass of clear water:

- If you merely *stir* the glass with a spoon, you are performing **convective (distributive) mixing** — the red drop is simply *moved around* into different regions, but the drop itself remains intact.
- If you then stir very *vigorously* near the drop, the high local **shear stress** in the water tears the drop into thousands of smaller droplets — this is **dispersive mixing**.
- At the microscopic scale, individual red dye molecules slowly wander through the water due to random thermal motion — this is **molecular diffusion** (Fickian mixing), the slowest but most intimate form.

In a **polymer extruder**, the screw flights perform convective mixing (by dragging the melt forward and pushing it across the channel), the high-shear regions near the barrel wall perform dispersive mixing (by breaking filler agglomerates), and inter-diffusion of polymer chains across interfaces completes the homogenisation at the molecular level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The dimensionless **Peclet number** $Pe = \frac{\dot{\gamma} L^2}{D}$ (where $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate, $L$ = characteristic length, $D$ = diffusivity) compares convective to diffusive mixing. For polymer melts, $Pe \gg 1$ because $D \sim 10^{-12}$ m²/s, so **convection dominates** and diffusion is negligible at processing times.

---

## 1.3 Why Mixing Mechanisms Matter in KTU Polymer Engineering

Mixing governs the final **mechanical, optical, barrier, and rheological properties** of polymer products:

- **Compounded polymers** (e.g., carbon-black filled rubber) require uniform dispersion to achieve consistent conductivity.
- **Polymer blends** (e.g., PP/PA, PS/PMMA) need controlled morphology to reach desired impact strength.
- **Reactive extrusion** relies on mixing intensity to control reaction conversion.
- **Masterbatch let-down** demands distributive mixing to avoid colour streaks.

Hence, *modelling* the mixing mechanism — whether via residence time distribution (RTD), striation thickness analysis, or CFD-DEM coupling — is central to PEPOT755.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Mixing Mechanisms

Polymer mixing is fundamentally a **multi-scale phenomenon** operating simultaneously at four length scales:

| Length Scale | Dominant Mechanism | Typical Process |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Macro** ($10^{-1}$ – $10^0$ m) | **Convection** (bulk displacement) | Screw flights, rotors |
| **Meso**  ($10^{-4}$ – $10^{-3}$ m) | **Laminar shear / elongation** | Intermeshing zones |
| **Micro**  ($10^{-6}$ – $10^{-4}$ m) | **Dispersive rupture / erosion** | High-shear clearances |
| **Nano**   ($10^{-9}$ – $10^{-7}$ m) | **Molecular diffusion** | Interfacial healing |

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Convective (Distributive) Mixing
- The bulk flow field is split into **streamlines**; material elements are transported along these paths.
- A *well-designed mixer* increases the **number of independent flow paths** (analogous to increasing entropy of distribution).
- Quantified by the **number of striations** $n_s$ produced after $N$ cutting-and-rearrangement operations:

$$n_s = 2^{N}$$

- Each striation has a thickness $s$, and the total interfacial area $A$ between components scales as:

$$A \propto \frac{1}{s} \propto 2^{N/2}$$

- **Why this matters:** A doubling of striations halves the striation thickness — a logarithmic gain per operation.

### Step 2 — Dispersive Mixing
- Governed by a **stress balance**: the hydrodynamic stress $\sigma_{hydro}$ must exceed the cohesive stress $\sigma_{coh}$ of the agglomerate.
- The **Critical Weber Number** for droplet rupture is:

$$We_{crit} = \frac{\eta_m \dot{\gamma} R}{\Gamma} \ge We_{crit}^{*}$$

- where $\eta_m$ = matrix viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate, $R$ = drop radius, $\Gamma$ = interfacial tension.
- The cohesive stress for rigid agglomerates (e.g., carbon black) is given by the **Rumpf model**:

$$\sigma_{coh} = \frac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon} \cdot \frac{F_H}{d^2}$$

- where $\epsilon$ = packing fraction, $F_H$ = inter-particle adhesion force, $d$ = particle diameter.

### Step 3 — Molecular Diffusion (Fickian)
- Fick's second law governs inter-diffusion across interfaces:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \nabla^2 C$$

- Penetration depth $\delta$ scales as:

$$\delta \sim \sqrt{Dt}$$

- For polymer melts, $D \sim 10^{-12}$ to $10^{-14}$ m²/s, so $\delta \sim 1\ \mu\text{m}$ in typical processing times — *insignificant* for macroscopic mixing.

### Step 4 — Peclet Number Regime Determination

$$Pe = \frac{\dot{\gamma} L^2}{D}$$

- $Pe \gg 1$ → Convection-dominated (typical for processing).
- $Pe \sim 1$ → Mixed regime.
- $Pe \ll 1$ → Diffusion-dominated (only at very long annealing times).

---

## 2.3 Danckwerts' Theory of Mixing Quality

For a binary system, mixing quality is decomposed into two orthogonal metrics:

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Ideal Value |
|--------|--------|------------|-------------|
| **Scale of Segregation** | $S$ | Characteristic length of unmixed clumps | $S \to 0$ |
| **Intensity of Segregation** | $I$ | Normalised RMS of concentration fluctuations | $I \to 0$ |

The **correlation coefficient** between two points separated by vector $\vec{r}$ is:

$$R(\vec{r}) = \frac{\overline{(C_A - \bar{C}_A)(C_B - \bar{C}_B)}}{\overline{(C_A - \bar{C}_A)^2}}$$

- $S$ is the integral correlation length:

$$S = \int_0^{\infty} R(r)\, dr$$

- $I$ is the normalised variance:

$$I = \frac{\overline{(C - \bar{C})^2}}}{\bar{C}(1-\bar{C})}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $n_s = 2^{N}$ | Number of striations after N reorientations | $N$ = reorientation count | dimensionless |
| 2 | $s = s_0 \, 2^{-N/2}$ | Striation thickness reduction | $s_0$ = initial thickness | m |
| 3 | $A = A_0 \, 2^{N/2}$ | Interfacial area growth | $A_0$ = initial area | m² |
| 4 | $\sigma_{hydro} = \eta_m \dot{\gamma}$ | Hydrodynamic shear stress | $\eta_m$, $\dot{\gamma}$ | Pa |
| 5 | $We = \dfrac{\eta_m \dot{\gamma} R}{\Gamma}$ | Weber number (dispersive) | $R$ = drop radius, $\Gamma$ = interfacial tension | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_{coh} = \dfrac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon} \cdot \dfrac{F_H}{d^2}$ | Rumpf cohesive stress (agglomerates) | $\epsilon$, $F_H$, $d$ | Pa |
| 7 | $\delta = \sqrt{Dt}$ | Fickian diffusion depth | $D$ = diffusivity, $t$ = time | m |
| 8 | $Pe = \dfrac{\dot{\gamma} L^2}{D}$ | Peclet number (regime) | $L$ = characteristic length | dimensionless |
| 9 | $S = \int_0^{\infty} R(r)\, dr$ | Scale of segregation | $R(r)$ = correlation | m |
| 10 | $I = \dfrac{\overline{(C - \bar{C})^2}}{\bar{C}(1-\bar{C})}$ | Intensity of segregation | $C$ = local concentration | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\gamma_{tot} = \dot{\gamma} \cdot t_{res}$ | Total strain (residence time) | $t_{res}$ = residence time | dimensionless |
| 12 | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_{hydro}}{\sigma_{coh}}$ | Dispersive mixing index | ratio | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the *regime* ($Pe$ value) before solving a mixing problem. KTU examiners award a 2-mark bonus for identifying whether the system is convection- or diffusion-limited.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Twin-screw extruder design:** Selecting kneading disc stagger angles is an exercise in optimising $n_s$ and $\gamma_{tot}$.
- **Static mixers (Kenics, Sulzer):** Performance predicted by the striation thickness formula.
- **Carbon-black dispersion in rubber:** Direct application of the Rumpf cohesive stress criterion.
- **Pharmaceutical polymer blending:** CFD simulation calibrated against $S$ and $I$ to qualify mixing protocols.
- **Reactive blending (compatibiliser addition):** The Weber number controls final droplet size, which dictates impact strength.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Striation Thickness Reduction Under Repeated Reorientation

### Problem Statement
A polymer compound initially has a striation thickness $s_0 = 4.0\ \text{mm}$. The mixer performs $N = 10$ successive *cut-and-stack* operations (analogous to a Kenics static mixer). Derive the final striation thickness $s$.

### Step 1: Establish the geometric relationship
Each cut-and-stack operation doubles the number of striations and halves the striation thickness in **one spatial direction**.

$$n_s(N) = 2^N$$

$$s(N) = \frac{s_0}{2^{N/2}} \quad \text{(isotropic assumption)}$$

### Step 2: Justify the $N/2$ exponent
Because the cut-and-stack operation acts on a 2D cross-section:

$$A_{striation} \propto s^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{total} = n_s \cdot s^2 = \text{const}$$

Therefore:

$$2^N \cdot s^2 = s_0^2$$

Solving for $s$:

$$s = s_0 \cdot 2^{-N/2}$$

### Step 3: Substitute numerical values

$$s = (4.0\ \text{mm}) \cdot 2^{-10/2} = 4.0 \cdot 2^{-5} = 4.0 \cdot \frac{1}{32}$$

$$s = 0.125\ \text{mm} = 125\ \mu\text{m}$$

### Step 4: Compute the interfacial area growth

$$A(N) = A_0 \cdot 2^{N/2} = A_0 \cdot 2^5 = 32\,A_0$$

**Conclusion:** Ten operations increase the interfacial area by a factor of **32** and reduce striation thickness to **125 μm**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Shear Rate for Dispersive Mixing of a Droplet

### Problem Statement
A polyamide (PA) droplet of radius $R = 10\ \mu\text{m}$ is dispersed in a polypropylene (PP) matrix. Given:
- Matrix viscosity: $\eta_m = 500\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- Interfacial tension: $\Gamma = 5.0\ \text{mN/m}$
- Critical Weber number: $We_{crit}^{*} = 1.0$

Derive the critical shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_{crit}$.

### Step 1: Recall the Weber number definition

$$We = \frac{\eta_m \dot{\gamma} R}{\Gamma}$$

### Step 2: Set the rupture condition $We = We_{crit}^{*}$

$$\eta_m \dot{\gamma}_{crit} R = We_{crit}^{*} \cdot \Gamma$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\dot{\gamma}_{crit}$

$$\dot{\gamma}_{crit} = \frac{We_{crit}^{*} \cdot \Gamma}{\eta_m \cdot R}$$

### Step 4: Substitute values

$$\dot{\gamma}_{crit} = \frac{(1.0)(5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N/m})}{(500\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s})(10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m})}$$

Numerator: $5.0 \times 10^{-3}$
Denominator: $500 \times 10^{-5} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$

$$\dot{\gamma}_{crit} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
A modest shear rate of **1 s⁻¹** is sufficient to rupture a 10-μm PA droplet in PP. The required $\dot{\gamma}$ *decreases* linearly with droplet radius — a key insight for KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Strain and Mixing Time in a Batch Mixer

### Problem Statement
A batch internal mixer (e.g., Banbury) operates at a rotor speed $N = 60\ \text{rpm}$ for $t = 5$ minutes. The average shear rate in the mixing chamber is $\dot{\gamma} = 100\ \text{s}^{-1}$. Compute the total strain and verify if it is sufficient for dispersive mixing (typical threshold $\gamma_{tot} > 10^4$).

### Step 1: Convert to SI units

$$N = 60\ \text{rpm} = 1.0\ \text{rev/s}$$

$$t = 5\ \text{min} = 300\ \text{s}$$

### Step 2: Compute total strain

$$\gamma_{tot} = \dot{\gamma} \cdot t = (100\ \text{s}^{-1})(300\ \text{s})$$

$$\gamma_{tot} = 3 \times 10^4$$

### Step 3: Check against threshold

$$3 \times 10^4 > 10^4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Sufficient for dispersive mixing.}$$

### Step 4: Compute the number of effective reorientations
Using Mohr's analysis for batch mixers:

$$N_{reor} \approx \frac{\gamma_{tot}}{5} = \frac{3 \times 10^4}{5} = 6 \times 10^3$$

This corresponds to a striation thickness of:

$$s = s_0 \cdot 2^{-N_{reor}/2} \approx \frac{s_0}{2^{3000}}$$

— an astronomically small value, well below the polymer chain radius of gyration, confirming that the *limiting step* becomes molecular diffusion rather than mechanical reduction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Peclet Number Regime Classification

### Problem Statement
A polymer melt is processed in a single-screw extruder channel. Given:
- Average shear rate: $\dot{\gamma} = 50\ \text{s}^{-1}$
- Channel depth: $H = 5\ \text{mm}$
- Polymer self-diffusivity: $D = 1 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$

Determine the dominant mixing mechanism.

### Step 1: Characteristic length

$$L = H = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$

### Step 2: Compute Peclet number

$$Pe = \frac{\dot{\gamma} L^2}{D} = \frac{(50)(5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{1 \times 10^{-12}}$$

Numerator: $50 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$
Denominator: $1 \times 10^{-12}$

$$Pe = 1.25 \times 10^{9}$$

### Step 3: Regime classification
Since $Pe \gg 1$, the system is **strongly convection-dominated**. Molecular diffusion contributes negligibly during the residence time.

### Step 4: Diffusion penetration depth during residence

$$\delta = \sqrt{Dt}$$

For $t = 30\ \text{s}$ (typical residence time):

$$\delta = \sqrt{(1 \times 10^{-12})(30)} = \sqrt{3 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 5.5\ \mu\text{m}$$

A mere **5.5 μm** in 30 seconds — confirming that diffusion is the rate-limiting *final* step but contributes minimally to bulk homogenisation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Selection Tree

| Observed Phenomenon | Likely Mechanism | Diagnostic Test |
|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Colour streaks, no agglomerates | Distributive only | $S$ large, $I$ moderate |
| Visible gel particles / specks | Dispersive failure | $We < We_{crit}$ |
| Property drift after long storage | Diffusion-limited | $Pe \sim 1$ or annealing |
| Viscosity-dependent mixing quality | Shear-dominated | $\gamma_{tot} > 10^4$ |
| Pressure oscillation in extruder | Improper distributive | RTD spread $\sigma_t^2$ |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Mixing Mechanism Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Mixing System] --> B[Distributive Mixing]
    A --> C[Dispersive Mixing]
    A --> D[Molecular Diffusion]

    B --> B1[Convective transport]
    B --> B2[Laminar reorientation]
    B1 --> B1a[Screw flight drag]
    B1 --> B1b[Kenics element twist]
    B2 --> B2a[Cut and stack N times]
    B2 --> B2b[Striation thinning]

    C --> C1[Droplet rupture]
    C --> C2[Agglomerate erosion]
    C1 --> C1a[We exceeds We crit]
    C1 --> C1b[Shear stress over interfacial tension]
    C2 --> C2a[Rumpf cohesive model]
    C2 --> C2b[Surface erosion mechanism]

    D --> D1[Fickian second law]
    D --> D2[Polymer chain interdiffusion]
    D1 --> D1a[Penetration depth delta]
    D2 --> D2a[Reptation controlled]

    B1a --> Q{Quality Metric}
    B1b --> Q
    B2a --> Q
    C1a --> Q
    C2a --> Q
    D1a --> Q
    D2a --> Q
    Q --> M1[Scale of Segregation S]
    Q --> M2[Intensity of Segregation I]
    Q --> M3[Striation Thickness s]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INLET[Feed Hopper] --> CONV[Conveying Zone]
    CONV --> MELT[Melt Zone]
    MELT --> KNEAD[Kneading Disc Block]
    KNEAD --> MIX1[Distributive Mixing Stage]
    MIX1 --> MIX2[Dispersive Mixing Stage]
    MIX2 --> REVERSE[Reverse Elements]
    REVERSE --> MIX3[High Shear Zone]
    MIX3 --> VENT[Degassing Vent]
    VENT --> PUMP[Metering Zone]
    PUMP --> DIE[Extrusion Die]

    CONV --> RTD1[RTD Sensor]
    MELT --> RTD1
    MIX2 --> RTD1

    CTRL --> FEEDBACK1[Adjust Screw Speed]
    CTRL --> FEEDBACK2[Adjust Feed Rate]
    CTRL --> FEEDBACK3[Adjust Temperature]
```

```mermaid

    M1 --> P1

    M3 --> P2
```

> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These schematics map the *causal chain* from raw mixing operations to product-level engineering properties, which is the central modelling paradigm emphasised in PEPOT755.
