<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "coarse-grained simulation" in polymer modelling. Mention any one such method and its application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Coarse-grained (CG) simulation is a computational technique in which groups of atoms (typically 3–4 heavy atoms) are lumped into single "beads" or interaction sites, reducing the degrees of freedom and enabling access to longer length- and time-scales than fully atomistic simulations.
- **(1 mark)** Example: **MARTINI force field** (developed by Marrink et al., University of Groningen), which maps ~4 heavy atoms to one CG bead with 4–5 interaction levels.
- **(1 mark)** Application: Simulation of **block-copolymer self-assembly**, **vesicle formation**, and **polymer membrane phase behavior** on 10–100 nm scales inaccessible to atomistic MD.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the Metropolis Monte Carlo acceptance criterion? Write the equation and explain its physical meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The Metropolis criterion is a probabilistic rule used in Monte Carlo simulations to accept or reject a trial configuration change.
- **(1 mark)** Equation:
$$P_{accept} = \min\left(1, \exp\left[-\frac{\Delta U}{k_B T}\right]\right)$$
- **(1 mark)** **Physical meaning:** Moves that *lower* the energy (ΔU < 0) are always accepted (P = 1); moves that *raise* the energy are accepted with probability $\exp(-\Delta U/k_BT)$, ensuring that the ensemble of configurations sampled is the **canonical (NVT) Boltzmann distribution** $\propto \exp(-U/k_BT)$.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEPOT755]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a classical Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulation for a polymer melt. Discuss the role of force fields, integration algorithm, and ensemble choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Flory scaling laws, derive the radius of gyration $R_g$ for:
   (i) an ideal Gaussian chain in θ-solvent,
   (ii) a real chain in good solvent. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — MD Architecture for a Polymer Melt (7 marks)

**(i) Initialization (1 mark):**
- Build initial coordinates of the polymer chain (e.g., 100 repeat units of polyethylene).
- Place the chain in a periodic cubic box of size L ≈ 5 nm.
- Use the **self-avoiding random walk** to avoid atomic overlap; alternatively, use **packing algorithms** (e.g., Pulley, PolySource).

**(ii) Force Field Selection (1.5 marks):**
- Choose a polymer-compatible FF such as **OPLS-AA** (for polyolefins, polyethers) or **PCFF** (for engineering plastics like PC, PET, PEEK).
- The FF provides:
  - **Bonded parameters:** $k_b, r_0, k_\theta, \theta_0, k_\phi$, phase $\delta$.
  - **Non-bonded parameters:** Lennard-Jones $\varepsilon, \sigma$ and partial charges $q_i$.

**(iii) Energy Minimization (0.5 mark):**
- Perform 5000–10000 steps of **steepest descent** or **conjugate gradient** to remove steric clashes.
- Converge to $F_{max} < 10$ kJ/(mol·nm).

**(iv) Equilibration (1 mark):**
- Heat from 0 K → 450 K over 100 ps (NVT).
- Switch to **NPT** (isothermal–isobaric) at 1 atm for 1 ns to allow density to equilibrate.
- Use **Berendsen** or **Nosé-Hoover** thermostat and **Parrinello-Rahman** barostat.

**(v) Production (1 mark):**
- Run NPT or NVE for 10–100 ns with Δt = 1 fs.
- Save coordinates every 1 ps for analysis.

**(vi) Analysis (1 mark):**
- Compute $R_g(t)$, RDF, MSD, end-to-end distance, cohesive energy density (for δ).

---

#### Part (b) — Flory Scaling Derivation (7 marks)

**(i) Ideal Gaussian chain in θ-solvent (3.5 marks):**

In a θ-solvent, excluded-volume interactions are exactly cancelled by attractive monomer–monomer interactions, so the chain behaves as an **ideal random walk**.

The probability distribution of the end-to-end vector $\vec{R}$ is Gaussian:

$$P(\vec{R}, N) = \left(\frac{3}{2\pi \langle R^2 \rangle}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(-\frac{3R^2}{2\langle R^2\rangle}\right)$$

For N bonds of length l, the **mean-square end-to-end distance**:

$$\langle R^2 \rangle = N l^2$$

The relationship between $R_g$ and $R$ for a Gaussian chain is:

$$\langle R_g^2 \rangle = \frac{\langle R^2 \rangle}{6} = \frac{N l^2}{6}$$

For a real chain with fixed bond angles (Flory's characteristic ratio):

$$\langle R^2 \rangle = C_\infty \, N \, l^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\langle R_g^2 \rangle = \frac{C_\infty \, N \, l^2}{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_g \sim N^{1/2}}$$

**(ii) Real chain in good solvent (3.5 marks):**

In a good solvent, excluded-volume repulsions dominate, and the chain swells. The **Flory mean-field argument** balances elastic energy with monomer–solvent interaction.

**Elastic (conformational) energy:**
$$F_{el} \approx k_B T \frac{R^2}{N l^2}$$

**Excluded-volume energy:**
$$F_{ev} \approx k_B T \, v \frac{N^2}{R^3}$$

where $v$ is the excluded-volume parameter.

**Total free energy:**
$$F(R) = k_B T \left[ \frac{R^2}{N l^2} + v \frac{N^2}{R^3} \right]$$

**Minimize with respect to R:**
$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial R} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{2R}{N l^2} - 3 v \frac{N^2}{R^4} = 0$$

$$R^5 = \frac{3 v}{2} N^3 l^2 \;\Rightarrow\; R \sim N^{3/5}$$

Converting to $R_g$ ($R_g \sim R$):

$$\boxed{R_g \sim N^{3/5} = N^{0.6}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> More refined **renormalization group theory** (de Gennes, 1972) gives the exact exponent **ν = 0.588** for 3D good-solvent chains, in excellent agreement with both experiment and simulation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – PEPOT755 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** workflow for simulating injection molding of a polypropylene (PP) component. List the input material data required and the typical outputs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **schematic energy vs reaction coordinate diagram**, explain how DFT can be used to predict the activation energy of polymer degradation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (Synopsis — 14 marks)**

#### Part (a) FEA for Injection Molding (7 marks)

**Workflow Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Geometry import:** 3D CAD model of part (e.g., automotive PP dashboard).
2. **Mesh generation:** 3D tetrahedral mesh, refined at corners and ribs.
3. **Material database:** Provide Cross-WLF viscosity model parameters for PP:
$$\eta(T, p, \dot{\gamma}) = \frac{\eta_0(T, p)}{1 + \left(\frac{\eta_0 \dot{\gamma}}{\tau^*}\right)^{(1-n)}}$$
4. **Boundary conditions:** Mold surface temperature, injection flow rate, cooling channel layout.
5. **Solver:** Navier–Stokes + energy equation (3D non-isothermal).
6. **Outputs:** Fill time, pressure, weld-line location, **warpage**, residual stress, fiber orientation (for glass-filled PP).

**Required Inputs (2 marks):**
- PVT data (specific volume vs T, p)
- Viscosity vs T, p, shear rate
- Thermal conductivity, specific heat
- Clamp force, melt temperature

**Outputs (1 mark):**
- Weld-line positions, air traps, sink marks
- Cycle-time estimation, warpage map
- Fiber orientation tensors $A_{ij}$ in fiber-reinforced composites.

#### Part (b) DFT for Polymer Degradation (7 marks)

**Energy diagram (described, 4 marks):**
Plot E (vertical) vs reaction coordinate (horizontal). Identify:
- Reactant (intact polymer)
- Transition state (highest point, E_a)
- Product (degraded fragment + radical)

DFT calculates $E_{TS}$ and $E_{Reactant}$, so:

$$E_a = E_{TS} - E_{Reactant}$$

**Numerical Example (1.5 marks):**
For C–C bond scission in PE backbone (DFT/B3LYP/6-31G*):
$$E_a \approx 350 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This agrees with experimental pyrolysis TGA data showing degradation onset near **400 °C** for HDPE.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- Predicts **thermal stability ranking** of polymers
- Helps design **heat stabilizers** (e.g., hindered phenols) by computing their radical-scavenging energetics
- Saves costly trial-and-error synthesis.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Mark Loss Hotspots):**
> 1. **Mixing up $C_\infty$ with the Flory exponent ν** — they are unrelated: $C_\infty$ is a property of the local chain stiffness, ν is a property of the long-range scaling.
> 2. **Writing Δt = 1 s instead of 1 fs** for atomistic MD — this is a **10¹⁵ factor** error and destroys energy conservation.
> 3. **Forgetting the k_B T in Metropolis MC** — students often write only $\exp(-\Delta U)$, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing DPD with MD** — DPD uses *soft* conservative forces (linear in r) and dissipative + random forces, NOT Lennard-Jones. Markers deduct 1 mark if you say "DPD uses LJ potential".
> 5. **Not specifying the ensemble** (NVE/NVT/NPT) in MD questions — examiners expect explicit mention.
> 6. **Units of χ** — Flory-Huggins χ is *dimensionless*; do not give it J/mol.
> 7. **Equating $R_g$ with $R$ (end-to-end distance)** — these are related by $R_g^2 = R^2/6$ only for Gaussian chains.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Top 15 High-Yield Points**

- [x] **Computational analysis** = use of numerical methods to predict polymer properties across scales (Å → m, fs → s).
- [x] **DFT (Kohn-Sham eq.)** → for electronic properties, bond energies, degradation energetics.
- [x] **Classical MD (Newton's law)** → for $R_g$, $T_g$, diffusion, modulus, density.
- [x] **Force field = bonded + non-bonded**; OPLS-AA and PCFF for polymers.
- [x] **Velocity-Verlet** is the standard MD integrator; **Δt = 1 fs** for atomistic.
- [x] **Metropolis MC** criterion: $P = \min(1, \exp(-\Delta U / k_B T))$.
- [x] **Coarse-graining** (MARTINI, DPD) speeds up simulations by **100–1000×** and reaches **μs–ms** time.
- [x] **DPD** uses three forces: Conservative + Dissipative + Random.
- [x] **FEA (Moldflow, ANSYS)** for injection molding, warpage, rheology.
- [x] **Flory scaling:** $R_g \sim N^{1/2}$ (θ-solvent), $N^{0.588}$ (good solvent), $N^{1/3}$ (poor solvent).
- [x] **End-to-end distance:** $\langle R^2 \rangle = C_\infty n l^2$.
- [x] **Flory χ parameter** governs miscibility: χ < 0.5 → miscible, χ > 0.5 → immiscible.
- [x] **MSD → Diffusion** via Einstein relation: $D = \lim_{t→∞}\langle \vert \Delta r \vert^2 \rangle / 6t$.
- [x] **Multi-scale pyramid:** DFT → Atomistic MD → CG-MD → Mesoscale (DPD) → Continuum (FEA).
- [x] **Always specify ensemble** in MD: NVE (microcanonical), NVT (canonical), NPT (isothermal–isobaric).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid — "FAME-S"** for the 5 key methods:
> **F** – Finite Element (FEA)
> **A** – Atomistic MD
> **M** – Monte Carlo
> **E** – Electronic (DFT)
> **S** – (CG, DPD) — *S*elf-assembly methods
>
> When asked for a computational method, the answer is **always one of FAME-S** (or a hybrid).

---

topics.title: Simulation of reactive polymer processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulation of reactive polymer processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Reactive Polymer Processing (RPP)** is a class of manufacturing operations in which the **shaping (forming)** of a polymeric part and the **polymerisation/cross-linking (chemical reaction)** occur *simultaneously* inside the processing equipment, rather than as two separate, sequential steps. The polymer is not fully polymerised when it enters the machine; the conversion from low-viscosity monomer/oligomer to a high-viscosity, cross-linked network takes place *during* filling, mixing, extrusion, or moulding.

Mathematically, reactive polymer processing is the **coupled, transient, multi-physics simulation** of:

$$ \underbrace{\text{Momentum transport}}_{\text{Cavity filling / extrusion}} + \underbrace{\text{Energy transport}}_{\text{Heat conduction + reaction heat}} + \underbrace{\text{Species transport}}_{\text{Curing chemistry (conversion } \alpha\text{)}} + \underbrace{\text{Constitutive rheology}}_{\eta(\dot{\gamma}, T, \alpha, p) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Reactive polymer processing is treated as the *most complex* module in PEPOT755 because it demands simultaneous solution of **flow, heat transfer, and reaction kinetics** — a true *multiphysics* problem. Expect questions that couple at least two of these phenomena.

The two industrially dominant processes covered in the syllabus are:

1. **Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM)** — two highly reactive liquid streams are impingement-mixed and then injected into a closed mould where polymerisation/cross-linking produces the part.
2. **Reactive Extrusion (REX / REXTRU)** — a single- or twin-screw extruder acts as a continuous chemical reactor where polymerisation, grafting, or cross-linking is conducted in the melt state.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Self-Baking Cake" Concept

Imagine a cake batter that you *pour* into a baking tin as a thin, runny liquid (the monomer mix), and *while it is still flowing* into the corners of the tin, an oven inside the tin switches on (initiated by the mould walls) and **bakes the cake as it is being poured**. The liquid that reaches the far corner first is already partially set; the liquid entering last is still completely runny. The baker (the simulator) must predict *where* in the tin, *when*, and *how much* baking has occurred, so that the cake has uniform texture (uniform conversion $\alpha$) and no burnt edges (no thermal runaway).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** In normal polymer processing (e.g., injection moulding of polyethylene), the plastic is already fully polymerised — we only *shape* it. In **reactive** polymer processing, the plastic is shaped **and** built (polymerised) at the same time. Modelling this requires tracking *how far the chemical reaction has progressed* (a new variable, $\alpha$) at every point and at every instant.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Hard to Model

| Difficulty Factor | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Coupled PDE system** | Flow field, temperature field and conversion field are all inter-dependent. |
| **Strongly non-linear rheology** | Viscosity changes by 4–6 orders of magnitude from liquid to solid. |
| **Exothermic reaction** | Cure releases heat → temperature rises → reaction rate increases → more heat (potential thermal runaway). |
| **Moving boundary (gel point)** | The material changes from liquid-like to solid-like at a specific conversion $\alpha_{gel}$, altering boundary conditions. |
| **Multi-component chemistry** | Often two streams of different chemistry meeting in the mixing chamber. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Recap:** Degree of cure / conversion is symbolised by the Greek letter $\alpha$, where $\alpha = 0$ means *uncured monomer* and $\alpha = 1$ means *fully cured network*. The **gel point** $\alpha_{gel}$ is typically around **0.2–0.6** for thermosets (Flory-Stockmayer theory gives an exact value for a given functionality).

## 1.4 Governing Physical Phenomena — A Roadmap

The four *simultaneous* physical phenomena that must be simulated are:

1. **Cure kinetics (chemistry):** How fast does $\alpha$ grow with time at a given temperature?
2. **Heat transfer:** How does the exothermic heat of reaction distribute through the part and the mould?
3. **Fluid flow / rheology:** How does the resin flow and how does its viscosity evolve with $T$, $\alpha$ and shear rate?
4. **Free-surface / moving interface tracking:** Where exactly is the flow front during mould filling?

> [!NOTE]
> Every commercial reactive processing simulator (Moldflow, Moldex3D, REM3D, OpenFOAM-reactingFoam) solves these four phenomena in a tightly coupled manner. The KTU exam tests the *mathematical formulation* of this coupling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sub-Models of Reactive Processing Simulation

### 2.1.1 Cure Kinetics Sub-Model

The cure kinetics relates the rate of conversion to temperature and current conversion. Two empirical models are **mandatory for KTU**:

**(a) Kamal–Ryan (autocatalytic) model** — used for *epoxy/thermosets*:

$$ \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = \left(k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^{m}\right)(1-\alpha)^{n} $$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ follow **Arrhenius temperature dependence**:

$$ k_i = A_i\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_i}{RT}\right),\quad i=1,2 $$

* $k_1$ captures the *uncatalysed* (initial) reaction rate.
* $k_2\,\alpha^{m}$ captures the *autocatalytic* acceleration.
* Exponents $m, n$ are fit from **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)**.

**(b) nth-order model** — used for *polyurethane RIM*:

$$ \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k\,(1-\alpha)^{n} $$

This is the **Kamal model with $k_2 = 0$**; it cannot reproduce the autocatalytic S-shape, but is computationally cheaper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When the question says "epoxy", write the **Kamal** model. When the question says "polyurethane RIM", write the **nth-order** model. Marks are awarded for choosing the *correct* model, not just any model.

### 2.1.2 Heat Transfer Sub-Model (with reaction source)

The transient energy equation inside the mould cavity is:

$$ \rho\,C_p\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k\nabla^{2}T + \rho_r\,H_r\,\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial t} $$

where:
* $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
* $C_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
* $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
* $\rho_r$ = density of reactive mixture
* $H_r$ = **total heat of reaction** (J/kg) — obtained from DSC integration
* The term $\rho_r H_r (\partial \alpha/\partial t)$ is the **volumetric heat generation rate** (W/m³) from cure.

**Boundary conditions** at the cavity wall (typical):

$$ -k\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\right|_{\text{wall}} = h\,(T_w - T_{\infty}) $$

where $h$ is the mould-wall heat transfer coefficient and $T_w$ is the mould wall temperature (often held isothermal by the machine's oil-circulation system).

### 2.1.3 Rheology Sub-Model

The viscosity depends on **four** state variables: temperature $T$, shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$, pressure $p$ (in compression), and conversion $\alpha$. The most widely used phenomenological form (White–Kohlmann–Bauer / Castro–Macosko) is:

$$ \eta(T,\alpha,\dot{\gamma}) = \underbrace{\eta_\infty}_{\text{high-T limit}} \exp\!\left(\frac{E_\eta}{RT}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g - \alpha}\right)^{A_1 + A_2\,\alpha} \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} $$

* $E_\eta$ = activation energy of flow (J/mol)
* $\alpha_g$ = conversion at **gel point**
* $A_1, A_2$ = fitted constants controlling the viscosity blow-up near gelation
* The third factor $(\alpha_g/(\alpha_g-\alpha))^{A_1 + A_2\alpha}$ is the **chemical-thermorheologically complex** term that diverges as $\alpha \to \alpha_g$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** As $\alpha$ approaches $\alpha_g$, $\eta \to \infty$ — the material effectively **locks** (vitrification / gelation). At this moment, the flow equations are *no longer valid*; the material must be treated as a solid with thermal expansion and cure-shrinkage constitutive laws.

### 2.1.4 Flow Sub-Model (Hele-Shaw for thin cavities)

For thin mould cavities (typical in RIM, thickness $H \ll L, W$), the 3D Navier-Stokes reduces to a 2D **Hele-Shaw** formulation with a gap-wise-averaged velocity. The general form is:

$$ \dot{m}_x = -\frac{S}{12}\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}, \qquad \dot{m}_y = -\frac{S}{12}\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} $$

where

$$ S = \int_0^{H} \frac{z^{2}}{\eta(T,\alpha,\dot{\gamma})}\,dz $$

Pressure is recovered by enforcing **mass conservation**:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\left(\rho H\right) + \frac{\partial \dot{m}_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \dot{m}_y}{\partial y} = 0 $$

## 2.2 The Coupling Loop — Why Iterative Solution is Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** The four sub-models are *strongly* coupled. A change in $T$ changes $k$ (Arrhenius), which changes $d\alpha/dt$, which changes the heat-generation term, which changes $T$ again. The flow solution needs the current $\eta(T,\alpha)$, but the $\eta$ field depends on $T$ and $\alpha$ which depend on the flow advection of heat. This is a **non-linear, two-way coupled multiphysics problem**.

The iteration loop is:

1. Solve flow field using current $\eta(T,\alpha,\dot{\gamma})$ → get velocity & pressure.
2. Advect heat with that velocity → get new $T$ field.
3. Compute $d\alpha/dt$ from cure kinetics at each node using the new $T$.
4. Update $\alpha$ by time-integration: $\alpha^{n+1} = \alpha^{n} + (d\alpha/dt)\,\Delta t$.
5. Update $\eta$ using new $T$ and $\alpha$.
6. Repeat from step 1 until residuals fall below tolerance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Where it is used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^{m})(1-\alpha)^{n}$ | **Kamal cure kinetics** | Epoxy, unsaturated polyester |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k\,(1-\alpha)^{n}$ | **nth-order cure kinetics** | Polyurethane RIM |
| 3 | $k_i = A_i\,\exp(-E_i/RT)$ | **Arrhenius rate constant** | All temperature-dependent rates |
| 4 | $\rho C_p \partial T/\partial t = k\nabla^{2}T + \rho_r H_r\,\partial\alpha/\partial t$ | **Energy equation with cure source** | Heat transfer in mould |
| 5 | $\eta = \eta_\infty \exp(E_\eta/RT)\left(\dfrac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g-\alpha}\right)^{A_1+A_2\alpha}\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | **Castro–Macosko rheology** | Viscosity evolution during cure |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_x = -(S/12)\,\partial p/\partial x$ | **Hele-Shaw flow** | Thin-cavity filling (RIM) |
| 7 | $S = \int_0^{H} z^{2}/\eta(T,\alpha,\dot\gamma)\,dz$ | **Fluidity integral** | 1D rheology averaging |
| 8 | $\alpha_c = 2/f_{\text{avg}}$ (Flory) | **Gel point** (functionality $f_{\text{avg}}$) | Thermoset gelation |
| 9 | $\Delta V_{\text{shrinkage}} = V_m\,\beta_s\,\alpha$ | **Cure shrinkage** | Dimensional prediction |
| 10 | $T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 + H_r/C_p$ | **Adiabatic temperature rise** | Worst-case thermal runaway |

> [!TIP]
> Memorise the **Kamal model (Eq. 1)**, the **energy equation with reaction source (Eq. 4)**, and the **Castro–Macosko rheology (Eq. 5)** — these three appear in nearly every KTU Part-B question on reactive processing.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive bumpers & body panels (PU-RIM):** Predicting cycle time, demould time, and warpage from non-uniform cure.
* **Wind-turbine blade root-foam infusion:** Simulating epoxy infusion to predict exotherm temperature and prevent matrix cracking.
* **Reactive extrusion of TPE / TPV:** Designing screw elements to achieve target graft/branching levels.
* **Pultrusion of composite profiles:** Predicting the *gel line* (the location inside the die where $\alpha = \alpha_g$).
* **3D printing of thermoset resins (VAT photopolymerisation):** Layer-by-layer cure simulation, where each layer is a transient reactive problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Energy Equation with Reaction Source (from First Principles)

We begin with the general **energy balance for a fluid element** of volume $dV$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of energy accumulation} &= \text{Net conductive influx} + \text{Convective influx} + \text{Volumetric source} \\
\rho\,C_p\,\frac{DT}{Dt} &= k\nabla^{2}T + \dot{q}_{\text{gen}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla$ is the material derivative (accounts for motion of the fluid element).

The **volumetric source** $\dot{q}_{\text{gen}}$ arises from the chemical reaction. Per unit mass, the heat released is $H_r\,d\alpha/dt$. Per unit volume, this becomes:

$$
\dot{q}_{\text{gen}} = \rho_r\,H_r\,\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial t}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho\,C_p\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla T\right) = k\nabla^{2}T + \rho_r\,H_r\,\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial t}
$$

For a **filled cavity** (no flow, $\mathbf{v}=0$) — which is the situation *after* mould filling is complete — this simplifies to:

$$
\rho\,C_p\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k\nabla^{2}T + \rho_r\,H_r\,\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial t} \tag{Energy Equation for curing thermoset}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic explained:** The first term on the right ($k\nabla^{2}T$) is conduction *redistribution* of heat from hot spots to cold spots. The second term ($\rho_r H_r\,d\alpha/dt$) is *new* heat being produced by the cure reaction. The left side is the rate at which thermal energy is being stored in the material.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Castro–Macosko Viscosity Model

The empirical model is *fitted* to data, but its form can be *justified* from free-volume theory and the principle of **thermo-rheological simplicity near gelation**. We sketch the derivation.

**Step 1:** Treat viscosity as a product of three independent contributions (a common assumption in polymer rheology):

$$
\eta(T,\alpha,\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_T(T)\cdot\eta_\alpha(\alpha)\cdot\eta_{\dot{\gamma}}(\dot{\gamma})
$$

**Step 2:** Temperature dependence follows an **Arrhenius** form (Eyring theory of activated flow):

$$
\eta_T(T) = \eta_\infty\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** The conversion dependence diverges at the gel point $\alpha_g$ because the molecular weight of the growing polymer network diverges there. The simplest functional form that diverges at $\alpha_g$ and is smooth for $\alpha < \alpha_g$ is:

$$
\eta_\alpha(\alpha) = \left(\frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g - \alpha}\right)^{f(\alpha)}
$$

where $f(\alpha) = A_1 + A_2\,\alpha$ is a *linear* function (empirically found adequate; $A_1$ controls how steeply viscosity rises near gel, $A_2$ is a small correction).

**Step 4:** Shear-thinning (power-law) accounts for non-Newtonian behaviour:

$$
\eta_{\dot{\gamma}}(\dot{\gamma}) = \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}
$$

**Step 5:** Combine all three:

$$
\eta(T,\alpha,\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)\cdot\left(\frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g-\alpha}\right)^{A_1 + A_2\alpha}\cdot\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} \tag{Castro–Macosko model}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the gel-point divergence matters:** The simulation algorithm must check $\alpha < \alpha_g$ at every node. Once $\alpha \geq \alpha_g$, the material is no longer a fluid — flow equations are *switched off* and only the energy + cure equations are integrated. This is a **discrete change in the PDE system** and is one of the most delicate parts of the code.

## 3.3 Adiabatic Temperature Rise — Closed-Form Derivation

The worst-case thermal scenario is **adiabatic** (no heat loss through mould walls). In that case $k\nabla^{2}T = 0$, so the energy equation becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho\,C_p\,\frac{dT}{dt} &= \rho_r\,H_r\,\frac{d\alpha}{dt} \\
C_p\,\frac{dT}{d\alpha} &= H_r \quad \text{(since } \rho=\rho_r \text{ for incompressible resin)} \\
\int_{T_0}^{T_{\text{ad}}} dT &= \frac{H_r}{C_p}\int_{0}^{1} d\alpha \\
T_{\text{ad}} - T_0 &= \frac{H_r}{C_p} \\
\boxed{\,T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 + \frac{H_r}{C_p}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical check (KTU favourite):** For a typical epoxy with $H_r = 350$ kJ/kg and $C_p = 1200$ J/(kg·K), and resin injection temperature $T_0 = 60\,^\circ$C:

$$
T_{\text{ad}} = 60 + \frac{350{,}000}{1200} = 60 + 291.7 = 351.7\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering warning:** $351.7\,^\circ$C far exceeds the degradation temperature of most thermosets (~$250\,^\circ$C). This is why **mould cooling** and **controlled cure schedules** are mandatory in industry. The simulator's job is to predict whether the *actual* (non-adiabatic) peak temperature stays below the degradation limit.

## 3.4 Gel Point from Flory–Stockmayer Theory (Mean-Field)

For an $f$-functional monomer reacting with a 2-functional comonomer, the **Flory–Stockmayer gel-point** is:

$$
\alpha_c = \frac{1}{(f-1)\,\rho_{\text{branch}}}
$$

where $\rho_{\text{branch}}$ is the stoichiometric ratio of branch units. For a stoichiometric mixture ($f$-functional epoxy + 2-functional amine):

$$
\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic explained:** Higher functionality $f$ lowers the gel-point conversion. For a tetrafunctional epoxy ($f=4$), $\alpha_c = 1/3 \approx 0.33$ — i.e., the network gels at just 33% cure. This is why the simulator must capture viscosity rise *well before* $\alpha=1$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style

**Problem (typical KTU Part-B 7-mark sub-question):**

An epoxy system has the following Kamal cure kinetics parameters:
$k_1 = 1.0\times 10^{3}\,\exp(-6000/T)$ s⁻¹, $\;k_2 = 2.0\times 10^{4}\,\exp(-7500/T)$ s⁻¹, $\;m = 0.5$, $\;n = 1.2$.
The resin is cured isothermally at $T = 400$ K. Compute the conversion $\alpha$ at $t = 100$ s using a single explicit Euler step with $\Delta t = 10$ s, starting from $\alpha(0) = 0$.

**Solution — step by step:**

**Step 1:** Compute $k_1$ and $k_2$ at $T = 400$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 1000 \cdot e^{-6000/400} = 1000 \cdot e^{-15} \\
e^{-15} &\approx 3.059\times 10^{-7} \\
k_1 &\approx 3.059\times 10^{-4}\;\text{s}^{-1} \\
k_2 &= 20000 \cdot e^{-7500/400} = 20000 \cdot e^{-18.75} \\
e^{-18.75} &\approx 7.31\times 10^{-9} \\
k_2 &\approx 1.46\times 10^{-4}\;\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** At $\alpha = 0$, evaluate the right-hand side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} &= (k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^{0.5})(1-\alpha)^{1.2} \\
&= (3.059\times 10^{-4} + 0)(1)^{1.2} \\
&= 3.059\times 10^{-4}\;\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Explicit Euler advance by $\Delta t = 10$ s:

$$
\alpha(10) = 0 + 3.059\times 10^{-4}\times 10 = 3.059\times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4:** At $\alpha = 3.059\times 10^{-3}$, $\alpha^{0.5} = 0.0553$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_2\,\alpha^{0.5} &= 1.46\times 10^{-4}\times 0.0553 = 8.07\times 10^{-6} \\
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} &= (3.059\times 10^{-4} + 8.07\times 10^{-6})\times(1-3.059\times 10^{-3})^{1.2} \\
&\approx 3.14\times 10^{-4}\times 0.9963 \approx 3.13\times 10^{-4}\;\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Continue to $t = 100$ s (10 such steps, here summarised):

$$
\alpha(100) \approx 3.13\times 10^{-2} \approx 0.031
$$

**Conclusion:** After 100 s at 400 K, the resin is ~3% cured. The reaction is slow at this temperature; in practice $T$ would be ramped higher.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** You must show *all* numerical values explicitly. A common mistake is to leave the final answer as an expression — full credit requires a numeric answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Reactive Processing Simulation Workflow

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a typical reactive-polymer-processing simulator. The flow captures the iterative multiphysics coupling.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Simulation: Define Geometry Mesh] --> stepB[Set Initial Conditions T0, alpha0, v0]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Current Viscosity eta using T, alpha, gammadot]
    stepC --> stepD[Solve Flow Field Momentum + Continuity to get v and p]
    stepD --> stepE[Advect Heat Convective Update of T field]
    stepE --> stepF[Compute Reaction Rate dAlpha dt using Kamal or nth order]
    stepF --> stepG[Update Conversion alpha via Time Integration]
    stepG --> checkH{Check Gel Point alpha greater than alpha_gel}
    checkH -- No --> stepI[Apply Conduction Solve Laplacian of T]
    checkH -- Yes --> stepJ[Switch to Solid Mechanics Module for warpage]
    stepI --> stepK[Compute Residuals]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> checkL{Residual less than Tolerance}
    checkL -- No --> stepC
    checkL -- Yes --> stepM[Time Step Forward dt]
    stepM --> checkN{Time greater than End Time}
    checkN -- No --> stepC
    checkN -- Yes --> endO[Post Process: Plot T, alpha, eta, p, von Mises stress]
```

## 4.2 Process Architecture: Sub-Model Coupling Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Data]
        i1[Material Parameters A, E, m, n, Hr, Cp, k]
        i2[Process Parameters Twall, Tinlet, Qinj, pclamp]
        i3[Geometry Mesh]
    end

    subgraph Coupling[Multiphysics Solver Core]
        c1[Module Cure Kinetics ODE for alpha]
        c2[Module Heat Transfer PDE for T]
        c3[Module Flow PDE for v and p]
        c4[Module Rheology Constitutive for eta]
    end

    subgraph Output[Simulation Output]
        o1[Conversion Field alpha x y z t]
        o2[Temperature Field T x y z t]
        o3[Pressure Field p x y z t]
        o4[Cycle Time and Demould Time]
    end

    i1 --> c1
    i1 --> c2
    i1 --> c3
    i1 --> c4
    i2 --> c2
    i2 --> c3
    i3 --> c2
    i3 --> c3

    c1 --> c2
    c1 --> c4
    c2 --> c3
    c2 --> c4
    c3 --> c4

    c1 --> o1
    c2 --> o2
    c3 --> o3
    c4 --> o4
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM) Physical Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tankA[Tank A Polyol] --> pumpA[Pump A]
    tankB[Tank B Isocyanate] --> pumpB[Pump B]
    pumpA --> mixer[High Pressure Impingement Mixer]
    pumpB --> mixer
    mixer --> nozzle[Mixing Nozzle]
    nozzle --> cavity[Closed Mould Cavity]
    cavity --> reaction[In situ Polymerisation and Cross linking]
    reaction --> part[Finished PU Part]
    cavity -.-> mouldctrl[Oil Circulating Mould Temperature Controller]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for the Diagrams:**
> * The first diagram shows the **iterative loop** at every time step — this is the heart of the simulator.
> * The second diagram shows the **modular software architecture** — each sub-model is its own code module, and arrows show the data dependencies.
> * The third diagram shows the **physical RIM process** — the simulator's job is to model what happens *inside the cavity* and *inside the mixer*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*Differentiate between "reactive polymer processing" and "conventional polymer processing" with one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conventional Processing | Reactive Processing |
|---|---|---|
| Polymerisation stage | Polymerisation is **completed** before processing (e.g., pellets of PE, PP). | Polymerisation **occurs during** processing. |
| State entering machine | Solid pellets or fully polymerised melt. | Low-viscosity monomer/oligomer mix. |
| Process | Injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding. | Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM), Reactive Extrusion. |
| Modelling complexity | Flow + heat transfer. | Flow + heat + cure kinetics (multiphysics). |
| Example | Injection moulding of polypropylene cups. | PU-RIM automotive bumper. |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each valid difference in tabular form, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
*State the Kamal–Ryan cure kinetics model and identify each term.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^{m})(1-\alpha)^{n}
$$

* $d\alpha/dt$ = rate of conversion (s⁻¹).
* $k_1 = A_1\exp(-E_1/RT)$ = Arrhenius rate constant for the **uncatalysed** (initial) reaction.
* $k_2 = A_2\exp(-E_2/RT)$ = Arrhenius rate constant for the **autocatalysed** reaction.
* $\alpha$ = instantaneous degree of cure.
* $m$ = autocatalytic order (typically 0.5–1).
* $n$ = reaction order in $(1-\alpha)$ (typically 1–2).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for correct equation, 1 mark for identifying four terms, 1 mark for the Arrhenius form.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B (A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the **transient energy equation with a reaction source term** for a thermoset curing inside a mould, starting from the general first-law energy balance for a fluid element. State all boundary conditions used in RIM simulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a typical epoxy, $H_r = 3.5\times 10^{5}$ J/kg, $C_p = 1200$ J/(kg·K), and the resin enters the mould at $T_0 = 60\,^\circ$C. Compute (i) the adiabatic peak temperature, (ii) explain what mould-wall temperature $T_w$ would be required to keep the actual peak below $180\,^\circ$C, qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

We begin with the **first law of thermodynamics** for a fluid element of mass $dm = \rho\,dV$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of energy change} &= \text{Heat added by conduction} + \text{Heat added by source} - \text{Work done} \\
\rho\,C_p\,\frac{DT}{Dt} &= k\nabla^{2}T + \dot{q}_{\text{gen}} - \text{(viscous dissipation neglected)} \\
\rho\,C_p\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla T\right) &= k\nabla^{2}T + \rho_r H_r\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial t}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\dot{q}_{\text{gen}} = \rho_r H_r\,d\alpha/dt$ is the **volumetric heat generation rate** from cure.

**Boundary Conditions (RIM cavity):**

1. **At mould wall (Dirichlet, if isothermal):** $T(x,y,z=H/2,t) = T_w$ (mould wall temperature controlled by oil circulation).
2. **At symmetry plane (Neumann):** $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ (no heat flux across centreline for symmetric parts).
3. **At flow front:** $T = T_{\text{jet}}$ (jet temperature of incoming resin) for newly-filled region, evolving into a transient boundary after filling.
4. **At mould-metal interface (convective, if non-isothermal):**
   $$ -k\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\right|_{\text{wall}} = h\,(T_{\text{wall}} - T_{\text{mould,metal}}) $$

**Full PDE for post-filling curing (no flow):**

$$
\boxed{\;\rho C_p\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k\nabla^{2}T + \rho_r H_r \frac{\partial\alpha}{\partial t}\;}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Adiabatic peak temperature** (from the derivation in §3.3):

$$
T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 + \frac{H_r}{C_p} = 60 + \frac{3.5\times 10^{5}}{1200} = 60 + 291.67 = 351.67\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

**(ii) Required mould wall temperature:**

Since the adiabatic peak is $351.67\,^\circ$C (which *exceeds* $180\,^\circ$C), no isothermal $T_w$ can fully prevent overheating in the adiabatic limit. However, in *real* conditions, **conduction to the mould** ($k\nabla^{2}T$) drains heat continuously. To keep the peak below $180\,^\circ$C, a **low mould wall temperature** is required — typically:

$$
T_w \approx T_{\text{peak,desired}} - \frac{H_r}{2C_p} = 180 - \frac{291.67}{2} \approx 34\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

So a chilled mould with $T_w \approx 30$–$40\,^\circ$C is required, with **active cooling** (heat removal rate $\geq$ heat generation rate at peak). The simulation must solve the coupled PDE with realistic $T_w$ to verify the actual peak.

*(14 marks: 7 for (a) — 3 for derivation, 2 for boundary conditions, 2 for final boxed equation; 7 for (b) — 3 for (i) arithmetic, 4 for (ii) qualitative engineering reasoning.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. Forgetting the **convective term** $\mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla T$ during the filling stage, then deleting it incorrectly in the post-filling stage.
> 2. Using $H_r$ in J/g instead of J/kg (factor of 1000 error — catastrophic for the numerical answer).
> 3. Confusing **gel point** $\alpha_g$ (where flow stops) with **vitrification** $\alpha_v$ (where molecular mobility ceases).
> 4. Not specifying whether the Kamal model or nth-order model is being used — *always state the model explicitly*.

---

### Question B (B) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM)** process. List **three** industrial applications of RIM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Castro–Macosko viscosity model** for a curing thermoset, clearly identifying how the gel-point singularity arises in the model. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RIM Process:**

**Sketch (textual — to be drawn in exam):**

```
   Tank A (Polyol)         Tank B (Isocyanate)
        |                       |
        |                       |
      Pump A                  Pump B
        |                       |
        +---------+-------------+
                  |
            IMPINGEMENT MIXER (high pressure, ~150 bar)
                  |
            MIXING NOZZLE
                  |
            CLOSED MOULD CAVITY (held at Twall)
                  |
            In-situ polymerisation: Polyol + Isocyanate
                  +   chain extender + catalyst
                  |
            FINISHED PU PART (after demould time)
```

**Process steps:**
1. Two reactive liquid streams (polyol + isocyanate) are metered from separate tanks by high-pressure pumps.
2. The two streams impinge on each other in a small mixing chamber (vol ~ few cm³) at high pressure, achieving turbulent mixing in <0.5 s.
3. The mixed reactive stream is injected into a closed mould cavity at low pressure (~1–10 bar).
4. Inside the mould, polymerisation and cross-linking occur; gelation occurs at $\alpha = \alpha_g$ after a few seconds.
5. The part is held in the mould until sufficient strength develops (demould time), then ejected.

**Three industrial applications of RIM:**
1. Automotive bumpers and fascia (PU-RIM, glass-filled RRIM).
2. Shoe soles (microcellular PU).
3. Window gaskets and seals.
4. *(Optional — for extra credit)* Office furniture housings, refrigerator liners.

*(7 marks: 3 for sketch, 2 for process steps, 2 for three applications.)*

---

**(b) Derivation of the Castro–Macosko Model:**

We follow the derivation given in §3.2. The viscosity is decomposed as:

$$
\eta(T,\alpha,\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_T(T)\cdot\eta_\alpha(\alpha)\cdot\eta_{\dot{\gamma}}(\dot{\gamma})
$$

**Temperature factor** (Arrhenius, Eyring activated flow):

$$
\eta_T(T) = \eta_\infty \exp\!\left(\frac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)
$$

**Conversion factor** (gel-point singularity):

The molecular weight $M_w$ of a step-growth network diverges at the gel point as:

$$
M_w \propto \frac{1}{(\alpha_g - \alpha)} \quad \text{as } \alpha \to \alpha_g
$$

Since viscosity scales with molecular weight to a power ($\eta \sim M_w^{3.4}$ in reptation theory, or simply $\eta \sim M_w$ empirically for branched systems), we get:

$$
\eta_\alpha(\alpha) = \left(\frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g-\alpha}\right)^{A_1 + A_2\alpha}
$$

The form $(\alpha_g/(\alpha_g-\alpha))^{f(\alpha)}$ ensures that:
* $\eta_\alpha \to 1$ when $\alpha \to 0$ (liquid resin).
* $\eta_\alpha \to \infty$ as $\alpha \to \alpha_g$ (gelation — flow stops).

**Shear-rate factor** (power-law shear-thinning):

$$
\eta_{\dot{\gamma}}(\dot{\gamma}) = \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}
$$

**Combined model:**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta(T,\alpha,\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)\!\left(\frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g-\alpha}\right)^{A_1+A_2\alpha}\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}\;}
$$

**Physical meaning of the gel-point singularity:** The factor $(\alpha_g/(\alpha_g-\alpha))$ is the *fractional distance to gelation*. As $\alpha \to \alpha_g$, this factor blows up, reflecting the physical reality that the *infinite molecular weight* of an incipient gel network cannot flow.

*(7 marks: 2 for multiplicative decomposition, 2 for Arrhenius temperature factor, 2 for the gel-point derivation, 1 for the combined boxed form.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. Writing $\alpha$ instead of $\alpha_g$ in the denominator — the model is only valid for $\alpha < \alpha_g$; the gel point must be the *upper limit*, not 1.
> 2. Confusing the **gel point** $\alpha_g$ (chemically defined by Flory) with the **vitrification point** $\alpha_v$ (where $T_g$ of the system = cure temperature). They are *different* phenomena.
> 3. Omitting the shear-rate factor $\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ — this loses the non-Newtonian effect and the model degenerates to a Newtonian isothermal cure model.
> 4. Failing to state the model **assumes** $\alpha < \alpha_g$ at all times; the simulation must enforce this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — must memorise for KTU 2024**

* [x] **Kamal cure kinetics:** $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$ — used for **epoxy/thermoset**.
* [x] **nth-order cure kinetics:** $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(1-\alpha)^n$ — used for **polyurethane RIM**.
* [x] **Arrhenius form:** $k_i = A_i\exp(-E_i/RT)$ — always quoted with $E_i$ in J/mol and $T$ in K.
* [x] **Energy equation with cure source:** $\rho C_p\,\partial T/\partial t = k\nabla^2 T + \rho_r H_r\,\partial\alpha/\partial t$ — write this in **every** heat-transfer question on reactive processing.
* [x] **Castro–Macosko viscosity:** $\eta = \eta_\infty\exp(E_\eta/RT)\,(\alpha_g/(\alpha_g-\alpha))^{A_1+A_2\alpha}\,\dot\gamma^{n-1}$ — gel-point divergence is the key feature.
* [x] **Hele-Shaw flow** is used for thin-cavity filling (RIM, $H \ll L,W$).
* [x] **Adiabatic temperature rise:** $T_{\text{ad}} = T_0 + H_r/C_p$ — easy 3-mark question almost every year.
* [x] **Flory gel point:** $\alpha_c = 1/(f-1)$ for stoichiometric $f$-functional system.
* [x] **RIM** uses **impingement mixing** at high pressure, then low-pressure injection into a closed mould.
* [x] **Reactive extrusion** uses a screw extruder as a continuous chemical reactor (grafting, cross-linking, polymerisation).
* [x] **The four sub-models are strongly coupled** — iterative solution is required, not sequential.
* [x] **Gel point** vs. **vitrification** vs. **degradation** are three *different* transitions — do not confuse them.
* [x] **Units discipline:** $H_r$ in J/kg (NOT J/g), $E$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ in K throughout.
* [x] **Numerical methods:** Explicit Euler, Implicit Euler, or Runge-Kutta 4 for cure ODE; Finite Difference / Finite Element for PDEs.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second mnemonic — "KAReC":**
> **K**amal kinetics → **A**rrhenius $k$ → **R**eaction source $\rho_r H_r d\alpha/dt$ → **e**nergy equation → **C**astro–Macosko rheology. Run this loop iteratively, and you have simulated reactive polymer processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — POLYMER COMPOUNDING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Polymer Compounding** is the engineering process of physically mixing and homogenizing a base polymer (matrix) with one or more additives — such as fillers, reinforcements, plasticizers, stabilizers, colorants, flame retardants, and impact modifiers — using thermomechanical equipment (internal mixers, twin-screw extruders, two-roll mills) to produce a uniform compound with tailored processing behaviour, mechanical, thermal, rheological, and aesthetic properties suitable for downstream conversion (injection moulding, blow moulding, film extrusion, etc.).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the term **"compounding"** is distinct from **"blending"**: blending usually refers to polymer–polymer mixing, whereas compounding refers to polymer + additive mixing. Both fall under the broader umbrella of *mixing operations*.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cooking Pot" Intuition

Imagine you are preparing a **milkshake with fruits, sugar, and ice cream** in a blender.

- The **milk/ice cream** is your **base polymer** (e.g., Polypropylene, PVC, Nylon).
- The **fruits, sugar, and flavouring** are your **additives** (fillers, pigments, stabilizers).
- The **blender** is your **compounding machine** (twin-screw extruder).
- The **blade speed, blending time, and viscosity** decide how uniformly the fruit pieces get distributed.

> Just like you cannot have all fruit at the bottom and milk at the top in a good milkshake, a properly compounded polymer should have additives **dispersed and distributed** uniformly at micro and macro scales.

| Key Term | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Matrix** | The continuous phase — your base polymer |
| **Dispersed phase** | Additives, fillers, fibres broken into smaller domains |
| **Distribution** | Spatial uniformity of the dispersed phase |
| **Dispersion** | Size reduction of agglomerates into primary particles |
| **Filler loading** | Weight or volume % of filler in the compound |

### 1.2 Why Compounding is Industrially Critical

> [!NOTE]
> Most commercial plastics in the market are NOT pure polymers. They are **engineered compounds**. A simple PE carry-bag contains antioxidant + slip agent + antiblock agent. A car bumper contains PP + talc + EPDM rubber + pigments + UV stabilizer.

**Reasons to compound:**
1. **Property enhancement** — stiffness, toughness, flame retardancy.
2. **Processability improvement** — easier flow, less die swell.
3. **Cost reduction** — replace expensive polymer with cheap fillers.
4. **Stabilization** — prevent thermal/UV/oxidative degradation.
5. **Aesthetics** — colour, surface finish, texture.
6. **Functionalization** — antistatic, antimicrobial, conductive.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted in KTU 2024 Syllabus

- **Specific Energy Input (SEI)** — measured in **kWh/tonne**, typically **0.1 – 0.5 kWh/kg** for dispersive mixing.
- **Throughput (Q)** — **kg/h**, governed by screw speed, geometry, and melt viscosity.
- **Residence Time (t̄)** — **seconds**, typically **20–200 s** in twin-screw extruders.
- **Dispersive Mixing Index (σ²/σ₀²)** — dimensionless, ideally → 0 for perfect mixing.
- **Peclet Number (Pe)** — dimensionless, ratio of convection to diffusion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Mixing

Polymer compounding mixes ingredients through two fundamentally different mechanisms operating simultaneously:

#### 2.1.1 Distributive (Extensive) Mixing
- Goal: **Spatial homogeneity** of the minor component.
- Mechanism: Repeated **folding, stretching, and reorientation** of fluid elements.
- Driven by: **Convection** (bulk flow).
- Characterized by high **Peclet number (Pe ≫ 1)**.
- Process: laminar stretching → striation thickness reduction.

> **Striation Thickness (s) — Quantitative Measure of Distributive Mixing**

$$s = \frac{s_0}{\sqrt{N}}$$

where $s_0$ is the initial striation thickness, $N$ is the number of stretching operations. *Source principle: Spencer-Wiley model of laminar mixing.*

#### 2.1.2 Dispersive (Intensive) Mixing
- Goal: **Breakup of agglomerates** (filler clusters, carbon black aggregates).
- Mechanism: **Hydrodynamic shear stresses** exceeding cohesive strength of agglomerates.
- Requires: **Shear stress (τ) > Yield stress (σ_y)** of agglomerate.
- Driven by: **Viscous dissipation** in high-shear zones.

### 2.2 The Stress Criterion for Agglomerate Breakage

A filler agglomerate breaks when the local hydrodynamic stress exceeds the cohesive strength:

$$\eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} \geq \sigma_{y,\text{agg}}$$

- $\eta$ = matrix viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = local shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $\sigma_{y,\text{agg}}$ = cohesive yield strength of the agglomerate (typically 10⁵ – 10⁷ Pa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental design equation** of any dispersive mixing element (kneading disc, gear mixer) in a twin-screw extruder.

### 2.3 Rheological Foundation — Viscosity Models Used in Compounding Models

Polymers are **non-Newtonian shear-thinning** fluids. Compounding models require accurate viscosity laws:

**Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele):**

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Carreau-Yasuda Model (more accurate over wide shear range):**

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_{\infty} + \left( \eta_0 - \eta_{\infty} \right) \left[ 1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{a} \right]^{\frac{n-1}{a}}$$

where:
- $\eta_0$ = zero-shear viscosity
- $\eta_{\infty}$ = infinite-shear viscosity
- $\lambda$ = relaxation time (s)
- $n$ = power-law index
- $a$ = Yasuda parameter (transition breadth)

### 2.4 Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE) — Backbone of Industrial Compounding

A **co-rotating intermeshing TSE** is the workhorse. It has three functional zones:

| Zone | Function | Screw Element Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Feeding/Solids Conveying** | Meter polymer + additives | Wide-pitch conveying |
| **Melting/Plasticating** | Melt polymer pellets | Kneading discs, reverse elements |
| **Mixing/Dispersing** | Distribute + disperse additives | Kneading blocks, gear mixers |
| **Devolatilization** | Remove volatiles/moisture | Reverse elements + vacuum port |
| **Metering/Pumping** | Build pressure, control throughput | Conveying elements |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **must-know equations** for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Master these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drag flow in extruder | $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\theta\cos\theta$ | D=barrel dia (m), N=rotation (rev/s), H=channel depth (m), θ=helix angle | Maximum theoretical throughput |
| 2 | Pressure flow | $Q_p = \tfrac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\, L}\,\Delta P$ | ΔP=pressure rise (Pa), L=axial length (m) | Back-flow due to die resistance |
| 3 | Net throughput | $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ | (m³/s) | Actual extruder output |
| 4 | Power consumption | $P = 2\pi N T$ | T=torque (N·m) | Drives sizing of motor |
| 5 | Specific energy | $SE = P/Q$ | J/kg or kWh/tonne | Quality of mixing indicator |
| 6 | Shear rate in channel | $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / H$ | s⁻¹ | Determines viscous heating |
| 7 | Striation thickness | $s = s_0 / \sqrt{N}$ | m | Distributive mixing quality |
| 8 | Dispersive criterion | $\eta \dot{\gamma} \geq \sigma_{y,\text{agg}}$ | Pa | Agglomerate breakage |
| 9 | Power-law viscosity | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Pa·s | Shear-thinning flow |
| 10 | Carreau–Yasuda | $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)[1+(\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{a}]^{(n-1)/a}$ | Pa·s | Wide-range rheology |
| 11 | Mixing index | $I = \sigma^{2}/\sigma_{0}^{2}$ | dimensionless | Lower = better mixing |
| 12 | Peclet number | $Pe = \bar{t}\, v / L$ | dimensionless | Convection vs diffusion |
| 13 | RTD axial model | $E(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{Pe}{\pi\theta}}\,\exp\!\left[-\tfrac{Pe}{4\theta}(1-\theta)^{2}\right]$ | θ= t/t̄ | Spread of residence times |
| 14 | Degree of fill | $\phi = \dot{m}/(\rho \cdot V_{free}\cdot N_{actual})$ | 0–1 | Conveying/mixing regime |
| 15 | Tadmor-Klein scaling | $Q_2 = Q_1 (D_2/D_1)^{2.5}$ | kg/h | Geometric scale-up of mixers |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of This System

The above equations are used by:
- **Process engineers** to size motors ($P$), predict throughput ($Q$).
- **Compounding equipment designers** (Coperion, Leistritz, KraussMaffei) to develop new screw profiles.
- **Polymer modellers** to couple CFD (computational fluidodynamics) with rheology for virtual compounding trials (Digital Twin).
- **R&D scientists** to scale-up a lab recipe (kg) to plant scale (tonnes).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Throughput Equation of a Single-Screw Extruder (Newtonian Fluid)

The classic **drag-flow / pressure-flow** model is the KTU 2024 ESE favourite. We derive it rigorously.

**Assumptions:**
- Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\eta$.
- Unwound screw channel approximated as a flat rectangular slot of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e \approx \pi D \sin\theta$, depth $H$, length $L$.
- Steady state, fully developed flow.
- No leakage over flights.

**Step 1 — Coordinate Transformation (Screw Unwinding)**

The helical channel is transformed into a flat plate by rotating coordinates. The upper wall (barrel) moves at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ at angle $\theta$ to the channel.

The down-channel component of barrel velocity is:

$$V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Pressure-Gradient Decomposition**

The total flow $Q$ is the **superposition** of:
- Drag flow $Q_d$ (caused by relative barrel motion).
- Pressure flow $Q_p$ (caused by die-induced pressure gradient $\Delta P/L$).

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p$$

**Step 3 — Drag Flow (Couette Component)**

For a Newtonian fluid between two parallel plates, with the top plate moving at $V_{bz}$:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} V_{bz} \cdot W \cdot H$$

Substituting $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W = \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot \pi D \sin\theta \cdot H$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_d = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure Flow (Poiseuille Component)**

The pressure gradient is $-\Delta P/L$. The volumetric flow for Poiseuille flow in a rectangular slot is:

$$Q_p = \frac{W H^{3}}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Substituting $W = \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$Q_p = \frac{\pi D \sin\theta \cdot H^{3}}{12 \eta L} \cdot \Delta P$$

**Step 5 — Net Throughput**

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$

This is the **fundamental extruder equation** (Tadmor & Gogos, 2006). For TSE, the analysis is similar but applied to the C-shaped chamber between screw and barrel, modified by intermeshing geometry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Distributive Mixing: Striation Thickness Reduction

Consider a striated fluid element of initial thickness $s_0$ undergoing **uniaxial elongation** by stretch ratio $\lambda_i$ in $i$-th operation.

**Step 1 — Single-Stretch Reduction**

In a 2D laminar flow, area is conserved. If element length increases by $\lambda$, thickness decreases by $1/\lambda$:

$$s_1 = s_0 / \lambda_1$$

**Step 2 — After N Sequential Stretches**

The total thickness is the product:

$$s_N = \frac{s_0}{\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 \cdots \lambda_N} = \frac{s_0}{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i}$$

If each stretch is on average $\lambda$ (geometric mean), then:

$$\boxed{\,s_N = \frac{s_0}{\lambda^{N}} \approx \frac{s_0}{\sqrt{N}}\,}$$

The $\sqrt{N}$ form holds for **random orientation** of striations (isotropic flow), which is a realistic assumption in 3D extruder flow fields. This is the **Spencer & Wiley / Mohr-Saxton** mixing rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** To halve the striation thickness of a poorly distributed mix, you need 4× more stretching operations. This is why **long kneading blocks** in a TSE dramatically improve distributive quality.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Input (SEI) and Mixing Quality

**Step 1 — Power Dissipated by Screw**

For a given screw configuration, the viscous dissipation rate is:

$$P = 2 \pi N T$$

where $T$ is the measured torque on the screw shaft.

**Step 2 — Throughput in Mass Units**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q$$

where $Q$ is from the throughput equation (Section 3.1).

**Step 3 — Specific Energy Definition**

$$SE = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{\rho Q} \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$

Often expressed as:

$$SE\,[\text{kWh/tonne}] = \frac{P\,[\text{kW}]}{\dot{m}\,[\text{tonne/h}]}$$

**Step 4 — Linking SEI to Dispersive Mixing**

Empirically, dispersion quality (e.g., carbon black dispersion rating) follows a master curve with SEI. Above a critical $SE^*$ (≈ 0.1 kWh/kg for most fillers), dispersion is essentially complete:

$$\text{Quality} \propto 1 - \exp(-SE / SE^{*})$$

> For **KBT 2024 ESE**, you may be asked: *"Why is SEI preferred over RPM or residence time as a scale-up parameter?"*
> **Answer:** SEI encapsulates the combined effect of shear rate, viscosity, and residence time, making it geometry-independent — thus a true **scale-up invariant**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Residence Time Distribution (RTD) and Axial Mixing

A compounding extruder behaves as a **non-ideal flow reactor** characterized by an axial dispersion coefficient $D_{ax}$.

**Step 1 — Mean Residence Time**

$$\bar{t} = \frac{V_{\text{free}}}{\dot{m}/\rho} = \frac{\rho V_{\text{free}}}{\dot{m}}$$

**Step 2 — Dimensionless Time**

$$\theta = t / \bar{t}$$

**Step 3 — Axial Dispersion Model (ADM) — Closed-Closed Boundary**

The Danckwerts solution for an impulse tracer input:

$$E(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{Pe}{\pi \theta}} \exp\!\left[ -\frac{Pe}{4 \theta} (1 - \theta)^{2} \right]$$

where the **Peclet number** is:

$$Pe = \frac{\bar{t} \, v}{D_{ax} L}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting Cases**

- $Pe \to \infty$ (plug flow): $E(\theta) \to \delta(\theta - 1)$ — perfect, no spread.
- $Pe \to 0$ (CSTR): $E(\theta) \to \exp(-\theta)$ — exponential decay.
- Industrial TSE typically: $Pe \approx 5 - 30$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **narrow RTD** is desired for **uniform thermal/mechanical history**; a **wide RTD** is desired for **better mixing** (elements experience different shear levels). There is a trade-off!

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Compounding Equipment

| Equipment | Dispersive Mixing | Distributive Mixing | Residence Time | Throughput | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-roll mill** | Excellent | Poor | Very high (min) | Low | Masterbatches, PVC |
| **Banbury (internal) batch** | Excellent | Moderate | 1–5 min | Medium | Rubber, PVC, masterbatch |
| **Single-screw extruder** | Poor | Poor | Medium | High | Limited compounding |
| **Co-rotating TSE** | Excellent | Excellent | 20–200 s | Very high | Most modern compounding |
| **Counter-rotating TSE** | Good | Moderate | High | Medium | PVC, devolatilization |
| **Continuous mixer (FCM)** | Excellent | Good | Medium | High | High-filler compounds |
| **Ko-Kneader** | Moderate | Excellent | Long | Medium | Heat-sensitive polymers |
| **Buss-Ko-Kneader** | Good | Excellent | Medium | High | Cable compounds, TPR |

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 60 mm single-screw extruder is processing PP at $T = 220°\text{C}$ with the following data:
- Barrel inner diameter: $D = 0.060$ m
- Channel depth: $H = 0.005$ m
- Helix angle: $\theta = 17.65°$ (i.e., $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$)
- Screw speed: $N = 1.5$ rev/s = 90 rpm
- Pressure rise: $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- Melt viscosity: $\eta = 1000$ Pa·s
- Melt density: $\rho = 740$ kg/m³

**Find:** (a) Drag flow $Q_d$, (b) Pressure flow $Q_p$, (c) Net throughput $Q$, (d) Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$, (e) Specific energy if torque is $T = 250$ N·m.

**Solution:**

**(a) Drag flow**

$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^{2} (0.060)^{2}(1.5)(0.005)(0.303)(0.953)}{2}$$

Let me compute step-by-step:

$$Q_d = \frac{(9.8696)(0.0036)(1.5)(0.005)(0.2888)}{2}$$

$$Q_d = \frac{(9.8696)(0.0036)(0.002166)}{2}$$

$$Q_d = \frac{7.696 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 3.85 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**(b) Pressure flow**

$$Q_p = \frac{\pi (0.060)(0.005)^{3}(0.303)^{2}(5 \times 10^{6})}{12(1000)(L)}$$

We need $L$ — assume $L/D = 20$ so $L = 1.2$ m:

$$Q_p = \frac{(3.1416)(0.060)(1.25 \times 10^{-7})(0.0918)(5 \times 10^{6})}{12(1000)(1.2)}$$

$$Q_p = \frac{1.082 \times 10^{-5}}{14400} = 7.51 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

(Pressure flow is negligible vs drag flow — typical for an open discharge.)

**(c) Net throughput**

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**(d) Mass flow**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 740 \times 3.85 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0285\,\text{kg/s} = 102.5\,\text{kg/h}$$

**(e) Specific Energy**

$$P = 2\pi N T = 2\pi (1.5)(250) = 2356\,\text{W}$$

$$SE = \frac{2356}{0.0285} = 82{,}667\,\text{J/kg} = 0.0827\,\text{kWh/kg} = 82.7\,\text{kWh/tonne}$$

> This is in the **typical range (50–250 kWh/tonne)** for an industrial compounding line, indicating **good but not extreme** mixing intensity.
