## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
>
> **Q1.** Differentiate between *distributive mixing* and *dispersive mixing* with one industrial example for each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Distributive Mixing | Dispersive Mixing |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Goal** | Spatial uniformity of minor component | Size reduction of agglomerates |
> | **Mechanism** | Laminar stretching & folding | Rupture under hydrodynamic stress |
> | **Equation** | $s = s_0/\sqrt{N}$ (striation thinning) | $\eta \dot{\gamma} \geq \sigma_{y,\text{agg}}$ |
> | **Driving force** | Bulk convection (Pe ≫ 1) | Local shear stress |
> | **Industrial example** | Distributing colour masterbatch in PP via gear-mixer | Breaking carbon-black agglomerates in tire-tread rubber via kneading discs |
> | **Measure** | Coefficient of variation of concentration | Particle/aggregate size distribution |
>
> **(2 marks for distinction table; 1 mark for example.)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** Define **Specific Energy Input (SEI)**. Why is it used as a scale-up parameter in polymer compounding?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Specific Energy Input is the mechanical energy delivered to the polymer melt per unit mass, defined as:
>
> $$SE = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = \frac{2\pi N T}{\rho Q} \quad \text{[J/kg or kWh/tonne]}$$
>
> It is preferred over RPM or residence time for scale-up because (i) it combines shear rate, viscosity, and residence time into a single **geometry-independent** quantity, (ii) it correlates directly with **dispersive mixing quality** (master curve vs. SEI), and (iii) it allows comparison between machines of different sizes, screw designs, and operating points. **(1 mark definition + 2 marks for three reasons.)**

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice-1**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
>
> **Q(a).** With the help of a clearly labelled diagram, describe the **construction and working of a co-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruder (TSE)** for polymer compounding. Discuss the role of each functional zone. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> A co-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruder consists of **two identical screws mounted on parallel shafts** rotating in the same direction, housed inside a figure-of-eight barrel. The screws intermesh, with one screw's flight wiping the channel of the other.
>
> **Functional Zones (with screw elements):**
>
> 1. **Solids Conveying Zone (Feeding)** — Wide-pitch (≈ 1D) right-handed conveying elements. Polymer pellets and additives enter the hopper. Mechanical conveying pushes material forward by frictional drag against the barrel.
>
> 2. **Melting/Plasticating Zone** — Combination of **kneading discs** (45°–90° staggering) and reverse-pitch elements. Kneading discs create high-shear regions that melt the polymer and wet the filler surfaces.
>
> 3. **Mixing/Dispersing Zone** — High stagger-angle (90°) kneading blocks for **dispersive mixing**; gear-type (toothed) mixers for **distributive mixing**. The C-shaped chamber between screw and barrel experiences high local shear ($\dot{\gamma} \sim 10^{3}$–$10^{4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$).
>
> 4. **Devolatilization Zone** — Reverse elements create a **pressure peak** upstream; downstream a vacuum port removes volatiles, monomers, moisture.
>
> 5. **Metering/Discharge Zone** — Conveying elements build pressure to push melt through the die.
>
> **Why co-rotating?** It is **self-wiping** — the relative motion of one flight across the other's channel prevents material stagnation, gives **short residence time distribution**, and enables **good mixing at high throughput**. **(Marks: 1 diagram + 1 zone identification + 1 mechanism per zone, ≈ 5 marks + 2 marks for self-wiping justification.)**
>
> **Block diagram (text representation):**
>
> `[Feeding Hopper] → [Conveying] → [Kneading 45°] → [Kneading 90°] → [Gear Mixer] → [Reverse + Vacuum] → [Conveying] → [Die → Pelletizer]`

> **Q(b).** Derive the **net throughput equation** for a single-screw extruder processing a Newtonian polymer melt. State clearly all assumptions. How does the equation modify for **co-rotating TSE**? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Assumptions** (1 mark):
> 1. Newtonian fluid of constant viscosity $\eta$.
> 2. Helical channel unwound into a flat rectangular slot of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta$, depth $H$, length $L$.
> 3. Steady-state, fully developed, laminar flow.
> 4. No flight leakage.
> 5. Isothermal, no slip at walls.
>
> **Derivation Steps** (4 marks — see Section 3.1 for full derivation):
>
> - Drag flow (from barrel moving at $V_b = \pi D N$):
>
> $$Q_d = \frac{1}{2}\pi D N \cos\theta \cdot \pi D \sin\theta \cdot H = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$$
>
> - Pressure flow (Poiseuille component due to $\Delta P/L$):
>
> $$Q_p = \frac{\pi D \sin\theta \cdot H^{3}}{12 \eta L}\cdot \Delta P$$
>
> - Net throughput:
>
> $$Q = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$
>
> **Modification for Co-rotating TSE** (2 marks):
> - The "channel" becomes a **C-shaped chamber** bounded by the screw root, the barrel, the leading flight of the same screw, and the trailing flight of the mating screw.
> - The width is $W_C = \pi D \sin\theta - e/\cos\theta$ where $e$ is flight width.
> - Additional **calender flow** and **leakage flow** over the intermeshing region must be subtracted.
> - The net equation retains the form $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_{leak}$ with $Q_{leak}$ being geometry-dependent (Janssen & Meijer model).
> - In TSE, the operation is typically **starve-fed** (Q set by feeder, not screw), so the **degree of fill $\phi < 1$** — this *decouples* throughput from screw speed.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice-2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
>
> **Q(a).** Explain the **rheological modelling** of a compounding polymer melt. Derive the **Carreau–Yasuda** model and show how it reduces to the power-law at high shear rates. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> Polymer melts are **non-Newtonian** and exhibit:
> 1. **Shear-thinning** — viscosity drops with shear rate.
> 2. **Viscosity plateau** at low shear (Newtonian region) and high shear (second Newtonian region).
> 3. **Elastic effects** — die swell, normal stress differences (not captured by viscosity alone).
>
> **Carreau-Yasuda Model Derivation** (5 marks):
>
> The starting point is the **generalized Cross / Carreau** form, fitted to data:
>
> $$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_{\infty} + \left( \eta_0 - \eta_{\infty} \right) \left[ 1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{a} \right]^{\frac{n-1}{a}}$$
>
> where:
> - $\eta_0$ = zero-shear viscosity
> - $\eta_{\infty}$ = infinite-shear viscosity (often $\approx 0$ for polymers)
> - $\lambda$ = characteristic relaxation time (s)
> - $n$ = power-law index ($0 < n < 1$ for shear-thinning)
> - $a$ = Yasuda parameter controlling the breadth of transition
>
> **Limiting cases** (1 mark):
> - $\dot{\gamma} \to 0$: $\eta \to \eta_0$ (zero-shear plateau).
> - $\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$: $\eta \to \eta_{\infty}$ (infinite-shear plateau).
>
> **Reduction to Power Law** at high shear rate (1 mark):
>
> For $(\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{a} \gg 1$ and $\eta_{\infty} \ll \eta_0$:
>
> $$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) \approx \eta_0 \left[ (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{a} \right]^{\frac{n-1}{a}} = \eta_0 \lambda^{n-1} \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$
>
> Define $K = \eta_0 \lambda^{n-1}$ (consistency index):
>
> $$\boxed{\,\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}\,}$$
>
> This is the **Ostwald–de Waele power law**, valid in the high-shear region typical of extruder channels ($\dot{\gamma} \approx 100$–$10{,}000\,\text{s}^{-1}$).

> **Q(b).** What is the **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)**? Derive the **Axial Dispersion Model** expression for a TSE, and explain how RTD affects compounding uniformity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** RTD is the probability distribution $E(t)$ (or $E(\theta)$) of the time individual fluid elements spend in the extruder. It is measured by injecting a tracer (e.g., coloured pigment pulse) at the feed and recording its concentration at the die.
>
> **Derivation (4 marks):**
>
> The axial dispersion model is based on Fick's-law modification of plug flow with a superimposed back-mixing. The convection–diffusion equation is:
>
> $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + v \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = D_{ax} \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial z^{2}}$$
>
> For a pulse input at $z = 0$, $t = 0$, with closed-closed boundaries (Danckwerts, 1953), the solution is:
>
> $$E(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{Pe}{\pi \theta}}\,\exp\!\left[-\frac{Pe}{4\theta}\left(1 - \theta\right)^{2}\right]$$
>
> where $\theta = t/\bar{t}$ and the Peclet number is:
>
> $$Pe = \frac{\bar{t}\, v}{D_{ax} L} = \frac{v L}{D_{ax}}$$
>
> **Effect on Compounding Uniformity (2 marks):**
>
> - **Narrow RTD** (high $Pe$, $Pe \to \infty$): All elements see the same thermal/mechanical history → uniform properties. Preferred for **temperature-sensitive polymers** (e.g., PVC) and **colour uniformity**.
> - **Wide RTD** (low $Pe$): Elements see different shear and temperature → some get over-worked (degraded) and others under-mixed (poor dispersion). This is beneficial for **mixing efficiency** because a wider RTD increases the probability of interaction between different striations, but at the cost of **property consistency**.
> - In practice, screw profile is tuned to balance: **reverse elements widen RTD** (more mixing), **conveying elements narrow it** (more uniformity).
> - **Industrial design goal:** $Pe \approx 5$–$30$ is common in TSE compounding; validated using a tracer pulse experiment with salt or pigment.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Polymer Compounding Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing compounding with blending.** Compounding = polymer + additives. Blending = polymer + polymer. Examiners explicitly check this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions** before derivations. The throughput derivation needs Newtonain fluid + slot approximation + isothermal + no-leakage. Skipping these = −2 marks.
>
> 3. **Units mismatch in numericals.** Mixing kW with kg/s instead of converting through ρ. Always verify the dimensional consistency of your final answer.
>
> 4. **Mixing up dispersive vs. distributive.** Dispersive = size reduction (kneading). Distributive = spatial uniformity (gear). Don't swap.
>
> 5. **Using $\sqrt{N}$ striation formula for ordered (not random) flow.** Random orientation gives $\sqrt{N}$; ordered (e.g., single-axis) gives $N$ in the denominator. The question wording must be parsed carefully.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the pressure flow subtraction** in $Q = Q_d - Q_p$. Many students write $Q = Q_d$ alone. This is a guaranteed −2 mark penalty.
>
> 7. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** = guaranteed loss of 1–2 marks. Always include a labelled block diagram or schematic.
>
> 8. **Neglecting $\eta_{\infty}$** in Carreau-Yasuda reductions. At high shear, the correct asymptotic is the **power-law** form $K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$, with $K = \eta_0 \lambda^{n-1}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (15 Must-Know Items)**
>
> - [x] **Compounding = Polymer + Additives** (NOT polymer + polymer; that's blending).
> - [x] **Distributive mixing → striation thickness** $s = s_0/\sqrt{N}$.
> - [x] **Dispersive mixing → agglomerate rupture** when $\eta \dot{\gamma} \geq \sigma_{y,\text{agg}}$.
> - [x] **Throughput equation:** $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ (both terms must be written).
> - [x] **Specific Energy Input** $SE = P/\dot{m}$ is the **scale-up invariant**.
> - [x] **Carreau-Yasuda** reduces to **power law** at high $\dot{\gamma}$.
> - [x] **RTD model** → Danckwerts solution with **Peclet number**.
> - [x] **Co-rotating TSE is self-wiping** — narrow RTD, high throughput, dominant for compounding.
> - [x] **Kneading discs (90°)** = dispersive; **Gear mixers** = distributive.
> - [x] **Screw speed (N)** ↑ ⇒ shear rate ↑ ⇒ dispersive mixing ↑.
> - [x] **Fillers** reduce cost but increase viscosity (use viscosity models with filler concentration, e.g., Mooney or Krieger–Dougherty).
> - [x] **Reverse elements** create local pressure peaks → force fillers through high-shear zones.
> - [x] **Tadmor–Klein scaling:** $Q_2/Q_1 = (D_2/D_1)^{2.5}$ for geometric similarity.
> - [x] **SEI range** = 50–250 kWh/tonne for typical compounding.
> - [x] **Twin-screw extruder** is the workhorse of modern compounding; **single-screw** is unsuitable for additives > 5% loading.
>
> **Mnemonic — "DDS-PRC"** for the 6 main additive classes:
> **D**yes & pigments, **D**efoamers, **S**tabilizers (UV/thermal), **P**lasticizers, **R**einforcements (fibres), **C**atalysts/crosslinkers. Add **F**illers (CaCO₃, talc) and **FR** (flame retardants) for the full list.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capillary flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capillary flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Capillary flow** refers to the *pressure-driven, fully-developed, isothermal, incompressible flow* of a polymer melt or polymer solution through a cylindrical tube (capillary) of small bore diameter $D$ and length $L$. It is the foundational constitutive flow geometry used to characterize the **rheological response** of polymeric fluids under high shear rates typically encountered in polymer processing operations such as extrusion, injection molding, and fiber spinning.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, capillary flow forms the analytical backbone of **capillary rheometry**, which is the standard experimental method for measuring shear viscosity $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ of polymer melts at shear rates $\dot{\gamma} \in [1,\; 10^{6}]\;\text{s}^{-1}$.

Mathematically, capillary flow of a viscous fluid is described by the steady-state, fully-developed form of the **Navier–Stokes equation** in cylindrical coordinates $(r,\theta,z)$:

$$\rho v_z \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right)\right] + \rho g_z$$

For fully-developed laminar flow, the convective inertia term $\rho v_z (\partial v_z / \partial z) = 0$, and the equation reduces to a simple **ordinary differential equation** in $r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4, Capillary Flow**
> Students must master the derivation of velocity profiles for both **Newtonian fluids** (parabolic Poiseuille profile) and **non-Newtonian Power-law fluids** (flattened/plug-like profile), along with the two classical corrections — the **Bagley correction** (end pressure losses) and the **Rabinowitsch correction** (non-Newtonian shear rate).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Polymer Squeeze"

Imagine you are squeezing a thick toothpaste tube. The toothpaste (a polymer melt) is forced by your hand-pressure through the narrow nozzle (the capillary). The flow is:

- **Slow near the walls** (where the polymer "sticks" to the nozzle surface),
- **Fastest along the centerline** (where there is least resistance).

Now imagine the toothpaste is *thinning* as you press harder — this is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) behavior**, characteristic of almost all polymer melts. A capillary rheometer mimics this geometry precisely with controlled pressure drop $\Delta P$ and measured volumetric flow rate $Q$, from which we deduce the *viscosity vs. shear rate* master curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Capillary Flow Matters in Polymer Engineering**
> The capillary geometry is the most common flow path in industrial polymer processing — extruder dies, spinnerets for synthetic fibers, and injection-mold runner/gate systems are all miniaturized capillaries. The mathematical analysis of capillary flow therefore directly predicts the **pressure drop**, **output rate**, and **die-swell (extrudate swell)** of these real processes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|----------|--------|----------------------|--------------|
| Capillary diameter | $D$ | $0.5$ – $2.0$ mm | Industrial capillary rheometer standard |
| Capillary length-to-diameter ratio | $L/D$ | $10$ – $40$ | Long die assumption validity |
| Wall shear stress | $\tau_w$ | $10^{4}$ – $10^{5}$ Pa | Polymer melt range |
| Apparent shear rate | $\dot{\gamma}_a$ | $10$ – $10^{4}$ s$^{-1}$ | Processing range |
| Polymer melt density | $\rho$ | $700$ – $1100$ kg/m$^3$ | Typical for PE, PP, PS |
| Bagley correction factor | $n_c$ | $1$ – $15$ | End-effect magnitude |
| Rabinowitsch correction exponent | $n$ | $0.2$ – $0.7$ | Pseudoplasticity index |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.81}$ m/s² | Used in reservoir head correction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Layer-by-Layer Logical Breakdown

### Step 1: Governing Equation Setup
For steady, laminar, fully-developed, axisymmetric flow in a cylindrical capillary of radius $R = D/2$, the only non-zero velocity component is $v_z(r)$. The Navier–Stokes $z$-momentum equation reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\tau_{rz}\right) = \frac{dP}{dz}$$

where $P = p - \rho g z$ is the *modified pressure* (piezometric pressure) and the total pressure gradient $dP/dz$ is constant and negative for flow in the $+z$ direction.

> **Why this step?** The simplification is valid because $v_\theta = v_r = 0$, $v_z = v_z(r)$ only, and the convective inertia vanishes in fully-developed flow.

### Step 2: Newtonian Constitutive Substitution
For a **Newtonian fluid**, $\tau_{rz} = -\eta (dv_z/dr)$, giving:

$$\eta\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{dP}{dz}$$

### Step 3: Boundary Conditions (BCs)
- **No-slip at wall:** $v_z(R) = 0$
- **Finite velocity at centerline:** $dv_z/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$ (symmetry)

### Step 4: Integration & Parabolic Velocity Profile (Hagen–Poiseuille Law)
Two successive integrations yield the **parabolic velocity profile**:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\eta}\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

with **maximum (centerline) velocity** $v_{\max} = v_z(0) = \dfrac{R^{2}}{4\eta}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$.

### Step 5: Volumetric Flow Rate (Newtonian)
Integration of the parabolic profile over the tube cross-section gives the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\eta}\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right) = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{128\,\eta}\cdot\frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

### Step 6: Wall Shear Stress & Apparent Shear Rate
The wall shear stress is obtained from a **force balance on a cylindrical fluid element**:

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{D\,\Delta P}{4L}$$

The **apparent (Newtonian) shear rate** is defined by assuming a parabolic profile:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32\,Q}{\pi D^{3}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

The **apparent viscosity** then is:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{\pi D^{4}\,\Delta P}{128\,L\,Q}$$

### Step 7: Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Generalization
Polymer melts obey the **Power-law model** (a constitutive equation in the KTU syllabus):

$$\tau_{rz} = -K\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr}$$

where $K$ is the **consistency index** (Pa·s$^n$) and $n$ is the **power-law index** (dimensionless).

Substituting this in the momentum equation and integrating with no-slip BC gives the **flattened velocity profile**:

$$v_z(r) = \left(\frac{-\frac{1}{2}\frac{dP}{dz}}{K}\right)^{1/n}\!\frac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{\frac{n+1}{n}} - r^{\frac{n+1}{n}}\right)$$

The corresponding **volumetric flow rate** is:

$$Q = \pi\left(\frac{-\frac{1}{2}\frac{dP}{dz}}{K}\right)^{1/n}\!\frac{n}{3n+1}\,R^{\frac{3n+1}{n}}$$

The **true wall shear rate** for a Power-law fluid is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \left(\frac{-\frac{dP}{dz}}{2K}\right)^{1/n}\!\frac{3n+1}{4n} = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot \frac{3n+1}{4n}$$

This is the famous **Rabinowitsch equation**.

### Step 8: Bagley Correction
Polymer melts exhibit **entrance (end) pressure losses** $\Delta P_{end}$ in addition to the fully-developed die pressure $\Delta P_{fd}$. The total pressure drop is:

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{fd} + \Delta P_{end} = 2\tau_w\,\frac{L}{R} + \Delta P_{end}$$

Plotting $\Delta P_{\text{total}}$ vs. $L/D$ at constant $\dot{\gamma}_a$ yields a straight line whose **negative x-intercept** is the **Bagley correction factor** $n_c$:

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 2\tau_w\left(\frac{L}{D} + n_c\right)$$

The corrected (true) wall shear stress becomes:

$$\tau_{w,\text{true}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}}{2\left(\dfrac{L}{D} + n_c\right)}$$

### Step 9: Wall Slip in Capillary Flow
At high shear stresses, polymer melts may **slip at the wall**, characterized by a slip velocity $v_s$. The Mooney method detects slip by comparing flow rates in capillaries of different radii at the same wall shear stress:

$$v_s(\tau_w) = \frac{8\,Q_{\text{slip}} / \pi D^{3}}{1} = \text{function of }\tau_w$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}\,\Delta P}{128\,\eta\,L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow rate (Newtonian) | m³/s |
| 2 | $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\eta L}\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)$ | Parabolic velocity profile | m/s |
| 3 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{D\,\Delta P}{4L}$ | Wall shear stress (force balance) | Pa |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{32\,Q}{\pi D^{3}}$ | Apparent (Newtonian) wall shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}\,\Delta P}{128\,L\,Q}$ | Apparent viscosity | Pa·s |
| 6 | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P\,D^{2}}{16\,\eta\,L}$ | Centerline (max) velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $Q = \pi\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2K L}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{3n+1}R^{(3n+1)/n}$ | Power-law volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dot{\gamma}_a\!\left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)$ | Rabinowitsch correction (true shear rate) | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\!\left(\dfrac{\tau_w}{K}\right)^{1/n}$ | Power-law shear rate at wall | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{tot} = 2\tau_w\!\left(\dfrac{L}{D}+n_c\right)$ | Bagley-corrected pressure drop | Pa |
| 11 | $v_s = \dfrac{8}{\pi D^{3}}\!\left(Q_{R}-\!\dfrac{Q_{R'}\,R^{3}}{R'^{3}}\right)\!\Big/\!\left(1-\!\dfrac{R^{3}}{R'^{3}}\right)$ | Mooney wall-slip velocity | m/s |
| 12 | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic reservoir correction | Pa |
| 13 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\eta} = \dfrac{\rho D^{2} \dot{\gamma}_a}{32 \eta}$ | Reynolds number for tube flow | — |
| 14 | $\text{Re}_{gen} = \dfrac{\rho D^{n} v^{2-n}}{K\!\left(\tfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^{n}}$ | Generalized Reynolds number (Power-law) | — |

> **Real-World Utility in Polymer Engineering**
> - **Capillary rheometer data** is fed into process simulation packages (e.g., Moldflow, Polyflow) to predict fill times and pressure profiles in injection molding.
> - **Bagley + Rabinowitsch corrections** are mandatory in raw rheometer data reduction — without them, viscosity data of polymer melts is grossly inaccurate at high $\dot{\gamma}$.
> - **Wall-slip detection** is critical for fluoropolymer processing (PTFE, FEP) where slip dramatically affects extrusion throughput.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Parabolic Velocity Profile for Newtonian Capillary Flow

### Given
A Newtonian fluid of constant viscosity $\eta$ flows under steady-state in a horizontal, cylindrical capillary of radius $R$ and length $L$ under an imposed pressure drop $\Delta P = P_0 - P_L$.

### Assumptions
1. Steady-state: $\partial(\cdot)/\partial t = 0$
2. Laminar, fully-developed: $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$
3. Axisymmetric: $v_\theta = 0$, $v_r = 0$
4. Negligible gravity: $g_z = 0$ (horizontal tube)

### Step 1 — Cylindrical Navier–Stokes $z$-momentum equation

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial\theta} + v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial \theta^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial z^{2}}\right] + \rho g_z$$

### Step 2 — Apply assumptions to simplify
With $v_r = v_\theta = 0$ and $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$, the left side is identically zero. On the right side, the $\theta$-derivative vanishes (axisymmetric) and the $z$-derivative vanishes (fully-developed). The reduced form is:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the pressure gradient is constant
In a fully-developed flow, the pressure is linear in $z$:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L} = -\frac{P_0 - P_L}{L}$$

so it is independent of $r$. Rewriting the equation:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

### Step 4 — First integration (multiply by $r$ and integrate)

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\Delta P\,r}{\mu L}$$

Integrating both sides with respect to $r$:

$$r\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P\,r^{2}}{2\mu L} + C_1$$

### Step 5 — Apply symmetry BC at the centerline
At $r = 0$, the velocity gradient must vanish (no singularity, by symmetry of the profile):

$$\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{r=0} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 = 0$$

Hence:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P\,r}{2\mu L}$$

### Step 6 — Second integration

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P\,r^{2}}{4\mu L} + C_2$$

### Step 7 — Apply no-slip BC at the wall
At $r = R$, $v_z(R) = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P\,R^{2}}{4\mu L} + C_2 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = \frac{\Delta P\,R^{2}}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 8 — Final parabolic velocity profile

$$\boxed{\,v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\,}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille parabolic profile**, with maximum at the centerline ($r=0$).

> **Physical Interpretation:** The profile is a downward-opening paraboloid. The centerline velocity $v_{\max} = \Delta P\,R^{2}/(4\mu L)$ scales with the *square* of the radius — a small die-size reduction causes a large velocity drop.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Volumetric Flow Rate $Q$ (Hagen–Poiseuille)

The volumetric flow rate is the integral of the velocity profile over the tube cross-section:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r)\,(2\pi r)\,dr$$

### Step 1 — Substitute the parabolic profile

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)(2\pi r)\,dr = \frac{\pi\Delta P}{2\mu L}\int_{0}^{R}\left(R^{2}r - r^{3}\right)dr$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the integral

$$\int_{0}^{R}\left(R^{2}r - r^{3}\right)dr = \left[\frac{R^{2}r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Assemble the result

$$Q = \frac{\pi\Delta P}{2\mu L}\cdot\frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi R^{4}\Delta P}{8\mu L}$$

Substituting $R = D/2$:

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi D^{4}\Delta P}{128\,\mu L}\,}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** — the cornerstone of capillary rheometry.

> **Engineering Note:** The $D^{4}$ dependence means halving the die diameter reduces the flow rate by a factor of **16** at the same pressure — a critical design constraint for extrusion dies.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Power-Law Velocity Profile in a Capillary

### Step 1 — Start from the general momentum equation
The constant-pressure-gradient form:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\tau_{rz}\right) = \frac{dP}{dz}$$

### Step 2 — Insert the Power-law constitutive equation
For a Power-law fluid:

$$\tau_{rz} = -K\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left[-Kr\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right] = \frac{dP}{dz}$$

### Step 3 — First integration (multiply by $r$)

$$-Kr\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r^{2}}{2}\frac{dP}{dz} + C_1$$

By symmetry at $r = 0$, the LHS vanishes; therefore $C_1 = 0$:

$$-K\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r}{2}\frac{dP}{dz}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the velocity gradient
Since $dv_z/dr < 0$ (decelerating toward the wall) and $dP/dz < 0$ (pressure drops in flow direction), the product $(-)(-) = +$. We can write:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\left(\frac{-1}{2K}\frac{dP}{dz}\right)^{1/n}\!r^{1/n}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate to get the velocity profile

$$v_z(r) = -\left(\frac{-1}{2K}\frac{dP}{dz}\right)^{1/n}\frac{n}{n+1}r^{(n+1)/n} + C_2$$

### Step 6 — Apply the wall no-slip BC: $v_z(R) = 0$

$$0 = -\left(\frac{-1}{2K}\frac{dP}{dz}\right)^{1/n}\frac{n}{n+1}R^{(n+1)/n} + C_2$$

$$C_2 = \left(\frac{-1}{2K}\frac{dP}{dz}\right)^{1/n}\frac{n}{n+1}R^{(n+1)/n}$$

### Step 7 — Final Power-law velocity profile

$$\boxed{\,v_z(r) = \left(\frac{-\frac{dP}{dz}}{2K}\right)^{1/n}\frac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)\,}$$

> **Comparison to Newtonian:** Setting $n = 1$ recovers the parabolic profile with $K \to \mu$. For $n < 1$ (pseudoplastic polymer melt), the profile is *flatter* (plug-like) — the polymer slips as a plug in the core, with steep shear near the wall.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Rabinowitsch Correction (True Wall Shear Rate)

### Step 1 — Express the apparent shear rate
From $Q$ for the Power-law fluid, the apparent wall shear rate is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32Q}{\pi D^{3}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Power-law $Q$ expression

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4}{\pi R^{3}}\cdot\pi\left(\frac{-\frac{dP}{dz}}{2K}\right)^{1/n}\frac{n}{3n+1}R^{(3n+1)/n}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4n}{3n+1}\left(\frac{-\frac{dP}{dz}}{2K}\right)^{1/n}R^{(3n+1)/n-3} = \frac{4n}{3n+1}\left(\frac{-\frac{dP}{dz}}{2K}\right)^{1/n}R^{(1-n)/n}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the true wall shear rate
The true wall shear rate is the velocity gradient magnitude at $r = R$:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{r=R} = \left(\frac{-\frac{dP}{dz}}{2K}\right)^{1/n}R^{1/n}$$

### Step 4 — Form the ratio

$$\frac{\dot{\gamma}_a}{\dot{\gamma}_w} = \frac{4n}{3n+1}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the true wall shear rate

$$\boxed{\,\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dot{\gamma}_a\cdot\frac{3n+1}{4n}\,}$$

This is the **Rabinowitsch–Mooney equation**. It is the multiplicative correction factor that converts an *assumed-Newtonian* shear rate into the *true* shear rate for a Power-law fluid.

> **Engineering Insight:** For $n = 1$ (Newtonian), the factor is $1$ (no correction). For $n = 0.4$ (typical HDPE), the factor is $1.6$ — the true shear rate is **60% higher** than the apparent value, with major consequences for viscosity data reduction.

## 3.5 Derivation V — Bagley Correction (End-Pressure Loss)

### Step 1 — Decompose the total pressure drop
For a real capillary, the total pressure drop has two contributions:

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{entry}} + \Delta P_{\text{die}} + \Delta P_{\text{exit}}$$

For a long die, $\Delta P_{\text{exit}}$ (kinetic-energy recovery) is small, so:

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{end}} + 2\tau_w\cdot\frac{L}{R} = \Delta P_{\text{end}} + \tau_w\cdot\frac{4L}{D}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange into a linear form

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 2\tau_w\left(\frac{L}{D}\right) + \Delta P_{\text{end}}$$

Or, defining the **Bagley correction factor** $n_c$ such that $\Delta P_{\text{end}} = 2\tau_w n_c$:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 2\tau_w\!\left(\frac{L}{D}+n_c\right)\,}$$

### Step 3 — Experimental determination
Run a capillary rheometer at constant $Q$ (or constant $\dot{\gamma}_a$) using dies of different $L/D$ ratios (e.g., $L/D = 10, 20, 30, 40$). Plot $\Delta P_{\text{total}}$ vs. $L/D$. The plot is a straight line with:

- **Slope** = $2\tau_w$ (true wall shear stress)
- **Negative $x$-intercept** = $n_c$ (Bagley factor)

### Step 4 — Corrected wall shear stress

$$\tau_{w,\text{true}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}}{2\left(\dfrac{L}{D}+n_c\right)}$$

> **Why end effects occur:** Polymer melts are viscoelastic. The converging flow into the die entrance stores elastic energy (the *Bagley entrance pressure drop* $\Delta P_{end}$), which can exceed 50% of the total for short dies. Neglecting $n_c$ would falsely inflate the measured viscosity.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Newtonian vs. Power-Law Capillary Flow

| Feature | Newtonian ($n=1$, $K=\mu$) | Power-Law ($n<1$, pseudoplastic) | Power-Law ($n>1$, dilatant) |
|---------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Velocity profile | Parabolic (full) | Flattened, plug-like core | Sharper centerline spike |
| $v_{\max}/v_{\text{avg}}$ | $2.0$ | $\dfrac{n+3}{n+1}$ (> 2) | $\dfrac{n+3}{n+1}$ (< 2) |
| Wall shear rate vs. $\dot{\gamma}_a$ | Equal | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a\!\left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)$ | Same form, factor < 1 |
| Apparent viscosity vs. true | Equal | $\eta_a > \eta_{\text{true}}$ at high $\dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta_a < \eta_{\text{true}}$ |
| Practical example | Glycerin, water, dilute solutions | HDPE, PP, PS melts, PVC plastisol | Cornstarch–water (high conc.) |
| Bagley end-effect | Negligible | Substantial (viscoelastic) | Mild |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capillary Rheometer — Functional Architecture

The schematic below describes a constant-pressure or constant-rate capillary rheometer. The polymer melt is forced from a heated reservoir through a precisely-machined capillary die, with pressure transducers at the barrel and die-entry positions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Heated Barrel Reservoir] --> A2[Piston with Load Cell]
    A2 --> A3[Polymer Melt Column]
    A3 --> A4[Capillary Die Entry Region]
    A4 --> A5[Bagley End Pressure Zone]
    A5 --> A6[Fully Developed Capillary Die]
    A6 --> A7[Exit Velocity Profile Zone]
    A7 --> A8[Extrudate Sample]
    A9[Pressure Transducer P1 at Barrel] --- A3
    A10[Pressure Transducer P2 at Die Entry] --- A4
    A11[Capillary of Length L and Diameter D] --- A6
    A12[Heating Bands and Temperature Controller] --- A1
    A12 --- A6
    A13[Data Acquisition System] --- A9
    A13 --- A10
    A13 --- A2
```

> **Caption:** Schematic of a typical capillary rheometer showing the polymer melt path from the heated reservoir through the entry (Bagley) zone into the fully-developed capillary die, with pressure measurement points $P_1$ and $P_2$ and a force-measuring piston.

## 4.2 Newtonian vs. Power-Law Velocity Profile Comparison

The diagram below compares the velocity distributions in the capillary for a Newtonian fluid and a pseudoplastic Power-law fluid ($n < 1$). Note the markedly flatter profile of the shear-thinning fluid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Capillary Cross Section Radius R]
    n2[Centerline r equals 0]
    n3[Wall r equals R]
    n4[Newtonian Parabolic Profile v max at r=0]
    n5[Power Law Flattened Profile Plug like Core]
    n6[High Shear Zone Near Wall]
    n7[Low Shear Plug Core]
    n1 --> n2
    n1 --> n3
    n2 --> n4
    n2 --> n5
    n3 --> n6
    n5 --> n7
```

## 4.3 Data Reduction Workflow — Bagley & Rabinowitsch Corrections

The block diagram below shows the data reduction pipeline used in capillary rheometry. Raw measurements of $Q$ and $\Delta P$ are first corrected for end effects (Bagley) to obtain the true wall shear stress, then for non-Newtonian velocity profile (Rabinowitsch) to obtain the true wall shear rate, and finally the true viscosity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r1[Raw Volumetric Flow Rate Q] --> s1[Compute Apparent Shear Rate gamma a equals 32Q over pi D cubed]
    r2[Raw Pressure Drop Delta P measured] --> s2[Bagley Plot Delta P vs L over D at constant gamma a]
    s2 --> s3[Determine Bagley Factor nc from negative x intercept]
    s3 --> s4[Compute True Wall Shear Stress tau w true equals Delta P over 2 times L over D plus nc]
    s1 --> s5[Determine Power Law Index n from log tau w vs log gamma a plot slope equals 1 over n]
    s5 --> s6[Apply Rabinowitsch Correction gamma w true equals gamma a times 3n plus 1 over 4n]
    s4 --> s7[Compute True Viscosity eta true equals tau w true over gamma w true]
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8[Plot Viscosity vs True Shear Rate Master Curve]
```

## 4.4 Wall-Slip Mechanism (Mooney Analysis)

When wall slip is suspected, flow curves from two capillaries of different radii $R$ and $R'$ are compared at the same wall shear stress $\tau_w$. The slip velocity is extracted as the difference between total and no-slip flow rates.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Capillary 1 Radius R1] --> p2[Measure Q1 at given tau w]
    p3[Capillary 2 Radius R2 greater than R1] --> p4[Measure Q2 at same tau w]
    p2 --> p5[Plot Q versus 1 over R cubed at constant tau w]
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6[Slope gives Slip Velocity vs tau w]
    p5 --> p7[Intercept gives No slip Flow Component]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define apparent shear rate and apparent viscosity in the context of capillary rheometry of a polymer melt. Why are these "apparent" quantities and not the true material response?*

**Model Answer:**

The **apparent shear rate** is the wall shear rate computed from the measured volumetric flow rate $Q$ by *assuming* a Newtonian (parabolic) velocity profile:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32\,Q}{\pi D^{3}}$$

The **apparent viscosity** is the wall shear stress divided by the apparent shear rate:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{\pi D^{4}\,\Delta P}{128\,L\,Q}$$

They are called *apparent* because polymer melts are **non-Newtonian** (pseudoplastic); the true velocity profile deviates from the parabolic form, and the actual wall shear rate is obtained only after the **Rabinowitsch correction**:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a\cdot\frac{3n+1}{4n}$$

For $n < 1$, $\dot{\gamma}_w > \dot{\gamma}_a$, so the true viscosity is *lower* than the apparent viscosity. Using $\eta_a$ directly would over-predict viscosity at high $\dot{\gamma}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark formula of $\dot{\gamma}_a$, 1 mark formula of $\eta_a$, 1 mark explanation of non-Newtonian nature.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the Rabinowitsch equation and explain its physical significance in capillary rheometry of polymer melts.*

**Model Answer:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dot{\gamma}_a\cdot\frac{3n+1}{4n}$$

**Physical Significance:**
1. It **converts the apparent shear rate** (calculated from $Q$ using the Newtonian formula) to the **true wall shear rate** for a Power-law fluid.
2. The factor $\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}$ is the **Rabinowitsch correction factor** — it equals 1 for Newtonian fluids ($n=1$) and exceeds 1 for pseudoplastic polymer melts ($n<1$).
3. **Typical values:** For HDPE at processing temperature ($n \approx 0.4$), the correction factor is $1.6$ — i.e., the true shear rate is **60% higher** than the apparent value.
4. Without this correction, **viscosity vs. shear rate** master curves of polymer melts are systematically in error at high $\dot{\gamma}$, leading to poor predictions of processing behavior in extrusion and injection molding.

*(3 marks: 1 mark equation, 1 mark factor interpretation, 1 mark engineering implication.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Derivation Track
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Starting from the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates, derive the **velocity profile** for steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a **Newtonian fluid** in a horizontal capillary of radius $R$ under pressure drop $\Delta P$. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **volumetric flow rate** (Hagen–Poiseuille equation) from the velocity profile obtained in (a). Hence obtain the **wall shear stress** and the **apparent shear rate** in terms of the measurable quantities $\Delta P$, $L$, $D$ and $Q$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state: $\partial(\cdot)/\partial t = 0$
2. Incompressible: $\rho = \text{constant}$
3. Axisymmetric flow: $v_\theta = 0$, no $\theta$-dependence
4. Fully developed: $v_r = 0$ and $v_z = v_z(r)$ only
5. Newtonian: $\tau_{rz} = -\eta\,dv_z/dr$
6. Horizontal tube: $g_z = 0$

**Reduced Navier–Stokes equation** (z-momentum):

$$0 = -\frac{dp}{dz} + \eta\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)$$

Since $dp/dz = -\Delta P / L$ is constant:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\eta L}$$

**First integration:**

$$r\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P\,r^{2}}{2\eta L} + C_1$$

**Boundary Condition 1** — symmetry at centerline: $dv_z/dr \vert_{r=0} = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.

**Second integration:**

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P\,r^{2}}{4\eta L} + C_2$$

**Boundary Condition 2** — no-slip at wall: $v_z(R) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = \dfrac{\Delta P\,R^{2}}{4\eta L}$.

**Final velocity profile:**

$$\boxed{\,v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L}\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)\,}$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r)\,(2\pi r)\,dr = \frac{\pi\Delta P}{2\eta L}\int_{0}^{R}\left(R^{2}r - r^{3}\right)dr = \frac{\pi\Delta P}{2\eta L}\cdot\frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi R^{4}\Delta P}{8\eta L} = \frac{\pi D^{4}\Delta P}{128\,\eta\,L}\,}$$

**Wall shear stress** (force balance on a cylindrical fluid plug of length $L$ and radius $R$):

$$\Delta P\cdot\pi R^{2} = \tau_w\cdot 2\pi R\cdot L \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P\,R}{2L} = \frac{\Delta P\,D}{4L}\,}$$

**Apparent shear rate** (from $Q$ assuming parabolic profile):

$$\boxed{\,\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{32\,Q}{\pi D^{3}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting the $R^{2}$ in the parabolic profile:** A common error is writing $v_z(r) \propto (R-r)$ instead of $(R^{2}-r^{2})$; this would only be valid for a *linear Couette* (plane) profile, not a *parabolic* Poiseuille profile.
> - **Mixing up $D$ and $R$:** Many students write $\tau_w = \Delta P D / (2L)$; the correct form is $\Delta P D / (4L)$. Always derive from a force balance: $\Delta P \cdot A_{\text{cs}} = \tau_w \cdot A_{\text{lateral}}$, giving $\tau_w = \Delta P R / (2L) = \Delta P D / (4L)$.
> - **Omitting assumptions:** KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the assumptions (steady, laminar, fully developed, axisymmetric, no-slip) are not explicitly listed before the derivation begins.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Non-Newtonian & Correction Track
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** For a **Power-law fluid** (Ostwald–de Waele model) flowing in a capillary, derive the **velocity profile** and the **volumetric flow rate** in terms of the pressure gradient, consistency index $K$, and power-law index $n$. Show that the Newtonian result is recovered as a special case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Bagley correction** for end effects in capillary flow of polymer melts. Describe the experimental procedure to determine the Bagley correction factor $n_c$, and write the final corrected expression for the wall shear stress. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Power-law constitutive equation:**

$$\tau_{rz} = -K\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr}$$

**Momentum equation** in cylindrical coordinates (constant pressure gradient):

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\tau_{rz}\right) = \frac{dp}{dz}$$

**Substitute and integrate:**

$$-K\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{dp}{dz}$$

Multiplying by $r$ and integrating:

$$-Kr\left|\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|^{n-1}\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r^{2}}{2}\frac{dp}{dz} + C_1$$

By symmetry at $r=0$: $C_1 = 0$. Solving for the velocity gradient (noting $dp/dz < 0$):

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\left(\frac{-1}{2K}\frac{dp}{dz}\right)^{1/n}r^{1/n}$$

**Integrate a second time** with the no-slip BC $v_z(R) = 0$:

$$v_z(r) = \left(\frac{-1}{2K}\frac{dp}{dz}\right)^{1/n}\frac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)$$

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r)\,(2\pi r)\,dr = \pi\left(\frac{-1}{2K}\frac{dp}{dz}\right)^{1/n}\frac{n}{3n+1}\,R^{(3n+1)/n}$$

**Newtonian limit:** Setting $n = 1$ and $K = \mu$:

$$Q = \pi\left(\frac{-\frac{dp}{dz}}{2\mu}\right)\frac{1}{4}R^{4} = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\left(-\frac{dp}{dz}\right) = \frac{\pi D^{4}\Delta P}{128\,\mu L}$$

which recovers the Hagen–Poiseuille equation. ✓

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Bagley Correction — Conceptual Basis:**
Polymer melts are **viscoelastic**. As the melt converges from the barrel into the capillary entrance, it undergoes an extensional flow that *stores elastic energy*. This generates an additional **entrance pressure drop** $\Delta P_{end}$ (the "Bagley loss") beyond the fully-developed die pressure $\Delta P_{fd}$.

**Total pressure drop decomposition:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{end} + \Delta P_{fd} = \Delta P_{end} + 2\tau_w\cdot\frac{L}{R} = \Delta P_{end} + \tau_w\cdot\frac{4L}{D}$$

Define the **Bagley correction factor** $n_c$ via $\Delta P_{end} = 2\tau_w\,n_c$:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 2\tau_w\!\left(\frac{L}{D}+n_c\right)\,}$$

**Experimental Procedure:**
1. Use a set of capillary dies with the same diameter $D$ but varying lengths, e.g., $L/D = 10, 20, 30, 40$.
2. At a fixed apparent shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_a$ (fixed $Q$), measure $\Delta P_{\text{total}}$ for each die.
3. Plot $\Delta P_{\text{total}}$ (y-axis) vs. $L/D$ (x-axis). The plot is a straight line.
4. The **negative x-intercept** of this line is the **Bagley factor** $n_c$.
5. The **slope** of the line is $2\tau_w$, giving the true wall shear stress.

**Corrected wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_{w,\text{true}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}}{2\!\left(\dfrac{L}{D}+n_c\right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Confusing Bagley with Rabinowitsch:** Bagley corrects the *stress* (vertical axis of the data); Rabinowitsch corrects the *shear rate* (horizontal axis). They are independent corrections applied sequentially — first Bagley, then Rabinowitsch.
> - **Using $L/D$ vs. $L/R$:** Always plot $\Delta P$ vs. $L/D$ (not $L/R$) for consistency with the standard Bagley form $\Delta P = 2\tau_w(L/D + n_c)$. Mixing these will give an $n_c$ off by a factor of 2.
> - **Short dies and non-linearity:** For very short dies ($L/D < 5$), the $\Delta P$ vs. $L/D$ plot may show curvature. Use only $L/D \geq 10$ dies for the linear Bagley fit.
> - **Forgetting to apply the correction:** Many students compute $\eta_a$ directly from $\Delta P$ without applying Bagley, leading to grossly overestimated viscosities — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Capillary Flow**

- ✅ Capillary flow = pressure-driven, fully-developed, axisymmetric laminar flow in a cylindrical tube of small bore.
- ✅ **Newtonian velocity profile** is *parabolic*: $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\eta L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$.
- ✅ **Hagen–Poiseuille equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}\Delta P}{128\,\eta L}$ — note the $D^{4}$ dependence.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{D\Delta P}{4L}$ — from a *force balance*, not a velocity gradient.
- ✅ **Apparent shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{32Q}{\pi D^{3}} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ — *assumes* parabolic profile.
- ✅ **Apparent viscosity:** $\eta_a = \tau_w / \dot{\gamma}_a$ — *not* the true viscosity for polymer melts.
- ✅ **Power-law velocity profile** has form $\propto\left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)$ — flattened (plug-like) for $n < 1$.
- ✅ **Power-law volumetric flow rate:** $Q = \pi\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{3n+1}R^{(3n+1)/n}$.
- ✅ **Rabinowitsch equation:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a\!\left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)$ — true wall shear rate correction.
- ✅ **Bagley correction:** $\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 2\tau_w(L/D + n_c)$; $n_c$ is the negative $x$-intercept of $\Delta P$ vs. $L/D$ at constant $\dot{\gamma}_a$.
- ✅ **Sequence of corrections:** First apply **Bagley** (corrects $\tau_w$), then **Rabinowitsch** (corrects $\dot{\gamma}_a$ to $\dot{\gamma}_w$). True viscosity: $\eta_{\text{true}} = \tau_{w,\text{true}}/\dot{\gamma}_{w,\text{true}}$.
- ✅ **Wall slip** (Mooney method): compare flow curves from capillaries of different $D$ at the same $\tau_w$; slip shows as a $D$-dependent $Q$.
- ✅ **Long-die assumption:** For Bagley plot linearity, use $L/D \geq 10$.
- ✅ **Capillary number & dimensionless groups:** Recall generalized Reynolds number $\text{Re}_{gen} = \rho D^{n} v^{2-n}/[K((3n+1)/4n)^{n}]$ for Power-law flow; laminar flow requires $\text{Re}_{gen} < 2100$ (approximate).
- ✅ **Engineering utility:** The capillary rheometer is the *primary* tool for characterizing polymer melts at processing shear rates ($10$ – $10^{6}$ s$^{-1}$); data feeds directly into Moldflow/Polyflow simulation packages.

> **One-Line Mantra for KTU Exam:** *"Bagley straightens the stress; Rabinowitsch straightens the rate — apply both or your viscosity is wrong."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sheet forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sheet forming ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sheet Forming** is a generic class of polymer processing operations in which a polymer melt, softened sheet, or pre-extruded parison is shaped into a continuous flat sheet, film, or three-dimensional thin-walled article through a combination of **converging flow geometry**, **biaxial stretching**, and **controlled solidification**. The principal commercial variants covered in this module are **cast film extrusion**, **blown film (tubular film) extrusion**, **calendering**, and **thermoforming**. From a **modelling & simulation** standpoint, sheet forming is treated as a **free-surface, non-isothermal, viscoelastic flow** of a **non-Newtonian fluid** (the polymer melt) that is simultaneously subjected to **heat transfer**, **crystallization/solidification kinetics**, and **membrane-type large deformation mechanics** in the post-die region. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy – "The Soap Film on a Wire Loop"** > Imagine dipping a wire loop into soap solution and blowing gently. The soap film stretches into a thin bubble — its thickness is governed by surface tension, the pressure inside, and the rate at which you pull. Now replace the soap with molten polymer, the wire loop with an annular die, the blowing pressure with internal air, and the surface tension with **melt strength (extensional viscosity)**. The polymer bubble rises, cools, and crystallises at a "frost line" before being flattened into a film. This is exactly how **blown film extrusion** works. Likewise, **thermoforming** is like pressing a warm, soft pizza dough into a bowl — the dough thins where it stretches most, and the thickness distribution is predictable from membrane mechanics. ### 1.3 Classification of Sheet Forming Processes | Process | Driving Force | Typical Products | Polymers | |---|---|---|---| | Cast Film Extrusion | Chill-roll extraction | Packaging film, photographic film | LDPE, PP, PET, PA | | Blown Film Extrusion | Internal air pressure + take-up | Bags, agricultural film | LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE | | Calendering | Rotating heated rolls | PVC sheets, flooring | PVC, ABS, rubbers | | Thermoforming | Vacuum / pressure / plug assist | Trays, cups, dashboards | PS, PP, PET, ABS, PMMA | > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics** used throughout sheet forming analysis: > - **Blow-up Ratio (BUR)** = final bubble diameter / die diameter (typically 2–4) > - **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** = take-up velocity / melt exit velocity > - **Total Draw Ratio (TDR)** = BUR × DDR > - **Frost Line Height (FLH)** = axial distance from die to onset of crystallinity > - **Neck-in** = lateral contraction of the extrudate width relative to die width (in mm or %) > - Standard draw ratios are limited by **melt strength** and **strain-hardening** behaviour of the polymer. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic #### A. Cast Film Extrusion — Modelling Chain 1. **Polymer melt** is delivered by an extruder at a controlled mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ and temperature $T_0$. 2. Melt passes through a **slit die** (rectangular channel of width $W$, thickness $h$, length $L$). 3. **Viscoelastic flow** in the die — pressure drop $\Delta P$ and velocity field $v_x(y,z)$ are computed using a generalised Newtonian constitutive equation. 4. At die exit, three key **post-die phenomena** occur: - **Extrudate swell (die swell)** due to recoverable elastic strain. - **Neck-in** — lateral contraction of the sheet as it is stretched. - **Draw-down** — thinning of the sheet by the chill roll. 5. The web is **quenched** on a chill roll, where **heat transfer** and (for semi-crystalline polymers) **crystallization kinetics** govern the final morphology. #### B. Blown Film Extrusion — Modelling Chain 1. Melt emerges from an **annular die** (diameter $D_0$, gap $h_0$). 2. Internal air inflates the tube; external cooling air (or air ring) is applied. 3. The **bubble** is treated as a **thin membrane** undergoing **non-uniform biaxial extension**: - Machine direction (MD) extension at take-up speed $v_L$. - Transverse direction (TD) extension by internal pressure $P_i$. 4. The system is governed by **membrane equilibrium** (Young-Laplace type), **force balance in air**, and **energy balance**. 5. Crystallisation sets in at the **frost line**, where the bubble becomes opaque; above this point, only the **melt rheology** controls the deformation, while below it the solidified film is inextensible. #### C. Thermoforming — Modelling Chain 1. A **pre-extruded sheet** (thermoplastic) is **re-heated** above its glass-transition temperature $T_g$ (for amorphous) or melting point $T_m$ (for semi-crystalline) until it reaches a rubbery, pliable state. 2. The hot sheet is forced against a mould by **vacuum, compressed air, mechanical plug, or a combination**. 3. The sheet is modelled as a **non-linear viscoelastic membrane** undergoing **biaxial stretching** until contact with the mould. 4. After contact, the **thinning distribution** in the final part is computed from a **free-stretching analysis** in the region of the mould that has not yet been contacted. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Simulation Matters**: Industrial sheet-forming operations run at very high production rates (e.g. blown film at 100 m/min) and produce tonnes of material per hour. Trial-and-error on a real line is prohibitively expensive. Predictive simulation of thickness, orientation, residual stress, and crystallinity is therefore a central activity in modern polymer engineering. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta(\dot\gamma) = K\,\dot\gamma^{\,n-1}$ | Power-law viscosity model | Pa·s | | 2 | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (K\dot\gamma)^m}$ | Cross model (full shear-rate range) | – | | 3 | $\eta = \eta_0 \left[1 + (\lambda \dot\gamma)^2\right]^{(n-1)/2}$ | Carreau model | Pa·s | | 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12\,\eta\,L\,Q}{W\,h^{3}}$ | Pressure drop in a slit die (Newtonian) | Pa | | 5 | $Q = \dfrac{W\,h^{3}}{12\,\eta}\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\right)$ | Volumetric flow rate in slit die | m³/s | | 6 | $\dot\gamma_w = \dfrac{6Q}{W\,h^{2}}$ | Wall shear rate in slit die | s⁻¹ | | 7 | $\sigma_{MD} = F_{MD}/(2\pi R h)$ | Machine-direction membrane stress | Pa | | 8 | $\sigma_{TD} = (P_i - P_a)R/h$ | Transverse-direction membrane stress | Pa | | 9 | $\Delta P = \sigma_{MD}/R_1 + \sigma_{TD}/R_2$ | Young-Laplace for cylindrical membrane | Pa | | 10 | $X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k\,t^{n_a})$ | Avrami crystallisation kinetics | – | | 11 | $h_{\text{final}} = h_0 \cdot \dfrac{v_0}{v_L}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\text{BUR}}$ | Final film thickness | m | | 12 | $\tau_{ij} = 2\eta(\dot\gamma)\, D_{ij}$ | Generalised Newtonian constitutive law | Pa | | 13 | $\rho\,c_p\!\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \vec v\cdot\nabla T\right) = k\nabla^{2}T + \eta \dot\gamma^{2}$ | Energy equation (viscous dissipation term) | W/m³ | | 14 | $\text{Neck-in} = (W_{\text{die}} - W_{\text{web}})/W_{\text{die}}$ | Neck-in (dimensionless) | – | | 15 | $S = \dfrac{1}{h_0}\dfrac{dh}{dt}$ | Local stretch rate in thermoforming | s⁻¹ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility**: Equations 1–6 are the **rheology toolkit** used in die design; Equations 7–9 form the **membrane-mechanics backbone** of blown-film simulation; Equations 10 and 13 capture **solidification** and **thermal effects**; Equations 11, 14, 15 are **process-performance metrics** directly used by industrial process engineers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop in a Slit Die for a Power-Law Fluid We treat a slit die of width $W \gg$ thickness $h$ so that the flow is essentially **plane Poiseuille flow** between parallel plates. The melt obeys the power-law model $$ \eta(\dot\gamma) = K\,|\dot\gamma|^{\,n-1}. $$ **Step 1 — Simplify the momentum equation.** For steady, fully-developed, incompressible flow in the $x$-direction, the $x$-component of the momentum equation reduces to $$ 0 = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y}. $$ **Step 2 — Use the constitutive law.** For a power-law fluid, the shear stress is $$ \tau_{yx} = K\left\vert \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}\right\vert^{n-1}\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}. $$ **Step 3 — Integrate the stress.** Integrate once with respect to $y$: $$ \tau_{yx}(y) = \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,y + C_1. $$ By symmetry about the centreline ($y = 0$) the shear stress is zero, hence $C_1 = 0$. **Step 4 — Solve for the velocity profile.** Take the absolute value and raise to the appropriate power: $$ \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} = \left(\dfrac{1}{K}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,y\right)^{1/n}. $$ Integrate with the no-slip boundary condition $v_x(\pm h/2) = 0$: $$ v_x(y) = \left(\dfrac{1}{K}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{n+1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{(n+1)/n} - y^{(n+1)/n}\right]. $$ **Step 5 — Compute the volumetric flow rate.** Integrate the velocity over the cross-section: $$ Q = W\!\int_{-h/2}^{h/2}\! v_x(y)\,dy. $$ Carrying out the integration and using $\Delta P/L = -\partial p/\partial x$: $$ \boxed{\;Q = \dfrac{W\,n}{2n+1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{(2n+1)/n}\;} $$ This is the **power-law slit-die flow rate equation**. The Newtonian case is recovered by setting $n = 1$ and $K = \eta$, which collapses to Equation (4) of the cheat sheet. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** As $n$ decreases (more shear-thinning), the effective viscosity drops, so for a given $\Delta P$ the flow rate $Q$ increases sharply. This is why highly shear-thinning polymers (e.g. LLDPE) can be processed at lower melt pressures. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Membrane Equilibrium of a Blown-Film Bubble Treat the bubble as a **thin cylindrical membrane** of variable radius $R(z)$ and thickness $h(z)$, with internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_a$. The membrane carries two principal stresses: machine-direction $\sigma_{MD}(z)$ and transverse-direction $\sigma_{TD}(z)$. **Step 1 — Force balance in the transverse direction.** The hoop stress in a thin cylinder balances the internal–external pressure difference: $$ \sigma_{TD}(z) = \dfrac{\big(P_i - P_a\big)\,R(z)}{h(z)}. $$ **Step 2 — Force balance in the machine direction.** Consider a small slice of the bubble between $z$ and $z+dz$. The net MD force must support the weight and the friction of the cooling air (often neglected in simplified models). In the absence of body forces: $$ \dfrac{d}{dz}\!\left(\sigma_{MD}\,h\right) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sigma_{MD}(z)\,h(z) = \text{const} = F_{\text{take-up}}/(2\pi R_0). $$ **Step 3 — Apply the membrane (Young-Laplace) equation.** For a surface with two principal radii of curvature $R_1(z)$ (circumferential) and $R_2(z)$ (longitudinal): $$ P_i - P_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{MD}}{R_2} + \dfrac{\sigma_{TD}}{R_1}. $$ For a long, slender bubble, $R_2 \approx \infty$, so the second term dominates: $$ \boxed{\;P_i - P_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{TD}}{R_1} = \dfrac{P_i - P_a}{\text{self-consistent}}\;} $$ Substituting $\sigma_{TD}$ from Step 1 and solving for $h$: $$ h(z) = \dfrac{\big(P_i - P_a\big)\,R(z)}{\sigma_{TD}(z)}. $$ **Step 4 — Combine with the take-up force.** Using the conservation of mass (constant mass flow rate $\dot m$): $$ \rho\,Q = \rho\,(2\pi R_0\,h_0\,v_0) = \rho\,(2\pi R(z)\,h(z)\,v(z)) = \text{const}, $$ so $$ v(z) = \dfrac{R_0\,h_0\,v_0}{R(z)\,h(z)}. $$ **Step 5 — Couple to a viscoelastic constitutive law.** For a viscoelastic fluid such as a Lodge-type rubber-liquid, $$ \sigma_{MD}(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{t}\!M(t - t')\,\dot\varepsilon_{MD}(t')\,dt', $$ which, when coupled with Step 2, gives a closed system for $(R(z), h(z), v(z), T(z))$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The bubble is a self-regulating system — increasing $P_i$ enlarges $R$, thins $h$, and amplifies $\sigma_{TD}$. The system stabilises at a stable operating point determined by **extensional rheology**, **cooling rate**, and **take-up force**. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Stretching Thickness in Thermoforming For **negative thermoforming** (vacuum forming) of a circular sheet of initial radius $R_0$ and initial thickness $h_0$ into a hemispherical dome of radius $R_f$, the deformation is equibiaxial. **Step 1 — Volume (area × thickness) is constant for an incompressible membrane:** $$ \pi R_0^{2}\,h_0 = 2\pi R_f^{2}\,h_f. $$ **Step 2 — Solve for the final thickness:** $$ \boxed{\;h_f = \dfrac{R_0^{2}}{2R_f^{2}}\,h_0\;} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the engineering biaxial stretch ratio:** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{R_f}{R_0} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{h_f}{h_0} = \dfrac{1}{2\lambda^{2}}. $$ **Step 4 — Local thinning rate.** The local areal stretch rate during deformation is $$ \dot\varepsilon_{A} = \dfrac{1}{A}\dfrac{dA}{dt}. $$ For a sphere of growing radius $R(t)$, $A = 4\pi R^{2}$, so $$ \dot\varepsilon_{A} = \dfrac{2}{R}\dfrac{dR}{dt}. $$ **Step 5 — Stress in a biaxially stretched membrane (Neo-Hookean model):** $$ \sigma = \dfrac{E}{6}\!\left(2\lambda^{2} - \dfrac{2}{\lambda^{4}}\right), $$ where $E$ is the small-strain Young's modulus of the rubbery polymer above $T_g$. This equation is then coupled with the membrane equilibrium to compute the **pressure–time profile** required to bring the sheet into full contact with the mould. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Implication:** The factor $1/(2\lambda^2)$ is the basis of the well-known **"rule-of-thumb"** that a thermoformed part at $\lambda = 1.5$ has only $\approx 22\%$ of the original sheet thickness at the centre. This is why simulation is essential — a small geometric change can halve the wall thickness in a critical region. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Coupling Crystallisation Kinetics to Process Simulation For a semi-crystalline polymer (e.g. PP, PET) processed in cast or blown film, the **relative crystallinity** $\theta(t)$ evolves as the temperature crosses the crystallisation window. The **Avrami equation** is $$ \theta(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-k\,t^{n_a}\right). $$ The Avrami rate constant is temperature-dependent via a Hoffmann-Lauritzen-type nucleation-growth law: $$ k(T) = k_0\,\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{U^{*}}{R(T - T_\infty)}\right]\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{K_g}{T\,\Delta T\,f}\right], $$ where $\Delta T = T_m^{0} - T$ and $f = 2T/(T + T_m^{0})$. The evolution of $\theta$ feeds back into the **thermal conductivity** and **specific volume** of the polymer: $$ k_{\text{eff}}(T,\theta) = k_a + \theta\,(k_c - k_a), $$ which closes the energy balance (Equation 13 of the cheat sheet). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Process Significance:** Faster cooling shifts the crystallisation window to lower temperatures, producing **smaller, more numerous spherulites** and a **more transparent, less rigid** film. Slower cooling allows larger spherulites, increasing opacity and stiffness. This is why **process-induced morphology** is a central topic in polymer processing simulation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagrams follow KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE naming safeguards: every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters, and labels contain only clean uppercase / alphanumeric text. ### 4.1 Process Architecture of Cast Film Extrusion ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[EXTRUDER

GEOMETRY, MATERIAL, BC] --> E2[RHEOLOGY FIT

MESH FOR DIE AND FREE SURFACE] E3 --> E4[FLOW SOLVER
NAVIER STOKES + ENERGY] E4 --> E5[POST-PROCESSOR
VELOCITY, STRESS, TEMPERATURE] E5 --> E6[CRYSTALLISATION KINETICS
AVRAMI EVOLUTION] E6 --> E7[ORIENTATION AND BIAXIAL STRETCH
FINAL PROPERTIES] E7 --> E8[OUTPUT
FILM THICKNESS, MORPHOLOGY, STRESS] E1 -.feedback.-> E3 E5 -.iterate.-> E4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Define the term "neck-in" in polymer sheet extrusion and state the two process variables that predominantly control it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Neck-in** is the lateral contraction of a polymer extrudate (sheet or film) immediately after it leaves a slot or slit die, expressed as $$ \text{Neck-in} = \dfrac{W_{\text{die}} - W_{\text{web}}}{W_{\text{die}}}. $$ It arises from the **elastic recovery** of the viscoelastic polymer melt and from the **biaxial stress state** developed during draw-down. The two dominant controlling variables are: 1. **Melt temperature** — higher temperature reduces elastic strain energy and hence neck-in. 2. **Take-up speed (draw-down ratio)** — higher draw-down increases neck-in because the lateral elastic recoil is unopposed in the transverse direction. *(Auxiliary credit: die land length and polymer molecular weight distribution are also relevant.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **What is the "frost line" in blown film extrusion? What does its height signify in process simulation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **frost line** (or **freeze line**) is the axial location on the blown film bubble where the polymer melt undergoes rapid **crystallisation** (for semi-crystalline polymers) or **vitrification** (for amorphous polymers), producing a sudden visual transition from transparent to opaque. Its **height (FLH)** is a key process variable because it: 1. Marks the boundary between the **melt-controlled region** (above) and the **solid-film region** (below), setting the domain for viscoelastic simulation. 2. Determines the **cooling rate** experienced by the film, and hence the **final crystallinity, orientation, and mechanical properties**. 3. Is a direct **tuning knob** for process engineers — moving FLH closer to the die increases cooling rate, reduces crystallinity, and changes shrinkage. ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` > **(a)** Derive, with all intermediate steps, the **membrane equilibrium equations** for a tubular blown film bubble. Show how the bubble radius, film thickness, and machine-direction stress are coupled to the take-up force and the internal bubble pressure. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Using the power-law rheological model, derive the expression for the **volumetric flow rate** in a slit die of width $W$, thickness $h$, and length $L$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P$. Show that the Newtonian result is recovered for $n=1$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Membrane equilibrium of the blown film bubble** Consider a thin cylindrical bubble of radius $R(z)$ and thickness $h(z)$. The two principal stresses are $\sigma_{MD}(z)$ in the machine direction and $\sigma_{TD}(z)$ in the transverse (hoop) direction. **Step 1 — Hoop stress from internal pressure.** Balancing the radial forces on a unit length of the bubble: $$ \sigma_{TD}(z) = \dfrac{(P_i - P_a)\,R(z)}{h(z)}. $$ **Step 2 — MD force balance.** The vertical component of the membrane force must be continuous in the absence of body forces: $$ \dfrac{d}{dz}\!\left[\sigma_{MD}(z)\,h(z)\right] = 0. $$ Integrating from the die ($z=0$) to the take-up point ($z = L$): $$ \sigma_{MD}(z)\,h(z) = \dfrac{F_{MD}}{2\pi R_0}, $$ where $F_{MD}$ is the take-up force per circumference and $R_0$ the die radius. **Step 3 — Young-Laplace equation for the membrane.** The pressure difference across a curved membrane is $$ P_i - P_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{MD}}{R_2} + \dfrac{\sigma_{TD}}{R_1}, $$ with $R_1 = R(z)$ and $R_2 \approx \infty$ for a slender bubble. Substituting, $$ P_i - P_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{TD}}{R} = \dfrac{P_i - P_a}{\text{(self-consistent)}}. $$ Using Step 1, $$ h(z) = \dfrac{(P_i - P_a)\,R(z)}{\sigma_{TD}(z)}. $$ **Step 4 — Mass conservation.** The mass flow rate is constant: $$ 2\pi R_0 h_0 v_0 = 2\pi R(z) h(z) v(z) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v(z) = \dfrac{R_0 h_0 v_0}{R(z) h(z)}. $$ **Step 5 — Closure.** Equations (Step 1, 2, 3, 4) form a closed system that is solved for $R(z), h(z), v(z), \sigma_{MD}(z), \sigma_{TD}(z)$ given the inputs $P_i, v_L, T(z)$ and a viscoelastic constitutive law. **(b) Power-law slit die flow** For a power-law fluid in a slit die, the shear stress is $$ \tau_{yx} = K\left\vert\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}\right\vert^{n-1}\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}. $$ Momentum balance gives $$ 0 = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y}. $$ Integrating and applying symmetry $\tau_{yx}(0) = 0$: $$ \tau_{yx}(y) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,y. $$ Inverting the constitutive law: $$ \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\,y\right)^{1/n}. $$ Integrating with $v_x(\pm h/2) = 0$: $$ v_x(y) = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{KL}\right)^{1/n}\dfrac{n}{n+1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{(n+1)/n} - \vert y\vert^{(n+1)/n}\right]. $$ Integrating over the cross-section $W \times h$: $$ Q = W\!\int_{-h/2}^{h/2}\!v_x(y)\,dy = \dfrac{W n}{2n+1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{(2n+1)/n}. $$ **Newtonian limit.** Setting $n = 1$, $K = \eta$: $$ Q \to \dfrac{W}{3}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\eta L}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{3} = \dfrac{W h^{3}}{12\eta}\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}, $$ which is the standard Hagen-Poiseuille slit result. --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` > **(a)** Explain, with suitable equations, the **three main stages of thermoforming simulation**: pre-heating, free-stretching, and mould contact. Show the governing equations for the free-stretching stage and derive the thickness distribution in a hemispherical dome. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the coupling of **heat transfer, crystallisation kinetics (Avrami) and orientation development** in a cast film process. Derive the Avrami equation and the form of the temperature-dependent rate constant. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Three stages of thermoforming** **Stage 1 — Pre-heating (radiation + convection).** A flat sheet of thickness $h_0$ is heated by IR radiators from both sides. The 1-D transient heat conduction equation is $$ \rho c_p\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k\dfrac{\partial^{2}T}{\partial y^{2}}, $$ with convective/radiative boundary conditions at $y = \pm h_0/2$: $$ -k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}\bigg\vert_{h_0/2} = h_c(T_s - T_\infty) + \epsilon\sigma(T_s^{4} - T_\text{sur}^{4}). $$ The sheet is heated until the centre plane reaches $T_g + \Delta T$ (or near $T_m$ for semi-crystalline). **Stage 2 — Free-stretching.** The sheet is treated as a **non-linear viscoelastic membrane**. The stress in a biaxially stretched neo-Hookean sheet is $$ \sigma = \dfrac{E}{6}\!\left(2\lambda^{2} - \dfrac{2}{\lambda^{4}}\right), $$ and the membrane equilibrium in spherical coordinates (hemispherical dome) yields $$ P(t) = \dfrac{2 h(t)\,\sigma(\lambda)}{R(t)}. $$ The **continuity equation** in spherical coordinates reduces to $$ R(t)^{2} h(t) = R_0^{2} h_0 = \text{const}. $$ For a final dome of radius $R_f$: $$ \boxed{\;h_f = \dfrac{R_0^{2}\,h_0}{2 R_f^{2}} = \dfrac{h_0}{2\lambda^{2}}\;} $$ with $\lambda = R_f/R_0$. This is the **free-stretching thickness distribution** before mould contact. **Stage 3 — Mould contact and cooling.** After contact, the sheet is held against the cold mould, where heat is removed by conduction. The energy equation now includes the mould–sheet contact resistance, and a **Cauchy-type** boundary condition: $$ -k_{\text{poly}}\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\text{interface}} = h_{\text{contact}}(T_s - T_{\text{mould}}). $$ The final part is removed when its average temperature is below the **demoulding temperature** (typically $T_g - 10\,\text{K}$ for amorphous polymers). **(b) Coupling heat transfer, crystallisation and orientation in cast film** **Heat transfer.** The 1-D energy equation in the moving film is $$ \rho c_p v\dfrac{dT}{dz} = k\dfrac{d^{2}T}{dy^{2}} + \eta(\dot\gamma)\dot\gamma^{2}. $$ The viscous dissipation term is often significant at high extrusion rates. **Avrami crystallisation.** The relative crystallinity is $$ \theta(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^{n_a}), $$ where $k$ is the kinetic constant and $n_a$ the Avrami index (typically 2–4 for polymer melts). The temperature dependence of $k$ follows a Hoffmann–Lauritzen form: $$ k(T) = k_0\,\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{U^{*}}{R(T - T_\infty)}\right]\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{K_g}{T\,\Delta T\,f}\right], $$ with $\Delta T = T_m^{0} - T$, $f = 2T/(T + T_m^{0})$, $U^{*} \approx 6280$ J/mol, and $T_\infty = T_g - 30\,\text{K}$. **Orientation development.** The molecular orientation in the machine direction is tracked using a **tube-model** or **conformational tensor** approach, e.g. the Doi-Edwards model: $$ \mathbf{S} = \dfrac{1}{\langle\vert \mathbf{u}'\cdot\mathbf{E}\cdot\mathbf{u}\vert\rangle_{\text{iso}}}\langle\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{u}'\cdot\mathbf{E}\cdot\mathbf{u}\vert}{\vert \mathbf{E}\cdot\mathbf{u}\vert}\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}\rangle - \dfrac{1}{3}\mathbf{I}, $$ which couples back to the **stress tensor** through the **stress-optical rule** for birefringence measurements. **Coupling cycle.** $$ \boxed{\;\text{Temperature} \;\to\; \text{viscosity} \;\to\; \text{flow} \;\to\; \text{stretch} \;\to\; \text{orientation, stress, crystallisation} \;\to\; \text{properties}\;} $$ This closed loop is the foundation of **process–structure–property** simulation in sheet forming. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing "neck-in" with "die swell":** Neck-in is a **post-die lateral contraction** of the extrudate, primarily driven by biaxial stress, while die swell is an **expansion** at the die exit driven by elastic recovery. They are physically different. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in hemispherical thermoforming:** A common error is writing $h_f = h_0/\lambda^2$ instead of the correct $h_f = h_0/(2\lambda^2)$. The factor 2 comes from the surface area of the hemisphere being $2\pi R_f^{2}$, not $\pi R_f^{2}$. This single missing factor routinely costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Mixing up BUR, DDR, and TDR:** BUR is the transverse (hoop) stretch; DDR is the machine-direction stretch; TDR = BUR × DDR. Confusing them is a common KTU pitfall in blown-film problems. > 4. **Applying the Newtonian slit-die equation to power-law fluids:** Always verify whether the question specifies Newtonian or non-Newtonian behaviour. The two answers are structurally different. > 5. **Ignoring the temperature dependence of viscosity in process simulations:** A serious modelling error; always state that $\eta = \eta(T, \dot\gamma, p)$ in real polymer melts. > 6. **Sign convention for pressure drops:** $\Delta P$ is always **die-inlet minus die-exit** and is positive for a pumping flow. Writing it the other way will flip signs throughout the derivation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sheet forming** encompasses cast film, blown film, calendering and thermoforming. - **Cast film** is dominated by **slit-die flow + chill-roll quenching**; key parameters are die dimensions, draw-down ratio, cooling rate. - **Blown film** is dominated by **biaxial membrane mechanics** above the **frost line**; below it the film is essentially inextensible. - **Calendering** involves **pressure flow in the roll nip** and is critical for rigid PVC sheets. - **Thermoforming** is dominated by **free-stretching membrane mechanics** in the pre-contact phase; thickness distribution follows $h_f = h_0/(2\lambda^2)$ for a hemispherical dome. - **Power-law model** $\eta = K\dot\gamma^{n-1}$ is the workhorse of die-flow analysis; **Cross** and **Carreau** models extend to broader shear-rate ranges. - **Slit-die flow rate** for a power-law fluid: $Q = \dfrac{W n}{2n+1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{(2n+1)/n}$. - **Membrane equilibrium** in a blown film bubble: $P_i - P_a = \sigma_{TD}/R$ (hoop) and $d(\sigma_{MD} h)/dz = 0$ (axial). - **Avrami equation** $\theta(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^{n_a})$ describes crystallisation; $k$ depends on temperature through Hoffmann-Lauritzen. - **Process-structure-property** linkage: temperature → viscosity → flow → stretch → orientation → properties. - **Key performance metrics:** **BUR** (2–4), **DDR** (5–30), **TDR** = BUR × DDR, **Neck-in** (mm or %), **Frost-line height** (mm), **Final thickness** ($\mu$m). - **Always couple** heat transfer, non-Newtonian rheology, and (for semi-crystalline polymers) crystallisation kinetics in a serious sheet-forming simulation. - **Standard KTU mark distribution** for derivation problems: 1 mark for the starting equation, 2 marks for the constitutive law, 2 marks for the integration, 1 mark for boundary conditions, and 1 mark for the final boxed result.

topics.title: Injection moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Injection moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Injection Moulding** is a high-volume, closed-loop, cyclic polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic or thermoset material is melted (plasticized) inside a heated barrel, injected under high pressure into a closed mould cavity, cooled/solidified to retain the cavity geometry, and finally ejected as a finished part — with the entire cycle governed by coupled **non-Newtonian fluid mechanics**, **transient heat transfer**, and **thermo-mechanical constitutive behaviour** of the polymer melt.
