In the context of **PEPOT755 (Modelling & Simulation of Polymer Materials)**, the focus is not just on the manufacturing process but on the **mathematical representation** of:
1. Melt flow through the runner, gate, and cavity.
2. Heat transfer during filling, packing, and cooling.
3. Pressure–Volume–Temperature (P-V-T) behaviour.
4. Residual stress, shrinkage, and warpage prediction using **CAE tools (Moldflow, Moldex3D, OpenFOAM)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Polymer Pipette Cake" 🧁

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of injection moulding like decorating a cake with a piping bag (the polymer syringe):**
>
> - **Barrel (the piping bag):** Holds the molten polymer — your "icing."
> - **Screw/Ram (your hand):** Pushes the icing forward.
> - **Nozzle & Sprue (the metal tip):** Directs the flow into the mould.
> - **Mould Cavity (the cake-shaped stencil):** Defines the final geometry.
> - **Cooling Channels (refrigerator):** Solidifies the shape.
> - **Ejection System (the spatula):** Pushes the finished part out.
>
> Just as a baker must control **pressure, temperature, and timing** to get a clean shape, the process engineer must model **flow rate, melt temperature, mould temperature, and cooling time** to predict part quality *before* mould fabrication.

This analogy sets the foundation for understanding why each parameter must be **quantified mathematically** rather than guessed empirically — the heart of polymer process simulation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Injection Moulding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following KTU-favoured metrics must be memorized:
>
> - **Injection Pressure (P):** **70 – 140 MPa** (typical range).
> - **Melt Temperature (T_m):** **200 – 300 °C** for commodity plastics.
> - **Mould Surface Temperature (T_w):** **25 – 80 °C**.
> - **Injection Speed (v):** **10 – 200 mm/s**.
> - **Clamping Tonnage (F_c):** **50 – 10,000 kN**.
> - **Cycle Time (t_cycle):** **5 – 120 seconds**.
> - **Volumetric Shrinkage (S_v):** **0.3 % – 3.0 %** depending on semi-crystallinity.

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Moulding Variant | Polymer State | Cycle Characteristic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conventional Thermoplastic IM** | Molten thermoplastic | Cyclic, ~10–60 s |
| **Thermoset Injection** | Reacting cross-linking | Longer cure-dominated |
| **Micro-Injection Moulding (μIM)** | Micro-volume fills | Sub-gram shot weight |
| **Gas-Assisted IM (GAIM)** | Melt + inert gas core | Hollow sections |
| **Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM)** | Two reactive liquids | Mixing-head feed |
| **Insert Moulding / Overmoulding** | Polymer + metal insert | Multi-shot capable |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Injection Moulding Cycle — Four Canonical Phases

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Describe the stages of the injection moulding cycle with the corresponding pressure profile."* Master the four-phase model below.

### Phase 1 — Plastication (Melting + Metering)
- Polymer granules are fed from the hopper into the rotating screw.
- **Shear + external band heaters** melt the polymer.
- The screw rotates, conveying melt forward while retracting (translational) to accumulate a metered **shot volume V_shot** in front of the screw tip.
- **Residence time** governs thermal degradation.

### Phase 2 — Injection (Filling)
- Screw acts as a non-rotating plunger, advancing axially at controlled velocity.
- Melt is forced through the **nozzle → sprue → runner → gate → cavity**.
- This phase is **fill-pressure dominated**; 95–99 % of the cavity volume is filled.
- The simulation challenge: tracking the **moving melt front** using the **Hele-Shaw / 2.5D lubrication approximation**.

### Phase 3 — Packing (Holding) Pressure Phase
- After cavity is ~98 % full, the controller switches from **velocity control** to **pressure control**.
- Additional melt (typically **2 – 5 %** more volume) is packed in to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** as the part cools.
- **Gate freeze time t_gf** determines when packing ends.

### Phase 4 — Cooling + Ejection
- Cooling water flows through the mould channels.
- Part solidifies sufficiently so that the **ejection stress** does not deform it.
- Mould opens, ejector pins push the part out.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Injection Moulding Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table consolidates every governing equation you will need in the KTU 2024 examination. **No pipe `|` characters used; absolute values use `\vert`.**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{V_{cavity}}{t_{fill}}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $t_{fill}$ in s |
| 2 | Average shear rate (rectangular channel) | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{6Q}{w\,h^{2}}$ | $w$ width, $h$ thickness (m) |
| 3 | Pressure drop (Hagen–Poiseuille, circular runner) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8\mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | $\mu$ viscosity (Pa·s), $L$ length (m), $R$ radius (m) |
| 4 | Pressure drop (rectangular slit) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}}$ | valid for $w \gg h$ |
| 5 | Power-law viscosity | $\mu = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | $K$ consistency, $n$ power-law index |
| 6 | Clamping force required | $F_{c} = A_{proj} \cdot P_{cavity}$ | $F_c$ in N, $A_{proj}$ projected area (m²), $P$ in Pa |
| 7 | Cooling time (1-D transient) | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{m}-T_{w}}{T_{e}-T_{w}}\right]$ | $h$ part thickness, $\alpha$ thermal diffusivity, $T_e$ ejection temperature |
| 8 | Volumetric shrinkage | $S_v = \dfrac{\rho_{melt}-\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{solid}} \times 100\%$ | density ratio |
| 9 | Heat to be removed | $Q_{cool} = m \, c_{p}\, \Delta T + m \, h_{f}$ | $m$ mass, $c_p$ specific heat, $h_f$ latent heat (semi-crystalline) |
| 10 | Cycle time | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{open/close}$ | sum of phase times |
| 11 | Cavity pressure from p-V-T (Spencer–Gilmore) | $(P + \pi)(V - \omega) = R' \, T$ | modified gas-law form |
| 12 | Reynolds number (melt flow in runner) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | $D_h$ hydraulic diameter |
| 13 | Gate freeze time | $t_{gf} \approx \dfrac{h_{gate}^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{m}-T_{w}}{T_{g}-T_{w}}\right]$ | $T_g$ glass transition |

---

## 2.3 Governing Physics of Each Phase

### 2.3.1 Filling — Fluid Mechanics
- Polymer melt is a **shear-thinning non-Newtonian fluid**.
- Generalized Newtonian fluid (GNF) model with **Cross-WLF** or **Carreau-Yasuda** viscosity is widely used in **Moldflow**.
- Conservation of mass (incompressible melt):
  $$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$$
- Conservation of momentum (lubrication approximation, neglect inertia + gravity):
  $$-\nabla P + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} = 0$$

### 2.3.2 Packing — P-V-T Coupling
- Specific volume is a function of both pressure and temperature: $v = v(P,T)$.
- **Spencer-Gilmore EOS:** $(P + \pi)\,(v - \omega) = R' T$
- Modified **Tait EOS** is the industry-standard in commercial simulators.
- Packing compensates shrinkage → reduces sink marks, voids, and warpage.

### 2.3.3 Cooling — Fourier Heat Equation
- 3-D transient conduction in the polymer, coupled with convection in cooling channels.
- Most simulations use **1-D slab approximation** in the part-thickness direction.
- Cooling channel heat-transfer coefficient: $h_c \approx 100$–$5000\ \mathrm{W/m^{2}K}$.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Model Injection Moulding?

> [!NOTE]
> Simulation-driven mould design enables:
>
> 1. **Weld-line prediction** → relocate gates.
> 2. **Air-trap identification** → add venting.
> 3. **Sink mark minimization** → optimize packing profile.
> 4. **Cycle-time reduction** → balanced cooling line layout.
> 5. **Material substitution** → validate recyclate use before tooling.
> 6. **Reduced prototyping cost** → up to **70 % saving** in development.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop in a Rectangular Runner (Lubrication Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **guaranteed KTU derivation question** — practice the algebra carefully.

**Starting assumptions:**
- Steady, laminar, incompressible flow.
- Pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ is constant.
- No-slip at walls: $v(0) = v(h) = 0$.
- Pure shear: only velocity component $v_x(y)$ exists.

**Step 1 — Momentum equation (z-component, fully developed):**
$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \mu\,\frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{d y^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once with respect to y:**
$$\frac{d v_{x}}{d y} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\, y + C_{1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply symmetry (no shear at centre) $\left.\dfrac{dv_x}{dy}\right\vert_{y=h/2} = 0$:**
$$0 = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\,\frac{h}{2} + C_{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{1} = -\frac{h}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back and integrate:**
$$v_{x}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\!\left(y^{2} - \frac{h^{2}}{4}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow per unit width $w$:**
$$Q = w \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} v_x(y)\, dy$$

$$Q = \frac{w}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\!\left(y^{2}-\frac{h^{2}}{4}\right)dy$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\!\left(y^{2}-\frac{h^{2}}{4}\right)dy = \left[\frac{y^{3}}{3}-\frac{h^{2}y}{4}\right]_{-h/2}^{+h/2} = -\frac{h^{3}}{6}$$

**Step 7 — Result:**
$$Q = -\frac{w\,h^{3}}{12\mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 8 — Integrate over length $L$ to get pressure drop:**
$$\Delta P = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,Q}{w\,h^{3}} \quad \text{(rectangular slit, plane Poiseuille flow)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Pressure drop scales **inversely with the cube of the channel thickness**. Halving the runner depth increases $\Delta P$ by **8×** — critical in mould design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cooling Time for a Thick Polymer Slab

We seek the time required for the **centreline** of a polymer slab (half-thickness $h$) to cool from melt temperature $T_m$ to ejection temperature $T_e$.

**Step 1 — 1-D transient conduction in slab, with both surfaces at $T_w$:**
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial y^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Initial & boundary conditions:**
$$T(y,0) = T_m, \quad T(\pm h, t) = T_w$$

**Step 3 — Series solution (separation of variables):**
$$T(y,t) - T_w = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi y}{2h}\right) \exp\!\left(-\alpha \frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}}{4h^{2}}t\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply initial condition by Fourier cosine series:**
$$A_n = \frac{4}{\pi}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}(T_m-T_w)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at centreline $y=0$:**
$$\frac{T(0,t)-T_w}{T_m - T_w} = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}\exp\!\left(-\alpha \frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}}{4h^{2}}t\right)$$

**Step 6 — Retain only the leading term ($n=0$, valid at $t$ large enough):**
$$\frac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \approx \frac{4}{\pi}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^{2}\alpha}{4h^{2}}t_{cool}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t_{cool}$:**
$$t_{cool} = \frac{4h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{4}{\pi}\,\frac{T_m-T_w}{T_e-T_w}\right] = \frac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\,\frac{T_m-T_w}{T_e-T_w}\right]$$

(after using $\ln(4/\pi)=\ln(8/\pi^{2})/2$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooling time scales with the square of part thickness** — doubling $h$ quadruples cycle time.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Clamping Force from Cavity Pressure

**Step 1 — Project the part onto the mould parting plane** to get $A_{proj}$.

**Step 2 — Average cavity pressure during packing** is $P_{cav}$ (peak may be 2× higher near the gate).

**Step 3 — Force tending to open the mould:**
$$F_{open} = A_{proj}\cdot P_{cav}$$

**Step 4 — Safety factor (typical 1.1–1.3):**
$$F_{clamp,\,required} = 1.2 \cdot A_{proj}\cdot P_{cav}$$

**Step 5 — Selection rule:**
$$F_{clamp,\,machine} \geq F_{clamp,\,required}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students often use $A_{proj} = $ total surface area, forgetting that only the **parting-plane projection** resists opening. Always project perpendicular to the mould opening direction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A rectangular plaque of dimensions 200 mm × 100 mm × 3 mm is injection moulded in polypropylene. Given: $T_m = 220\,°C$, $T_w = 30\,°C$, $T_e = 90\,°C$, $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{m^{2}/s}$. Estimate cooling time.

**Step 1 — Slab half-thickness:**
$$h = \dfrac{3\,\text{mm}}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting values:**
$$t_{cool} = \dfrac{(1.5\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{\pi^{2}(1.0\times 10^{-7})}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{220-30}{90-30}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the prefactor:**
$$\dfrac{(2.25\times 10^{-6})}{9.87\times 10^{-7}} = 2.28\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm argument:**
$$\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} = 0.8106;\quad \dfrac{190}{60} = 3.1667$$
$$0.8106 \times 3.1667 = 2.5667$$
$$\ln(2.5667) = 0.943$$

**Step 5 — Final cooling time:**
$$t_{cool} = 2.28 \times 0.943 \approx 2.15\ \text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *minimum* cooling time at the centreline; commercial cycle planners add a 30–50 % margin for safety and ejectability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Injection Moulding Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOP[Hopper] --> BAR[Heated Barrel with Band Heaters]
    BAR --> SCR[Rotating Screw Plastication Unit]
    SCR --> NZL[Nozzle]
    NZL --> SPR[Sprue and Runner System]
    SPR --> GAT[Gate]
    GAT --> CAV[Mould Cavity]
    CAV --> COOL[Cooling Channel Network]
    COOL --> EJ[Ejector Pin Assembly]
    EJ --> OUT[Finished Part Output]

    CTRL[Process Controller PLC] -. controls .-> SCR
    CTRL -. controls .-> BAR
    CTRL -. controls .-> COOL
    CTRL -. controls .-> EJ

    CLMP[Hydraulic Clamping Unit] -. holds shut .-> CAV

    SENS1[Temperature Sensor] --> CTRL
    SENS2[Pressure Transducer] --> CTRL
    SENS3[Linear Position Encoder] --> CTRL
```

---

## 4.2 Process Cycle Block Diagram with Pressure Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Phase 1 Plastication] --> B[Phase 2 Injection Filling]
    B --> C[Phase 3 Packing Holding]
    C --> D[Phase 4 Cooling and Solidification]
    D --> E[Phase 5 Mould Opening and Ejection]
    E --> A

    A -.->|Screw retracts to meter shot| N1[Pressure stays low and stable]
    B -.->|Velocity controlled fill| N2[Pressure rises sharply near cavity fill]
    C -.->|Pressure controlled pack| N3[Pressure decays exponentially with gate freeze]
    D -.->|Cavity pressure ambient| N4[Pressure equals atmospheric after gate seal]
    E -.->|Clamp opens| N5[No internal pressure ejector stroke begins]
```

---

## 4.3 Simulation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[CAD Geometry STEP or IGES] --> P2[Mid-surface or 3D Mesh Generation]
    P2 --> P3[Material Selection from Database]
    P3 --> P4[Process Parameter Definition]
    P4 --> P5[Solver Execution Fill Pack Cool Warp]
    P5 --> P6[Post Processing Visualization]
    P6 --> P7{Results Acceptable?}
    P7 -->|Yes| P8[Generate Mould Design Report]
    P7 -->|No| P4
```

---

## 4.4 Heat and Mass Transfer Block Schematic During Filling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MELT[Melt Stream T_melt high] -->|Convective heat transfer h_c| WALL[Mould Wall at T_wall]
    WALL -->|Conduction through steel| COOL[Cooling Channel Water at T_water]
    MELT -->|Lubrication flow through Hele-Shaw cavity| FP[Fill Pressure Field P x y t]
    FP -->|Shear heating dissipation| MELT
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `node1` is avoided; instead named with semantic tags like `HOP`, `SCR`) and **plain-text labels without bold/italic/HTML** — strictly compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 schematic rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the four main stages of the injection moulding cycle and state the controlling variable during each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Controlling Variable |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. Plastication | Screw rotational speed (rpm) and back pressure |
| 2. Injection (Filling) | Injection velocity / flow rate |
| 3. Packing / Holding | Packing pressure profile |
| 4. Cooling & Ejection | Mould cooling water temperature and cycle time |

*(1 mark per stage identification + 1 mark collectively for controller. Total = 4 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]
**Differentiate between *filling* and *packing* phases of injection moulding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Filling | Packing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Objective** | Fill 95–99 % of cavity volume | Compensate for shrinkage after cavity is full |
| **Control Mode** | Velocity (flow rate) controlled | Pressure controlled |
| **Duration** | ~0.5 – 3 seconds | ~2 – 10 seconds |
| **Cavity Pressure** | Rises sharply to peak | Decays as gate freezes |
| **Simulation Variable** | Fill time, weld lines, air traps | Pressure profile, density gradient |

*(2 marks for ≥3 differences + 1 mark for clarity. Total = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 module-internal choice: **Answer ANY ONE full question.**

---

### ✦ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for **pressure drop in a rectangular runner** using the lubrication approximation. Starting from the momentum equation, clearly state all assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Assumptions (2 marks):**
   - Steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian melt.
   - Fully developed, unidirectional velocity $v_x(y)$.
   - No-slip at the runner walls $v_x(\pm h/2)=0$.
   - Inertia and body forces neglected (lubrication/Hele-Shaw approximation).
   - Pressure gradient $dP/dx$ is constant along the runner.

2. **Governing equation (1 mark):**
   $$\frac{dP}{dx}=\mu\frac{d^{2}v_{x}}{dy^{2}}$$

3. **First integration and application of symmetry** $\left.\dfrac{dv_x}{dy}\right\vert_{y=h/2}=0$ **(1 mark):**
   $$\frac{dv_{x}}{dy}=\frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\!\left(y-\frac{h}{2}\right)$$

4. **Second integration + no-slip at $y=-h/2$ (1 mark):**
   $$v_{x}(y)=\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\!\left(y^{2}-hy\right)$$

5. **Flow rate per unit width (1 mark):**
   $$Q/w=\int_{-h/2}^{h/2}v_{x}\,dy = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

6. **Final pressure-drop expression (1 mark):**
   $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,Q}{w\,h^{3}}}$$

---

**(b)** For an injection mould, a rectangular runner of length 80 mm, width 6 mm, and height 3 mm carries melt of viscosity 350 Pa·s at a flow rate of $3 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s. Compute the pressure drop. Comment on how this changes if the runner depth is halved. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula (3 marks):**
$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 350 \times 80\times 10^{-3} \times 3\times 10^{-6}}{6\times 10^{-3} \times (3\times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator (2 marks):**
$$12 \times 350 = 4200$$
$$4200 \times 80\times 10^{-3} = 336$$
$$336 \times 3\times 10^{-6} = 1.008\times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (1 mark):**
$$6\times 10^{-3} \times 27\times 10^{-9} = 1.62\times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 4 — Result (1 mark):**
$$\Delta P = \frac{1.008\times 10^{-3}}{1.62\times 10^{-10}} = 6.22\times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \approx 6.22\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Halving the depth $h \to h/2$ (extra credit):**
$$\Delta P_{\text{new}} = 8 \times \Delta P_{\text{old}} \approx 49.8\,\text{MPa}$$
The pressure drop increases by a factor of **8** (cubic dependency on $h$).

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]

**(a)** With the aid of a clearly labelled diagram, describe the **four phases of the injection moulding cycle** and the corresponding **pressure-time profile**. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Phase 1 – Plastication (1 mark):** Screw rotates, melt accumulates in front of screw tip. Cavity pressure ≈ 0 (mould is empty/closed from previous cycle).
2. **Phase 2 – Filling (2 marks):** Screw advances axially. Velocity-controlled. Pressure rises sharply as the cavity is filled; peak occurs just before fill completion.
3. **Phase 3 – Packing (2 marks):** Switch to pressure-controlled mode. Pressure decays exponentially as the part cools and the gate seals.
4. **Phase 4 – Cooling + Ejection (1 mark):** Internal cavity pressure returns to atmospheric; mould opens, ejector pushes part out.
5. **Sketch of P–t curve (1 mark):** Labelled axes showing fill peak, packing plateau, exponential decay.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention that the *initial* pressure of the next cycle is zero — drawing the curve as continuous from previous cycle's decay is a common mark-losing error.

---

**(b)** A 4 mm thick PP plaque ($T_m = 230\,°C$, $T_w = 35\,°C$, $T_e = 100\,°C$, $\alpha = 1.1 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$) is to be moulded. Estimate the **cooling time** using the 1-D slab formula and compute the **minimum clamp tonnage** required if projected area is 0.025 m² and average cavity pressure is 35 MPa. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Half-thickness (1 mark):**
$$h = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Cooling time formula (1 mark):**
$$t_{cool}=\frac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha}\ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\cdot\frac{T_m-T_w}{T_e-T_w}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Prefactor (1 mark):**
$$\frac{(2\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{\pi^{2}(1.1\times 10^{-7})}=\frac{4\times 10^{-6}}{1.086\times 10^{-6}}\approx 3.683\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Logarithm (1 mark):**
$$\frac{8}{\pi^{2}} = 0.8106;\quad \frac{230-35}{100-35} = \frac{195}{65} = 3.0$$
$$\ln(0.8106\times 3.0)=\ln(2.432)=0.889$$

**Step 5 — Result (1 mark):**
$$t_{cool}\approx 3.683\times 0.889 = 3.27\ \text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Clamping force (2 marks):**
$$F_{clamp}=1.2\cdot A_{proj}\cdot P_{cav}=1.2\times 0.025\times 35\times 10^{6}=1.05\times 10^{6}\,\text{N}$$
$$F_{clamp}\approx 1050\,\text{kN}\approx 107\,\text{ton}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often omit the **1.2 safety factor** in the clamp-force calculation. KTU evaluators explicitly deduct 1 mark for ignoring the safety margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the exam hall, confirm you can tick every box below:**

- [ ] I can **define** injection moulding with the four-phase cycle terminology.
- [ ] I can **draw and label** the P–t curve across all four phases.
- [ ] I can **derive** $\Delta P = 12\mu LQ/(wh^3)$ from the lubrication momentum equation.
- [ ] I know the **cubic dependence** of pressure drop on runner depth.
- [ ] I can **derive** the 1-D slab cooling-time formula.
- [ ] I know $t_{cool} \propto h^2$.
- [ ] I can **compute** clamp force $F_c = 1.2\, A_{proj}\, P$ with safety factor.
- [ ] I understand **velocity-controlled filling** vs. **pressure-controlled packing**.
- [ ] I can explain **gate freeze time** and its role in determining packing duration.
- [ ] I am familiar with **Cross-WLF** / **Carreau-Yasuda** viscosity models used in Moldflow.
- [ ] I can describe **P-V-T behaviour** using **Tait EOS**.
- [ ] I can list **Moldflow / Moldex3D** outputs: weld lines, air traps, sink marks, fibre orientation.
- [ ] I can justify **simulation-driven mould design** benefits (cost, time, quality).
- [ ] I know the standard metric ranges for $P$, $T_m$, $T_w$, $v$, $F_c$, $t_{cycle}$.
- [ ] I understand the **difference between filling, packing, cooling, and ejection**.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:** *Modelling = Coupling (Fluid + Heat + P-V-T). Simulation = Predicting (Sink + Warp + Weld + Air-trap) before steel is cut.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Compression moulding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Compression moulding** is a closed-mould, high-pressure thermoset (and certain thermoplastic) processing technique in which a defined mass of polymer charge — in the form of a powder, granular pellet, preform, SMC/BMC dough, or preheated sheet — is placed directly into the lower half of a heated, matched-metal die cavity. The mould is then closed by a hydraulic (or mechanical) press, the applied pressure forces the material to flow, fill the cavity, and conform to the cavity geometry, while the elevated mould temperature initiates the cross-linking (cure) reaction for thermosets or sintering/cooling consolidation for thermoplastics. The part is held under pressure and temperature until the crosslink density reaches a degree of cure (α) sufficient for demoulding, after which the mould opens and the part is ejected. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Examiner Acceptable Form):** > *“Compression moulding is a high-pressure, heated-die forming process in which a polymer charge is shaped between two halves of a matched metal mould by the application of external pressure and heat, the part being consolidated and cured (for thermosets) or cooled (for thermoplastics) within the closed mould before ejection.”* **Standard Process Metrics (KTU Board Weightage Terms):** - **Mould Pressure:** 10 – 200 MPa (typical) - **Mould Temperature:** 130 °C – 200 °C (thermosets) - **Cure Time:** 30 s – 10 min (material dependent) - **Part Shrinkage:** 0.1 % – 1.5 % (post-cure allowance) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Idli Mould (South-Indian Steamed Cake Maker) 🍲** > Imagine pouring spiced lentil batter (the polymer charge) into the bottom tray of a two-plate hot idli mould. When you press the top plate down and lock the clamp, the batter **spreads sideways** (flow under pressure), touches the hot surface and **begins to set** (cross-linking / cure). After a few minutes, the steam + heat cooks the batter solid. You then open the mould and pop out a perfectly-shaped idli (the finished part). The idli mould is essentially a low-temperature, low-pressure analog of a compression mould. Industrial compression moulding is the *same physics* — *flow + heat + cure* — but executed at 150 °C and 15–100 MPa on steel dies. ## 1.3 Why Compression Moulding? — The Engineering Justification - **Net-shape or near-net-shape** production — minimal secondary machining. - Best suited for **large, thick-walled, fibre-reinforced parts** where injection moulding's high shear would break fibres. - Low tooling cost relative to injection moulds for short/medium runs. - Capability to mould **very high fibre loadings** (up to 65 wt% in SMC). - Excellent for **electrical-grade thermosets** (phenolic, melamine, epoxy BMC) due to low voids and low residual stress. ## 1.4 Classification Snapshot | Family | Charge Form | Typical Materials | Cycle Time | |---|---|---|---| | **Conventional Compression Moulding** | Powder / granules / preform | Phenolics, melamine, urea | 2 – 10 min | | **SMC Compression Moulding** | Sheet Moulding Compound | Polyester + glass fibre | 1 – 5 min | | **BMC Compression Moulding** | Bulk Moulding Compound (dough) | Polyester, epoxy + fillers | 1 – 3 min | | **GMT Compression Moulding** | Glass-Mat Thermoplastic | PP / PA + glass mat | 30 – 90 s | | **Hot/ Cold Compression Moulding** | Prepreg | Phenolic prepregs (aerospace) | 30 – 60 min | | **Matched-Die Compression Moulding** | Charge in matched dies | Rubber (vulcanization) | 5 – 30 min | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Compression Moulding Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic The complete cycle consists of **six distinct stages**. Each stage has its own physics, which is what a Modelling & Simulation course must capture. ### Step 1 — Charge Preparation The polymer charge is pre-weighed (mass-controlled, not volume-controlled like injection moulding). The mass is calculated to give the desired part thickness considering bulk factor: $$m_{charge} = \rho_p \cdot V_{part} \cdot (1 + f_{BF})$$ where $\rho_p$ is the cured part density, $V_{part}$ is the part volume, and $f_{BF}$ is the **bulk factor** (typically 1.5 – 3.0 for loose powder). ### Step 2 — Charge Loading Charge is placed manually or robotically in the lower (cavity) half of the mould. The **charge geometry** and **placement location** are critical — they determine the flow path during closure. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for simulation:** Non-uniform initial charge distribution produces asymmetric flow fronts → uneven fibre orientation → warpage prediction errors. Mould-flow simulators (e.g., Moldflow, Moldex3D) require accurate charge placement as boundary input. ### Step 3 — Mould Closure & Flow Phase The press moves the upper (force) plug down. As the gap $h(t)$ decreases, the polymer flows radially (or in a complex 3D path) to fill the cavity. This phase is governed by **continuity + momentum + energy + cure** equations simultaneously. - The flow is typically modelled as a **Hele-Shaw (lubrication) flow** because the cavity gap is much smaller than the lateral dimensions. - Pressure builds non-uniformly; regions far from the charge require higher pressure to reach. ### Step 4 — Cure / Consolidation Phase Once the cavity is filled, the press reaches final closure force. No further macroscopic flow occurs. Heat conducted from the mould walls raises the polymer temperature, and the cure reaction proceeds from the wall inward (the centre is the last to cure). ### Step 5 — Demoulding Decision The part is ejected when the **degree of cure at the centre** $\alpha_c$ exceeds a critical value $\alpha_{cr}$ (typically 0.7 – 0.9 for thermosets), ensuring the part is rigid enough to handle without distortion. ### Step 6 — Post-Cure (Optional) For elevated-temperature-service parts (e.g., aerospace prepregs), a free-standing oven post-cure at $T > T_{mould}$ completes the cure to $\alpha \to 1$. ## 2.2 The Cure Kinetics — Heart of the Simulation For thermosets (the dominant compression-moulded material), the degree of cure $\alpha$ evolves as: $$\boxed{\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = \left(k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m\right)(1 - \alpha)^n}$$ This is the **Kamal autocatalytic model** — the most commonly tested in KTU exams for cure modelling. The rate constants follow **Arrhenius temperature dependence**: $$k_i(T) = A_i \exp\left(-\frac{E_i}{R\,T}\right), \quad i = 1, 2$$ where $A_i$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_i$ is the activation energy, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature. ## 2.3 The Governing Energy Equation with Cure The 1-D transient heat equation in the through-thickness direction $z$ (the dominant direction due to high surface-area-to-volume ratio of mould): $$\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \rho \, H_r \, \frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$ where $H_r$ is the **total heat of reaction** (J/kg). The exothermic cure term is the heat source that can cause the centreline temperature to **overshoot** the wall temperature — a major KTU essay point. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Initial and Boundary Conditions:** > - $T(z, 0) = T_0$ (charge temperature) > - $T(\pm h, t) = T_{wall}$ (Dirichlet, isothermal mould — a good first approximation) > - $\alpha(z, 0) = \alpha_0$ (initial cure, often 0) ## 2.4 The Flow Equation — Hele-Shaw Approximation Using the lubrication approximation, the local cavity pressure $P(x,y,t)$ satisfies: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{h}{E^*}\right) = \nabla \cdot \left(\frac{h^3}{12 \eta} \nabla P\right)$$ where: - $h(x,y,t)$ is the local cavity gap thickness - $E^*$ is the effective compression modulus of the compound - $\eta(\alpha, T, \dot{\gamma})$ is the viscosity, which itself depends on cure state, temperature, and shear rate. For SMC, a power-law viscosity is commonly used: $$\eta = \eta_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_\eta}{R T}\right) \left(\frac{\alpha_g - \alpha}{\alpha_g - \alpha_0}\right)^{\!\kappa}$$ ## 2.5 The Force / Pressure Relationship The press force is related to cavity pressure by integrating over the projected area: $$F_{press} = \int_A P(x,y,t) \, dA + F_{clamp}$$ > [!CAUTION] > The KTU exam pitfall: confusing **clamp tonnage** with **cavity pressure**. Clamp tonnage is the press capacity; cavity pressure is the *resultant* within the mould, typically 5 – 50 MPa, while the press reads total force in tonnes. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$ | Kamal cure kinetics | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ | | 2 | $k_i = A_i \exp(-E_i / R T)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ | | 3 | $\rho c_p \partial T/\partial t = \partial_z(k \partial T/\partial z) + \rho H_r \, d\alpha/dt$ | Energy equation with cure source | $\mathrm{W/m^3}$ | | 4 | $\partial_t(h/E^*) = \nabla \cdot [(h^3/12\eta) \nabla P]$ | Hele-Shaw flow in cavity | $\mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$ | | 5 | $F = P \cdot A_{proj} + F_{clamp}$ | Press force / cavity pressure | $\mathrm{N}$ | | 6 | $m_{charge} = \rho_p V (1+f_{BF})$ | Charge mass (with bulk factor) | $\mathrm{kg}$ | | 7 | $\eta = \eta_0 \exp(E_\eta/RT)[(\alpha_g-\alpha)/(\alpha_g-\alpha_0)]^\kappa$ | Chemo-rheological viscosity | $\mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$ | | 8 | $\alpha_{cr} \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ | Demoulding criterion | — | | 9 | $Shrinkage = (1 - \rho_c/\rho_s) \times 100\%$ | Volumetric / linear shrinkage | % | | 10 | $Bi = h_t L / k$ | Biot number (wall vs. internal resistance) | dimensionless | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Carbon-fibre/phenolic prepregs (e.g., rocket nozzle throats) are compression moulded; cure models predict exotherm overshoot to avoid thermal degradation. - **Automotive:** SMC car body panels (Class-A surfaces) require coupled flow-cure simulation to predict fibre orientation and eliminate weld lines. - **Electrical:** Phenolic switchgear housings — cure simulation ensures complete crosslinking to meet dielectric strength standards. - **Cookware/Bakelite:** Melamine-phenolic compression moulded plates — cure models predict demould time to optimize cycle. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Kamal Cure Equation from Mass-Action Kinetics > **Objective:** Derive the rate expression $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1 - \alpha)^n$ for an autocatalysed thermoset cure. **Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction.** Let $\alpha$ be the degree of cure, defined as the fraction of total reactive groups consumed at time $t$: $$\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C(t)}{C_0}$$ where $C_0$ is the initial concentration of reactive groups (e.g., epoxide equivalents) and $C(t)$ is the remaining unreacted concentration at time $t$. Differentiating: $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = -\frac{1}{C_0}\,\frac{dC}{dt}$$ **Step 2 — Write the reaction rate.** For an autocatalysed cure, the reaction rate is *proportional* to both the remaining unreacted groups **and** the product groups (which act as catalysts for further reaction). The empirical kinetic law is: $$-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_1 \, C \, C^* + k_2 \, C^m \, (C^*)^n$$ where $C^*$ is the concentration of catalytic species (often the hydroxyl group formed in epoxy-amine cure). At time $t$, $C^* \propto \alpha$ (more product = more catalyst), and $C = C_0(1-\alpha)$. **Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.** Substituting $C = C_0(1-\alpha)$ and $C^* = C_0 \alpha$ (with appropriate stoichiometric factors absorbed into the rate constants): $$-\frac{dC}{dt} = C_0 \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k_1 C_0(1-\alpha) \cdot C_0 \alpha + k_2 (C_0(1-\alpha))^m (C_0 \alpha)^n$$ Dividing through by $C_0$ and absorbing powers of $C_0$ into redefined $k_1$, $k_2$: $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k_1 \alpha (1-\alpha) + k_2 \alpha^n (1-\alpha)^m$$ Reordering terms and renaming exponents ($m \leftrightarrow n$) yields the canonical Kamal form: $$\boxed{\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n}$$ **Step 4 — Verify limiting behaviour.** - At $\alpha = 0$: $\;d\alpha/dt = k_1$, confirming a non-zero initial rate (autocatalysis). - At $\alpha \to 1$: $\;d\alpha/dt \to 0$ (reaction halts as reactants deplete). ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Equation with Internal Heat Generation **Step 1 — Start from 1-D Fourier conduction + volumetric source.** $$q_z = -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}$$ Energy balance on a differential slab of thickness $dz$ (no flow assumption during cure phase): $$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial q_z}{\partial z} + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$ **Step 2 — Expand the divergence term.** $$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$ **Step 3 — Express $\dot{Q}_{gen}$ from cure kinetics.** The heat generated per unit volume per unit time by the cure reaction is the total heat of reaction $H_r$ (J/kg) multiplied by the cure rate and density: $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = \rho H_r \frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$ **Step 4 — Final governing PDE.** $$\boxed{\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \rho H_r \frac{d\alpha}{dt}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Centreline Temperature Overshoot > **Problem (KTU ESE style):** > A 10 mm-thick epoxy slab is compression moulded at $T_w = 180$ °C. The initial temperature is $T_0 = 25$ °C, $\rho = 1200$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1200$ J/(kg·K), $k = 0.25$ W/(m·K), $H_r = 350$ kJ/kg. Approximate the steady-state adiabatic temperature rise due to complete cure. **Step 1 — Adiabatic energy balance (no conduction):** Set $k = 0$ in the energy equation: $$\rho c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho H_r \frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate from $\alpha = 0$ to $\alpha = 1$:** $$\int_{T_0}^{T_{ad}} c_p \, dT = H_r \int_0^1 d\alpha$$ **Step 3 — Assume $c_p$ constant:** $$c_p (T_{ad} - T_0) = H_r$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the adiabatic temperature rise $\Delta T_{ad}$:** $$T_{ad} = T_0 + \frac{H_r}{c_p} = 25 + \frac{350\,000}{1200}$$ $$T_{ad} = 25 + 291.67 = 316.67 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** $$\Delta T_{ad} \approx \frac{H_r}{c_p} \approx 292 \text{ °C}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight for the Board Exam:** The adiabatic temperature rise (≈ 292 °C) is enormous. In practice, conduction to the mould walls limits the rise, but a centreline overshoot of $T_w + 50$ to $100$ °C is realistic. If $T_{overshoot}$ exceeds the **degradation temperature** $T_d$ of the polymer, the part will be scorched — a common compression-moulding defect. This is the central trade-off: high $T_w$ speeds cure but risks exotherm damage; low $T_w$ is safe but slow. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity Summary | Process Parameter | Effect on Cure Time | Effect on Exotherm | Effect on Shrinkage | Simulation Input | |---|---|---|---|---| | ↑ $T_w$ (mould temp) | ↓ (Arrhenius) | ↑ | Slight ↑ | Boundary condition | | ↑ $H_r$ (heat of reaction) | ↓ (faster cure) | ↑↑ | ↑ | Material property | | ↑ Part thickness | ↑ (slow conduction) | ↑ (less heat loss) | Slight ↑ | Geometry | | ↑ Filler loading | ↑ (inert dilution) | ↓ | ↓ | Modified $c_p$, $H_r$ | | ↑ Initial $\alpha_0$ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Initial condition | | ↑ $k_1, k_2$ (kinetic) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | Material fit | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Compression Moulding Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Part Design and Charge Calculation] --> B[Weigh Polymer Charge
m = rho_p V 1+ f_BF] B --> C[Load Charge into Lower Die Cavity] C --> D[Close Mould - Plunger Descends] D --> E{Flow Phase
h t decreases} E -->|Cavity Filled| F[Cure / Consolidation Phase
Heat conducted from walls] F --> G{Alpha at Centre
Greater Than Alpha_cr?} G -->|No| F G -->|Yes| H[Open Mould] H --> I[Eject Part] I --> J{Post-Cure Required?} J -->|Yes| K[Oven Post-Cure at T greater Than T_wall] J -->|No| L[Finish - Part Inspection] K --> L ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Coupled Simulation Framework ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Geometry Input
CAD and Mesh] --> B1[Pre-processor] A2[Material Database
k, cp, H_r, Eta, Kamal Constants] --> B1 A3[Process Inputs
T_wall, P_clamp, t_cycle, alpha_0] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Flow Solver
Hele-Shaw / Navier Stokes] B1 --> C2[Energy Solver
Fourier with Cure Source] B1 --> C3[Cure Kinetics Module
Kamal + Arrhenius] B1 --> C4[Viscosity Module
Chemo-rheological] C1 <--> C4 C2 <--> C3 C1 --> D1[Post-processor] C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> E1[Predicted Outputs
P field, T field, alpha field, Shrinkage] D1 --> E2[Defect Indicators
Voids, Weld lines, Exotherm Overshoot] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Coupled Physics of Compression Moulding ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Inputs I1[Temperature T z t] I2[Pressure P x y t] I3[Cure State alpha z t] end subgraph Governing PDEs G1[Energy Equation] G2[Hele Shaw Flow Equation] G3[Kamal Cure Kinetics] G4[Chemo rheological Viscosity] end subgraph Outputs O1[Deflection and Warpage] O2[Cure Time Prediction] O3[Cycle Time Optimisation] end I1 --> G1 I2 --> G2 I3 --> G3 G1 --> G4 G2 --> G4 G3 --> G4 G4 --> G2 G1 --> O1 G3 --> O2 G1 --> O3 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Through-Thickness Cure and Temperature Evolution ```mermaid flowchart TD A[T equals T_zero at t equals zero
alpha equals alpha_zero] --> B[Mould Closes
Heat Conduction Begins] B --> C[Wall Region Heats First
alpha at Wall increases] C --> D[Cure Heat Released at Wall
Local T rises] D --> E[Heat Conducts Inward
Centreline T rises] E --> F[Centreline alpha increases] F --> G{alpha_centre Greater Than alpha_cr?} G -->|No| E G -->|Yes| H[Safe to Demould] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define compression moulding. List any four materials commonly processed by this technique. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]` **Model Answer (Board Key):** Compression moulding is a high-pressure, heated-die forming process in which a pre-measured polymer charge is shaped between the two halves of a matched metal mould by the simultaneous application of external pressure and heat, the part being consolidated and cured in-situ before ejection. Four materials commonly processed: 1. **Phenolic resins** (switchgear, cookware) 2. **Melamine-formaldehyde** (kitchenware, tableware) 3. **Sheet Moulding Compound — SMC** (automotive panels) 4. **Bulk Moulding Compound — BMC** (electrical housings) *(Acceptable alternatives: urea-formaldehyde, epoxy-novolac prepregs, GMT-PP, natural rubber compounds.)* --- ### Q2. State the Kamal autocatalytic cure equation. What is the physical significance of the term $k_1$? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Model Answer:** $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$$ **Physical significance of $k_1$:** $k_1$ is the **non-catalysed (initiation) rate constant**. It represents the baseline rate of the cure reaction that proceeds even in the absence of the autocatalytic species (i.e., at $\alpha = 0$, $d\alpha/dt = k_1$). It follows Arrhenius temperature dependence: $k_1 = A_1 \exp(-E_1/RT)$. A non-zero $k_1$ is what distinguishes autocatalytic cure from nth-order cure. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two full questions. Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the step-by-step procedure of the compression moulding process for a Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC) automotive bumper. Discuss the role of the bulk factor and charge placement. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Kamal cure rate expression starting from mass-action kinetics. Hence, for an epoxy-amine system with $A_1 = 1.2 \times 10^4\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$, $E_1 = 60\,\mathrm{kJ/mol}$, $A_2 = 4.5 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$, $E_2 = 70\,\mathrm{kJ/mol}$, $m = 1$, $n = 1.5$, compute the initial cure rate at $T = 160$ °C and $\alpha = 0$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Sketch (5 marks verbal, 2 marks for the cycle description):** The compression moulding cycle for SMC consists of six steps: 1. **Charge preparation** — SMC sheets are cut to weigh $m = \rho_p V_{part}(1 + f_{BF})$ with $f_{BF} \approx 1.2$ for SMC; the charge covers 30 – 60 % of the projected area. 2. **Charge loading** — Sheets are stacked in the lower die at a strategic location to balance the flow path. 3. **Mould closure** — The press closes at 10 – 25 mm/s; from gap 50 mm to ~3 mm, material flows radially. 4. **Cure phase** — Mould held at 140 – 160 °C and 7 – 15 MPa for 60 – 180 s; cure advances from walls to centre. 5. **Demoulding** — Opened when $\alpha_{centre} \geq 0.85$ (verified by simulation or in-mould sensors). 6. **Post-processing** — Trimming of flash and edge finishing. **Role of bulk factor (1 mark):** SMC sheet has higher volume than the cured part because of trapped air and fibre loft. $f_{BF}$ corrects the charge mass so the final part has the design thickness after the air is squeezed out and the resin flows. **Role of charge placement (1 mark):** Asymmetric placement causes asymmetric flow → fibre orientation gradient → warpage and sink marks. In simulation, charge geometry is a critical input for accurate flow-front prediction. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Derivation (5 marks):** (See Section 3.1 of these notes.) Start from $d\alpha/dt$ proportional to reactant × catalyst, define $\alpha = (C_0 - C)/C_0$, substitute, and obtain the Kamal form. **Numerical computation (2 marks):** At $T = 160\,°C = 433.15\,K$ and $\alpha = 0$: $$k_1 = A_1 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_1}{RT}\right) = 1.2 \times 10^4 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{60\,000}{8.314 \times 433.15}\right)$$ Compute the exponent: $$-\frac{60\,000}{8.314 \times 433.15} = -\frac{60\,000}{3601.3} = -16.66$$ $$k_1 = 1.2 \times 10^4 \times e^{-16.66} = 1.2 \times 10^4 \times 5.83 \times 10^{-8} \approx 6.99 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$$ At $\alpha = 0$, the $k_2$ term vanishes, so: $$\boxed{\left.\frac{d\alpha}{dt}\right|_{\alpha=0} = k_1 \approx 7.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}}$$ This is the initial cure rate. The time to reach $\alpha = 0.5$ (order of magnitude estimate) is $\sim 0.5/k_1 \approx 715$ s at this temperature, indicating cure at 160 °C is moderate. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]` **(a)** Write the energy equation for the through-thickness direction during the cure phase of compression moulding, identifying each term. Explain why the centreline temperature may exceed the wall temperature, and state its practical consequence. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A phenolic SMC charge with $H_r = 280$ kJ/kg, $c_p = 1300$ J/(kg·K), $\rho = 1450$ kg/m³ is moulded at $T_w = 170$ °C from $T_0 = 30$ °C. Calculate the adiabatic temperature rise and the maximum safe part thickness assuming the exotherm is limited to 30 °C above $T_w$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Energy equation (3 marks):** $$\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \rho \, H_r \, \frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$ - $\rho c_p \partial T/\partial t$ : rate of internal energy accumulation - $\partial_z(k \partial T/\partial z)$ : heat conduction through the thickness - $\rho H_r \, d\alpha/dt$ : volumetric heat generation from the exothermic cure **Centreline overshoot mechanism (2 marks):** At the wall, heat is conducted *out* into the mould steel (high $k$ acts as a heat sink). The wall cures first and the heat generated there is rapidly removed. At the centreline, heat is generated but can only escape by conduction through the half-thickness of cured material, which has a much lower $k$. As the reaction accelerates autocatalytically and heat cannot escape fast enough, the centreline temperature overshoots $T_w$. **Practical consequence (2 marks):** If the centreline temperature exceeds the **degradation temperature** $T_d$ of the polymer, thermal degradation occurs, causing discolouration, loss of mechanical properties, voids from volatile by-products, and even charring. For most thermosets, mould temperatures are deliberately chosen so the exotherm overshoot stays $20$ – $30$ °C below $T_d$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Adiabatic temperature rise (3 marks):** $$\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{H_r}{c_p} = \frac{280\,000}{1300} = 215.4\,°C$$ Final adiabatic temperature: $$T_{ad} = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} = 30 + 215.4 = 245.4\,°C$$ **Step 2 — Maximum safe overshoot criterion (1 mark):** Maximum centreline temperature allowed: $$T_{max} = T_w + 30 = 170 + 30 = 200\,°C$$ **Step 3 — Heat balance to find allowable thickness (3 marks):** Assume the heat generated during the time the centreline reaches $T_{max}$ equals the energy stored in the centreline plus the heat conducted away. A first-order estimate uses the lumped-capacitance approach at the centreline, requiring Biot number $Bi < 0.1$: $$Bi = \frac{h_t L}{k_p} = \frac{h_t (L/2)}{0.3} < 0.1$$ $$h_t < \frac{0.1 \times 0.3 \times 2}{1} = 0.06\,\mathrm{m} = 60\,\mathrm{mm}$$ (Using typical phenolic SMC thermal conductivity $k_p \approx 0.3$ W/(m·K) and an effective surface coefficient $h_t \approx 1$ W/(m²·K) for the steel mould contact.) **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} = 215.4\,°C; \quad h_{t,max} \approx 60\,\mathrm{mm}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in the Arrhenius calculation — always convert °C to K and kJ to J. > 2. **Mixing up $k_1$ and $k_2$** — the Kamal model has both; many students write only the nth-order form. > 3. **Ignoring the energy source term** $\rho H_r d\alpha/dt$ in the heat equation — a 2-mark loss in part-(a) of any cure question. > 4. **Wrong initial condition** — using $T_0$ as mould temperature instead of charge temperature. > 5. **Confusing adiabatic with actual centreline temperature** — they are not the same; the question asks for the adiabatic limit, which is a theoretical maximum. > 6. **Not specifying "thermally decomposed"** when discussing overshoot — losing 1 mark for incomplete hazard analysis. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision — Compression Moulding & Cure Simulation** - ☐ Compression moulding = **pressure + heat + cure** in a closed matched die; net-shape process. - ☐ Six stages: **weigh → load → close → cure → demould → optional post-cure**. - ☐ **Charge mass** uses bulk factor: $m = \rho V (1+f_{BF})$. - ☐ Cure models: **Kamal autocatalytic** $(k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$ — most-tested in KTU. - ☐ Rate constants obey **Arrhenius**: $k_i = A_i \exp(-E_i/RT)$ — always use **Kelvin** in the exponent. - ☐ Energy equation has an **internal heat source** from cure: $\rho H_r d\alpha/dt$. - ☐ **Hele-Shaw flow** governs the cavity fill: $h^3/(12\eta) \nabla P$. - ☐ **Centreline overshoot** occurs because heat is generated faster than it can conduct away. - ☐ **Adiabatic temperature rise** estimate: $\Delta T_{ad} = H_r / c_p$. - ☐ **Demoulding criterion**: $\alpha_{centre} \geq \alpha_{cr} \approx 0.7 - 0.9$. - ☐ **Press force ≠ cavity pressure**: $F = P \cdot A_{proj} + F_{clamp}$. - ☐ **Viscosity** is chemo-rheological: depends on $T$, $\alpha$, and $\dot{\gamma}$. - ☐ Major defects: **exotherm degradation, voids, weld lines, warpage, sink marks**. - ☐ Simulation requires coupled solution of **flow + energy + cure + viscosity**. - ☐ Key software: **Moldflow, Moldex3D, ABAQUS, Comsol** — for fibre orientation, cure, and warpage. - ☐ Material families processed: **phenolic, melamine, urea, epoxy, polyester SMC/BMC, GMT, rubber**. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "C-PRESS":** > **C**lose the mould → **P**ressure builds → **R**eaction starts (cure) → **E**nergy released (exotherm) → **S**olidify (crosslink) → **S**trip the part.

topics.title: Mould flow analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mould flow analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MOULD FLOW ANALYSIS (MFA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mould Flow Analysis (MFA)** is a **computer-aided engineering (CAE)** simulation methodology that numerically solves the coupled **conservation equations of mass, momentum, and energy** for a non-Newtonian polymer melt as it flows through, fills, packs, and cools inside an injection mould cavity. It is the digital twin of the injection moulding cycle (filling → packing → cooling → warpage) and predicts moulded part quality, processing parameters, and potential defects *before* any physical tooling is manufactured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Mould flow analysis* refers to the numerical simulation of polymer melt flow inside mould cavities using **Finite Element Method (FEM)** / **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** based solvers, employing appropriate **rheological constitutive equations** and **thermal boundary conditions** to predict filling patterns, pressure distribution, temperature profiles, weld/meld lines, air traps, fibre orientation, shrinkage, and warpage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS for Plastic Melt"

Think of mould flow analysis as a **GPS navigation system for molten plastic**:

| Real-World GPS | Mould Flow Analysis |
|---|---|
| Maps a route across a city | Maps melt front advancement across a cavity |
| Predicts traffic (slow zones) | Predicts high-pressure/short-shot zones |
| Detects blocked roads | Detects **weld lines**, **air traps**, **flow hesitation** |
| Reroutes you around obstacles | Optimises **gate location**, **runner balance** |
| Uses road data + traffic models | Uses **mould geometry + rheology + thermal data** |

Just as GPS uses satellite data and a routing algorithm to find the best path, MFA uses **CAD geometry** of the part + a **rheology database** + a **solver algorithm** to predict how plastic will fill the mould.

### 1.3 Why Mould Flow Analysis is Essential

- **Cost Reduction**: Eliminates expensive tooling rework — *a single mould can cost ₹10–50 lakhs*.
- **Time-to-Market**: Reduces physical prototyping cycles by **60–80%**.
- **Defect Prevention**: Predicts defects that are invisible until production.
- **Optimisation**: Fine-tunes gate location, runner system, cooling channels, and process window.
- **Sustainability**: Reduces material wastage and energy consumption.

### 1.4 Key Industrial Software Platforms

> [!NOTE]
> The global industry standard is **Autodesk Moldflow Insight (AMI)**. Other commercial solvers include **Moldex3D**, **SIGMA Engineering's SIGMASOFT**, **Cadmould**, and **Simpoe**. Open-source codes (e.g., OpenFOAM with custom rheology libraries) are used in research.

### 1.5 The Three Foundational Length Scales in MFA

- **Macro-scale**: Entire cavity (metres) — governed by **Hele-Shaw** or **3D Navier-Stokes** equations.
- **Meso-scale**: Fountain flow region at the melt front (~100 µm).
- **Micro-scale**: Fibre-matrix interaction, skin-core morphology (~µm).

For KTU 2024, the focus is on the **macro-scale** filling simulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Governing Conservation Laws

MFA is mathematically anchored in the conservation of **mass**, **momentum**, and **energy** applied to a viscous, incompressible (or weakly compressible) polymer melt.

### 2.1.1 Continuity (Mass Conservation) Equation

For an **incompressible melt** (assumed in most filling analyses):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

For a **compressible melt** (used in packing phase):

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

where $\rho$ is the **density** and $\vec{v}$ is the **velocity vector**.

### 2.1.2 Momentum (Cauchy) Equation

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v} \right) = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g}$$

where:
- $p$ = pressure
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ = **deviatoric stress tensor**
- $\vec{g}$ = gravitational acceleration
- The term $\rho \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v}$ is the **inertial term** — *in injection moulding, Re is typically 10⁻³ to 10⁻¹, so this term is **negligible** (creeping/Stokes flow).*

### 2.1.3 Energy Equation

$$\rho C_p \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla T \right) = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \boldsymbol{\tau} : \dot{\boldsymbol{\gamma}}$$

where:
- $C_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure
- $T$ = temperature
- $k$ = thermal conductivity
- $\boldsymbol{\tau} : \dot{\boldsymbol{\gamma}}$ = **viscous dissipation** (shear heating)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** In injection moulding filling, the **viscous dissipation term** is **comparable in magnitude** to conduction — it cannot be neglected (this is unlike metal flow).

## 2.2 Constitutive (Rheological) Equations — The Material Model

Polymer melts are **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning, and viscoelastic**. The **viscosity** $\eta$ is a strong function of **shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}$, **temperature** $T$, and **pressure** $p$.

### 2.2.1 Cross-WLF Model (Industry Standard in Moldflow)

The most widely used model for amorphous polymers combines the **Cross** model (shear-thinning) with the **Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF)** temperature dependence.

**Viscosity (Cross):**

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}, T, p) = \frac{\eta_0(T, p)}{1 + \left( \frac{\eta_0(T, p) \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\tau^*} \right)^{(1-n)}}$$

**Zero-shear viscosity (WLF):**

$$\eta_0(T, p) = D_1 \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{A_1 (T - T^*)}{A_2 + (T - T^*)} \right)$$

where:

$$A_2 = \tilde{A}_2 + D_3 \cdot p$$

$$T^* = D_2 + D_3 \cdot p$$

**Cross-WLF Parameters (typical for an amorphous polymer like PC):**
- $n$ = **power-law index** (typically 0.1–0.5)
- $\tau^*$ = critical shear stress at the onset of shear-thinning
- $D_1$ = viscosity constant at reference temperature (Pa·s)
- $D_2$ = reference temperature (K)
- $D_3$ = pressure coefficient (K/Pa)
- $A_1, \tilde{A}_2$ = WLF constants

### 2.2.2 Modified Cross Model (for semi-crystalline polymers)

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}, T, p) = \frac{\eta_0}{1 + \left( \frac{\eta_0 \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\tau^*} \right)^{(1-n)}}$$

where zero-shear viscosity is given by an **Arrhenius** form:

$$\eta_0(T, p) = B \cdot \exp\left(\frac{T_b}{T} + \beta \cdot p\right)$$

### 2.2.3 Carreau Model (Alternative)

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) \left[ 1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2 \right]^{(n-1)/2}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ | Incompressible continuity | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $0 = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Stokes flow momentum (filling) | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $\eta = \eta_0 / [1 + (\eta_0 \dot{\gamma}/\tau^*)^{(1-n)}]$ | Cross viscosity model | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $\eta_0 = D_1 \exp[-A_1(T-T^*)/(A_2+T-T^*)]$ | WLF zero-shear viscosity | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $T^* = D_2 + D_3 p$, $A_2 = \tilde{A}_2 + D_3 p$ | Pressure dependence | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = \int_S \vec{v} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Volumetric flow rate | ★★★ |
| 7 | $\rho C_p (DT/Dt) = \nabla \cdot (k\nabla T) + \tau : \dot{\gamma}$ | Energy balance | ★★★ |
| 8 | $h = k_{poly}/k_{steel}$ or $Nu = hL/k$ | Heat transfer coefficient | ★★ |
| 9 | $t_{fill} = V_{cavity}/Q_{inj}$ | Fill time (1D estimate) | ★★★ |
| 10 | $P_{peak} = K \cdot \eta_{avg} \cdot Q \cdot L/(A \cdot t)$ | Pressure drop scaling | ★★★ |

## 2.4 The Three Dimensionality Choices in MFA

| Type | Geometry Treatment | Equations | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **3D** | Full 3D tetrahedral/cubic mesh | Full Navier-Stokes + energy | Thick parts, complex 3D features |
| **2.5D (Mid-plane / Dual-domain)** | Mid-surface between mould halves | Hele-Shaw approximation | Thin-walled parts (most common — ~90% of parts) |
| **1D (Beam / Shell)** | Centreline flow | Simplified 1D | Runners, long channels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2.5D Mid-plane** is the **industry workhorse** because thin-walled parts dominate (packaging, housings, automotive interior). It reduces a 3D problem to a 2D mesh by averaging across the cavity thickness.

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions in Mould Flow Analysis

- **Inlet (Gate)**: Specified **injection flow rate** $Q(t)$ or **injection pressure** $p(t)$ (typically a ramp + hold profile).
- **Mould Wall**: **No-slip** ($\vec{v} = 0$) and **thermal contact** with heat transfer coefficient $h$.
- **Melt Front**: **Zero pressure** boundary (free surface) and **fountain flow** energy balance.
- **Symmetry Planes**: Zero normal velocity and zero tangential stress.
- **Vent**: Zero pressure, allows air escape.

## 2.6 The Four Sequential Analysis Phases

1. **Fill Phase**: Melt advances from gate to cavity end. Tracks **fill time**, **flow front shape**, **weld lines**, **air traps**, **shear rate/temperature/viscosity** at the front.
2. **Pack/Hold Phase**: Pressure-driven compensation for **volumetric shrinkage** during solidification. Determines **final part density** and **in-mould pressure**.
3. **Cool Phase**: Heat extraction through cooling channels and mould walls. Determines **cycle time** and **residual stress**.
4. **Warpage Phase**: Differential cooling/shrinkage → stress release → final deformation. Uses **viscoelastic/elastic** material models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hele-Shaw Approximation (Mid-plane Equation)

The Hele-Shaw approximation is the **mathematical heart of 2.5D mould flow analysis**. It reduces the full 3D Navier-Stokes equations to a 2D pressure equation under the assumption that the cavity thickness $b$ is much smaller than the in-plane dimensions $L$.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point

Consider a thin cavity with **gap thickness** $b(x,y)$ varying in the $(x,y)$ mid-plane. The melt flows predominantly in the $(x,y)$ plane, with the $z$-direction normal to the cavity wall.

The full momentum equation in the $x$-direction (neglecting inertia):

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z}$$

### 3.1.2 Thin-Cavity Approximation

**Assumption 1**: $\partial p / \partial z \approx 0$ — pressure is uniform across the gap (lubrication theory).

**Assumption 2**: $\partial / \partial z \gg \partial / \partial x, \partial / \partial y$ — gradients normal to the wall dominate.

**Assumption 3**: The normal stress components $\tau_{xx}$ and $\tau_{yy}$ are negligible compared to $\tau_{zx}$ and $\tau_{zy}$.

The momentum equation simplifies to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( \eta \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right)$$

### 3.1.3 Boundary Conditions

- **No-slip at mould wall**: $v_x(\pm b/2) = 0$
- **Symmetry at mid-plane**: $\partial v_x / \partial z \big|_{z=0} = 0$

### 3.1.4 Integration Across the Gap

Integrating twice with respect to $z$:

$$v_x(x, y, z) = \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \int_{-b/2}^{z} \frac{z'}{\eta} \, dz'$$

For a **Newtonian fluid** (constant $\eta$), this gives the classical parabolic profile:

$$v_x = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \left[ z^2 - \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 \right]$$

### 3.1.5 Volumetric Flow Rate Per Unit Width

The volumetric flow rate per unit width $q_x$ is:

$$q_x = \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} v_x \, dz$$

$$q_x = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \int_0^{b/2} \left( z^2 - \frac{b^2}{4} \right) dz$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$q_x = -\frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{b^3}{6}$$

$$q_x = -\frac{b^3}{12\eta} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

Similarly: $q_y = -\frac{b^3}{12\eta} \frac{\partial p}{\partial y}$

### 3.1.6 Final Hele-Shaw Pressure Equation

Substituting into the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{q} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( \frac{b^3}{12\eta} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left( \frac{b^3}{12\eta} \frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \right) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **quasi-harmonic equation** in pressure is the **governing PDE** solved in mid-plane mould flow analysis. It is **non-linear** because $\eta = \eta(\dot{\gamma}, T, p)$ — hence requires **iterative FEM solution**.

The **generalised Hele-Shaw** form (non-Newtonian) becomes:

$$\nabla \cdot \left( S \nabla p \right) = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad S = \int_0^{b/2} \frac{z^2}{\eta} \, dz$$

$S$ is the **flow conductance** — a function of position, temperature, and pressure.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Average Shear Rate in a Thin Cavity

For a non-Newtonian fluid flowing in a gap, the **apparent (wall) shear rate** is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{6 Q}{b^3} = \frac{6 q}{b^2}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate and $q = Q/b$ is the flow rate per unit width.

For non-Newtonian flow with the Cross model, the wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w = \frac{b}{2} \left\vert \frac{\partial p}{\partial s} \right\vert$$

and the wall shear rate is obtained implicitly from:

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}_w) \cdot \dot{\gamma}_w = \tau_w$$

## 3.3 Energy Equation Simplification for Mid-Plane Analysis

For thin cavities, the temperature field is averaged across the thickness:

$$\rho C_p b \left( \frac{\partial \bar{T}}{\partial t} + \bar{v} \cdot \nabla \bar{T} \right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( b \bar{k} \frac{\partial \bar{T}}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left( b \bar{k} \frac{\partial \bar{T}}{\partial y} \right) + \eta \dot{\gamma}^2 - h(T-T_w)$$

The last term is the **heat loss to the mould wall** — it dominates in injection moulding because the high surface-area-to-volume ratio enables rapid heat extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often drop the $\eta \dot{\gamma}^2$ viscous dissipation term. In thin-cavity high-shear-rate filling, this can contribute **20–40%** of total heat generation.

## 3.4 Analytical Calculation: Fill Time for a Disc Cavity

A polymer melt is injected at the centre of a thin disc cavity of radius $R$ and gap thickness $b$. Determine the fill time as a function of injection flow rate $Q_0$.

**Step 1 — Geometry:** Use cylindrical coordinates. At fill-time $t$, the melt front is at radius $r(t)$.

**Step 2 — Volume balance:**

$$Q_0 \cdot t = \pi r(t)^2 \cdot b$$

$$r(t) = \sqrt{\frac{Q_0 t}{\pi b}}$$

**Step 3 — Front velocity:**

$$v_r = \frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{Q_0}{\pi b \, t}}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure gradient (radial Hele-Shaw):**

$$q_r = \frac{Q_0}{2\pi r} = \frac{b^3}{12 \eta} \left( -\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = -\frac{6 \eta Q_0}{\pi b^3 r}$$

**Step 5 — Total pressure drop (integrate from gate to front):**

$$\Delta p = \int_0^r \frac{6 \eta Q_0}{\pi b^3 r'} dr' = \frac{6 \eta Q_0}{\pi b^3} \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right)$$

where $r_0$ is the gate radius.

**Step 6 — Fill time** (when $r = R$):

$$t_{fill} = \frac{\pi b R^2}{Q_0}$$

$$P_{peak} = \frac{6 \eta Q_0}{\pi b^3} \ln\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The logarithmic pressure dependence on the gate-to-cavity ratio means that **smaller gates** dramatically **increase peak pressure** — a critical design trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Mould Flow Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Geometry - STL/STEP File] --> B[Mesh Generation - Mid-plane, 3D, or Dual-domain]
    B --> C{Material Selection}
    C --> D[Polymer Database - Cross-WLF, Modified Cross, Carreau Parameters]
    C --> E[Reinforcement Data - Fibre Length, Aspect Ratio, Orientation]
    D --> F[Process Settings - Injection Profile, Mould Temperature, Coolant Setup]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solver Engine - FEM/FVM Discretisation]
    G --> H[Fill Phase Analysis - Flow Front, Pressure, Temperature, Viscosity]
    H --> I[Pack and Hold Phase - Pressure Driven Compensation, Density]
    I --> J[Cool Phase Analysis - Cooling Time, Temperature Distribution]
    J --> K[Warpage Phase - Residual Stress, Shrinkage, Deformation]
    H --> L[Defect Prediction - Weld Lines, Air Traps, Flow Hesitation, Short Shots]
    H --> M[Fibre Orientation Analysis - Skin-Core Structure, Anisotropy]
    K --> N[Post-Processing and Visualisation]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    J --> N
    N --> O[Design Recommendations - Gate Location, Runner Balance, Cooling Optimisation]
```

## 4.2 Material Model Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Type] --> B{Is it Semi-Crystalline?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Modified Cross Model with Arrhenius Temperature Dependence]
    B -- No --> D[Amorphous Polymer]
    D --> E[Temperature Range Check]
    E -- Above Tg + 100C --> F[Cross-WLF Model with WLF Temperature Dependence]
    E -- Below Tg + 100C --> G[Cross-Arrhenius Hybrid Model]
    C --> H[Fibre Reinforced]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Add Folgar-Tucker or RSC Fibre Orientation Model]
    H -- No --> J[Isotropic Matrix]
    I --> K[Final Material Card]
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Hele-Shaw Simulation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cavity Geometry b x y] --> B[Compute Conductance S x y from Gap and Viscosity]
    B --> C[Solve Pressure Equation - div S grad p equals 0]
    C --> D[Compute Velocity Field q from Pressure Gradient]
    D --> E[Compute Shear Rate from Velocity]
    E --> F[Update Viscosity from Cross WLF Model]
    F --> G[Update Temperature from Energy Equation]
    G --> B
    G --> H[Advance Melt Front via Volume Tracking]
    H --> I{Is Cavity Filled?}
    I -- No --> B
    I -- Yes --> J[Transition to Pack Phase]
```

## 4.4 Mapping of Defects to Mould Design Fixes

| Predicted Defect | Physical Cause | Mould Design Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Weld line | Two melt fronts meeting cold | Adjust gate location; raise mould T; add overflow tab |
| Air trap | Entrapped air pocket | Add vent at pocket location; relocate gate |
| Short shot | Insufficient flow | Increase injection P; raise melt T; thicken runner |
| Sink mark | Localised thick section | Core out thick region; extend cooling time |
| Weldline weakness | Cold melt re-joining | Raise mould T; use hot runner; sequence gates |
| Warpage | Differential shrinkage | Symmetric cooling; balanced wall thickness |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Mould Flow Analysis. List any four output parameters predicted by MFA software.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Mould Flow Analysis (MFA)** is a **computer-aided simulation technique** used to predict the behaviour of polymer melt inside an injection mould cavity by numerically solving the coupled **conservation equations of mass, momentum, and energy** using FEM/FVM methods, coupled with a suitable **constitutive (rheological) equation** for the polymer melt.

**Four Output Parameters:**
1. **Fill time** — Time for melt to reach the last point of the cavity.
2. **Pressure distribution** — Pressure field across the cavity at any instant.
3. **Melt front temperature** — Temperature of the advancing flow front.
4. **Weld/meld line location** — Regions where two flow fronts meet.
5. **Shear rate / shear stress distribution**
6. **Fibre orientation tensor**
7. **Shrinkage and warpage prediction**
8. **Cycle time estimation**

*(Any four — ½ mark each, definition — 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Cross-WLF viscosity model? Why is it preferred for injection moulding simulation?
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Cross-WLF model** is a constitutive equation that describes the **shear-thinning viscosity** $\eta$ of a polymer melt as a function of **shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}$, **temperature** $T$, and **pressure** $p$:

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}, T, p) = \frac{\eta_0(T, p)}{1 + \left( \frac{\eta_0(T, p) \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\tau^*} \right)^{(1-n)}}$$

where the zero-shear viscosity follows the **Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF)** form with pressure dependence.

**Reasons for Preference:**
1. **Captures shear-thinning** behaviour typical of polymer melts across 3–6 decades of shear rate.
2. **WLF** accurately represents the temperature dependence of amorphous polymers near $T_g$.
3. **Pressure dependence** is critical in injection moulding where pressures reach 50–100 MPa.
4. **Validated** against extensive experimental data and is the **default** in commercial codes like Moldflow.
5. **Smooth transition** from Newtonian plateau ($\eta_0$) to power-law region.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive the **Hele-Shaw pressure equation** for a thin non-Newtonian cavity in mould flow analysis. State clearly the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polymer melt with **Cross-WLF** parameters $D_1 = 1.5 \times 10^{12}$ Pa·s, $D_2 = 373$ K, $A_1 = 27.15$, $\tilde{A}_2 = 51.6$ K, $n = 0.35$, $\tau^* = 1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa flows through a thin cavity of gap $b = 2$ mm at temperature $T = 473$ K. The **zero-shear viscosity** is $\eta_0 = 480$ Pa·s at this condition. Calculate the **effective viscosity** at a shear rate of $\dot{\gamma} = 5000$ s⁻¹. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

The **Hele-Shaw equation** is derived by reducing the 3D momentum equation under thin-cavity assumptions.

**Step 1 — Starting equations (Stokes flow, incompressible):**

$$0 = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assumptions:**
1. Cavity thickness $b \ll$ in-plane dimensions.
2. $\partial p / \partial z \approx 0$ (pressure uniform across gap).
3. Gradients $\partial / \partial z \gg \partial / \partial x, \partial / \partial y$.
4. $\tau_{xx}, \tau_{yy} \ll \tau_{zx}, \tau_{zy}$.
5. No-slip at walls, symmetry at mid-plane.

**Step 3 — Simplified x-momentum:**

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( \eta \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate twice with boundary conditions $v_x(\pm b/2) = 0$:**

$$v_x = \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \int_{-b/2}^{z} \frac{z'}{\eta(z')} dz'$$

**Step 5 — Flow rate per unit width:**

$$q_x = \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} v_x \, dz = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \int_0^{b/2} \frac{z^2}{\eta} dz$$

**Step 6 — Define the flow conductance $S$:**

$$S = \int_0^{b/2} \frac{z^2}{\eta(z)} dz \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_x = -S \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

**Step 7 — Combine with continuity $\nabla \cdot \vec{q} = 0$:**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( S \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left( S \frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \right) = 0$$

**This is the Hele-Shaw pressure equation** solved by FEM in mid-plane MFA.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:**
- $\eta_0 = 480$ Pa·s
- $\tau^* = 1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $n = 0.35$
- $\dot{\gamma} = 5000$ s⁻¹

**Cross Model Equation:**

$$\eta = \frac{\eta_0}{1 + \left( \frac{\eta_0 \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\tau^*} \right)^{(1-n)}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the dimensionless group:**

$$\frac{\eta_0 \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\tau^*} = \frac{480 \times 5000}{1.0 \times 10^5} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^5} = 24$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent $(1 - n)$:**

$$1 - n = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$

**Step 3 — Compute the shear-thinning ratio:**

$$(24)^{0.65} = \exp(0.65 \cdot \ln 24) = \exp(0.65 \times 3.178) = \exp(2.066) \approx 7.89$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective viscosity:**

$$\eta = \frac{480}{1 + 7.89} = \frac{480}{8.89} \approx 53.99 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 54.0 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The effective viscosity drops by a factor of ~9 (from 480 to 54 Pa·s) at high shear rate, confirming the **shear-thinning** behaviour.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **three dimensionality options** in mould flow analysis (1D, 2.5D, 3D). For thin-walled plastic parts, justify which is preferred and why. (7 marks)

**(b)** A circular disc cavity of radius $R = 100$ mm and gap $b = 3$ mm is filled from a central gate with constant injection flow rate $Q_0 = 20$ cm³/s. Calculate (i) the **fill time**, and (ii) the **peak pressure** at the end of fill, given the melt has effective viscosity $\eta = 250$ Pa·s and the gate radius is $r_0 = 2$ mm. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Three Dimensionality Options:**

**1. 1D Analysis:** The cavity is reduced to a **centreline flow path** (e.g., a long runner or simple beam-like flow). Solves a 1D momentum balance. Used for **runner balancing** and simple channel flows. Cannot capture transverse flow features.

**2. 2.5D (Mid-plane / Dual-domain) Analysis:** The cavity is represented by a **mid-surface** mesh between the two mould halves. Solves the **Hele-Shaw equation** for pressure and the **energy equation** for temperature. The gap thickness $b(x,y)$ is variable.

- **Mid-plane variant:** Single layer of elements representing the centre of the gap. Skin and core are assumed symmetric.
- **Dual-domain variant:** Two coupled meshes (skin + core) capturing the asymmetric temperature profile across the thickness.

**3. 3D Analysis:** Uses a **full tetrahedral/hybrid mesh** of the entire cavity. Solves the complete **Navier-Stokes** + energy equation. No thin-cavity assumption is made.

**Preferred Choice for Thin-Walled Parts — 2.5D Mid-plane:**

**Justifications:**
1. **Thin-wall geometry dominance:** Over 90% of injection-moulded parts (housings, lids, containers) have $L/b > 50$ — exactly the regime where Hele-Shaw is valid.
2. **Computational efficiency:** Reduces 3D mesh (~10⁶ elements) to 2D mesh (~10⁴ elements) — **100× faster** solution.
3. **Adequate accuracy:** Wall shear, pressure, fill time, and weld line predictions match 3D within 5–10% for $L/b > 20$.
4. **Industry validated:** Decades of benchmarking against experiments and 3D simulations confirm mid-plane reliability.
5. **Practical for design iteration:** Allows multiple design alternatives to be tested quickly.

**3D is reserved for:** Thick parts ($L/b < 10$), components with significant 3D flow features, weld-line studies in thick sections, and validation of 2.5D assumptions.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- $R = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $b = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m
- $Q_0 = 20$ cm³/s $= 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s
- $\eta = 250$ Pa·s
- $r_0 = 2$ mm $= 0.002$ m

**(i) Fill Time:**

**Step 1 — Cavity volume:**

$$V_{cavity} = \pi R^2 \cdot b = \pi \times (0.1)^2 \times 0.003 = 9.42 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 94.25 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Fill time:**

$$t_{fill} = \frac{V_{cavity}}{Q_0} = \frac{94.25}{20} = 4.71 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{fill} \approx 4.71 \text{ s}}$$

**(ii) Peak Pressure at End of Fill:**

**Step 1 — Peak pressure formula (from Section 3.4 derivation):**

$$P_{peak} = \frac{6 \eta Q_0}{\pi b^3} \ln\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
