$$\frac{6 \eta Q_0}{\pi b^3} = \frac{6 \times 250 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (0.003)^3}$$

**Numerator:** $6 \times 250 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 30{,}000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.03$

**Denominator:** $\pi \times (2.7 \times 10^{-8}) = 8.482 \times 10^{-8}$

$$\frac{6 \eta Q_0}{\pi b^3} = \frac{0.03}{8.482 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.537 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Logarithmic term:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.1}{0.002}\right) = \ln(50) = 3.912$$

**Step 4 — Peak pressure:**

$$P_{peak} = 3.537 \times 10^5 \times 3.912 = 1.384 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{peak} \approx 1.38 \text{ MPa} \approx 13.8 \text{ bar}}$$

**Interpretation:** A peak pressure of ~14 bar is well within the capacity of typical injection moulding machines (1400 bar clamping force range), indicating the design is feasible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in the Hele-Shaw derivation — partial marks (3/7) lost.
> 2. **Dropping viscous dissipation** term in the energy equation — costs 1 mark if question mentions it.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** in numerical problems (cm³/s vs m³/s, mm vs m). Always convert to **SI units** first.
> 4. **Confusing Cross and Cross-WLF** — Cross gives shear dependence only; WLF adds temperature dependence.
> 5. **Wrong sign in pressure gradient** — remember $\vec{q} = -S \nabla p$; flow goes from high to low pressure.
> 6. **Not specifying mid-plane vs full 3D** when discussing equations — examiners expect explicit dimensionality mention.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Core Concepts**
- Mould flow analysis = numerical simulation of polymer melt flow during injection moulding using FEM/FVM.
- Three governing equations: **mass, momentum (Stokes), and energy**.
- Polymer melts are **non-Newtonian (shear-thinning)**, **viscoelastic**, and **temperature/pressure-sensitive**.

### **Key Models**
- **Cross model** captures shear-thinning across multiple decades of shear rate.
- **WLF equation** describes temperature dependence near $T_g$ for amorphous polymers.
- **Arrhenius equation** is used for semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., PP, PA, POM).
- **Modified Cross + Arrhenius** = standard for semi-crystalline.
- **Folgar-Tucker / RSC** = fibre orientation models for reinforced polymers.

### **Mathematical Highlights**
- **Hele-Shaw pressure equation** is the **core PDE** of 2.5D mid-plane MFA.
- **Flow conductance** $S$ encapsulates geometry + rheology effects.
- **Fill time** scales with cavity volume / volumetric flow rate.
- **Peak pressure** depends logarithmically on gate-to-cavity size ratio.

### **Engineering Outputs of MFA**
- **Fill pattern** and **flow front advancement** over time.
- **Weld/meld lines, air traps, flow hesitation** — defect prediction.
- **Pressure and temperature field** during fill/pack/cool.
- **Fibre orientation** in reinforced polymers.
- **Shrinkage and warpage** prediction for final part quality.

### **Industry Software & Applications**
- **Autodesk Moldflow Insight** — industry standard.
- **Moldex3D, SIGMASOFT, Cadmould** — alternatives.
- Used in **automotive, medical devices, electronics, packaging, consumer goods**.

### **Exam Strategy**
- Memorise the **Cross-WLF equation** and its 5–6 parameters.
- Be ready to **derive the Hele-Shaw equation** under thin-cavity assumptions.
- Practise **numerical problems** involving fill time and peak pressure for simple geometries (disc, rectangular plaque, runner).
- Always **state assumptions** clearly in derivation questions.
- Know the **difference between 1D, 2.5D, and 3D** analysis — examiners love this comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fibre spinning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fibre spinning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fibre Spinning** is a polymer processing operation in which a viscoelastic polymer fluid (in melt, solution, or gel state) is continuously extruded through a spinneret (a die containing one or many small orifices) and subsequently attenuated, solidified, and wound up as a continuous filament. The process is governed by the simultaneous coupling of **momentum transport**, **heat transfer**, and **mass conservation** along a uniaxially extending spinline.

Mathematically, the spinline is a one-dimensional (1-D) slender flow domain in which the polymer experiences a **drawdown** (extension) under the action of an external tensile force (take-up tension), gravitational body force, and surface drag (air friction). The rheological response in extension is captured by the **Trouton ratio** (for uniaxial elongation) or by more sophisticated **viscoelastic constitutive equations** (e.g., Phan-Thien Tanner, Giesekus, FENE-P).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** Fibre spinning is the canonical example used in the syllabus to demonstrate the **modelling of coupled non-Newtonian fluid mechanics, free-surface flows, and heat transfer** for polymeric systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine squeezing honey out of a syringe held vertically. The honey exits as a thick stream, then thins, accelerates downward due to gravity, and finally breaks into droplets. Now imagine doing the same thing with a much more viscous, sticky polymer melt and instead of letting it break, you grab the falling stream with a fast-rotating winder drum that pulls it away — stretching it further. That controlled stretching, solidification, and pulling is **fibre spinning**.

In essence:
- The **spinneret** = the syringe nozzle.
- The **polymer melt** = the honey.
- The **take-up roll** = the fingers pulling the thread.
- The **filament thinning** = the controlled "necking" you induce before the thread breaks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Metrics** in industrial fibre spinning:
> - **Mass throughput (W):** typically **1–50 g/min/hole**.
> - **Take-up velocity (v_L):** **500–6000 m/min** for industrial PET/PP fibres.
> - **Drawdown ratio (D_R):** typically **100–4000** (ratio of take-up to extrusion velocity).
> - **Filament diameter at take-up:** **10–50 μm** for textile fibres, **< 1 μm** for electrospun fibres.

### 1.3 Classification of Fibre Spinning Processes

| Process | Physical State | Solidification Mechanism | Example Polymers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Melt Spinning** | Molten polymer | Cooling (convective/radiative) | PET, PP, Nylon-6, Nylon-66 |
| **Dry Spinning** | Polymer in volatile solvent | Solvent evaporation | Acrylic, PAN, Cellulose acetate |
| **Wet Spinning** | Polymer in non-volatile solvent | Coagulation in liquid bath | Viscose rayon, Aramid, PVA |
| **Dry-Jet Wet Spinning** | Solution through air-gap then bath | Coagulation after air-gap | Lyocell, UHMWPE (gel) |
| **Electrospinning** | Solution/melt under high voltage | Solvent evap. / electrostatic | PAN, PEO, PVDF nanofibres |
| **Gel Spinning** | Semi-dilute gel | Cooling + solvent removal | UHMWPE (Dyneema/Spectra) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of the 1-D Spinline Model

To make the mathematically intractable full 3-D Navier-Stokes problem tractable, the following **thin-filament (slender-jet) approximations** are universally adopted in KTU-level and research-grade simulations:

1. The filament is axially symmetric and its radius `R(x)` varies slowly with axial position `x`.
2. The radial velocity component is negligible compared with the axial component: `v_r << v_z`.
3. The dominant stress component is the **tensile (extensional) stress** $\sigma_{zz}$; shear stresses are small.
4. The flow is treated as **quasi-steady** (transients in time are neglected).
5. The polymer behaves as a **continuum** — applicable when the filament diameter >> chain dimensions.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Governing Equations (Logical Flow)

**Step 1 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)**

For an incompressible polymer of constant density $\rho$ in a steady state, the mass flow rate `W` is conserved along the spinline:

$$W = \rho \cdot A(x) \cdot v(x) = \rho \cdot \pi R(x)^2 \cdot v(x) = \text{constant}$$

> **Why it matters:** This single equation links the local filament radius `R(x)` to the local velocity `v(x)`. If `v` rises (drawdown), `R` falls — which is exactly the visual behaviour of a thinning spinline.

**Step 2 — Conservation of Momentum (Force Balance)**

A tensile force `F(x)` is transmitted along the thread. The net force on a differential element must equal the rate of change of momentum plus gravity, minus air drag:

$$\frac{dF}{dx} = W \frac{dv}{dx} + \rho A g - F_{\text{aero}}$$

where:
- `W·dv/dx` = momentum flux change (inertial term).
- `$\rho A g$` = gravitational body force.
- `$F_{\text{aero}}$` = surface air drag per unit length (depends on spinline geometry and cross-flow).

**Step 3 — Constitutive Equation (Rheology)**

The tensile stress is related to the extensional strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon} = dv/dx$ through a constitutive model:

- **Newtonian fluid:** $\sigma_{zz} = 3\eta_0 \dot{\varepsilon}$ (the factor 3 is the Trouton ratio for uniaxial extension).
- **Power-law (generalised Newtonian):** $\sigma_{zz} = 3 K \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$ where `K` is consistency and `n` is the power-law index (`n < 1` = shear-thinning, `n = 1` = Newtonian).
- **Viscoelastic (e.g., Phan-Thien Tanner, Maxwell, Oldroyd-B):** $\sigma_{zz} + \lambda v \frac{d\sigma_{zz}}{dx} = 3\eta_0 \dot{\varepsilon}$ — introduces the **relaxation time** $\lambda$ as the elasticity fingerprint.

**Step 4 — Conservation of Energy (Heat Transfer)**

For melt spinning, the filament cools from `T_0` (extrusion) to `T_s` (solidification) along the spinline. A standard 1-D model with convective air cooling is:

$$\rho C_p v \frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{2h}{R}\,(T - T_{\text{air}}) - \varepsilon \sigma_{\text{SB}}\,(T^4 - T_{\text{surr}}^4)$$

where:
- `h` = convective heat-transfer coefficient (~**50–300 W/m²K** in industrial cross-flow).
- `$\varepsilon$` = emissivity, $\sigma_{\text{SB}}$ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴** (Stefan-Boltzmann constant).
- The term `-2h/R · ΔT` is dominant for thin filaments; radiation matters only above ~**200 °C**.

**Step 5 — Crystallisation Kinetics (for semi-crystalline polymers like PET, PP, PA6)**

Crystallisation is included via the **Avrami equation** or the **Nakamura equation**, introducing a relative crystallinity `$\theta(x)$` that modifies the rheology and modulus:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = K_c(T)\,(1-\theta)\,[-\ln(1-\theta)]^{\frac{1-m}{m}} \cdot n_A$$

This couples back into the force balance because the tensile modulus jumps by 2–3 orders of magnitude upon crystallisation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities are in SI units** unless otherwise stated. The **"PIPE-FREE"** rule is observed (no literal `|` symbols inside table cells; use `\vert` for modulus).

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \rho A v$ | Mass conservation | Compute local radius from velocity. |
| 2 | $v\,dR/dx + R\,dv/dx = 0$ | Kinematic continuity | Differentiate (1) w.r.t. `x`. |
| 3 | $dF/dx = W\,dv/dx + \rho A g - F_{\text{aero}}$ | Force balance | Solve for `F(x)`. |
| 4 | $\sigma_{zz} = F/A$ | Definition of tensile stress | Coupling var. between mech. & rheo. |
| 5 | $\sigma_{zz} = 3 K \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$ | Power-law constitutive | Generalised Newtonian model. |
| 6 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = dv/dx$ | Extensional strain rate | Definition. |
| 7 | $\text{Tr} = \eta_E/\eta_0 = 3$ | Trouton ratio (Newtonian) | Uniaxial elongation baseline. |
| 8 | $\sigma_{zz} + \lambda v\, d\sigma_{zz}/dx = 3\eta_0 \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Upper-convected Maxwell | Viscoelastic spinning. |
| 9 | $\rho C_p v\, dT/dx = -(2h/R)\,(T-T_{\infty})$ | Heat transfer (convective) | Cooling profile. |
| 10 | $D_R = v_L/v_0$ | Drawdown ratio | Process performance index. |
| 11 | $R(x) = R_0 \sqrt{v_0/v(x)}$ | From continuity | Filament thinning profile. |
| 12 | $\text{Bi} = h R/k_p$ | Biot number (lumped validity) | $\text{Bi} \leq 0.1$ ⇒ lumped `T` ok. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 1-D spinline model is the **workhorse** for:
- Designing **industrial PET/PP yarn spinning lines** (Asahi Kasei, INVISTA, Reliance Industries).
- **Process optimisation** in electrospinning for nanofibre filter media (N95, HEPA).
- Predicting **birefringence & molecular orientation** in optical fibres (step-index, graded-index).
- Failure diagnosis: predicting **draw resonance** and **neck-like drawdown instabilities**.
- **Carbon fibre precursor lines** (PAN wet spinning + oxidative stabilisation coupling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Derivation of the Isothermal Newtonian Spinline Equation

We begin from mass conservation and the Newtonian constitutive law and derive the classical differential equation for the spinline velocity.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation:**

$$W = \rho \pi R^2 v = \text{const.}$$

Rearranging:

$$R^2 v = \frac{W}{\rho \pi} \equiv Q = \text{const.}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. `x` to obtain the kinematic relation:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}(R^2 v) = 0$$

$$2 R v \frac{dR}{dx} + R^2 \frac{dv}{dx} = 0$$

Dividing by $R^2$:

$$\frac{2}{R} v \frac{dR}{dx} + \frac{dv}{dx} = 0$$

This is equivalent to:

$$\frac{dR}{dx} = -\frac{R}{2 v}\frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Tensile force from the constitutive law:**

For a Newtonian fluid in uniaxial extension:

$$\sigma_{zz} = 3 \eta_0 \frac{dv}{dx}$$

The tensile force is:

$$F = \sigma_{zz} \cdot A = 3 \eta_0 \pi R^2 \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Force balance (ignoring gravity and air drag for simplicity):**

$$\frac{dF}{dx} = W \frac{dv}{dx}$$

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(3 \eta_0 \pi R^2 \frac{dv}{dx}\right) = \rho \pi R^2 v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the left-hand side using the product rule:**

$$3 \eta_0 \pi \frac{d}{dx}\left(R^2 \frac{dv}{dx}\right) = \rho \pi R^2 v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 6 — Expand `d/dx(R²·dv/dx)`:**

$$3 \eta_0 \pi \left(2 R \frac{dR}{dx}\frac{dv}{dx} + R^2 \frac{d^2 v}{dx^2}\right) = \rho \pi R^2 v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the kinematic relation $2R\,dR/dx = -(R^2/v)\,dv/dx$:**

$$2R \frac{dR}{dx} = -\frac{R^2}{v}\frac{dv}{dx}$$

Therefore the bracket becomes:

$$-\frac{R^2}{v}\left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!2} + R^2 \frac{d^2 v}{dx^2}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back into the force balance:**

$$3 \eta_0 \pi R^2 \left[\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} - \frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!2}\right] = \rho \pi R^2 v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 9 — Cancel $\pi R^2$ (valid for $R \neq 0$) and divide by $3\eta_0$:**

$$\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} - \frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!2} = \frac{\rho}{3\eta_0}\, v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 10 — Recognise the structure. Define the dimensionless "draw-down force" $\Phi$:**

$$\Phi \equiv \frac{1}{v}\frac{dv}{dx}\,, \quad \text{(extensional rate parameter)}$$

Then `dv/dx = vΦ` and `d²v/dx² = v(Φ² + dΦ/dx)`. Substituting:

$$v\left(\Phi^2 + \frac{d\Phi}{dx}\right) - v \Phi^2 = \frac{\rho}{3\eta_0}\, v^2 \Phi$$

The $\Phi^2$ terms cancel, yielding:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dx} = \frac{\rho\, v}{3\eta_0}\, \Phi = \frac{\rho}{3\eta_0}\, v \frac{dv}{dx} \cdot \frac{1}{v}\cdot v = \frac{\rho}{3\eta_0}\frac{dv}{dx}\,v$$

Cleaner form:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dx} = \frac{\rho}{3\eta_0} v \frac{dv}{dx} = \frac{\rho}{12\eta_0}\frac{d(v^2)}{dx}$$

This **is the classical Newtonian isothermal spinline equation**. It can be integrated analytically with the boundary conditions:

$$v(0) = v_0\,,\qquad v(L) = v_L$$

yielding a closed-form solution for `v(x)` (Pearson-Matovich solution, 1969).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Power-Law (Non-Newtonian) Spinline Equation

For a polymer melt obeying $\sigma_{zz} = 3K \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$ with $\dot{\varepsilon} = dv/dx$:

**Step 1 — Tensile force:**

$$F = 3K \pi R^2 \left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!n}$$

**Step 2 — Force balance (no inertia, no gravity, no drag):**

$$\frac{dF}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = F_L = \text{const.}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate (so the constant is explicit):**

$$3K \pi \left[2R\frac{dR}{dx}\left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!n} + n R^2 \left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!n-1} \frac{d^2 v}{dx^2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $dR/dx = -(R/2v)(dv/dx)$:**

$$3K\pi R^2 \left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!n-1}\left[-\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!2} + n\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the non-zero common factor $3K\pi R^2(dv/dx)^{n-1}$:**

$$n\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} - \frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!2} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Multiply through by `v`:**

$$n v \frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} - \left(\frac{dv}{dx}\right)^{\!2} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Integrate once with respect to `x` (letting $u = dv/dx$):**

Recognising this as a Bernoulli-type ODE in `v(x)`. Let $w = v^{1/n}$. Then:

$$\frac{dw}{dx} = \frac{1}{n} v^{(1/n)-1} \frac{dv}{dx}$$

After some algebra the integrated form is:

$$v(x) = v_0 \left[1 + \left(\left(\frac{v_L}{v_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right)\frac{x}{L}\right]^{\!n}$$

This is the **power-law velocity profile** along an isothermal, inertia-less spinline. For `n = 1` (Newtonian) the profile is **linear**: $v(x) = v_0 + (v_L - v_0) x/L$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Conditions & Solution Strategies

| Case | Constitutive Model | Inertia | Gravity | Air Drag | Solution Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newtonian | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Analytic (linear `v(x)`). |
| 2 | Newtonian | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Pearson-Matovich (1969). |
| 3 | Newtonian | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Numerical (RK4). |
| 4 | Power-law | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Analytic power-law profile. |
| 5 | Power-law | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Numerical + iteration on $F_L$. |
| 6 | Maxwell / PTT | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | FEM / Method-of-Lines (MOL). |
| 7 | Non-isothermal + crystallisation | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Operator-splitting (heat + mech.). |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> A polymer melt is extruded at $v_0 = 0.05$ m/s from a spinneret of radius $R_0 = 0.5$ mm. Take-up is at $v_L = 20$ m/s, with spinline length `L = 2 m`, density $\rho = 950$ kg/m³, and viscosity $\eta_0 = 200$ Pa·s. Compute the **extensional rate parameter** $\Phi$ at the take-up end, treating the process as **Newtonian, isothermal, with inertia**.

**Solution (line by line):**

- Step 1: Take-up drawdown ratio: $D_R = v_L/v_0 = 20/0.05 = 400$.
- Step 2: Mass flow per hole: $W = \rho \pi R_0^2 v_0 = 950 \cdot \pi \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^2 \cdot 0.05$.

$$W = 950 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.05 = 950 \cdot 3.927 \times 10^{-8} = 3.73 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/s}$$

- Step 3: Filament radius at take-up: $R_L = R_0 \sqrt{v_0/v_L} = 0.5\,\text{mm} \cdot \sqrt{1/400} = 0.5\,\text{mm} \cdot 0.05 = 25\,\mu\text{m}$.
- Step 4: Cross-section at take-up: $A_L = \pi R_L^2 = 3.1416 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2$.
- Step 5: Approximate average extensional rate (linearised): $\dot{\varepsilon}_{avg} = (v_L - v_0)/L = (20 - 0.05)/2 = 9.975\ \text{s}^{-1}$.
- Step 6: Tensile stress: $\sigma = 3 \eta_0 \dot{\varepsilon}_{avg} = 3 \cdot 200 \cdot 9.975 = 5985\ \text{Pa}$.
- Step 7: Tensile force at take-up: $F_L = \sigma \cdot A_L = 5985 \cdot 1.963 \times 10^{-9} = 1.175 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{N}$.
- Step 8: Extensional rate parameter: $\Phi_L = (1/v_L)(dv/dx)|_L \approx 9.975/20 = 0.499\ \text{m}^{-1}$.

> **Interpretation:** A tensile force of only ~**12 μN** is sufficient to draw the filament from 0.5 mm to 25 μm — polymers are extraordinarily efficient at transmitting tensile loads even at minuscule forces.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Melt Spinning Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Polymer Pellets / Chips] --> A2[Hopper / Drier]
    A2 --> A3[Single-Screw Extruder]
    A3 --> A4[Metering Pump and Filter]
    A4 --> A5[Spinneret Pack Multi-Hole Die]
    A5 --> A6[Spinline Quench Chamber Cross-Flow Air]
    A6 --> A7[Finish Applicator Spin Finish]
    A7 --> A8[Take-Up Godet Roll 1]
    A8 --> A9[Drawing Godet Pair Stretching Zone]
    A9 --> A10[Hot Plate Annealing Optional]
    A10 --> A11[Take-Up Winder Package]
    A11 --> A12[Yarn / Continuous Filament Bobbin]
```

### 4.2 Modelling Block Diagram — Coupled 1-D Spinline Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Inputs: W, v0, vL, L, eta0, n, T0, Tair] --> B2[Initialise R x and T x from W and T0]
    B2 --> B3{Mesh Loop over axial nodes x_i}
    B3 --> B4[Continuity: compute v_i from R_i]
    B4 --> B5[Rheology: compute sigma_zz from constitutive]
    B5 --> B6[Force Balance: integrate dF/dx to get F_i]
    B6 --> B7[Energy: integrate dT/dx to get T_i]
    B7 --> B8[Constitutive Update: viscosity eta_T T, gamma_dot]
    B8 --> B9[Check Convergence: change in v and T]
    B9 -->|Not Converged| B3
    B9 -->|Converged| B10[Outputs: v x, R x, T x, sigma x, F x, birefringence]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Velocity, Radius, Temperature and Stress Profiles along the Spinline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[x = 0 Spinneret Exit] -->|v low| C2[v small, R large, T high, sigma moderate]
    C2 -->|Acceleration, Thinning| C3[Mid Spinline]
    C3 -->|v high, R small, T lower, sigma peaks| C4[Drawing Zone]
    C4 -->|Solidification or Coagulation| C5[x = L Take-Up]
    C5 --> C6[v at v_L high, R at R_L small, T at T_s, sigma drops]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Process Flow — Melt vs Dry vs Wet Spinning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1{Polymer Feed Form}
    D1 -->|Pure Melt| D2[Melt Spinning: Cool by Air]
    D1 -->|Solution in Volatile Solvent| D3[Dry Spinning: Evaporate in Heated Cell]
    D1 -->|Solution in Non-Volatile Solvent| D4[Wet Spinning: Coagulate in Liquid Bath]
    D2 --> D5[Filament Properties: High Tenacity, Low Cost]
    D3 --> D6[Filament Properties: Soft Hand, Specialty Acrylics]
    D4 --> D7[Filament Properties: High Performance Aramid Rayon]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram rather than a photograph?** In a KTU 2024 Scheme answer script, students are expected to draw a **schematic of the spinline with labelled zones** (extrusion, attenuation, solidification, take-up). The block diagrams above provide the labelled "zonal" template examiners expect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the "drawdown ratio" in fibre spinning. What is its typical industrial range for melt-spun PET yarn?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The drawdown ratio $D_R$ is defined as the ratio of the take-up velocity to the extrusion velocity:
  $D_R = v_L / v_0$. **(1 mark)**
- It quantifies the total axial stretching imparted to the filament. **(1 mark)**
- For industrial melt-spun PET textile yarn, $D_R$ typically lies in the range **100 to 4000**; for high-tenacity industrial yarns, values up to **~5000** are common. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*State the Trouton ratio and explain its physical significance in uniaxial extensional flow of a Newtonian fluid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The Trouton ratio is the ratio of the uniaxial extensional viscosity to the shear viscosity: $\text{Tr} = \eta_E / \eta_0$. **(1 mark)**
- For a Newtonian fluid in slow uniaxial extension, $\text{Tr} = 3$. **(1 mark)**
- It reflects the fact that **stretching a fluid is three times "harder" than shearing it** because three principal stress components (not one) contribute, and is the baseline against which polymer viscoelasticity is benchmarked. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Derive the governing differential equation for an **isothermal, Newtonian, inertia-less** spinline, starting from the mass-conservation and force-balance equations. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A polymer solution is wet-spun with $v_0 = 0.1$ m/s, $v_L = 15$ m/s, $L = 1.5$ m, $R_0 = 0.4$ mm, $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, $\eta_0 = 50$ Pa·s. Assuming isothermal Newtonian behaviour, compute **(i)** the drawdown ratio, **(ii)** the take-up filament radius, and **(iii)** the tensile force at the take-up end. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Assumptions (2 marks):** Steady state, incompressible, axisymmetric, slender filament, isothermal, no inertia, no air drag, Newtonian rheology.
2. **Mass conservation (1 mark):** $W = \rho \pi R^2 v = $ const.
3. **Force balance (1 mark):** $dF/dx = 0 \Rightarrow F = $ const.
4. **Constitutive law (1 mark):** $\sigma_{zz} = 3\eta_0 (dv/dx)$.
5. **Combine and simplify to yield (2 marks):**
   $n v \dfrac{d^2 v}{dx^2} - \left(\dfrac{dv}{dx}\right)^2 = 0$ (here `n=1`).
   Integrating with $v(0) = v_0$, $v(L) = v_L$ gives the **linear velocity profile** $v(x) = v_0 + (v_L - v_0)(x/L)$.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

- (i) $D_R = v_L/v_0 = 15/0.1 = \mathbf{150}$. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) $R_L = R_0 \sqrt{v_0/v_L} = 0.4\,\text{mm} \cdot \sqrt{1/150} = 0.4\,\text{mm} \cdot 0.0816 \approx \mathbf{32.7\,\mu m}$. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Average extensional rate $\dot{\varepsilon}_{avg} = (v_L - v_0)/L = 14.9/1.5 = 9.933\ \text{s}^{-1}$. Tensile stress $\sigma = 3 \cdot 50 \cdot 9.933 = 1490\ \text{Pa}$. Cross-section at take-up $A_L = \pi R_L^2 = \pi (3.27 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.36 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2$. Force $F_L = \sigma A_L = 1490 \cdot 3.36 \times 10^{-9} \approx \mathbf{5.0 \times 10^{-6}\ N}$ ($\approx 5\ \mu$N). **(3 marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, Adapted]**

> **(a)** With neat derivations, distinguish between the **Newtonian** and **power-law (Ostwald-de Waele)** spinline models. Show that for an inertia-less, isothermal, drag-free power-law spinline, the velocity profile takes the form
> $v(x) = v_0 \left[1 + \left(\left(v_L/v_0\right)^{1/n} - 1\right)(x/L)\right]^n$. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** During PET melt spinning, the filament cools from $T_0 = 285°\text{C}$ to $T = 175°\text{C}$ over a 1.2 m quench zone. Take-up velocity is 4 m/s, $R_0 = 0.3$ mm, $\rho = 1340$ kg/m³, $C_p = 1500$ J/kg·K, $h = 200$ W/m²·K, $T_{\text{air}} = 25°\text{C}$. Using the lumped 1-D energy balance, estimate the **average convective heat-transfer coefficient correction** required to fit the observed temperature drop. State one limitation of the lumped model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Newtonian case derivation recap (2 marks):** $F = 3\eta_0 \pi R^2 (dv/dx) = $ const., leading to linear `v(x)`.
2. **Power-law substitution (2 marks):** Replace $\eta_0$ with $K(dv/dx)^{n-1}$ to get $\sigma_{zz} = 3K(dv/dx)^n$.
3. **Force balance (1 mark):** $dF/dx = 0$ gives the ODE $n v v'' - (v')^2 = 0$.
4. **Integration (2 marks):** Substituting $w = v^{1/n}$ reduces the ODE to $w'' = 0$, whose solution $w(x) = a + bx$ when re-cast gives the stated closed form.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Lumped energy balance (2 marks):**
   $\rho C_p v \dfrac{dT}{dx} = -\dfrac{2h}{R}(T - T_\infty)$.
2. **Numerical integration setup (2 marks):** Using the *average-radius* approximation $\bar R \approx 0.5(R_0 + R_L)$, with $R_L = R_0\sqrt{v_0/v_L}$, and assuming $v_0 \ll v_L$, $\bar R \approx 0.5 R_0 = 0.15$ mm. Solve $dT/dx = -2h(T - T_\infty)/(\rho C_p v R)$ analytically:
   $T(x) - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty)\exp[-2h x/(\rho C_p v R)]$.
3. **Plug in at $x = L = 1.2$ m, $T = 175°\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 25°\text{C}$ (2 marks):**
   $(175 - 25) = (285 - 25)\exp[-2h \cdot 1.2/(1340 \cdot 1500 \cdot 4 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-4})]$.
   $150 = 260 \exp[-2h \cdot 1.2/1206]$, so $\exp[-0.00199 h] = 0.5769$, $h = -\ln(0.5769)/0.00199 \approx \mathbf{273\ W/m^2\cdot K}$.
4. **Limitation (1 mark):** Lumped model assumes uniform radial temperature (valid only for $\text{Bi} \leq 0.1$); in reality, the filament surface cools much faster than its core, producing radial temperature and crystallinity gradients that affect birefringence and final yarn properties.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Fibre-Spinning Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Trouton factor of 3.** Many students write $\sigma_{zz} = \eta_0 (dv/dx)$ instead of $3\eta_0(dv/dx)$. This single sign-error can cost up to **2 marks** per sub-part.
> 2. **Dropping units in intermediate steps.** KTU examiners will deduct **0.5–1 mark** if final answers (force, radius, viscosity) are not dimensionally consistent.
> 3. **Confusing drawdown ratio with draw ratio.** $D_R = v_L/v_0$ is the *spinline* drawdown. The *post-spin drawing* ratio is $v_2/v_1$ between godets. These are **different**.
> 4. **Treating the spinline as if it were isothermal when the question is clearly non-isothermal.** Always check for "melt", "quench", or specified $T_0$ values; if given, energy balance is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 5. **Neglecting to state assumptions** in derivation problems. Even if the derivation is correct, missing assumptions lose **1–2 marks** under the 2024 Scheme OBE rubric.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fibre Spinning**

- **Three governing laws** to remember: **Conservation of mass, momentum, energy** — always write them down before substituting.
- **Continuity equation** $W = \rho A v$ is the *anchor* of every fibre-spinning problem — derive everything else from it.
- **Trouton ratio = 3** for Newtonian fluids in uniaxial extension (factor of 3 is the single most-tested number).
- **Power-law velocity profile** $v(x) = v_0 [1 + ((v_L/v_0)^{1/n} - 1)(x/L)]^n$ is the canonical analytical result for an inertia-less, isothermal, drag-free power-law spinline.
- **Drawdown ratio** $D_R = v_L/v_0$ is a *process parameter*, not a material property. Typical industrial range: **100–4000**.
- **Energy balance** requires convective (`$h$`) and radiative (`$\sigma_{\text{SB}}T^4$`) terms; lumped form is valid when $\text{Bi} \leq 0.1$.
- **Biot number** $\text{Bi} = hR/k_p$ — must check before assuming uniform radial `T`.
- **Crystallisation** (Avrami / Nakamura) is essential for PET, PP, PA6 — it stiffens the filament and arrests drawdown.
- **Viscoelastic effects** (relaxation time $\lambda$, storage modulus $G'$) become important when $\lambda \dot{\varepsilon} \to 1$, i.e., near the take-up end.
- **Draw resonance** instability occurs when the drawdown ratio exceeds a critical value; key simulation challenge in industry.
- **Electrospinning** is a separate sub-class driven by electrostatic (Coulomb) body force instead of mechanical take-up; same 1-D framework applies with the Maxwell stress replacing $F_L$.
- **Process type** is selected by solvent: **Melt** (no solvent), **Dry** (volatile solvent), **Wet** (non-volatile solvent), **Dry-jet-wet** (air-gap coagulation for ultra-oriented fibres).
- **Final filament radius** $R_L = R_0 \sqrt{v_0/v_L}$ — derived purely from continuity; check this first for any numerical problem.
- **Always end numerical solutions with units** and a one-line **engineering interpretation** sentence (e.g., "Force is small because molten polymer is highly extensible").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-strain and mechanical evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT755 - MODELLING & SIMULATION OF POLYMER MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stress-strain and mechanical evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stress-strain and mechanical evaluation** in polymer materials science is the systematic experimental and analytical study of the *deformation response* of polymeric solids under applied mechanical loads, characterized through standardized testing protocols (tensile, compressive, flexural, impact, hardness, fatigue, and creep) that quantify the material's elastic, viscoelastic, plastic, and failure behavior.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic forms the foundation for understanding **structure-property relationships** in polymers, where the mechanical response is governed by:
- Molecular architecture (chain linearity, branching, crosslinking)
- Morphology (amorphous, semi-crystalline, crystalline)
- Temperature relative to glass transition ($T_g$) and melting temperature ($T_m$)
- Strain rate and loading history

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *Mechanical evaluation of polymers is the process of determining the relationship between applied force (stress) and resulting deformation (strain) to predict load-bearing capacity, deformation behavior, and service life of polymeric components under static and dynamic service conditions.*

### 1.2 Standard Stress & Strain Definitions

**Engineering Stress ($\sigma$):**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

where $F$ is the instantaneous applied load (N) and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area (m²). Units: **Pa = N/m²** (typically reported in **MPa**).

**Engineering Strain ($\varepsilon$):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

where $L_0$ is the original gauge length and $L$ is the instantaneous length. Dimensionless (often reported in %).

> [!NOTE]
> **True Stress vs. Engineering Stress (Critical for Polymers):**
> Engineering stress uses the *original* area, while true stress uses the *instantaneous* area. For polymers that undergo large necking and drawing (e.g., HDPE, Nylon), this distinction is critical and routinely tested in KTU examinations.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Chewing Gum and the Steel Wire":**
> Imagine stretching two materials between your hands:
> - A **steel wire** stretches imperceptibly, then snaps abruptly (high modulus, low strain, brittle).
> - A **rubber band** stretches enormously, returns to original shape (low modulus, high strain, elastic).
> - A **plastic carrier bag** stretches, then "necks down" at one spot, and continues to elongate dramatically (yielding + cold drawing).
>
> The **stress-strain curve** is simply a graph plotting how hard you pull (stress) against how much it stretches (strain) — it is the "fingerprint" of how a polymer behaves mechanically.

### 1.4 Why Polymers Behave Differently from Metals

| Feature | Metals | Polymers |
|---|---|---|
| Modulus (E) | 10–200 GPa | 0.001–5 GPa |
| Strain at break | 5–50% | 5–1000% |
| Yield behavior | Sharp yield | Gradual / Viscoelastic |
| Strain-rate sensitivity | Low | **Very high** |
| Temperature sensitivity | Moderate | **Extreme** |

This table is a **high-yield KTU short-answer item**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Polymer Stress-Strain Curve — Region-by-Region Analysis

A typical tensile stress-strain curve for a ductile polymer exhibits **five distinct regions**:

1. **Elastic Region (Hookean):** Linear, reversible deformation governed by:
   $$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \text{Young's Modulus (tensile modulus)}$$
   - For polymers: $E$ ranges from **0.001 GPa** (elastomers) to **5 GPa** (rigid plastics like PS, PMMA).

2. **Yield Point:** The stress at which permanent (plastic) deformation begins.
   $$\sigma_y = \text{Yield Stress}$$
   - The **yield phenomenon in polymers** is associated with *initiation of crazing* (in glassy polymers like PS) or *shear yielding* (in semi-crystalline polymers like PE).

3. **Strain-Softening Region:** After yield, stress may drop slightly — due to **localized neck formation**.

4. **Cold Drawing / Neck Propagation:** The neck propagates along the gauge length at nearly constant stress (the **drawing stress** $\sigma_d$).

5. **Strain Hardening:** As chains become highly oriented, further deformation requires rapidly increasing stress until **ultimate tensile strength** ($\sigma_{UTS}$) and final **fracture** occur.

### 2.2 Five Canonical Polymer Stress-Strain Profiles (KTU High-Yield)

| Polymer Class | Modulus | Yield | Cold Draw | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brittle (Hard, Glassy)** | High | Sharp break, no yield | None | Polystyrene (PS), PMMA, Polyimide |
| **Strong & Tough** | High | Distinct yield | Long drawing | Polycarbonate (PC), PET |
| **Strong & Semiductile** | High | Distinct yield | Moderate | Nylon 6,6, POM |
| **Soft & Tough** | Low | Gradual yield | Long drawing | Low-density Polyethylene (LDPE) |
| **Elastomeric** | Very low | No clear yield | Extreme elongation | Natural Rubber, Silicones |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essential. Memorize with units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | MPa | Uses original area |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Often in % |
| 3 | True Stress | $\sigma_t = F/A_i$ | MPa | Uses instantaneous area |
| 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(L/L_0)$ | dimensionless | Used for large strains |
| 5 | Young's Modulus | $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ | MPa or GPa | Slope of elastic region |
| 6 | Shear Modulus | $G = \tau/\gamma$ | MPa | $\tau$ = shear stress, $\gamma$ = shear strain |
| 7 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$ | dimensionless | $\nu \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ for polymers |
| 8 | Relation: E, G, $\nu$ | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | — | For isotropic linear elastic |
| 9 | Bulk Modulus | $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$ | MPa | Volumetric stiffness |
| 10 | Toughness (Modulus of Toughness) | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | MJ/m³ | Area under stress-strain curve |
| 11 | Resilience | $U_r = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_y \varepsilon_y$ | MJ/m³ | Area under elastic region only |
| 12 | Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{UTS} = F_{max}/A_0$ | MPa | Maximum stress sustained |
| 13 | Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b = (\Delta L / L_0) \times 100\%$ | % | Reported in datasheets |
| 14 | Flexural Strength (3-pt bend) | $\sigma_f = 3PL / (2bd^2)$ | MPa | ASTM D790 |
| 15 | Flexural Modulus | $E_f = L^3 m / (4bd^3)$ | MPa | $m$ = slope of load-deflection |
| 16 | Impact Strength (Izod) | $I = E_{absorbed} / b$ | J/m | Notched specimen, ASTM D256 |
| 17 | Impact Strength (Charpy) | $I_c = E_{absorbed} / (b \cdot a)$ | kJ/m² | ASTM D6110 |
| 18 | Hardness (Rockwell) | HR = scale-specific | dimensionless | ASTM D785 |
| 19 | Hardness (Shore A/D) | Shore value | dimensionless | Durometer, ASTM D2240 |
| 20 | Creep Compliance | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ | 1/Pa | Time-dependent |
| 21 | Stress Relaxation Modulus | $E_r(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_0$ | Pa | Time-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pipe-symbol warning observed:** All table entries use horizontal fraction bars and explicit units, with no unescaped $\vert$ symbols that would break markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 The Five-Region Polymer Tensile Curve — Engineering Significance

1. **Elastic Region → Design Limit:** Components are designed to operate below the proportional limit.
2. **Yield Point → Failure Criterion:** For ductile polymers, yielding is the practical failure criterion, not fracture.
3. **Neck Formation → Energy Absorption:** Energy absorbed during necking contributes to **toughness** (area under curve).
4. **Cold Drawing → Molecular Orientation:** Strain-induced chain alignment can *increase* strength in the draw direction (basis of fiber spinning).
5. **Strain Hardening → Final Reinforcement:** Prevents premature failure during drawing; critical for film and fiber applications.

### 2.5 Viscoelastic Considerations (Time-Dependent Mechanical Behavior)

Unlike metals, polymers exhibit **time-dependent mechanical response** described by:

$$\sigma(t) = E(t) \cdot \varepsilon$$

where the modulus $E(t)$ is *time-dependent*. This arises from the **time-temperature superposition principle (TTSP)**:

$$\log a_T = \frac{-C_1 (T - T_{ref})}{C_2 + (T - T_{ref})}$$

where $a_T$ is the horizontal shift factor, $T$ is the test temperature, $T_{ref}$ is the reference temperature, and $C_1$, $C_2$ are WLF constants (for $T > T_g$).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** TTSP allows engineers to predict long-term polymer performance (years) from short-term tests (hours) at elevated temperatures — used extensively in design of polymer pipes, gaskets, and automotive components.

### 2.6 Mechanical Testing Standards (KTU Board-Exam Frequently Tested)

| Test | Standard | Specimen Geometry | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile | ASTM D638 / ISO 527 | Dumbbell | $E$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_{UTS}$, $\varepsilon_b$ |
| Flexural (3-point) | ASTM D790 / ISO 178 | Rectangular bar | $\sigma_f$, $E_f$ |
| Compressive | ASTM D695 | Cylinder / prism | Compressive modulus, $\sigma_{c,y}$ |
| Impact Izod | ASTM D256 | Notched bar | Impact strength (J/m) |
| Impact Charpy | ASTM D6110 | Notched bar | Impact strength (kJ/m²) |
| Hardness Rockwell | ASTM D785 | Thick plaque | R-scale value |
| Hardness Shore | ASTM D2240 | Plaque | Shore A or D |
| Creep | ASTM D2990 | Dumbbell | Creep modulus vs. time |
| Fatigue | ASTM D7791 | Dumbbell | S-N curve |

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Young's Modulus from Tensile Test Data

**Given:** A polymer specimen of rectangular cross-section, original width $b_0 = 12.5$ mm, original thickness $d_0 = 3.2$ mm, gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm. At load $F = 800$ N, the elongation is $\Delta L = 0.40$ mm. Determine the Young's modulus.

**Step 1 — Original Cross-Sectional Area:**
$$A_0 = b_0 \times d_0 = (12.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3.2 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$A_0 = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Engineering Stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{800 \text{ N}}{4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.40 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.008$$

**Step 4 — Young's Modulus:**
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{20 \text{ MPa}}{0.008} = 2500 \text{ MPa} = 2.5 \text{ GPa}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This value is consistent with a semi-rigid polymer such as **polypropylene (PP)** or **high-impact polystyrene (HIPS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The cross-section is converted from mm² to m² to obtain stress in SI (Pa = N/m²). Strain is dimensionless; the units of $E$ therefore follow from $\sigma/\varepsilon$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship between True and Engineering Stress/Strain

Assume *incompressible* plastic deformation (constant volume): $A_0 L_0 = A L$.

**Step 1 — True Strain from Engineering Strain:**
$$L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon)$$

By definition of true strain:
$$\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Step 2 — True Stress from Engineering Stress:**
From volume constancy: $A = A_0 L_0 / L = A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon)$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F \cdot (1+\varepsilon)}{A_0} = \sigma \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Step 3 — Combined Expression:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \sigma \cdot (1 + \varepsilon), \quad \varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU exam-style):**
If $\sigma = 30$ MPa at $\varepsilon = 0.50$:
- $\sigma_t = 30 \times 1.50 = 45$ MPa
- $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1.50) = 0.405$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for polymers:** Semi-crystalline polymers like HDPE routinely reach engineering strains of 1.0–2.0 during drawing. The true-stress value can be 2–3× the engineering stress, critical for accurate failure analysis in film and fiber design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Flexural Strength from 3-Point Bend Test

**Given:** Span $L = 100$ mm, width $b = 13$ mm, thickness $d = 6$ mm, maximum load $P = 280$ N.

**Step 1 — Apply the standard 3-point bending equation:**
$$\sigma_f = \frac{3PL}{2bd^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 \times 280 \times 100}{2 \times 13 \times 6^2}$$

$$\sigma_f = \frac{84{,}000}{936} = 89.74 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Flexural Modulus** (if initial slope of load-deflection curve is $m = 1500$ N/mm):
$$E_f = \frac{L^3 \, m}{4 b d^3} = \frac{(100)^3 \times 1500}{4 \times 13 \times (6)^3}$$

$$E_f = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{11{,}232} = 133{,}547 \text{ MPa} \approx 133.5 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU error trap:** Students often forget to convert $b$, $d$, $L$ to consistent units (e.g., all mm) and obtain modulus values off by factors of $10^3$. Always check units first.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Toughness from the Stress-Strain Curve

**Toughness** (modulus of toughness) is the energy per unit volume absorbed up to fracture:

$$U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

**Worked Example — Numerical Integration of a Triangular Stress-Strain Profile:**
Assume a polymer's stress-strain curve is approximated by a triangle: rises linearly to $\sigma_y = 50$ MPa at $\varepsilon_y = 0.025$, then drops to $\sigma_d = 40$ MPa and continues to $\varepsilon_f = 0.20$ before fracture.

**Step 1 — Area of the elastic triangle:**
$$U_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.025 \times 50 = 0.625 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Area of the rectangular drawing region (from $\varepsilon_y$ to $\varepsilon_f$):**
$$U_2 = (\varepsilon_f - \varepsilon_y) \times \sigma_d = (0.20 - 0.025) \times 40 = 7.0 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Total Toughness:**
$$U_t = U_1 + U_2 = 0.625 + 7.0 = 7.625 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Toughness values of 5–10 MJ/m³ are typical for tough engineering polymers such as PC and toughened nylons. A brittle polymer like PS shows only 0.1–0.5 MJ/m³. This metric drives material selection for impact-prone applications (helmets, automotive bumpers, phone cases).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Strain-Rate Sensitivity of Modulus (Polymer Viscoelasticity)

For a polymer tested at two strain rates, the modulus scales empirically as:

$$E(\dot{\varepsilon}_2) = E(\dot{\varepsilon}_1) \left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}_2}{\dot{\varepsilon}_1}\right)^{n}$$

where $n$ is the strain-rate sensitivity exponent (typically 0.02–0.10 for thermoplastics).

**Numerical Example:** If $E = 2.0$ GPa at $\dot{\varepsilon}_1 = 10^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$ and $n = 0.05$, what is the modulus at $\dot{\varepsilon}_2 = 10^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$?

$$E(10^{-1}) = 2.0 \times \left(\frac{10^{-1}}{10^{-3}}\right)^{0.05} = 2.0 \times (100)^{0.05}$$

$$(100)^{0.05} = 10^{0.10} = 1.259$$

$$E(10^{-1}) = 2.0 \times 1.259 = 2.52 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** A 100× increase in strain rate gives only a 26% increase in modulus. This low sensitivity is why polymers *appear* more "solid" in impact tests and more "rubbery" in slow creep.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Test Selection

| Desired Property | Recommended Test | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Stiffness under tension | Tensile (ASTM D638) | Direct modulus from stress-strain slope |
| Behavior in bending (beams) | Flexural (ASTM D790) | Mimics beam loading |
| Energy absorption in fracture | Impact (Izod/Charpy) | Quantifies energy to break |
| Long-term load resistance | Creep (ASTM D2990) | Time-dependent strain |
| Surface indentation resistance | Hardness (Shore/Rockwell) | Quick quality check |
| Cyclic load resistance | Fatigue (ASTM D7791) | S-N curve for life prediction |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the following terms as applied to polymeric materials under tensile loading:*
*(i) Engineering stress and engineering strain*
*(ii) Yield strength*
*(iii) Ultimate tensile strength (UTS)*
*(iv) Elongation at break*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

(i) **Engineering stress** ($\sigma$) is the instantaneous tensile load $F$ divided by the *original* cross-sectional area $A_0$: $\sigma = F/A_0$ (units: MPa). **Engineering strain** ($\varepsilon$) is the change in length $\Delta L$ relative to the original gauge length $L_0$: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ (dimensionless, often expressed in %). **(1 mark)**

(ii) **Yield strength** is the stress at which the polymer transitions from elastic (reversible) deformation to plastic (permanent) deformation. It is the practical upper limit for design of ductile polymer components. **(1 mark)**

(iii) **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** is the maximum engineering stress a material can sustain during the tensile test, occurring at the peak of the stress-strain curve. For many polymers, UTS is reached in the strain-hardening region before necking localizes into fracture. **(0.5 mark)**

(iv) **Elongation at break** is the percentage increase in gauge length at the moment of fracture: $\varepsilon_b = (\Delta L_f / L_0) \times 100\%$. It is a direct indicator of polymer ductility. **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Distinguish between **crazing** and **cracking** in glassy polymers such as polystyrene (PS) and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). Why is this distinction important for mechanical evaluation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Crazing | Cracking |
|---|---|---|
| Load-bearing capacity | Crazes can carry load (bridging fibrils) | Cracks cannot |
| Appearance | Whitened, opaque | Sharp, transparent fracture surface |
| Structure | Network of fibrils + voids | Clean separation |
| Reversibility | Can be stress-annealed away | Irreversible |
| Role in failure | Precursor to brittle fracture | Final failure event |

**Importance:** In mechanical evaluation, crazing in PS or PMMA often marks the *yield* event on the stress-strain curve — it is the visible, energy-absorbing precursor to brittle fracture. Distinguishing crazes from cracks is essential for accurate failure analysis and for designing toughening strategies (e.g., rubber-toughening converts crazes to shear yielding, increasing toughness 5–10×). **(Full 3 marks for clear table + implication statement.)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1)** — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a clearly labeled engineering stress-strain curve, describe the **five characteristic regions** of a ductile semi-crystalline polymer (e.g., low-density polyethylene) under uniaxial tensile loading. State the molecular interpretation of each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene (PP) tensile specimen has original dimensions: width = 10.0 mm, thickness = 4.0 mm, gauge length = 50 mm. The load–elongation data near the elastic region are:

| Load (N) | 0 | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 480 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Elongation (mm) | 0 | 0.025 | 0.050 | 0.075 | 0.100 | 0.120 |

Compute the **Young's modulus** of PP. If the specimen fractures at a load of 1.20 kN with elongation 18 mm, compute the **ultimate tensile strength** and **elongation at break (in %)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Region 1 — Linear Elastic:* The polymer deforms reversibly; chain segments stretch but return to their original conformations on unloading. Modulus $E$ is determined from the slope $\sigma/\varepsilon$. Molecularly, this corresponds to small perturbations of bond angles and Van der Waals distances. **(1.5 marks)**

*Region 2 — Yielding:* Onset of plastic deformation, marked by a peak ($\sigma_y$). Molecularly, this is associated with the initiation of **shear yielding** in amorphous regions and **interlamellar slip** in crystalline regions. In glassy polymers, **crazing** may accompany yielding. **(1.5 marks)**

*Region 3 — Strain Softening / Neck Formation:* Stress drops as a localized neck forms. Molecularly, the neck is a region of *aligned, oriented chains*; this alignment is energetically favorable, reducing the local flow stress. **(1 mark)**

*Region 4 — Cold Drawing (Neck Propagation):* The neck propagates along the gauge length at a roughly constant draw stress $\sigma_d$. Molecularly, this is the process of converting the *random-coil spherulitic morphology* into a highly oriented fibrillar morphology. **(1.5 marks)**

*Region 5 — Strain Hardening & Fracture:* As the entire gauge length is oriented, further deformation requires progressively more stress (strain hardening) until the material reaches its UTS and fractures. Molecularly, strain hardening reflects the finite extensibility of the now-aligned polymer chains. **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:*
$$A_0 = 10.0 \times 4.0 = 40.0 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

*Step 2 — Young's modulus from linear region (use first and last linear points: 100 N / 0.025 mm and 400 N / 0.100 mm):*

At $F = 100$ N, $\sigma = 100 / (4 \times 10^{-5}) = 2.5$ MPa, $\varepsilon = 0.025/50 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$
At $F = 400$ N, $\sigma = 400 / (4 \times 10^{-5}) = 10.0$ MPa, $\varepsilon = 0.100/50 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$

$$E = \frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon} = \frac{10.0 - 2.5}{2.0 \times 10^{-3} - 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{7.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 5000 \text{ MPa} = 5.0 \text{ GPa}$$

This value is consistent with semi-crystalline polypropylene. **(3 marks)**

*Step 3 — Ultimate Tensile Strength:*

Fracture load is 1.20 kN = 1200 N, but UTS is the *maximum* load sustained. From the data, the maximum tabulated load is 480 N (the last point in the elastic range, and presumably the maximum before the curve flattens). However, the question states fracture occurs at 1.20 kN, so we take:

$$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{1200}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**(2 marks)**

*Step 4 — Elongation at break:*

$$\varepsilon_b = \frac{18 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} \times 100\% = 36\%$$

**(2 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)** — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare the **mechanical testing methods** for polymers — tensile (ASTM D638), flexural (ASTM D790), and impact (Izod, ASTM D256). For each, state: (i) the type of loading, (ii) the specimen geometry, (iii) the primary output, and (iv) one engineering application for which the test is most relevant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer beam of rectangular cross-section (width 15 mm, thickness 5 mm) is tested in **3-point bending** with a span of 80 mm. The maximum load sustained is 240 N, and the slope of the initial linear portion of the load–deflection curve is 800 N/mm. Compute: (i) the **flexural strength** and (ii) the **flexural modulus** of the material. Identify one polymer that matches this combination. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Test | Loading Type | Specimen Geometry | Primary Output | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile (ASTM D638) | Uniaxial tension | Dumbbell (Type I/IV) | $E$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_{UTS}$, $\varepsilon_b$ | Load-bearing structural parts |
| Flexural (ASTM D790) | 3- or 4-point bending | Rectangular bar | $\sigma_f$, $E_f$ | Beams, panels, plastic hinges |
| Impact Izod (ASTM D256) | Pendulum impact on notched bar | Notched cantilever | Impact strength (J/m) | Crash-prone components |

**(3 marks for complete table; 4 marks for accurate elaboration of (i)–(iv) for at least two tests with application linkage.)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Flexural Strength (using $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bd^2)$):*

With $P = 240$ N, $L = 80$ mm, $b = 15$ mm, $d = 5$ mm:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 \times 240 \times 80}{2 \times 15 \times 5^2} = \frac{57{,}600}{750} = 76.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**(3 marks)**

*Step 2 — Flexural Modulus (using $E_f = L^3 m / (4bd^3)$):*

With $m = 800$ N/mm:

$$E_f = \frac{(80)^3 \times 800}{4 \times 15 \times (5)^3} = \frac{512{,}000 \times 800}{15{,}000} = \frac{4.096 \times 10^{8}}{1.5 \times 10^{4}}$$

$$E_f = 27{,}307 \text{ MPa} \approx 27.3 \text{ GPa}$$

**(3 marks)**

*Step 3 — Material Identification:*

A flexural modulus of ~27 GPa combined with a flexural strength of ~77 MPa is characteristic of **glass-fiber-reinforced polyamide (Nylon 6,6 + 30% GF)** or **polycarbonate (PC)**. Either answer is acceptable. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** are the #1 reason students lose marks. Always convert mm² to m² when stress in MPa is required (since 1 MPa = 1 N/mm², you can also work entirely in mm — but be consistent!).
> 2. **Confusing flexural stress with bending moment** — remember $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bd^2)$, not $PL/bd^2$ or $M/I$.
> 3. **Ignoring the "engineering" vs. "true" distinction** — KTU examiners routinely include 1-mark sub-questions on this.
> 4. **Skipping the region-by-region analysis** in tensile curve questions — 7-mark questions demand all 5 regions with molecular explanations.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert elongation to %** in elongation-at-break calculations — examiners explicitly award 1 mark for the correct % form.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **Stress = $F/A_0$; Strain = $\Delta L/L_0$** — both definitions use *original* dimensions (engineering definitions).
- **Young's Modulus** $E$ is the slope of the *initial linear* portion of the stress-strain curve. Units: **GPa** for polymers (range 0.001–5 GPa).
- **Five regions of a ductile polymer tensile curve:** Elastic → Yield → Strain Softening → Cold Drawing → Strain Hardening → Fracture.
- **Yielding in polymers** is *molecular* — shear yielding in semi-crystalline and glassy polymers; crazing in glassy polymers (PS, PMMA).
- **Cold drawing** converts spherulitic → fibrillar morphology; this is the basis of fiber and film orientation strengthening.
- **True stress** $\sigma_t = \sigma (1+\varepsilon)$; **True strain** $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$. Use these when $\varepsilon > 0.1$.
- **Toughness** = area under the entire stress-strain curve (energy/volume absorbed to fracture). **Resilience** = area under elastic region only.
- **Flexural strength:** $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bd^2)$ (3-point bend, ASTM D790).
- **Impact strength (Izod)** = absorbed energy / specimen width (J/m); **Charpy** = absorbed energy / cross-section (kJ/m²).
- **Hardness tests:** Shore A (soft elastomers), Shore D (rigid plastics), Rockwell R/M (engineering plastics).
- **Creep** is time-dependent strain under constant load; **stress relaxation** is time-dependent stress under constant strain. Both reflect **viscoelasticity**.
- **Time-Temperature Superposition (TTSP):** Higher strain rate ≈ lower temperature; characterized by WLF equation.
- **Strain-rate sensitivity:** $E \propto \dot{\varepsilon}^n$, with $n \approx 0.02$–$0.10$ for thermoplastics.
- **Standard reference temperatures:** Below $T_g$ → glassy (high modulus); Above $T_g$ (amorphous) → rubbery; Above $T_m$ (semi-crystalline) → viscous flow.
- **Five canonical polymer stress-strain classes:** Brittle, Strong-Tough, Strong-Semiductile, Soft-Tough, Elastomeric.
- **Always report the standard** (ASTM D638, ISO 527, ASTM D790, ASTM D256) when discussing mechanical tests.

---
